topics.title: The Need for Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Need for Security # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Computer Security (Cybersecurity)** is the collection of tools, policies, security safeguards, guidelines, risk management approaches, training, best practices, assurance, and technologies that can be used to protect the cyber environment, the organization, and the user's assets. According to the **International Telecommunication Union (ITU) Recommendation X.1205**, the overall goal of cybersecurity is to provide **availability, integrity, authenticity, confidentiality, and non-repudiation** of data and services in the cyber environment. **Computer Security** is broadly categorized into three primary pillars: - **Prevention** – Stopping security incidents before they occur. - **Detection** – Identifying incidents when they happen. - **Response** – Recovering and mitigating the impact after an incident. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Secured House** > Imagine your home is a computer system. You have: > - **Doors & Windows** → These are *Access Control Points* (like firewalls and login systems). > - **A Safe** → This is *Confidentiality* (protecting valuables/information). > - **Alarms** → These are *Intrusion Detection Systems*. > - **Insurance** → This is *Disaster Recovery & Backup*. > - **A Watchman** → This is *Authentication & Authorization*. > > Just as you wouldn't leave your house open in a crime-prone area, you cannot leave an internet-connected computer without protection. **The Need for Security arises because valuable information assets attract attackers.** ## 1.3 Why Is Security Needed? (Driving Forces) The **three critical forces** that drive the need for security are: | Force | Description | | :--- | :--- | | **Confidentiality Breach** | Sensitive data (medical, financial, military) is exposed. | | **Integrity Violation** | Data is modified or corrupted in unauthorized ways. | | **Service Disruption** | Critical services (banking, healthcare, power) become unavailable. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The famous 2017 WannaCry Ransomware Attack** affected over **230,000 computers in 150 countries**, causing an estimated **\$4 billion** in damages. This single incident demonstrates the catastrophic consequences of weak security. ## 1.4 The CIA Triad — The Core of Security > [!NOTE] > **The CIA Triad** is the foundational model upon which all cybersecurity policies are built. - **Confidentiality** – Ensures that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it. - **Integrity** – Ensures that information is accurate and has not been tampered with. - **Availability** – Ensures that authorized users have reliable access to information and resources when needed. ## 1.5 Extended Security Goals Modern cybersecurity expands on the CIA triad to include: - **Authenticity** – The identity of a sender/recipient can be verified. - **Accountability** – Actions of an entity can be traced uniquely to that entity. - **Non-Repudiation** – A sender cannot deny having sent a message, and a recipient cannot deny having received it. ## 1.6 Standard Security Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Industry Metrics (NIST & Verizon DBIR 2023):** > - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)**: ~**204 days** average > - **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)**: ~**73 days** average > - **Global cybercrime cost projection (2025)**: ~**\$10.5 trillion annually** (Cybersecurity Ventures) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of Computer Security The discipline of computer security is built upon **four intersecting concepts**: 1. **Confidentiality** – Privacy protection. 2. **Integrity** – Trustworthiness of data. 3. **Availability** – Reliability of access. 4. **Authenticity / Non-Repudiation** – Verification of identity. A failure in **any one** of these compromises the entire system. ## 2.2 Types of Threats A **threat** is any potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a system. > [!NOTE] > **Categories of Threats:** > - **Interruption** – An asset of the system becomes unavailable (Availability failure). > - **Interception** – An unauthorized party gains access to an asset (Confidentiality failure). > - **Modification** – An unauthorized party alters an asset (Integrity failure). > - **Fabrication** – An unauthorized party creates counterfeit objects (Authenticity failure). These are collectively known as the **four types of security threats** and map directly to the security goals. ## 2.3 Types of Attackers | Attacker Type | Profile | Motivation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Hackers (Black Hat)** | Skilled programmers; break security | Financial gain, malicious intent | | **Crackers** | Break into systems illegally | Ego, challenge | | **Script Kiddies** | Use pre-written scripts/tools | Curiosity, thrill | | **Insiders** | Employees or contractors | Revenge, financial gain | | **Hacktivists** | Politically/socially motivated | Ideology | | **State-Sponsored APTs** | Government-backed | Espionage, sabotage | | **Cybercriminals** | Organized criminal groups | Monetary profit | | **Phishers** | Use social engineering | Credential theft | ## 2.4 Vulnerabilities, Threats, and Risks — The Relationship > [!IMPORTANT] > **Risk Equation (Foundational Formula):** > $$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$$ - **Vulnerability** – A weakness in the system (e.g., unpatched software, weak passwords). - **Threat** – A potential danger that could exploit a vulnerability. - **Impact** – The consequence if the threat is realized. - **Risk** – The probability that a threat will exploit a vulnerability and cause harm. ## 2.5 Quantitative Risk Analysis (KTU Formula Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Quantitative Risk Formulas (NIST SP 800-30 Compliant):** | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Single Loss Expectancy** | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | $AV$ = Asset Value, $EF$ = Exposure Factor | | **Annualized Rate of Occurrence** | $ARO = \text{Estimated frequency per year}$ | Frequency of threat realization | | **Annualized Loss Expectancy** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Expected yearly loss | | **Risk Score** | $R = P \times C$ | $P$ = Probability, $C$ = Consequence | | **Return on Security Investment** | $ROSI = \frac{ALE - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Cost-effectiveness measure | > [!WARNING] > **Note on Notation:** In the above table, $\times$ represents multiplication, and $\vert$ (single vertical line) is NOT used. Use $\vert x \vert$ notation only outside table rows to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ## 2.6 Security Services & Mechanisms (ISO/IEC 7498-2) **Security Services:** - Data Confidentiality - Data Integrity - Authentication - Non-repudiation - Access Control - Availability **Security Mechanisms:** - Encipherment (Encryption) - Digital Signature - Access Control - Data Integrity - Authentication Exchange - Traffic Padding - Routing Control - Notarization ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in the industry:** > - **Banking & Finance** – Protects trillions in transactions. > - **Healthcare** – HIPAA compliance requires confidentiality of patient records. > - **Defense** – National security depends on secure communication channels. > - **E-Commerce** – Customer trust is built on transaction security. > - **IoT & Critical Infrastructure** – Power grids, water systems, and transportation rely on cybersecurity. ## 2.8 The OSI Security Architecture (X.800) The **ITU-T Recommendation X.800 (OSI Security Architecture)** defines a systematic framework: - **Security Attack** – Any action that compromises security. - **Security Mechanism** – A process designed to detect, prevent, or recover from an attack. - **Security Service** – A service that enhances the security of data processing and information transfer. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) Formula This is the most tested quantitative formula in KTU examinations. We derive it step-by-step. ### Step 1: Define Asset Value (AV) The **Asset Value (AV)** is the monetary worth of a system or data asset to the organization. $$AV = \text{Total replacement cost} + \text{Value of data}$$ **Explanation:** Asset value includes hardware, software, and the data it stores. For example, a customer database worth **\$500,000**. ### Step 2: Define Exposure Factor (EF) The **Exposure Factor (EF)** is the percentage of asset value that is lost in a single security incident. It is expressed as a decimal. $$EF = \frac{\text{Loss from single incident}}{\text{Asset Value}}$$ **Explanation:** If a ransomware attack encrypts 60% of a database, then $EF = 0.60$. ### Step 3: Derive Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) The **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** is the expected monetary loss from a single occurrence of a threat. $$\begin{aligned} SLE &= \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor} \\ SLE &= AV \times EF \end{aligned}$$ **Explanation:** Multiply the total asset value by the fraction of loss expected. This gives the loss for one incident. ### Step 4: Define Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) The **ARO** is the estimated frequency with which a threat will occur in a year. $$ARO = \text{Expected number of incidents per year}$$ **Explanation:** If a phishing attack is expected to succeed twice a year, then $ARO = 2.0$. ### Step 5: Derive Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) The **ALE** is the expected monetary loss per year due to a specific threat. $$\begin{aligned} ALE &= SLE \times ARO \\ ALE &= (AV \times EF) \times ARO \end{aligned}$$ **Explanation:** The yearly loss is the single-incident loss multiplied by the number of times it occurs annually. ### Step 6: Derive Return on Security Investment (ROSI) **ROSI** measures whether a security control is financially justified. $$\begin{aligned} ROSI &= \frac{(ALE\ before\ control - ALE\ after\ control) - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control} \\ ROSI &= \frac{(ALE_1 - ALE_2) - C}{C} \end{aligned}$$ **Explanation:** Subtract the cost of the control from the loss it prevents, and divide by the control cost. A positive ROSI means the control is worth the investment. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Question Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A company has a customer database worth **\$1,000,000**. A SQL Injection attack is expected to expose 40% of the data. Historical data shows the attack occurs **3 times per year**. Calculate SLE, ARO, and ALE. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Identify given values:** $$AV = \$1{,}000{,}000, \quad EF = 0.40, \quad ARO = 3$$ **Step 2 — Calculate SLE:** $$\begin{aligned} SLE &= AV \times EF \\ &= 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.40 \\ &= \$400{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — State ARO:** $$ARO = 3 \text{ incidents/year}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate ALE:** $$\begin{aligned} ALE &= SLE \times ARO \\ &= 400{,}000 \times 3 \\ &= \$1{,}200{,}000 \text{ per year} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** The company stands to lose approximately **\$1.2 million annually** if no security control is implemented. Any control costing less than this and reducing the risk is justified. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Security Investment > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite: Justify Security Investments Using ALE** | Scenario | ALE Before Control | ALE After Control | Control Cost (C) | ROSI | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Firewall** | \$1,200,000 | \$100,000 | \$200,000 | Positive (Worth it) | | **Biometric** | \$50,000 | \$5,000 | \$60,000 | Negative (Not worth it) | | **IDS** | \$800,000 | \$150,000 | \$100,000 | Positive (Worth it) | The decision rule: **Implement the control if and only if ROSI > 0.** ## 3.4 Threat × Vulnerability × Impact (TVI) Calculation $$\begin{aligned} R_{total} &= T \times V \times I \\ R_{total} &= (0.7) \times (0.8) \times (10) \\ R_{total} &= 5.6 \text{ (on a scale of 10)} \end{aligned}$$ Where $T = 0.7$ (threat probability), $V = 0.8$ (vulnerability score), and $I = 10$ (impact magnitude). **Interpretation:** A risk score of **5.6/10** indicates a **HIGH risk**, requiring immediate mitigation. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The CIA Triad — Architectural Overview ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef triadNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#4a90e2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold classDef subNode fill:#2d5a87,stroke:#5dade2,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef attackNode fill:#7b241c,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef defenseNode fill:#1e8449,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff C[Confidentiality]:::triadNode I[Integrity]:::triadNode A[Availability]:::triadNode C1[Data Privacy]:::subNode C2[Access Control]:::subNode C3[Encryption]:::subNode I1[Data Accuracy]:::subNode I2[Hashing]:::subNode I3[Digital Signatures]:::subNode A1[System Uptime]:::subNode A2[Redundancy]:::subNode A3[Backup Recovery]:::subNode A1a[Interception Attack]:::attackNode A1b[Modification Attack]:::attackNode A1c[Interruption Attack]:::attackNode D1[Firewalls]:::defenseNode D2[IDS IPS]:::defenseNode D3[Authentication Systems]:::defenseNode C --- C1 C --- C2 C --- C3 I --- I1 I --- I2 I --- I3 A --- A1 A --- A2 A --- A3 C1a[Attack] -.->|threatens| C I1a[Attack] -.->|threatens| I A1a[Attack] -.->|threatens| A D1 -.->|protects| C D2 -.->|protects| I D3 -.->|protects| A ``` ## 4.2 Threat-Vulnerability-Impact Risk Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef inputNode fill:#2e4053,stroke:#5d6d7e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef procNode fill:#b9770e,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef outputNode fill:#922b21,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef mitigationNode fill:#196f3d,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff A[Asset
Database Server]:::inputNode T[Threat Source
External Hacker]:::inputNode V[Vulnerability
Unpatched SQLi Flaw]:::inputNode R{Risk Calculation
T x V x I}:::procNode I[Impact
Data Breach]:::outputNode M1[Apply Patch]:::mitigationNode M2[Deploy WAF]:::mitigationNode M3[Network Segmentation]:::mitigationNode A --> R T --> R V --> R R --> I I --> M1 I --> M2 I --> M3 ``` ## 4.3 Security Incident Lifecycle (NIST Incident Response Framework) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef phase1 fill:#1a5276,stroke:#3498db,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef phase2 fill:#117a65,stroke:#1abc9c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef phase3 fill:#9a7d0a,stroke:#f1c40f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef phase4 fill:#922b21,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef phase5 fill:#5b2c6f,stroke:#8e44ad,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef phase6 fill:#212f3d,stroke:#34495e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff P1[1 Preparation]:::phase1 P2[2 Identification]:::phase2 P3[3 Containment]:::phase3 P4[4 Eradication]:::phase4 P5[5 Recovery]:::phase5 P6[6 Lessons Learned]:::phase6 P1 -->|proactive controls| P2 P2 -->|confirm breach| P3 P3 -->|isolate affected| P4 P4 -->|remove malware| P5 P5 -->|restore operations| P6 P6 -->|feedback| P1 ``` ## 4.4 OSI Security Architecture Mapping (X.800) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef layerNode fill:#1b4f72,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef serviceNode fill:#76448a,stroke:#8e44ad,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef mechanismNode fill:#1e8449,stroke:#239b56,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff L1[Application Layer]:::layerNode L2[Presentation Layer]:::layerNode L3[Session Layer]:::layerNode L4[Transport Layer]:::layerNode L5[Network Layer]:::layerNode L6[Data Link Layer]:::layerNode L7[Physical Layer]:::layerNode S1[Data Confidentiality]:::serviceNode S2[Data Integrity]:::serviceNode S3[Authentication]:::serviceNode S4[Access Control]:::serviceNode S5[Non Repudiation]:::serviceNode M1[Encipherment]:::mechanismNode M2[Digital Signature]:::mechanismNode M3[Access Control Lists]:::mechanismNode M4[Data Integrity Checks]:::mechanismNode L1 --> S1 L1 --> S2 L1 --> S3 L1 --> S5 L2 --> M1 L3 --> M2 L4 --> M4 L5 --> M3 S1 --- M1 S2 --- M4 S3 --- M2 S5 --- M2 ``` ## 4.5 Security Attack Categorization (Passive vs. Active) ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef rootNode fill:#1a237e,stroke:#3949ab,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef passiveNode fill:#00695c,stroke:#00897b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef activeNode fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#e53935,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef leafNode fill:#37474f,stroke:#546e7a,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff A[Security Attacks]:::rootNode B1[Passive Attacks]:::passiveNode B2[Active Attacks]:::activeNode C1[Release of Message Contents]:::leafNode C2[Traffic Analysis]:::leafNode D1[Masquerade]:::leafNode D2[Replay Attack]:::leafNode D3[Modification of Messages]:::leafNode D4[Denial of Service]:::leafNode A --> B1 A --> B2 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B2 --> D1 B2 --> D2 B2 --> D3 B2 --> D4 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Computer Security. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Model Answer:** Computer Security refers to the collection of measures, protocols, tools, policies, and best practices designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of computer systems, networks, and data from unauthorized access, modification, or destruction. It encompasses preventive, detective, and corrective controls to safeguard information assets against both internal and external threats. The primary goals are summarized by the **CIA triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability), along with authenticity and non-repudiation. --- ### Question 2: List and briefly explain the four types of security threats. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** The four types of security threats are: 1. **Interruption** – An asset of the system is destroyed or becomes unavailable (threatens Availability). Example: Server crash. 2. **Interception** – An unauthorized party gains access to an asset (threatens Confidentiality). Example: Wiretapping. 3. **Modification** – An unauthorized party tampers with an asset (threatens Integrity). Example: Changing database records. 4. **Fabrication** – An unauthorized party creates fake objects (threatens Authenticity). Example: Adding fictitious entries. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme, you must answer **ONE full question** from the choice provided. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **Q. (a)** Define the CIA Triad. With neat diagrams, explain how each component is threatened and the corresponding security mechanisms. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security comprising three core principles: **1. Confidentiality:** - Ensures that sensitive information is accessed only by authorized users. - **Threats:** Interception, eavesdropping, shoulder surfing. - **Mechanisms:** Encryption (AES, RSA), Access Control Lists, Multi-Factor Authentication. **2. Integrity:** - Ensures that data is accurate and unaltered during storage or transmission. - **Threats:** Modification, man-in-the-middle attacks, Trojan horses. - **Mechanisms:** Hashing (SHA-256), Digital Signatures, Message Authentication Codes (MAC). **3. Availability:** - Ensures that systems and data are accessible when needed by authorized users. - **Threats:** Denial-of-Service (DoS), hardware failures, ransomware. - **Mechanisms:** Redundancy, Backups, Firewalls, Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS). ``` [Diagram: Three overlapping circles labeled C, I, A showing their interrelation and common security mechanisms] ``` --- **Q. (b)** A company's online transaction server has an asset value of **₹80,00,000**. A DDoS attack is estimated to disrupt 70% of services per occurrence. If the attack is expected to occur **4 times per year**, calculate the SLE, ARO, and ALE. If a mitigation system costs **₹5,00,000 per year** and reduces the ALE to **₹2,00,000**, determine the ROSI. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Given Values:** $$AV = \text{₹}80{,}00{,}000, \quad EF = 0.70, \quad ARO = 4, \quad C = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Calculate SLE:** $$\begin{aligned} SLE &= AV \times EF \\ &= 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 \\ &= \text{₹}56{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — State ARO:** $$ARO = 4 \text{ incidents/year}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate ALE (Before Control):** $$\begin{aligned} ALE_1 &= SLE \times ARO \\ &= 56{,}00{,}000 \times 4 \\ &= \text{₹}2{,}24{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate ROSI:** $$\begin{aligned} ROSI &= \frac{(ALE_1 - ALE_2) - C}{C} \\ &= \frac{(2{,}24{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000) - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\ &= \frac{2{,}22{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\ &= \frac{2{,}17{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\ &= 43.4 \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** Since ROSI = **43.4 (positive)**, the mitigation system is highly cost-effective and **should be implemented**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Q. (a)** Explain the different categories of attackers in computer security. Compare passive and active security attacks with examples. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Categories of Attackers:** 1. **Hackers / Black Hats** – Individuals with technical skills who break into systems for personal gain or malicious intent. 2. **Crackers** – Specifically break into systems to cause damage or steal data. 3. **Script Kiddies** – Use pre-made tools and scripts without deep technical knowledge. 4. **Insiders** – Employees or contractors with legitimate access who misuse their privileges. 5. **Hacktivists** – Attack for political, social, or ideological reasons (e.g., Anonymous). 6. **State-Sponsored APTs** – Government-backed attackers conducting long-term espionage (e.g., Stuxnet). 7. **Cybercriminals** – Organized groups seeking financial profit (ransomware, banking fraud). **Comparison: Passive vs. Active Attacks:** | Aspect | Passive Attacks | Active Attacks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | Monitors/eavesdrops on data | Modifies, fabricates, or disrupts data | | **Detection** | Very difficult | Easier (changes system state) | | **System Effect** | No modification of data | Modifies resources or operations | | **Examples** | Traffic analysis, eavesdropping | DoS, Masquerade, Replay, Modification | | **Goal** | Information gathering | Disruption, damage, or fraud | **Examples:** - **Passive:** Wiretapping a phone line without altering the conversation. - **Active:** A DDoS attack flooding a server to make it unavailable. --- **Q. (b)** Explain the OSI Security Architecture (X.800). List the security services and mechanisms defined in the architecture. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **ITU-T X.800 – OSI Security Architecture:** The **X.800 recommendation** by ITU-T provides a systematic framework for security in OSI networks. It defines three key concepts: - **Security Attack** – Any action that compromises the security of information. - **Security Mechanism** – A process designed to detect, prevent, or recover from attacks. - **Security Service** – A service that enhances the security of data processing and information transfer. **Five Security Services (X.800):** 1. **Data Confidentiality** – Protection from unauthorized disclosure. 2. **Data Integrity** – Detection of modification, insertion, deletion, or replay. 3. **Authentication** – Assurance that the communicating entity is genuine. 4. **Non-Repudiation** – Prevention of denial of having sent/received data. 5. **Access Control** – Restriction of access to authorized entities only. **Eight Specific Security Mechanisms (X.800):** 1. Encipherment (Encryption) 2. Digital Signature 3. Access Control 4. Data Integrity 5. Authentication Exchange 6. Traffic Padding 7. Routing Control 8. Notarization **Pervasive Security Mechanisms:** - Trusted Functionality - Security Labels - Event Detection - Security Audit Trails - Security Recovery **Conclusion:** The X.800 framework ensures that security is implemented systematically at every layer of network communication, providing a holistic approach to protecting information assets. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing SLE and ALE** – Students often mix these up. Remember: **SLE is for ONE incident**; **ALE is for ONE YEAR** (SLE × ARO). > 2. **Forgetting the unit** – Always write the currency (₹ or \$) in your final answer. > 3. **Missing the CIA explanation** – Simply listing the triad is **NOT enough**. You must explain **threats** and **mechanisms** for each component. > 4. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark questions** – KTU examiners specifically deduct marks if visual aids (diagrams/tables) are absent in long answers. > 5. **No example** – A long answer without a real-world example (e.g., WannaCry, Equifax breach) loses 1–2 marks in the "Application" CO level. > 6. **Incorrect ROSI formula** – Some students use $\frac{ALE}{C}$ instead of the correct $(ALE_1 - ALE_2 - C) \div C$. Memorize it carefully. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points for "The Need for Security":** - ✅ **CIA Triad** = **C**onfidentiality + **I**ntegrity + **A**vailability (the core of security). - ✅ **Extended Goals** = Authenticity, Accountability, Non-Repudiation. - ✅ **Four Threat Types** = Interruption, Interception, Modification, Fabrication. - ✅ **Two Attack Categories** = Passive (eavesdropping) vs. Active (DoS, Replay, Masquerade). - ✅ **Risk Formula** = $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$. - ✅ **SLE Formula** = $SLE = AV \times EF$ (Single Loss Expectancy). - ✅ **ALE Formula** = $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ (Annualized Loss Expectancy). - ✅ **ROSI Formula** = $ROSI = \frac{(ALE_1 - ALE_2) - C}{C}$. - ✅ **X.800 Standards** define 5 security services and 8 security mechanisms. - ✅ **NIST Incident Response Lifecycle** = Preparation → Identification → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned. - ✅ **Global cybercrime cost** projected to reach **\$10.5 trillion annually by 2025** (Cybersecurity Ventures). - ✅ **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect)** industry average = **~204 days**. - ✅ **Attackers** include Black Hats, Crackers, Script Kiddies, Insiders, Hacktivists, APTs, and Cybercriminals. - ✅ **Always include** diagrams, examples, and currency units in your answers. - ✅ **KTU Bloom's Level** for this topic = **Remember (L1)**, **Understand (L2)**, and **Apply (L3)** — focus on definitions, comparisons, and numerical calculations.

topics.title: Security Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Approaches** are the structured, strategic methodologies, principles, and control frameworks employed to design, implement, and maintain the confidentiality, integrity, availability, authentication, authorization, and non-repudiation (commonly abbreviated as the **CIA-AAN Triad Plus**) of information assets within an information system.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, a *Security Approach* is not merely a single tool, but a holistic philosophy that defines *how* an organization identifies, classifies, prioritizes, and mitigates cyber risks. It governs decision-making across people, processes, and technology layers.

The fundamental categorizations of security approaches include:

1. **Functional Security Approaches** (Preventive, Detective, Corrective, Deterrent, Recovery, Compensating).
2. **Design Philosophies** (Security by Design, Security through Obscurity, Defense in Depth, Zero Trust).
3. **Risk-Based Approaches** (Asset-based, Threat-based, Vulnerability-based).
4. **Compliance-Based Approaches** (Regulatory, Standards-based).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bank Vault Metaphor**

Imagine a bank protecting its gold reserves. The bank does **not** rely on a single massive steel door. Instead, it layers its defenses:

- A **guard at the gate** (Deterrent — visible security cameras and armed guards discourage robbers).
- A **locked vault door** (Preventive — stops intruders from getting in).
- **Motion sensors inside the vault** (Detective — detects if someone is already inside).
- A **sprinkler and fire-suppression system** (Corrective — mitigates damage if a fire starts).
- A **duplicate copy of records stored in a separate branch** (Recovery — business continuity).
- A **timed lock with a secondary keypad** (Compensating — an alternative control when the main biometric fails).

This is precisely how **Security Approaches** work in cyber space. No single approach is enough; they must be combined in layers to form a resilient security posture.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Security Approaches

- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average time taken to identify a security incident. Industry standard target is **< 24 hours**.
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Average time to contain and remediate after detection. Standard target is **< 72 hours**.
- **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE):** Expected monetary loss per year from a specific threat.
- **Return on Security Investment (ROSI):** Quantifies the financial benefit derived from a security control.

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational metric pillar of every security approach:

$$\text{Confidentiality} + \text{Integrity} + \text{Availability} = \text{Security Posture}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Security Approaches

#### **A. Functional Security Controls (The Six Pillars)**

These are categorized based on **what they do** when triggered:

| # | Approach | Purpose | Real-World Example | Trigger Phase |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Preventive** | Stop an attack *before* it happens | Firewall, Access Control Lists, Encryption | Pre-incident |
| 2 | **Detective** | Identify that an attack *is happening* or has happened | IDS, SIEM, Audit Logs, CCTV | During/Post-incident |
| 3 | **Corrective** | Mitigate damage *after* an incident | Antivirus quarantine, Patching, System Restore | Post-incident |
| 4 | **Deterrent** | Discourage attackers from attempting an attack | Warning banners, Legal notices, Security guards | Pre-incident |
| 5 | **Recovery** | Restore systems to normal operation | Backups, Disaster Recovery Sites, BCP | Post-incident |
| 6 | **Compensating** | Alternative control when primary control is unavailable | Manual review when automated scanner fails | Continuous |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When asked "Classify the security approaches," students frequently forget **Deterrent** and **Compensating**. Memorizing all *six* is essential to secure full marks in 14-mark questions.

#### **B. Design Philosophies of Security**

| Philosophy | Core Principle | Weakness |
|------------|----------------|----------|
| **Security by Design** | Security is embedded from the *initial design* stage (shift-left) | Higher upfront cost; requires security-skilled architects |
| **Security through Obscurity** | Relying on secrecy of the system itself | Considered an *anti-pattern*; fails once discovered |
| **Defense in Depth** | Multiple overlapping layers of security controls | Complexity; can introduce configuration drift |
| **Zero Trust** | "Never trust, always verify" — no implicit trust | High operational overhead; requires mature IAM |
| **Least Privilege** | Users get the *minimum* access required | Role explosion; difficult to maintain |
| **Fail-Safe / Fail-Secure** | System defaults to a secure state on failure | May disrupt legitimate operations |

#### **C. Risk-Based vs. Compliance-Based Approach**

- **Risk-Based Approach:** Prioritizes security investments based on the *actual likelihood and impact* of threats. Driven by quantitative or qualitative risk assessments.
- **Compliance-Based Approach:** Implements security controls primarily to satisfy *regulatory mandates* (e.g., IT Act 2000, GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS). May leave gaps if compliance ≠ security.

> [!NOTE]
> The **best practice (KTU-recommended)** is a hybrid: use compliance as a *baseline* and risk analysis as the *differentiator* for additional controls.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Purpose | Units |
|------------------|------------|---------|-------|
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | Expected loss from a single occurrence | Currency (₹/\$) |
| **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** | Empirical count per year | Frequency of threat realization | Events/year |
| **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Expected annual loss from a threat | Currency/year |
| **Cost-Benefit (CBA)** | $CBA = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost\ of\ Control$ | Justifies investment in a control | Currency |
| **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** | $ROSI = \dfrac{(ALE \times Risk\ Reduction\%) - Control\ Cost}{Control\ Cost} \times 100$ | Measures financial viability | Percentage |
| **Risk Score** | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Qualitative risk ranking | Unitless index |
| **Mean Time to Detect** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum Detection\ Time_i}{N_{incidents}}$ | Detective control effectiveness | Hours |
| **Mean Time to Respond** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum Response\ Time_i}{N_{incidents}}$ | Corrective control effectiveness | Hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Questions on **ALE** and **ROSI** are direct **3-mark short-answer** favorites. Practice substituting values accurately. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for any division symbols to maintain table integrity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & FinTech:** Combines Preventive (firewall), Detective (fraud analytics), and Recovery (disaster recovery site) approaches.
- **Healthcare:** Compensating controls (manual patient-data reconciliation) safeguard against EHR system failures.
- **Critical Infrastructure (SCADA/ICS):** Defense in Depth with strict network segmentation is mandatory.
- **Cloud Computing:** Zero Trust + Least Privilege + Encryption-in-transit (the modern triad).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Threat to Risk Quantification

This is the **most repeated derivation** in KTU cyber security modules. We derive the *Annualized Loss Expectancy* step-by-step, beginning from a single incident.

**Step 1 — Define Asset Value (AV).**
Let the asset value be the replacement cost or business value of the resource being protected.

$$AV = \text{Quantitative worth of the information asset in monetary terms.}$$

*Textual Interpretation:* The asset value is the gross financial impact if the asset is fully compromised.

**Step 2 — Define Exposure Factor (EF).**
The exposure factor is the percentage of the asset that would be lost in a *single* successful incident. It is always expressed as a decimal between **0 and 1**.

$$EF \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 3 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE).**
The SLE is the expected monetary loss from one occurrence of the threat:

$$\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AV \times EF \\
\text{where: } & AV = \text{Asset Value},\ EF = \text{Exposure Factor}
\end{aligned}$$

*Textual Interpretation:* Multiply the total asset value by the fraction of loss expected in one event.

**Step 4 — Estimate the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO).**
The ARO is the estimated number of times the threat will materialize in a year, based on historical data or expert judgment.

$$ARO = \frac{\text{Number of expected incidents per year}}{1}$$

**Step 5 — Derive Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE).**
The ALE is the product of SLE and ARO. This is the **risk in monetary terms per year**:

$$\begin{aligned}
ALE &= SLE \times ARO \\
&= (AV \times EF) \times ARO
\end{aligned}$$

*Textual Interpretation:* The expected annual loss equals the loss per incident multiplied by the number of incidents per year.

**Step 6 — Cost-Benefit Justification of a Control.**
A proposed control has an *Annual Cost of Control (AC)* and reduces risk by *Risk Reduction % (RR)*. The net benefit is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= (ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}) - AC \\
&= (ALE_{before} \times RR) - AC
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Derive Return on Security Investment (ROSI).**

$$\begin{aligned}
ROSI &= \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{AC} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{(ALE_{before} \times RR) - AC}{AC} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

*Decision Rule:* If $ROSI > 0$, the control is financially justified.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A company's customer database is valued at **₹50,00,000**. A ransomware attack would compromise **60%** of the data. The ARO is estimated at **0.5 per year**. A proposed endpoint-detection system costs **₹5,00,000/year** and reduces the risk by **80%**. Calculate the SLE, ALE, and ROSI.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AV \times EF \\
&= 50,00,000 \times 0.60 \\
&= \text{₹}30,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

Next, compute the ALE before control:

$$\begin{aligned}
ALE_{before} &= SLE \times ARO \\
&= 30,00,000 \times 0.5 \\
&= \text{₹}15,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute the ALE after the control is deployed:

$$\begin{aligned}
ALE_{after} &= ALE_{before} \times (1 - RR) \\
&= 15,00,000 \times (1 - 0.80) \\
&= 15,00,000 \times 0.20 \\
&= \text{₹}3,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

Finally, compute the ROSI:

$$\begin{aligned}
ROSI &= \frac{(ALE_{before} \times RR) - AC}{AC} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{(15,00,000 \times 0.80) - 5,00,000}{5,00,000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{12,00,000 - 5,00,000}{5,00,000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7,00,000}{5,00,000} \times 100 \\
&= 140\%
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* A ROSI of **140%** means the control saves ₹1.40 for every ₹1.00 spent. The investment is **strongly justified**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Approaches to Threats

| Threat Scenario | Primary Approach | Secondary Approach | Justification |
|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Phishing email | Preventive (email filter) | Detective (SIEM alert) | Block known patterns, detect unknown |
| Insider data theft | Preventive (least privilege) | Detective (UEBA) | Limit access, monitor behaviour |
| Ransomware | Preventive (patching) | Recovery (offline backup) | Stop entry vector, ensure restore path |
| DDoS attack | Detective (anomaly detection) | Compensating (rate limiting) | Detect surge, throttle traffic |
| Stolen laptop | Preventive (encryption) | Deterrent (warning sticker) | Data unreadable, deterrent visible |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Preventive and Detective security controls with suitable examples.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive Control | Detective Control |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Purpose** | Stops an attack *before* it occurs | Identifies an attack *during* or *after* it occurs |
| **Timing** | Pre-incident | During/Post-incident |
| **Examples** | Firewall, Encryption, Access Control Lists | Intrusion Detection System, SIEM, Audit logs |
| **Metric** | Block-rate % | MTTD (Mean Time to Detect) |

> A preventive control is like a *locked door*; a detective control is like a *burglar alarm*.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. Define "Defense in Depth" as a security approach. Why is it preferred over a single-layer security model?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Defense in Depth is a layered security approach in which multiple independent security controls are deployed at different layers (perimeter, network, host, application, data) so that the failure of one control does not result in total compromise.

**Reasons for preference over single-layer security:**

- Provides **redundancy** — if one control fails, others compensate.
- **Increases attacker cost** — the attacker must defeat multiple heterogeneous controls.
- **Addresses diverse threats** — different layers counter different attack vectors.
- **Supports the principle of least privilege** and **Zero Trust** at granular levels.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Classify and explain the **six functional security approaches** in detail. Give one real-world example for each. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A server room contains equipment valued at **₹20,00,000**. A flood event would damage **40%** of the equipment. The ARO is estimated at **0.25 per year**. A flood sensor and water-removal system costs **₹1,50,000/year** and reduces the risk by **90%**. Calculate the **SLE, ALE before control, ALE after control,** and the **ROSI**. Conclude whether the investment is justified. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) The Six Functional Security Approaches:**

1. **Preventive Controls:** These are designed to *block* unauthorized activity *before* it happens. They are the first line of defence. *Example:* A firewall blocking an unauthorized IP from accessing the corporate network.

2. **Detective Controls:** These *identify* and *log* that an attack is happening or has happened. They do not prevent but ensure awareness. *Example:* An Intrusion Detection System raising an alert on a port-scan attempt.

3. **Corrective Controls:** These *minimize damage* and *restore proper functioning* after an incident. *Example:* An antivirus software quarantining a detected malware sample.

4. **Deterrent Controls:** These *discourage* potential attackers by making the risk of attack seem high. *Example:* A "This system is monitored" login banner, or visible CCTV cameras.

5. **Recovery Controls:** These *restore systems* to their normal operational state following a disruption. *Example:* Restoring a database from an off-site backup after a ransomware attack.

6. **Compensating Controls:** These are *alternative* controls that substitute for a primary control that is temporarily unavailable or impractical. *Example:* Manual review of transactions when an automated fraud-detection system is offline.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute SLE:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AV \times EF \\
&= 20,00,000 \times 0.40 \\
&= \text{₹}8,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE before control:**

$$\begin{aligned}
ALE_{before} &= SLE \times ARO \\
&= 8,00,000 \times 0.25 \\
&= \text{₹}2,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE after control:**

$$\begin{aligned}
ALE_{after} &= ALE_{before} \times (1 - RR) \\
&= 2,00,000 \times (1 - 0.90) \\
&= 2,00,000 \times 0.10 \\
&= \text{₹}20,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROSI:**

$$\begin{aligned}
ROSI &= \frac{(ALE_{before} \times RR) - AC}{AC} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{(2,00,000 \times 0.90) - 1,50,000}{1,50,000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1,80,000 - 1,50,000}{1,50,000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{30,000}{1,50,000} \times 100 \\
&= 20\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The ROSI is **20%** (positive), so the flood sensor investment is financially justified, though the margin is slim. Management may wish to negotiate a lower control cost.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the following security design philosophies: **Security by Design, Security through Obscurity, Defense in Depth,** and **Zero Trust.** Use a comparative table and discuss which is most suitable for a cloud-native microservices architecture. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Risk-Based Security Approach** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages over the **Compliance-Based Approach** in a modern enterprise. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Comparative Analysis of Design Philosophies:**

| Philosophy | Core Idea | Strength | Weakness | Cloud-Native Fit |
|------------|-----------|----------|----------|------------------|
| **Security by Design** | Embed security in initial design | Robust, fewer retro fixes | High upfront cost | High |
| **Security through Obscurity** | Rely on secrecy of system | Quick to implement | Anti-pattern; fragile | Very Low |
| **Defense in Depth** | Layered controls | Redundancy, resilience | Operational complexity | Medium-High |
| **Zero Trust** | Never trust, always verify | Strong for distributed systems | Heavy IAM overhead | Highest |

**Most Suitable for Cloud-Native Microservices:** **Zero Trust.** Cloud-native systems are inherently distributed, with workloads spanning multiple VPCs, regions, and providers. The "no implicit trust" model combined with microsegmentation, mTLS, and policy-based access (e.g., SPIFFE/SPIRE, OPA) makes Zero Trust the natural fit.

**(b) Risk-Based vs. Compliance-Based Approach:**

The **Risk-Based Approach** follows the flow:
1. Identify assets.
2. Assign asset values.
3. Identify threats and vulnerabilities.
4. Compute risk $= Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$.
5. Select controls proportional to risk.
6. Continuously monitor and reassess.

**Advantages of Risk-Based over Compliance-Based:**

- **Prioritization:** Focuses resources on the *highest-impact* risks, not on checklist items.
- **Cost-Efficiency:** Avoids over-investing in compliance-driven controls that may have low actual risk value.
- **Dynamic:** Adapts to the evolving threat landscape.
- **Holistic:** Considers business context, unlike compliance which is rule-bound.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students confuse *Recovery* with *Corrective*. *Corrective* stops the damage mid-flight (e.g., isolating an infected host); *Recovery* restores the system to working order (e.g., restoring from backup). This distinction is worth **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In ROSI problems, students forget to *subtract the control cost (AC)* from the benefit, leading to a wrong numerator. Marks lost here are typically **1–2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "Security through Obscurity" as a *good* approach. KTU strictly considers it an **anti-pattern**. Mentioning it positively will cost marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Omitting units (₹, hours) in the final answer of numericals. Always state units explicitly.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Security Approaches**

- **Six Functional Controls:** Preventive, Detective, Corrective, Deterrent, Recovery, Compensating — *memorize all six, including their trigger phase.*
- **Five Design Philosophies:** Security by Design, Security through Obscurity (anti-pattern), Defense in Depth, Zero Trust, Least Privilege.
- **Risk Equation:** $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$.
- **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO = (AV \times EF) \times ARO$.
- **ROSI Formula:** $ROSI = \dfrac{(ALE_{before} \times RR) - AC}{AC} \times 100$.
- **Decision Rule for ROSI:** Positive value ⇒ control is justified; Negative value ⇒ reject the control.
- **MTTD / MTTR:** Detective control efficiency metrics.
- **CIA Triad Plus:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, plus Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation.
- **Hybrid Best Practice:** Use *Compliance* as a baseline, *Risk-Based* analysis as the differentiator.
- **Cloud-Native Default:** Zero Trust + Least Privilege + Encryption in transit and at rest.
- **Avoid:** Over-relying on a single approach; treating compliance as a one-time project.
- **Remember:** Every security approach must align with one of the **CIA-AAN** properties to be considered valid in a KTU answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Principles of Security"?

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEOT741)**, the *Principles of Security* form the foundational pillar upon which the entire discipline of cyber security rests. The **Principles of Security** are the universally accepted axioms, design philosophies, and operational doctrines that govern how digital information, computing assets, and communication channels are protected from adversarial action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The *Principles of Security* are a set of confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA) requirements that, when satisfied, ensure the safe and reliable operation of computing systems and the trustworthy transit, storage, and processing of information across them. They include concepts such as authentication, authorization, non-repudiation, and access control.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars — The CIA Triad

The **CIA Triad** is the cornerstone of all information security thinking. Every security control, every cryptographic primitive, and every policy in the industry maps back to one of these three pillars.

| Pillar | Core Question It Answers | One-Line Summary |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | *Who is allowed to see this?* | Hiding information from unauthorized eyes. |
| **Integrity** | *Can I trust this data is unaltered?* | Guaranteeing data has not been tampered with. |
| **Availability** | *Can I use the system when I need it?* | Ensuring systems are operational on demand. |

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Three-Layer Bank Vault**
> - **Confidentiality** is the *reinforced steel door with biometric locks* — only pre-approved people can walk in.
> - **Integrity** is the *silent alarm and CCTV inside* — if someone moves a single coin, the system detects it.
> - **Availability** is the *24/7 generator and redundant power lines* — even during a storm, the vault is open to you.
>
> A vault that is *secret* but *you can never open it* fails. A vault that is *always open* but anyone can enter fails. A vault that is *locked and powered* but a thief alters the records fails. **All three must hold together.**

## 1.3 Extended Security Principles (Beyond CIA)

While CIA is the core, **KTU 2024** also expects mastery of these extended principles:

1. **Authentication** — Verifying the identity of a user/system (*Are you really who you claim?*).
2. **Authorization** — Granting specific rights to an authenticated identity (*What are you allowed to do?*).
3. **Non-Repudiation** — Preventing a party from denying having performed an action (*You cannot deny you sent that message*).
4. **Accountability (Auditability)** — Tracing actions back to a specific, responsible identity.
5. **Privacy** — The right of individuals to control how their personal data is collected and used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 Anchor Statement):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following as *core security goals*:
> **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, and Access Control.** Master these terms as definitional items — they appear in **2-mark short-answer questions** nearly every exam cycle.

## 1.4 Threats, Vulnerabilities, and Attacks — The Adversarial Vocabulary

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Threat** | A potential danger that could exploit a weakness. | A hacker group targeting your hospital. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in a system that can be exploited. | An unpatched buffer overflow in a web server. |
| **Attack (Threat Action)** | The deliberate attempt to exploit a vulnerability. | A SQL injection against that unpatched server. |
| **Risk** | The probability × impact of a threat being realized. | 30% chance of breach × ₹10 lakh loss = ₹3 lakh risk. |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "T-V-A" Chain:** *Threat exploits Vulnerability to mount an Attack.* — memorize this causal chain for short-answer questions.

## 1.5 Security Services vs. Security Mechanisms

- A **Security Service** is the *goal* we want to achieve (e.g., data confidentiality).
- A **Security Mechanism** is the *tool* used to provide that service (e.g., encryption).
- A **Security Attack** is any action that *compromises* the security of an information system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A frequent Module 1 question asks: *"Differentiate between a security attack, a security mechanism, and a security service."* Always answer with **Attack = harm**, **Mechanism = defense tool**, **Service = intended outcome**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CIA Triad in Depth

### 2.1.1 Confidentiality — The Science of Secrecy

- **Definition:** The property that information is *not made available or disclosed* to unauthorized individuals, entities, or processes.
- **Mechanisms Used:**
  - **Encryption** (symmetric: AES, DES; asymmetric: RSA, ECC)
  - **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**
  - **Steganography** (hiding data inside other data)
  - **Data Masking / Tokenization**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why encryption alone ≠ confidentiality:** A system that *encrypts* data but *stores the key in plaintext* provides **zero confidentiality**. Confidentiality is an *end-to-end* property.

### 2.1.2 Integrity — The Science of Trust

- **Definition:** The property whereby data has *not been modified or destroyed* in an unauthorized manner.
- **Two sub-categories:**
  - **Data Integrity** — bit-level / byte-level correctness.
  - **System Integrity** — the system performs its intended function without unauthorized manipulation.
- **Mechanisms Used:**
  - **Hash Functions** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-3)
  - **Message Authentication Codes (MAC, HMAC)**
  - **Digital Signatures** (RSA-DSA, ECDSA)
  - **Version Control / Audit Logs**

### 2.1.3 Availability — The Science of Uptime

- **Definition:** The property of being *accessible and usable on demand* by an authorized entity.
- **Measured by:** Uptime percentage, Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF), Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR).
- **Mechanisms Used:**
  - **Redundancy** (RAID, clustering, failover)
  - **Backups** (3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite)
  - **DDoS mitigation** (rate limiting, scrubbing centers, CDNs)
  - **Disaster Recovery Plans (DRP) and Business Continuity Plans (BCP)**

## 2.2 Authentication — The "Who Are You?" Gatekeeper

| Authentication Factor | Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Type 1 — Knowledge** | *Something you know* | Password, PIN, security question |
| **Type 2 — Possession** | *Something you have* | Smart card, OTP token, mobile device |
| **Type 3 — Biometric** | *Something you are* | Fingerprint, iris scan, face recognition |
| **Type 4 — Location / Behavior** | Contextual | Geolocation, keystroke dynamics, gait |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication):** Combining **two or more** of the above factors. *Password + OTP* is the most common MFA pattern. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is two passwords not MFA?"* — Answer: Both belong to the *same factor category* (knowledge), so it is **Single-Factor, Multi-Step**, which is **not MFA**.

## 2.3 Access Control Models

| Model | Full Form | Core Idea | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | Owner decides who accesses their resource. | File systems (Windows NTFS, Linux) |
| **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | System-enforced labels (e.g., Top Secret, Secret). | Military / Government (Bell-LaPadula) |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Access by job role (Admin, User, Guest). | Enterprise IT (most common in industry) |
| **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | Access by attributes (user, resource, environment). | Cloud, Zero-Trust architectures |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical metrics below are part of the **standard 2/3-mark short-answer toolkit** in PEEOT741 Module 1. Memorize the units and the meaning of each.

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Availability Uptime %** | $U\% = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage. *Two 9s* = 99%, *Four 9s* = 99.99%. |
| 2 | **Annual Downtime (approx.)** | $D = (1 - U) \times 525600$ | Minutes/year. 525600 = total minutes in a 365-day year. |
| 3 | **Risk Exposure** | $R = P \times I$ | $P$ = probability, $I$ = impact. Dimensionless product. |
| 4 | **Hash Collision Probability (Birthday Bound)** | $P_{coll} \approx 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{b})}$ | $b$ = hash bit-length, $n$ = number of hashes. |
| 5 | **Birthday Attack Complexity** | $\approx 2^{b/2}$ | For an $b$-bit hash, collision found in $2^{b/2}$ trials. |
| 6 | **Shannon Entropy (Information Content)** | $H(X) = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_{i} \log_{2} p_{i}$ | Bits/symbol. Measures uncertainty / information. |
| 7 | **Redundancy for Error Detection** | $P_{false\ accept} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$ | $p$ = per-bit error prob., $n$ = bit-length. |
| 8 | **Keyspace Size (Brute-Force Resistance)** | $K = 2^{k}$ | $k$ = key length in bits. AES-128 → $2^{128}$ keys. |
| 9 | **MTTR / MTBF Availability Relation** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Fraction (0 to 1). Industry standard reliability metric. |
| 10 | **Single-Point-of-Failure Reduction** | $N - 1$ rule | A system must survive the loss of any $1$ of $N$ components. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CIA Triad** is the universal language used in **ISO/IEC 27001**, **NIST SP 800-53**, and **PCI-DSS** audits.
- **Access Control Models** directly map to **cloud IAM policies** (AWS IAM uses RBAC + ABAC hybrid models).
- **MFA + Zero Trust** is the current enterprise standard, adopted post-2017 in response to credential-stuffing attacks.
- **Hash collision probability** dictates why MD5 (128-bit, $2^{64}$ attacks) is *broken* and SHA-256 ($2^{128}$ attacks) is *secure*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Downtime from Availability Uptime

**Problem Statement:** A cloud service provider advertises **99.9% uptime** (*three nines*). Compute the maximum allowed annual downtime in minutes.

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$
U\% = \frac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Express Downtime in terms of Uptime and $U\%$.**

$$
D = \frac{100 - U\%}{U\%} \times Uptime
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total minutes in one non-leap year.**

$$
Total = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525600\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug $U\% = 99.9$.**

$$
D = \frac{100 - 99.9}{99.9} \times 525600 = \frac{0.1}{99.9} \times 525600
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$
D = 0.001001001 \times 525600 = 526.05\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to hours.**

$$
D = \frac{526.05}{60} \approx 8.77\ \text{hours/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** 99.9% uptime ⇒ **~8.77 hours of allowed downtime per year**. 99.99% (*four nines*) reduces this to ~52.6 minutes/year.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Entropy of a Biased Coin

**Problem Statement:** A coin shows Heads with probability $p_{H} = 0.8$ and Tails with $p_{T} = 0.2$. Compute the information entropy in bits.

**Step 1 — State Shannon's formula.**

$$
H(X) = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_{i} \log_{2} p_{i}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand for two outcomes.**

$$
H(X) = - \left( p_{H} \log_{2} p_{H} + p_{T} \log_{2} p_{T} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the probabilities.**

$$
H(X) = - \left( 0.8 \log_{2} 0.8 + 0.2 \log_{2} 0.2 \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each logarithm.**

$$
\log_{2} 0.8 = \frac{\ln 0.8}{\ln 2} = \frac{-0.2231}{0.6931} \approx -0.3219
$$

$$
\log_{2} 0.2 = \frac{\ln 0.2}{\ln 2} = \frac{-1.6094}{0.6931} \approx -2.3219
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by probabilities.**

$$
0.8 \times (-0.3219) = -0.2575
$$

$$
0.2 \times (-2.3219) = -0.4644
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the negative sign and sum.**

$$
H(X) = -(-0.2575 - 0.4644) = 0.7219\ \text{bits}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A fair coin gives 1 bit. This biased coin carries only **0.72 bits** of information per flip — *less uncertainty means less information*. This is why compression algorithms exploit biased sources.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Risk Exposure Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A company estimates a 15% probability of a data breach occurring in a year, with an estimated financial impact of ₹50,00,000. Compute the annual risk exposure (ALE — Annual Loss Expectancy).

**Step 1 — State the risk formula.**

$$
R = P \times I
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
R = 0.15 \times 50{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
R = 7{,}50{,}000
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The company's **ALE = ₹7,50,000**. Any security control costing **less than ₹7.5 lakh/year** is a financially justified investment. This is the *quantitative risk analysis* pillar of Module 1.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Security Mechanism

| Security Goal | Best-Fit Mechanism | Why It Fits | Common Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | AES-256 encryption | Mathematically infeasible to brute force ($2^{256}$ keys) | Storing the key alongside the ciphertext. |
| **Integrity** | SHA-256 + RSA signature | Hash detects tampering, signature proves origin | Using MD5 (collisions exist). |
| **Authentication** | Password + OTP (MFA) | Combines knowledge + possession factors | SMS-OTP only (vulnerable to SIM-swap). |
| **Authorization** | RBAC with least privilege | Roles map cleanly to job functions | Default-admin accounts left enabled. |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Digital signatures (PKI) | Asymmetric — only the signer holds the private key | Symmetric MAC (both parties can forge). |
| **Availability** | Geographic redundancy + CDN | Eliminates single point of failure | Single data center with no backup. |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Birthday Bound for Hash Collisions

**Problem Statement:** Estimate how many SHA-256 hashes a brute-force attacker must compute to find a collision with **50% probability**.

**Step 1 — The birthday paradox formula.**

$$
P_{coll} \approx 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{b})}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $P_{coll} = 0.5$ and solve for $n$.**

$$
0.5 = 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2^{b+1})}
$$

$$
e^{-n^{2}/(2^{b+1})} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 — Take natural log.**

$$
-\frac{n^{2}}{2^{b+1}} = \ln 0.5 = -0.6931
$$

$$
n^{2} = 0.6931 \times 2^{b+1}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$.**

$$
n = \sqrt{0.6931 \times 2^{b+1}} \approx 2^{b/2} \times \sqrt{1.386}
$$

**Step 5 — Approximate.**

$$
n \approx 1.177 \times 2^{b/2}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply to SHA-256 ($b = 256$).**

$$
n \approx 1.177 \times 2^{128} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{38}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\approx 2^{128}$ trials. This is still **computationally infeasible** with current technology — so SHA-256 is *currently* secure against collision attacks, but MD5 ($b = 128$, $\approx 2^{64}$) is not.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CIA Triad — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["CIA TRIAD"] --> nodeB["CONFIDENTIALITY"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["INTEGRITY"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["AVAILABILITY"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Encryption AES RSA"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Access Control Lists"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Steganography"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Hash Functions SHA256"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Digital Signatures"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["MAC and HMAC"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Redundancy RAID"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["DDoS Mitigation"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Backups 3-2-1 Rule"]
    nodeA2["EXTENDED PRINCIPLES"] --> nodeE["AUTHENTICATION"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeF["AUTHORIZATION"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeG["NON-REPUDIATION"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeH["ACCOUNTABILITY"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Password Knowledge"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Token Possession"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["Biometric Inherence"]
    nodeF --> nodeF1["DAC MAC RBAC ABAC"]
```

## 4.2 Threat–Vulnerability–Attack–Risk Causal Chain

```mermaid

    nodeT["THREAT"] -- "exploits" --> nodeV["VULNERABILITY"]
    nodeV -- "enables" --> nodeA["ATTACK"]
    nodeA -- "causes" --> nodeI["IMPACT"]
    nodeP["PROBABILITY"] -- "modulates" --> nodeA
    nodeR["RISK = P times I"] -- "scored by" --> nodeI
    nodeM["MITIGATION CONTROLS"] -- "reduces" --> nodeR

    nodeM --> nodeM3["Corrective"]
```

## 4.3 Authentication Factor Hierarchy

```mermaid

    nodeAuth["AUTHENTICATION"] --> nodeF1["FACTOR 1: KNOWLEDGE"]
    nodeAuth --> nodeF2["FACTOR 2: POSSESSION"]
    nodeAuth --> nodeF3["FACTOR 3: BIOMETRIC"]
    nodeAuth --> nodeF4["FACTOR 4: CONTEXT"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1a["Password"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1b["PIN"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF2a["Smart Card"]

    nodeF3 --> nodeF3b["Iris Scan"]

```

## 4.4 Security Service vs. Mechanism vs. Attack — Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSS["SECURITY SERVICE GOAL"] --> nodeATT["ATTACK THREAT"]
    nodeSS --> nodeMECH["MECHANISM DEFENSE"]
    nodeATT --> nodeAT1["Interruption"]

    nodeATT --> nodeAT3["Modification"]
    nodeATT --> nodeAT4["Fabrication"]
    nodeMECH --> nodeMC1["Encryption"]
    nodeMECH --> nodeMC2["Hashing"]
    nodeMECH --> nodeMC3["Digital Signature"]
    nodeMECH --> nodeMC4["Access Control"]
    nodeMC1 -.defeats.-> nodeAT2
    nodeMC2 -.defeats.-> nodeAT3
    nodeMC3 -.defeats.-> nodeAT4
    nodeMC4 -.defeats.-> nodeAT1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The four classical attack classes — **Interruption (Availability), Interception (Confidentiality), Modification (Integrity), Fabrication (Authenticity)** — map cleanly to the four CIA pillars. This is the **most-asked conceptual mapping** in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **List and briefly explain the three core principles of security.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three core principles of security form the **CIA Triad**:
1. **Confidentiality** — Ensures that information is accessed only by authorized parties. Example: encryption of data at rest.
2. **Integrity** — Ensures that data is not altered or tampered with during storage or transit. Example: SHA-256 hash verification.
3. **Availability** — Ensures that systems and data are accessible to authorized users when needed. Example: redundant servers and DDoS protection.
*(1 mark each for name + 1-line meaning + example ≈ 3 marks)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`
> **Differentiate between authentication and authorization with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| **Question** | *Who are you?* | *What can you do?* |
| **Order** | Always happens **first**. | Happens **after** authentication. |
| **Mechanism** | Password, OTP, biometrics. | RBAC, ACLs, policies. |
| **Example** | Entering a username + password to log in. | An *Admin* role can delete users, but a *User* role cannot. |

*(2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** Explain the **CIA Triad** in detail. For each pillar, describe **two real-world threats** and **one mitigation mechanism**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** What is **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**? Explain the three primary authentication factors with examples. Why are **two passwords** not considered MFA? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Confidentiality (2 marks):**
   - Definition: Protection from unauthorized disclosure.
   - Threats: (i) Eavesdropping on unencrypted Wi-Fi; (ii) Insider data theft.
   - Mitigation: **AES-256 encryption** of data in transit and at rest.
2. **Integrity (2 marks):**
   - Definition: Data is unchanged from source to destination.
   - Threats: (i) Man-in-the-Middle tampering with packets; (ii) SQL injection modifying DB records.
   - Mitigation: **SHA-256 hashing** combined with **RSA digital signatures**.
3. **Availability (2 marks):**
   - Definition: Systems function when needed.
   - Threats: (i) DDoS attack flooding a server; (ii) Ransomware encrypting critical files.
   - Mitigation: **Geographic redundancy** + **offline backups** (3-2-1 rule).
4. *(1 mark for a clean concluding statement on why all three must hold.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

1. **MFA Definition (2 marks):** MFA = authentication requiring **two or more factors from different categories** (knowledge, possession, biometric).
2. **Three Factor Types (3 marks):**
   - *Knowledge:* Password, PIN.
   - *Possession:* OTP token, smartphone.
   - *Biometric:* Fingerprint, face.
3. **Why two passwords ≠ MFA (2 marks):** Both passwords are from the *same factor category* (knowledge). MFA explicitly requires **distinct categories**. Two passwords only raise the *single-factor* difficulty.
4. *(Conclusion — 0 mark allocation, optional bonus.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2022]`

> **(a)** Define a **security attack**. Classify security attacks into the **four classical categories** (Interruption, Interception, Modification, Fabrication) and map each to a security goal. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the **RBAC access control model** with a real-world example. Compare RBAC with **DAC** and **MAC**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** A *security attack* is any deliberate action that attempts to compromise the security of an information system.
2. **The Four Classical Attacks (4 marks):**
   - **Interruption** — An asset becomes unavailable (e.g., DoS attack). Goal attacked: **Availability**.
   - **Interception** — Unauthorized party gains access (e.g., packet sniffing). Goal attacked: **Confidentiality**.
   - **Modification** — Unauthorized change to data (e.g., MITM altering a transfer). Goal attacked: **Integrity**.
   - **Fabrication** — Counterfeit object is produced (e.g., spoofed IP). Goal attacked: **Authenticity**.
3. **Summary diagram (2 marks):** Draw a 4-row table linking attack → goal → typical defense.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

1. **RBAC Definition (2 marks):** *Role-Based Access Control* grants permissions based on a user's **role** within an organization. Users are assigned roles; roles are assigned permissions.
2. **Real-World Example (2 marks):** In a hospital, roles may be *Doctor*, *Nurse*, *Receptionist*, *Admin*. Only *Doctors* can prescribe medication; only *Admins* can view payroll.
3. **Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | DAC | MAC | RBAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control source | Resource owner | System policy | Administrator |
| Flexibility | High | Low | Medium-High |
| Use case | Personal files | Military / classified data | Enterprise IT |
| Granularity | Per-user | Per-label | Per-role |
| Administration effort | High | High | Low |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "threat" with "attack":** *Threat = potential danger*; *Attack = realized exploitation*. Mixing them costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing "two passwords" as MFA:** Examiners deduct **2 marks** instantly. Always state the *category-of-factor* rule.
> 3. **Omitting examples in 7-mark answers:** KTU rubrics award **1–2 marks** for an industry-relevant example. Always include one.
> 4. **Mixing CIA and Non-Repudiation:** Non-repudiation is *not* part of CIA — it is an *extended* principle. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always mention *bits, minutes/year, %, or ₹* where applicable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Principles of Security (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ The **CIA Triad** = **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability — universal in every framework.
>
> ✅ **Extended principles** = Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Accountability, Privacy.
>
> ✅ **Threat vs. Vulnerability vs. Attack** — memorize the *T-V-A causal chain*.
>
> ✅ **Attack formula:** Attack = Risk × Vulnerability (impact-based classification).
>
> ✅ **Authentication factors** = Knowledge, Possession, Biometric (plus contextual). **MFA = ≥2 distinct factors.**
>
> ✅ **Access control models** = DAC (owner), MAC (system labels), RBAC (roles), ABAC (attributes).
>
> ✅ **Four classical attacks** = Interruption, Interception, Modification, Fabrication — map them to CIA + Authenticity.
>
> ✅ **Shannon entropy** $H(X)$ measures information; **Birthday bound** $2^{b/2}$ governs hash collisions.
>
> ✅ **Risk = Probability × Impact (ALE)** — quantitative risk analysis foundation.
>
> ✅ **Availability metrics:** 99.9% → ~8.77 h downtime/yr; 99.99% → ~52.6 min/yr; **MTTR/MTBF ratio** drives uptime.
>
> ✅ **Key exam definitions to memorize verbatim** for 2-mark questions: *Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Threat, Vulnerability, Attack, Risk, Security Service, Security Mechanism.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Security Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Security Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Security Attack** is any malicious action that attempts to compromise the **security objectives** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity, and Non-Repudiation — collectively known as the **CIA-A-N triad**) of an information system. According to the *Information Security triad* model standardized by NIST (SP 800-30), every attack vector must be analyzed against these five properties to classify its impact.

In the KTU 2024 framework, security attacks are categorized along **two primary axes**:

$$
\text{Attack}_{\text{class}} \in \{\text{Passive}, \text{Active}\}
$$

And along a secondary axis:

$$
\text{Attack}_{\text{origin}} \in \{\text{Inside}, \text{Outside}\}
$$

These classifications determine the **detection strategy**, **countermeasure deployment**, and the **forensic response model** to be invoked.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Secure House" Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine your home as an **information system** and your valuables (jewelry, documents, photos) as **data**.
> - A **passive attack** is like a thief silently staking out your house — noting your mailbox contents, tracking when you leave, observing your daily routine. They learn a lot **without ever touching** your house.
> - An **active attack** is the thief **breaking the lock**, **smashing the window**, **rewriting your will** (modification), or **impersonating the mailman** to steal the package (fabrication).

This duality is the foundational concept taught in Module 1 of PEEOT741. Students often confuse *passive* with *"less dangerous"* — this is **wrong**. Passive attacks (like traffic analysis by nation-state actors) can be devastating over long periods.

---

## 1.3 The CIA-A-N Security Triad — Quick Reference

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Attack That Breaks It |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | $C$ | Data is read only by authorized parties | Eavesdropping, Sniffing |
| **Integrity** | $I$ | Data is unaltered during transit/storage | Tampering, MITM |
| **Availability** | $A$ | System is accessible when needed | DoS, DDoS |
| **Authenticity** | $A_u$ | Identity of sender is verified | Spoofing, Phishing |
| **Non-Repudiation** | $N$ | Sender cannot deny sending | Replay, Forgery |

> [!TIP] **KTU Mnemonic** — Remember **"CIA + 2 A's"** : **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability + **A**uthenticity + Non-repudiation.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Module 1 sets the **vocabulary foundation** for the entire PEEOT741 course. Every subsequent module (Cryptography, Firewalls, IDS/IPS, Web Security) references back to these attack classifications. Examiners test whether students can:
1. Correctly label an attack as *active* or *passive*.
2. Identify which CIA-A-N property is violated.
3. Suggest the appropriate countermeasure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Classification Tree

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups attacks into the following hierarchical taxonomy:

$$
T_{\text{attack}} = \{T_{\text{passive}}, T_{\text{active}}\}
$$

$$
T_{\text{passive}} = \{T_{\text{release}}, T_{\text{traffic}}\}
$$

$$
T_{\text{active}} = \{T_{\text{masquerade}}, T_{\text{replay}}, T_{\text{modification}}, T_{\text{DoS}}\}
$$

---

## 2.2 Passive Attacks — Operational Analysis

> [!NOTE] **Definition** — Passive attacks attempt to **learn or make use of information** from the system **without affecting system resources** (Stallings, Cryptography and Network Security Principles, classical model adopted by KTU).

### 2.2.1 Release of Message Content
- **What**: Attacker reads the actual content of a confidential transmission.
- **How**: Through wiretapping, packet sniffing (e.g., Wireshark on open Wi-Fi), or shoulder surfing.
- **Confidentiality Impact**: **Total breach**.
- **Countermeasure**: **Encryption** (symmetric AES-256 or asymmetric RSA).

### 2.2.2 Traffic Analysis
- **What**: Even if the message is encrypted, the attacker analyzes **metadata** (source IP, destination IP, packet size, timing, frequency).
- **How**: Tools like `tcpdump`, NetFlow analyzers, or nation-state SIGINT platforms.
- **Confidentiality Impact**: **Partial** — the **pattern** of communication is exposed.
- **Why it is dangerous**: A journalist contacting a sensitive source weekly at 11 PM is still identifiable even if messages are encrypted.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Passive attacks are inherently difficult to detect** because they do NOT alter the system. Prevention (encryption) is the only defence — not detection.

---

## 2.3 Active Attacks — Operational Analysis

Active attacks attempt to **alter system resources** or **affect their operation**. They fall into four canonical subcategories:

### 2.3.1 Masquerade
- **Definition**: An entity pretends to be a different entity (user, server, IP address).
- **Mechanism**: IP spoofing, ARP spoofing, DNS spoofing, email phishing.
- **Property Violated**: **Authenticity**.
- **Countermeasure**: Strong authentication (Kerberos, PKI digital certificates).

### 2.3.2 Replay Attack
- **Definition**: The attacker captures a valid transmission and **retransmits it later** to gain unauthorized access.
- **Mechanism**: Session token theft, capturing handshake packets and replaying them.
- **Property Violated**: **Non-Repudiation**.
- **Countermeasure**: **Nonces** (number used once), **timestamps**, and sequence numbers.

### 2.3.3 Modification of Messages
