> The following **attack-chain matrix** is a KTU-favourite "explain-the-attack-steps" model answer. The structure is deliberately laid out as an exhaustive, examiner-pleasing response for a **14-mark analytical question**.

## 3.1 The Five-Stage Exhaustive Analytical Matrix

For every stage, this section lists the **actor, action, tools used, indicators, and KTU key-phrase vocabulary** that examiners reward with full marks.

### **STAGE 1 — Target Selection & Profiling**

$$\textbf{Objective:} \quad \text{Identify a high-value target with SMS-2FA-protected assets}$$

| **Actor** | **Attacker** |
|-----------|--------------|
| **Action** | Identifies target with liquid cryptocurrency holdings or high-limit bank accounts via LinkedIn / X / public posts |
| **Tools** | Maltego, Sherlock, social-media crawlers, HaveIBeenPwned |
| **Indicator** | Target advertises crypto earnings, NFT sales, or "not your keys, not your coins" tweets |
| **KTU Key Phrase** | *"Attackers perform OSINT to identify high-value digital-asset holders."* |

---

### **STAGE 2 — PII Collection**

$$\textbf{Objective:} \quad \text{Build a credential dossier sufficient to satisfy carrier verification}$$

| **Actor** | **Attacker** |
|-----------|--------------|
| **Action** | Harvests DOB, address, last-4 SSN/Aadhaar, mother's maiden name, recent transactions, IMEI |
| **Sources** | Data breaches, phishing emails, vishing calls, dark-web markets (e.g., Genesis Market, Russian Market) |
| **Indicator** | Multiple login attempts on victim's email; spam indicating email is in a breach dump |
| **KTU Key Phrase** | *"PII is weaponized to bypass the carrier's knowledge-based authentication (KBA)."* |

---

### **STAGE 3 — Carrier Impersonation & SIM Port**

$$\textbf{Objective:} \quad \text{Convince the MNO to re-issue the victim's SIM}$$

| **Actor** | **Attacker (impersonating victim)** |
|-----------|-------------------------------------|
| **Action** | Calls customer care, claims lost/damaged SIM, requests port to attacker-controlled SIM |
| **Bypass Method** | Answering security questions with stolen PII; sometimes bribing a retail employee |
| **Indicator (Victim-side)** | Sudden "No Service", emergency-only mode, SIM icon disappears |
| **Indicator (Network-side)** | New IMSI registration from different device; location anomaly |
| **KTU Key Phrase** | *"The cellular identity (IMSI) is reassigned, severing the legitimate user from the network."* |

---

### **STAGE 4 — OTP Interception & Account Takeover**

$$\textbf{Objective:} \quad \text{Use the hijacked number to bypass 2FA on all linked services}$$

The attacker's SIM now receives the SMS-OTP sent by:

- Banks: *"Your OTP for ₹50,000 transfer is 482913"*
- Crypto exchanges: *"Withdrawal of 2.5 BTC — confirmation code 771204"*
- Email providers: *"Your Gmail recovery code is 920137"*
- Social media: *"Twitter verification code: 553820"*

$$\textbf{Mathematical Model of Attack Surface:}$$

Let $A$ = the set of accounts protected by SMS-OTP. If the attacker obtains control of the SIM, then:

$$
P(\text{Compromise}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - p_i\right)
$$

where $p_i$ is the probability of bypassing the *i-th* service's recovery flow. For typical users where every account uses SMS-OTP as the single recovery vector, $P(\text{Compromise}) \to 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marker Insight:**
> The above probability model is a *conceptual* illustration showing that **redundancy of weak factors (SMS only) does not equal security**. Real examiner-friendly phrasing: *"SIM swapping converts a possession-based factor (phone) into a knowledge-based factor, negating the entire purpose of 2FA."*

---

### **STAGE 5 — Asset Exfiltration & Persistence**

$$\textbf{Objective:} \quad \text{Monetize the hijacked identity and prevent victim recovery}$$

- **Crypto:** Transfer funds to attacker-controlled wallet (irreversible).
- **Banking:** Add new payees, initiate wire transfers, change notification settings.
- **Email:** Set up **email forwarding rules** to attacker address + **change recovery email/phone** to attacker-controlled equivalents → *persistent access* even after the victim regains their SIM.
- **Cloud:** Lock the victim out of iCloud / Google account → device wipe possible.
- **Social Media:** Post scams, sell hijacked handles (especially valuable for short usernames like @cash, @money).

---

## 3.2 Case Study: **Michael Terpin v. AT&T (2018–2020)** — Exhaustive Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-cited SIM swap case** in academic literature and the highest-probability KTU 14-marker.

| **Year** | **Event** |
|----------|-----------|
| **Jan 2018** | Nicholas Truglia (then 21) targeted Michael Terpin, a crypto investor, via SIM swap on his AT&T line. |
| **2018** | Terpin lost **$23.8 million** in crypto (cumulative across two attacks: $17M + $6.8M). |
| **2019** | Terpin filed a **$224 million** civil RICO lawsuit against AT&T for gross negligence. |
| **Aug 2020** | California jury awarded Terpin **$75.6 million** in damages — the largest-ever SIM-swap judgment at the time. |
| **2020 onward** | Truglia was sentenced to **18 years** in prison (separate criminal trial). |

### **Court's Findings (Key Takeaways for KTU):**

1. AT&T's employees were **bribed** with small amounts to perform unauthorized SIM swaps.
2. The carrier's KYC (Know Your Customer) procedures were **grossly inadequate**.
3. The judge ruled: *"A telecom carrier that fails to implement reasonable security measures can be held liable for foreseeable damages."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "5 C's of SIM Swap Liability":**
> - **C**arrier negligence
> - **C**ustomer PII leakage
> - **C**rypto as preferred monetization
> - **C**ourt precedents (Terpin case)
> - **C**ounter-measures (FIDO2 hardware keys)

---

## 3.3 Case Study: **Jack Dorsey Incident (August 30, 2019)**

The Twitter CEO's phone number was SIM-swapped via his carrier. The attacker posted offensive tweets from @jack for **15 minutes** before Twitter's security team intervened. The attack exploited a flaw in the carrier's **SMS-to-Tweet** integration, not a direct breach of Twitter's servers. This case is a textbook example of how **third-party identity providers** (telecom carriers) become the attack surface.

---

## 3.4 Indian Legal Framework — KTU-Relevant Statutes

| **Law** | **Section** | **Offence** | **Punishment** |
|---------|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| IT Act 2000 (amended 2008) | **§66C** | Identity theft (using PII of another person) | Up to **3 years** imprisonment + ₹1 lakh fine |
| IT Act 2000 (amended 2008) | **§66D** | Cheating by personation using communication device/computer | Up to **3 years** imprisonment + ₹1 lakh fine |
| IT Act 2000 (amended 2008) | **§66E** | Violation of privacy (if biometric/intimate images exposed) | Up to **3 years** or fine |
| Indian Penal Code 1860 | **§419, §420** | Cheating / impersonation | 7 years + fine |
| Indian Telegraph Act 1885 | **§20** | Unauthorised interception of telegraph messages | Up to **3 years** |
| RBI Cyber Security Framework 2016 | — | Mandates banks to move beyond SMS-OTP | Regulatory compliance |
| Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 | **§8, §44** | Telco breach of PII duty | Up to **₹250 crore** penalty |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Attack Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid `flowchart` visualizes the complete SIM swap kill chain. **Study this diagram carefully** — KTU examiners award **2–3 marks** for correctly drawn or described flow diagrams in a 14-marker question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Attacker Performs OSINT on Target"] --> B["Harvest PII from Social Media Data Breaches Dark Web"]
    B --> C["Build Dossier DOB SSN Address Phone IMEI"]
    C --> D{"Choose Attack Vector"}
    D --> E1["Phone Call to Carrier Impersonation"]
    D --> E2["In-Store Visit with Forged ID"]
    D --> E3["Bribed Carrier Insider"]
    D --> E4["SS7 Protocol Exploit Advanced"]
    E1 --> F["Carrier KBA Verification Attacker Answers Correctly"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G["SIM Port Approved IMSI Reassigned to Attacker SIM"]
    G --> H["Victim SIM Deauthenticated No Service on Legit Phone"]
    G --> I["Attacker SIM Activated Receives All SMS Calls"]
    I --> J["Trigger Account Recovery on Email Bank Crypto Social Media"]
    J --> K["Intercept SMS OTP Codes via Hijacked Number"]
    K --> L["Bypass 2FA on All Linked Accounts"]
    L --> M1["Drain Cryptocurrency Wallets Irreversible"]
    L --> M2["Initiate Unauthorized Wire Transfers"]
    L --> M3["Hijack Social Media Handles"]
    L --> M4["Sell Stolen Accounts on Dark Web"]
    M1 --> N["Cover Tracks Delete Logs Use Mixers Tumblers"]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    M4 --> N
    N --> O["Victim Realises Attack Reports to Carrier Police I4C Helpline 1930"]
    O --> P["Forensic Investigation IT Act 66C 66D Charges Filed"]
```

---

## 4.2 Defence-in-Depth Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram shows how a **multi-layered defence** (defence-in-depth) can mitigate SIM swap fraud at every layer — user, carrier, service provider, and legal.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1["USER LAYER"]
        U1["Use App Based TOTP Google Authenticator Authy"]
        U2["Adopt Hardware Security Keys FIDO2 YubiKey Titan"]
        U3["Minimize Public PII on Social Media"]
        U4["Set Unique Carrier PIN Not DOB Based"]
    end
    subgraph L2["CARRIER LAYER"]
        C1["Mandatory Video KYC for SIM Swap"]
        C2["Biometric Verification of Customer"]
        C3["24 Hour Cooling Period Before Port Activation"]
        C4["SMS Alert to Old SIM Before Port Execution"]
    end
    subgraph L3["SERVICE PROVIDER LAYER"]
        S1["Move from SMS OTP to Push Notification"]
        S2["Number Matching Verification FIDO Standards"]
        S3["Risk Based Adaptive Authentication"]
        S4["Allow Hardware Key as Primary 2FA"]
    end
    subgraph L4["LEGAL LAYER"]
        L4A["IT Act 66C Identity Theft 3 Years"]
        L4B["IT Act 66D Personation 3 Years"]
        L4C["DPDP Act 2023 250 Crore Penalty"]
        L4D["I4C Helpline 1930 Cyber Crime Reporting"]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — SMS-OTP vs. TOTP vs. Hardware Key

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SMS["SMS-OTP WEAK VULNERABLE TO SIM SWAP"]
        S1["Server generates OTP"] --> S2["Sent via SS7 to telecom"]
        S2 --> S3["Delivered to whichever SIM has that number"]
        S3 --> S4["Attacker SIM receives it"]
    end
    subgraph TOTP["TOTP APP BASED MEDIUM SECURE"]
        T1["Server and phone share a secret key"] --> T2["App computes HMAC SHA1 of current time"]
        T2 --> T3["Local generation NO SMS required"]
        T3 --> T4["Cannot be intercepted via SIM swap"]
    end
    subgraph FIDO["FIDO2 HARDWARE KEY STRONGEST"]
        F1["Public key cryptography"] --> F2["Private key stored in tamper proof secure element"]
        F2 --> F3["Phishing resistant origin bound"]
        F3 --> F4["Completely immune to SIM swap"]
    end
    SMS -- Upgrade --> TOTP
    TOTP -- Upgrade --> FIDO
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Phrase to Memorize:**
> *"SMS is a possession-based factor only as long as the SIM is in the legitimate user's possession. Once the SIM is swapped, possession is transferred to the attacker, rendering the second factor effectively useless. This is the fundamental cryptographic and identity-theoretic weakness of SMS-OTP."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following question bank is modelled on **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination)** paper pattern. All questions are mapped to **Course Outcomes (CO1, CO2)** and **Bloom's Taxonomy Levels (L1–L6)**. Each question includes a **full mark-wise model answer**.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
**Define SIM Swapping Fraud. List any four indicators that a victim's SIM has been swapped.** *(3 marks, CO1, Bloom's Level: L1 — Remember)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
SIM Swapping Fraud is a type of identity-based account takeover attack in which a malicious actor socially engineers a victim's mobile network operator into transferring the victim's phone number to a SIM card controlled by the attacker, thereby intercepting all SMS-based two-factor authentication codes.

**Indicators (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**

1. Sudden loss of cellular signal — phone shows "No Service" or "Emergency Calls Only."
2. Unexpected notification from the carrier about a SIM change or number transfer.
3. Inability to make or receive calls / SMS despite an active recharge plan.
4. Receiving unexpected OTPs (indicating someone is trying to log into accounts using your number).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between SIM Swapping and Phishing. Why is SMS-based 2FA considered vulnerable?** *(3 marks, CO1, Bloom's Level: L2 — Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SIM Swapping | Phishing |
|--------|--------------|----------|
| **Target** | Mobile carrier (impersonating victim) | Victim directly (via fake emails/links) |
| **Medium** | Phone call / in-store visit to carrier | Email / SMS / voice call to victim |
| **Goal** | Hijack phone number | Steal credentials or install malware |
| **2FA Impact** | Bypasses SMS-OTP entirely | Often steals OTP along with password |
| **Detection** | Harder (silent loss of signal) | Easier (user sees suspicious link) |

**Why SMS-based 2FA is vulnerable (1.5 marks):**
SMS-OTP relies on the assumption that the phone is in the *legitimate user's possession*. SIM Swapping breaks this assumption by transferring the number to the attacker's SIM, converting a possession-based factor into a knowledge-based factor — defeating the cryptographic premise of 2FA. Additionally, the underlying SS7 protocol lacks end-to-end authentication, and SMS messages are stored unencrypted in transit.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Module-Level)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. **Both Q-A and Q-B are equivalent in marks and cognitive level**. A student can attempt either.

---

### ❓ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*

> *"With a neat block diagram, explain the working of SIM Swapping Fraud. Use the Michael Terpin case study to illustrate the financial impact and legal consequences."*
> 
> *Sub-parts: (a) 7 marks — Attack Methodology + Diagram ; (b) 7 marks — Case Study + Legal Recourse*

#### **Part A(a) — Attack Methodology with Diagram (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Block Diagram + 5 phases):**

*(Refer to the kill-chain diagram in Section 4.1 — draw the flowchart: OSINT → PII Harvest → Carrier Impersonation → SIM Port → OTP Interception → ATO → Monetization.)*

**Step 1 (1.5 marks): Reconnaissance**
The attacker identifies a high-value target (e.g., cryptocurrency investor) and uses OSINT tools — Maltego, social media crawlers, breach databases — to collect PII including name, date of birth, address, phone number, and security-question answers.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks): Carrier Impersonation**
The attacker contacts the victim's mobile network operator (MNO), impersonating the victim, and claims a lost/damaged SIM. Using harvested PII, the attacker satisfies knowledge-based authentication (KBA) and requests a SIM port to a SIM card in their possession.

**Step 3 (1 mark): SIM Deauthentication & Reassignment**
The carrier deactivates the victim's legitimate SIM (assigning the IMSI to the attacker's SIM). The victim experiences sudden loss of service. The attacker's SIM now receives all incoming SMS, calls, and OTPs.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): OTP Interception & Account Takeover**
The attacker triggers account recovery on the victim's email, banking apps, crypto exchanges, and social media. SMS-OTPs are routed to the attacker's SIM, bypassing 2FA. Attacker resets passwords, changes recovery options, and locks the victim out.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks): Monetization & Persistence**
Cryptocurrency is transferred to attacker-controlled wallets (irreversible on blockchain). Banking fraud is executed. Attacker sets up email forwarding rules to maintain persistent access. Cover-up: deletion of logs, use of crypto mixers.

---

#### **Part A(b) — Michael Terpin Case Study & Legal Recourse (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Background (1.5 marks):**
In January 2018, crypto investor and entrepreneur **Michael Terpin** lost approximately **$23.8 million** in cryptocurrency across two SIM-swap attacks. The attacker, **Nicholas Truglia**, bribed AT&T retail store employees to perform unauthorized SIM swaps on Terpin's number. Once the number was ported, Truglia reset passwords on Terpin's crypto exchange accounts (primarily involving an algorithmic-trading bot wallet) and transferred funds to wallets he controlled.

**Financial Impact (1.5 marks):**
- First attack: ~**$17 million** lost.
- Second attack: ~**$6.8 million** lost.
- Total: **$23.8 million** (irrecoverable due to blockchain immutability).
- Court-awarded damages: **$75.6 million** (in 2020) — the largest civil judgment in a SIM-swap case in U.S. history.

**Legal Consequences (2 marks):**
- **Civil:** Terpin filed a RICO (Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act) lawsuit against AT&T for gross negligence. A California jury found AT&T liable and awarded **$75.6 million** in damages (Aug 2020).
- **Criminal:** Truglia was convicted and sentenced to **18 years** in prison.
- **Indian Legal Equivalent (1 mark):** Under the **IT Act 2000**, Section 66C (Identity Theft) and Section 66D (Cheating by Personation) carry up to **3 years imprisonment** and a fine of ₹1 lakh. The **DPDP Act 2023** imposes penalties up to **₹250 crore** for telecom PII breaches.

**Lessons Learnt (1 mark):**
- Hardware security keys (FIDO2/YubiKey) are essential.
- Carriers must enforce stronger KYC (Video KYC + biometric verification).
- SMS-OTP must be replaced with app-based or hardware-based 2FA.
- Public disclosure of crypto wealth on social media is a major risk factor.

---

### ❓ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]*

> *"Explain the technical and procedural weaknesses in SS7 and KYC that enable SIM swap attacks. Propose a defence-in-depth strategy covering user, carrier, and service-provider layers."*
> 
> *Sub-parts: (a) 7 marks — SS7 & KYC Weaknesses ; (b) 7 marks — Defence-in-Depth Architecture*

#### **Part B(a) — SS7 and KYC Weaknesses (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SS7 Protocol Weaknesses (3.5 marks):**

The **Signalling System No. 7 (SS7)** suite, designed in 1975, underpins global telecom call-setup, SMS routing, and number-porting. Its design flaws include:

1. **No end-to-end authentication** — SS7 messages trust any node that speaks the protocol, allowing attackers to inject location-update requests.
2. **Trust-based architecture** — designed when telecom operators were few and trusted. With thousands of operators globally, this assumption has failed catastrophically.
3. **Lack of encryption** — SS7 messages traverse in plaintext, allowing eavesdropping and message redirection.
4. **MAP (Mobile Application Part) vulnerabilities** — messages like `SendRoutingInfo` and `UpdateLocation` can be forged to hijack a subscriber's network identity.
5. **SMS is a "store-and-forward" service** — it can be intercepted at multiple SS7 nodes, with no integrity protection.

**KYC Procedural Weaknesses (3.5 marks):**

1. **Knowledge-Based Authentication (KBA) reliance** — carriers verify identity using static PII (DOB, SSN-last-4, mother's maiden name) that is widely available in breach dumps.
2. **Inconsistent verification across channels** — phone-based verification is weaker than in-store, but both are subject to social engineering.
3. **Inadequate employee vetting** — retail store employees have been bribed for as little as **$100** to perform unauthorized SIM swaps.
4. **No cooling-off period** — SIM ports are often executed in real-time with no 24-hour delay for the legitimate user to cancel.
5. **No video/face-match verification** for high-risk transactions like SIM swaps.
6. **Poor audit trails** — many carriers lack tamper-proof logs of porting transactions.

---

#### **Part B(b) — Defence-in-Depth Strategy (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to the layered defence diagram in Section 4.2.)*

**Layer 1 — User-Level Defences (2 marks):**
- Adopt **FIDO2 hardware security keys** (YubiKey, Titan Key) — completely immune to SIM swap.
- Use **app-based TOTP** (Google Authenticator, Authy) instead of SMS-OTP.
- Set a **unique carrier account PIN** that is *not* based on DOB or easily-guessable info.
- Minimize PII exposure on social media — avoid posting birth dates, addresses, phone numbers.

**Layer 2 — Carrier-Level Defences (2.5 marks):**
- **Mandatory Video KYC** for all SIM-swap requests.
- **Biometric verification** (fingerprint / face match against Aadhaar / national ID).
- **24-hour cooling period** between request and execution, with SMS alert to the *old* SIM.
- **Real-time anomaly detection** — flag port requests from unusual locations, times, or device profiles.
- **Employee rotation, vetting, and dual-control** for high-risk transactions (institutional accountability).

**Layer 3 — Service-Provider-Level Defences (Banking, Crypto, Email) (2.5 marks):**
- Migrate from **SMS-OTP to push notifications** with **number-matching verification** (FIDO standards).
- Implement **risk-based adaptive authentication** — block high-risk transactions from new device/IP.
- Allow **hardware security keys** as a primary 2FA method.
- Add **time-delayed withdrawals** for crypto (24-hour cooling period for large transfers).
- Allow **out-of-band confirmation** via a separate device/email.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Phishing" with "SIM Swapping":** Phishing targets the *victim* directly; SIM Swapping targets the *carrier* using the victim's identity. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this conflation.
> 2. **Skipping the Phase Order:** The kill chain is sequential. Listing steps out of order (e.g., "OTP interception before SIM port") costs marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Indian Legal Context:** KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks for **Indian legal frameworks (IT Act, DPDP Act 2023, I4C)**. Omitting these loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Weak Conclusions:** A 14-mark answer must end with **preventive recommendations**, not just a description of the attack. Always include **defence-in-depth**.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** If the question says *"with a neat diagram,"* an absent or poorly-labelled diagram costs **2 marks** minimum. Always draw a labelled flowchart.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **The "Final-Night" Cheat Sheet — Memorize These 15 Points Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Definition:** SIM Swapping is *identity-based account takeover* via telecom social engineering — *not* phishing.
- [x] **Attack Phases (in order):** OSINT → PII Harvest → Carrier Impersonation → SIM Port → OTP Intercept → ATO → Monetize.
- [x] **Biggest U.S. Case:** Michael Terpin — **$23.8M stolen, $75.6M awarded** (2020).
- [x] **Biggest Public Demo:** Jack Dorsey Twitter hack (Aug 2019) — 15 minutes, SMS-to-Tweet exploit.
- [x] **Key Weak Protocol:** **SS7** (1975 vintage, no end-to-end auth, plaintext).
- [x] **Key Weak Process:** **KBA** relying on static PII from breach dumps.
- [x] **Why SMS-OTP Fails:** Converts *possession* factor into *knowledge* factor — defeats 2FA.
- [x] **Best Defence (User):** **FIDO2 / YubiKey / hardware security key** — phishing-resistant, SIM-swap-immune.
- [x] **Best Defence (Carrier):** **Video KYC + Biometric + 24-hr cooling period + dual-control.**
- [x] **Best Defence (Bank/Crypto):** **App-based TOTP / Push notifications with number matching.**
- [x] **Indian Law:** **IT Act §66C** (Identity Theft) + **§66D** (Personation) — 3 years / ₹1 lakh fine.
- [x] **Modern Law:** **DPDP Act 2023 §44** — up to **₹250 crore** penalty for PII breach.
- [x] **Indian Reporting:** **I4C Helpline 1930** (cyber-crime reporting).
- [x] **MITRE ATT&CK:** T1656 (Impersonation) + T1111 (2FA Interception) + T1078 (Valid Accounts).
- [x] **Mnemonic — "5 C's":** Carrier Negligence, Customer PII Leakage, Crypto Monetization, Court Precedent (Terpin), Counter-measures (FIDO2).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ATM Card Cloning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ATM Card Cloning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ATM Card Cloning** is an unauthorized form of *digital forgery* in which an attacker illicitly copies the encoded data stored on the **magnetic stripe (magstripe)** of a legitimate Automated Teller Machine (ATM) or Point-of-Sale (POS) card, and subsequently writes this stolen data onto a blank plastic card to produce a fully functional *counterfeit duplicate* that can be used for fraudulent financial transactions.

In the formal taxonomy of cyber security (aligned with KTU 2024 Module-1 – *Cybercrime & Security Threats*), ATM card cloning falls under the category of **Identity Theft** combined with **Financial Fraud** and is most often realized through a physical-layer attack known as **Card Skimming**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a **photocopier** that secretly copies every key you ever type on a piano, then plays your exact melody on a duplicate piano placed in a stranger's house. ATM card cloning is the *financial equivalent* of this — the **skimmer** is the hidden copier, the **victim's card** is the original piano, and the **counterfeit card** is the duplicate piano that the thief plays in shops to spend *your* money.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Track 1 & Track 2** | ISO/IEC 7813 data tracks on the magstripe | Contain the **Primary Account Number (PAN)**, cardholder name, expiry, and service code |
| **EMV** | Europay, Mastercard, Visa chip standard | Encrypts transaction data using a **cryptogram** — resistant to cloning |
| **Skimmer** | A covert electronic card-reader overlay | Captures raw magstripe data |
| **Shimmer** | A thin chip-reader insert placed inside the ATM card slot | Captures data from **EMV chip** cards |
| **PIN Pad Overlay** | A fake keypad placed over the real one | Records the **Personal Identification Number (PIN)** |
| **MSR (Magnetic Stripe Reader/Writer)** | A device such as the *MSR605X* | Used by criminals to **encode** the stolen data onto a blank card |
| **BIN** | Bank Identification Number (first 6 digits of PAN) | Identifies the issuing bank |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEEOT741 – Module 1):** Under the KTU 2024 syllabus, ATM card cloning is covered as a **canonical real-world example of "Cybercrime against Individuals"** and illustrates the *gap* between legacy security (magstripe) and modern security (EMV chip + tokenization).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Technology — Magnetic Stripe vs. EMV Chip

To *understand* why cloning is possible, you must first understand what is stored on the card.

#### 2.1.1 Magnetic Stripe (Magstripe) Architecture

- The black/ brown strip on the back of legacy cards contains **three tracks** of F2F (Frequency Double Frequency) encoded data.
- **Track 1** (alphanumeric, **210 bpi**, 79 characters) – contains PAN, name, expiry, country code, service code, CVV1, discretionary data.
- **Track 2** (numeric, **75 bpi**, 40 characters) – contains PAN, expiry, service code, CVV2, discretionary data.
- **Track 3** (numeric, rarely used) – for financial institution use only.
- **Critical Flaw:** All these tracks are **static, unencrypted, and read-only**. Anyone with a *\$20 magstripe reader* can read the raw data in plaintext.

#### 2.1.2 EMV Chip Architecture

- Introduced in **1996 (Europay, Mastercard, Visa)** as a global standard.
- Contains a **microprocessor** that performs cryptographic operations *per transaction*.
- For every transaction the chip generates a unique, **one-time cryptogram** (Application Cryptogram – ARQC) using a secret key stored in its secure memory.
- Even if you *physically extract* the chip data, you **cannot forge** the next cryptogram without the issuer's key → **cloning is computationally infeasible** under EMV.

### 2.2 The Attack Surface — *Why* Cloning Works

The attack succeeds because of the **legacy reliance on magstripe fallback**, the **lack of end-to-end encryption at the physical reader**, and the **psychological vulnerability of the user** (PIN entry).

### 2.3 Anatomy of a Card-Cloning Attack — The Four Pillars

A successful cloning attack is built on four cooperating components:

1. **The Skimmer (Data Capture)**
   - *External skimmer*: A plastic overlay fits over the real ATM card slot. It looks identical to the ATM's original fascia.
   - *Internal skimmer*: A deep-insert device placed inside the card slot, invisible from outside.
   - *ATM Malware*: Programs like **Ploutus** or **Tyupkin** infect the ATM's internal computer and read card data directly from the dispenser head.

2. **The PIN Capture Module**
   - *Hidden Camera*: Pin-hole camera mounted on the ATM's overhead lighting fixture or brochure holder, angled at the keypad.
   - *Fake Keypad Overlay*: A thin silicone replica placed over the real keypad; pressing it both passes the keystroke to the real keys *and* logs the digit internally.

3. **The Encoder (Data Writing)**
   - A **MSR605X** or similar Magnetic Stripe Reader/Writer.
   - Cost: approximately **\$20 – \$50** on the dark web.
   - The criminal encodes the stolen Track 1 and Track 2 data onto a **blank white "PVC card"** (Hi-Co or Lo-Co coercivity).

4. **The Cashing-Out (Monetization)**
   - The cloned card is used at **non-EMV POS terminals** (older gas pumps, toll booths, foreign ATMs that still read magstripe) to withdraw cash or buy **easily resellable goods** (gift cards, electronics).

### 2.4 Attack Variants

| Attack Type | Target Card Type | Method | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Card Skimming** | Magstripe only | External/internal skimmer | Duplicate magstripe card |
| **Shimming** | EMV chip | Thin shim inside the slot intercepts chip-to-terminal APDU traffic | Used for *card-not-present* fraud, not always a working clone |
| **Cash Trapping** | Any | A physical sleeve traps the dispensed cash | Cash stolen, card not cloned |
| **ATM Malware** | Any | USB / CD boot of malicious OS | Direct cash dispenser command |
| **NFC/RFID Cloning** | Contactless | Wireless sniffing of the contactless transaction | Limited, short-range, hard to scale |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Key Point | One-Liner for Exam |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magstripe data is **static & unencrypted** | This is the root cause of skimming |
| 2 | Tracks 1 & 2 contain the **PAN + CVV** | Enough for **Card-Not-Present (CNP)** fraud |
| 3 | EMV generates a **unique cryptogram per transaction** | Breaks the cloning business model |
| 4 | A skimmer is a **read-only device** | It does not affect the original card |
| 5 | **Most common cloning site**: standalone ATM vestibules, gas stations, tourist hotspots | Where physical tampering goes unnoticed |
| 6 | **Liability shift** in 2015 (EMVCo mandate) | Merchants without chip readers bear fraud liability |
| 7 | 3-digit **CVV/CVC** is *printed* on the card | Different from the **iCVV** stored on the chip |
| 8 | KTU keyword to remember | "Skimming ≠ Hacking — it is **Physical-layer Eavesdropping**" |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although criminal, card-cloning research drives genuine engineering advances:
- **Hardware Security Modules (HSMs)** in bank back-ends store issuer keys for ARQC verification.
- **Tokenization** (used by Apple Pay, Google Pay) replaces the real PAN with a one-time token, rendering intercepted data useless.
- **Jamming sensors** in modern ATMs detect foreign objects (anti-skimming jitters, green-flash sensors).
- **Geolocation & velocity checks** in fraud-detection engines flag impossible travel between consecutive magstripe swipes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis (Exhaustive Walk-through)

This section replaces the mathematical derivation of Section 3 with a **forensically detailed, step-by-step technical procedure** of an end-to-end ATM card-cloning attack chain. Every step is enumerated and explained exactly as it would be presented in a KTU Module-1 university answer.

### 3.1 Stage 0 — Reconnaissance (Days 1–7)

> *Goal:* Identify a vulnerable ATM, gas pump, or POS terminal.

**Step 1:** Attacker performs physical surveillance of the target ATM.
- Notes opening hours, footfall, CCTV coverage, and whether the ATM is in an *attended* (bank branch) or *unattended* (convenience store, gas station) location.
- Higher success probability at **unattended, outdoor, low-traffic ATMs**.

**Step 2:** Attacker checks the ATM for **visible anti-skimming features** (green flashing light, anti-skimming jitters, card-slot embossing).
- Older ATMs without these are preferred.

### 3.2 Stage 1 — Deployment of the Skimmer (≈ 30 seconds)

**Step 3:** Attacker approaches the ATM during a *low-traffic window* (e.g., 03:00 AM).

**Step 4:** Attacher places the **external skimmer** over the card slot.
- The skimmer's outer mould is a 3D-printed or injection-moulded replica of the original ATM fascia.
- It is held in place by a thin layer of double-sided adhesive.

**Step 5:** Attacher places the **PIN-capture overlay** over the keypad.
- A silicone membrane no thicker than **1.5 mm** registers the keypresses.

**Step 6:** Attacher installs the **hidden camera** in a pre-drilled hole of an ATM-styled *leaflet holder* or *top-light diffuser*.
- Camera angle is pre-calibrated to frame the keypad 1:1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time-on-device for a professional skimmer: ~ 30 – 60 seconds.** This is why it is a *physical* crime, not a *remote* one.

### 3.3 Stage 2 — Data Harvesting (Days 1–14)

**Step 7:** Each time a customer inserts a card, the skimmer's internal **magnetic-head** reads Tracks 1 and 2.
- The data is buffered in a small **flash memory** chip (typically 1–8 MB, sufficient for **>5,000 cards**).

**Step 8:** The PIN overlay simultaneously captures the 4-digit PIN keyed in by the customer.

**Step 9:** Data packet structure stored on the skimmer (illustrative):

$$
\text{Record} = \{\,\text{Timestamp}\,\Vert\,\text{PAN}\,\Vert\,\text{Name}\,\Vert\,\text{Expiry}\,\Vert\,\text{SVC}\,\Vert\,\text{CVV1}\,\Vert\,\text{PIN}\,\}
$$

Each record is written to the next free sector in the on-board flash.

**Step 10:** Attacher returns (often at the same hour of the day, to keep the pattern un-noticeable) and **retrieves** the skimmer + overlay + camera.
- The harvested data is downloaded to a **laptop** via USB or Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE).

### 3.4 Stage 3 — Decoding & Verification (Off-line, 1–2 hours)

**Step 11:** Attacher runs the harvested dump through a software tool (e.g., *Magstripe Studio, Jerm*, or a custom Python script in illegal markets).

**Step 12:** Tool separates the records by **BIN** (Bank Identification Number) and **sorts by credit-limit / card-type** heuristics.

**Step 13:** Attacher selects **"high-value" cards** (typically cards with high pre-approved limits, premium BINs like *Platinum*/*World*/*Signature*).

**Step 14:** Attacher runs a **BIN attack / validity check** by attempting a **\$0.00 pre-authorization** on a payment gateway — confirming that the PAN, expiry, and CVV2 are *live*.

### 3.5 Stage 4 — Counterfeit Encoding (≈ 5 minutes per card)

**Step 15:** Attacher inserts a **blank white PVC card** into an **MSR605X** writer.

**Step 16:** Writes the captured **Track 1** at 210 bpi and **Track 2** at 75 bpi using the *Hi-Co* (4000 Oe) or *Lo-Co* (300 Oe) standard.

**Step 17:** Writes the captured PIN on a **handwritten note** (the PIN cannot be written to the magstripe; it must be memorized/typed during cash-out).

**Step 18:** Optionally, applies a **hologram sticker** and prints the issuing bank's logo on the card surface using a re-transfer card printer (e.g., *Magicard Rio Pro* or *Datacard SP75*) to make the counterfeit visually convincing.

### 3.6 Stage 5 — Cashing Out (Monetization, Hours to Days)

**Step 19:** Attacher travels to a region or merchant where **magstripe fallback** is still accepted.
- Common destinations: developing economies, U.S. gas pumps (still magstripe), older POS terminals, certain toll booths.

**Step 20:** Performs **"low-and-slow" cash-outs**:
- Multiple small withdrawals (e.g., **\$200** instead of **\$1,000**) to evade velocity-based fraud triggers.
- Uses different ATMs spaced geographically to avoid the bank's *geo-impossibility* heuristic.

**Step 21:** Alternatively, purchases **high-resale, low-friction goods**:
- **Gift cards** (the favourite, as they are nearly anonymous and instantly liquid).
- **Electronics** (smartphones, laptops) — often shipped via freight forwarders to obscure the final destination.

**Step 22:** Original victim discovers fraud only when the **monthly statement** is reviewed, by which time the cloned card has been used and discarded.

### 3.7 Counter-Cloning Engineering Matrix

| Layer | Defence | Mechanism | Defeats Which Attack Stage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Card | **EMV Chip** | Cryptogram per transaction | Stage 5 (counterfeit unusable at chip terminal) |
| Card | **Tokenization** (NFC payments) | One-time PAN token | Stage 4 (data useless) |
| ATM | **Anti-skimming jitters** | Electromagnetic disturbance of foreign reader | Stage 2 (skimmer can't read) |
| ATM | **Green-flash sensor** | Optical card-slot sensor | Stage 1 (skimmer detected) |
| Network | **Velocity / geo-fraud AI** | Real-time transaction analytics | Stage 5 (withdrawal blocked) |
| User | **2-Factor / OTP for cards** | OTP for high-value transactions | Stage 5 |
| User | **Physical inspection habit** | "Wiggle the card slot & keypad" | Stage 1 |
| Issuer | **Card alerts via SMS / app** | Real-time transaction push | Stage 5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagram & Schematic — The Cloning Kill-Chain

> *Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, the schematic below is rendered as a Mermaid **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**, mapping the *interactions* between attacker modules, victim actions, and bank infrastructure. This preserves accuracy even where a physical photo/illustration is not possible.*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Attacker Stage 0 Reconnaissance] --> A2[Attacker Stage 1 Deploy Skimmer and PIN Overlay and Camera]
    A2 --> A3[Attacker Stage 2 Harvest Card Data and PINs]
    A3 --> A4[Attacker Stage 3 Decode and Select High Value Cards]
    A4 --> A5[Attacker Stage 4 Encode Counterfeit on Blank PVC via MSR605X]
    A5 --> A6[Attacker Stage 5 Cash Out at Magstripe Only POS or ATM]

    V1[Victim Inserts Card Into ATM] --> V2[Skimmer Reads Track 1 and Track 2]
    V2 --> V3[PIN Overlay Records PIN Digit by Digit]
    V3 --> V4[Camera Records Video Backup of Keypad]
    V4 --> A3

    A6 --> B1[Bank Fraud Engine]
    A6 --> B2[Merchants Liability Shift]
    A6 --> B3[Cardholder Dispute and Chargeback]

    B1 --> C1[Block Card]
    B1 --> C2[Trigger OTP Re Issuance]
    B3 --> C1

    D1[Defence Layer EMV Chip] -.Defeats.-> A6
    D2[Defence Layer Tokenization NFC] -.Defeats.-> A5
    D3[Defence Layer ATM Anti Skim Jitter] -.Defeats.-> V2
    D4[Defence Layer User Wiggling Card Slot] -.Defeats.-> A2
    D5[Defence Layer Real Time SMS Alert] -.Triggers.-> B1
```

### 4.1 Reading the Diagram

- The **central horizontal chain (A1 → A6)** is the *kill-chain* — the sequential stages the attacker must complete.
- The **upper V1 → V4** branch shows the *victim's unwitting role* — the actions the customer takes that are silently observed and recorded.
- The **lower B / C branch** is the *detection and response* loop — what happens once the fraud is identified.
- The **defence layer (D1–D5)** uses *dotted lines* labelled `Defeats` or `Triggers` to show which countermeasure neutralizes which attack stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the most effective defences (D1 – EMV, D2 – Tokenization) work at the *right end* of the kill-chain, *after* the data is captured but *before* the cash-out. This is why EMV migration has globally reduced counterfeit-card fraud by an estimated **80 – 90 %** since the 2015 liability shift.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> *The following question bank is modelled strictly on the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for PEEOT741 — Fundamentals of Cyber Security. Marks, cognitive levels (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy), and choice structure follow the official KTU template.*

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B, Q1.a]**
> *Define **ATM card cloning**. Distinguish between **Track 1** and **Track 2** data stored on the magnetic stripe of an ATM card. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** ATM card cloning is the unauthorized process of *copying* the encoded information from a legitimate card's magnetic stripe and writing it onto a blank card to create a *functional duplicate* for fraudulent use.

**Distinction (2 marks):**

| Feature | Track 1 | Track 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Density | 210 bpi | 75 bpi |
| Characters | 79 (alphanumeric) | 40 (numeric only) |
| Contains | PAN, cardholder name, expiry, country code, CVV1, discretionary data | PAN, expiry, service code, CVV2, discretionary data |
| Use | International / airline | Financial / ATM |

Track 1 is *readable* by both financial and airline systems; Track 2 is *restricted* to financial use. Cloning tools extract **both** tracks to maximize fraud options.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary, Q1.b]**
> *Explain the role of a **skimmer** and a **PIN-pad overlay** in an ATM cloning attack. Why is the PIN considered the most critical data element? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- A **skimmer** is a covert magnetic-stripe reader installed *over* (or *inside*) the ATM card slot. It reads and stores **Track 1 and Track 2** data of every card inserted. (1 mark)
- A **PIN-pad overlay** is a thin, often silicone, keypad placed *over* the real keypad. It registers the customer's 4-digit PIN and stores it internally (or transmits it to a paired receiver). (1 mark)
- The PIN is the **most critical element** because, under the banking rules of all major card networks (Visa, Mastercard, RuPay), a transaction is considered *authorized* only if (a) the magstripe/chip data is valid **AND** (b) the correct PIN is supplied at an ATM. Without the PIN, a stolen magstripe dump is *useless* for ATM withdrawals and reduces the fraudster to lower-value card-not-present (online) abuse. (1 mark)

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Module 1, Q4.a]**
> *(a) [7 marks]* Describe, with a labelled diagram, the **end-to-end kill-chain of an ATM card-cloning attack**. Identify the *four* critical hardware components an attacker must possess.

> *(b) [7 marks]* Discuss **four defence mechanisms** (one user-level, one terminal-level, one card-level, one network-level) that can break the kill-chain at different stages. Justify *why* the EMV chip is the single most effective counter-measure.

**Model Answer A:**

**(a) End-to-End kill-chain (7 marks):**

The cloning attack can be decomposed into the following sequential stages:

1. **Reconnaissance** — Attacker surveys ATMs to find a vulnerable, low-surveillance, magstripe-accepting terminal. (1 mark)
2. **Deployment** — Attacher installs three physical artefacts within 30–60 seconds: (1 mark)
   - **Skimmer** over the card slot.
   - **PIN-pad overlay** over the keypad.
   - **Hidden pinhole camera** as a backup PIN recorder.
3. **Harvesting** — As customers transact over the next 1–14 days, the skimmer silently records PAN, name, expiry, CVV and the overlay records the PIN. (1 mark)
4. **Decoding & Selection** — Attacher downloads the device, separates records by BIN, and validates the cards via a micro-pre-auth check. (1 mark)
5. **Counterfeit Encoding** — Selected dumps are written to blank PVC cards using an **MSR605X** magnetic-stripe writer at 210 bpi (Track 1) and 75 bpi (Track 2). (1 mark)
6. **Cash-Out** — Counterfeits are used at magstripe-accepting merchants / foreign ATMs in "low-and-slow" mode (multiple \$200 withdrawals, different locations) to evade velocity fraud rules. (1 mark)
7. **Cover-Up** — The skimmer is removed; victim discovers fraud only at month-end statement review. (1 mark)

**Four critical hardware components (within the explanation above, awarded cumulatively):**
(i) External/Internal skimmer, (ii) PIN-pad overlay or hidden camera, (iii) MSR605X encoder, (iv) Blank PVC cards + (optionally) re-transfer card printer for cosmetics.

*(Refer to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4 for the labelled block diagram.)*

---

**(b) Four defence mechanisms (7 marks = 4 × 1 mark + 3 marks for EMV justification):**

| Layer | Defence | Which Stage It Breaks |
|---|---|---|
| **User-level (1 mark)** | **Physical inspection habit** — "Wiggle the card slot, the brochure holder, and the keypad before inserting the card." The user detects the skimmer/overlay at *Stage 2 (Deployment)*. | Breaks Stage 2 |
| **Terminal-level (1 mark)** | **Anti-skimming jitters** — Modern ATMs emit a low-amplitude electromagnetic pulse near the card slot. A foreign reader's signal is corrupted, so the skimmer cannot read the magstripe reliably. | Breaks Stage 3 |
| **Card-level (1 mark)** | **EMV chip** — Generates a unique, single-use **Application Cryptogram (ARQC)** for every transaction using a secret key sealed inside the chip. | Breaks Stage 6 |
| **Network-level (1 mark)** | **Real-time fraud analytics + SMS alerts** — Velocity checks, geo-impossibility rules, and instant transaction push-notifications let the issuer *block* the card within seconds of suspicious activity. | Breaks Stage 6 |

**Why EMV is the single most effective counter-measure (3 marks):**

(i) The EMV chip performs an *active cryptographic handshake* with the issuer's Host System using a secret key that **never leaves the secure element**. The **ARQC** is mathematically bound to (a) the transaction amount, (b) the terminal ID, (c) a random nonce, and (d) a transaction counter — so the cryptogram is *useless* to the attacker for any future transaction. (1 mark)

(ii) It shifts the attacker's business model. With magstripe cloning, **one dump → unlimited reuse**. With EMV, **one dump → zero reuse**, because forging the next ARQC is computationally infeasible (equivalent to breaking 3DES/RSA/AES used by the issuer key). (1 mark)

(iii) Empirical evidence: post the **2015 EMVCo Liability Shift**, the U.S. saw a **~ 76 % drop in counterfeit-card fraud** at chip-enabled merchants (per Federal Reserve reports), confirming EMV's systemic effectiveness. (1 mark)

---

> **Question B [Internal Choice for the above question — KTU ESE 2024 Module 1, Q4.b]**
> *(a) [7 marks]* Differentiate between **Card Skimming** and **Shimming**. Under what circumstances can an EMV chip card *still* be cloned? Explain with reference to **magstripe fallback** and **ARQC bypass** techniques.

> *(b) [7 marks]* As a cyber-security consultant, propose a **comprehensive defence-in-depth strategy** for a national bank to mitigate ATM card cloning. Address *people*, *process*, and *technology* dimensions.

**Model Answer B:**

**(a) Skimming vs. Shimming (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Skimming | Shimming |
|---|---|---|
| Target | **Magnetic stripe** of any card | **EMV chip** of newer cards |
| Device | Skimmer overlay (bulky) | Shimmer — a paper-thin flexible circuit slipped *inside* the card slot, between the chip and the reader's contacts |
| Output | **Working magstripe counterfeit** | Often produces *partial* or *non-functional* clones; data is mainly used for **card-not-present (CNP) online fraud** |
| Detection | Easy (visible bulges, mismatched colour) | Hard (essentially invisible) |
| Year popular | 2000s – present | 2017 – present |

**When an EMV card can *still* be cloned (3 marks):**

1. **Magstripe Fallback (2 marks):** If the terminal's chip reader is *damaged, dirty, or non-functional*, the terminal falls back to reading the magstripe. The shim/skimmer captures the static magstripe data, which can then be written onto a counterfeit and used at any *magstripe-only* terminal (older gas pumps, toll booths, foreign ATMs that have not yet migrated to EMV).

2. **ARQC Bypass (1 mark):** In rare, sophisticated attacks (e.g., the **"Chip-and-PIN is broken"** 2012 research by Cambridge), attackers exploit cryptographic *downgrade* vulnerabilities where a terminal accepts an offline PIN verification or fails to enforce ARQC validation. These are usually patched but represent a residual attack surface.

3. **Card-Not-Present (CNP) abuse:** Even without cloning the chip, harvested PAN + CVV2 + expiry enable online shopping fraud, since 3-D Secure (OTP) is not universal.

---

**(b) Defence-in-Depth Strategy (7 marks):**

| Dimension | Recommendation | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **People (2 marks)** | (i) Continuous customer awareness campaigns (e.g., "Wiggle, Look, Compare") via SMS and bank app banners. (ii) Mandatory annual cyber-security training for all branch staff and ATM custodians. (iii) Reward-based whistle-blower policy to encourage reporting of suspicious devices. | 2 |
| **Process (2 marks)** | (i) **Daily physical inspection SOP** for every ATM in the network — checklist with photographic proof uploaded to a central dashboard. (ii) **Incident response playbook** with 4-hour SLA from detection to card-block + customer notification. (iii) **Liability tracking** — clear audit trail so that the liability shift is *always* applied. | 2 |
| **Technology (3 marks)** | (i) **EMV + Tokenization for all cards**, with magstripe suppression for purely domestic customers. (ii) **AI-based fraud engine** using *impossible-travel* and *velocity* heuristics. (iii) **Real-time SMS / app push** for every transaction. (iv) **Anti-skimming hardware** (jammers, green-flash sensors, depth-sensing on the slot). (v) **Biometric / OTP-based** ATM authentication as a pilot. | 3 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Reasons Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Conflating "Skimming" with "Phishing".** Phishing is *online* social engineering; skimming is *physical-layer* data capture. Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Writing only the definition, not the *kill-chain*.** Module-1 questions on this topic usually award marks for the *stages* (deployment → harvesting → encoding → cash-out). A definition-only answer caps you at ~ 3 of 7.
> 3. **Forgetting the PIN.** It is astonishingly common for students to discuss the skimmer (which captures the card data) and *omit* the PIN-capture component. Examiners expect **both** data elements.
> 4. **Ignoring EMV's cryptographic basis.** "EMV prevents cloning" is *insufficient*. State **how** — i.e., per-transaction **ARQC cryptogram** generated using a chip-resident **secret key** that is mathematically infeasible to clone.
> 5. **Mixing up CVV1 (magstripe) vs. CVV2 (printed) vs. iCVV (chip).** Examiners love to test this — know the three values and where each one lives.
> 6. **No diagram in 14-mark answers.** A labelled block-diagram of the kill-chain is worth at least 1–2 marks. Use a Mermaid-style flowchart or a hand-drawn block diagram.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition (must-memorize):** Card cloning = illicit duplication of card data onto a blank card via magnetic-stripe technology.
- **Primary vulnerability:** Magnetic stripe stores **static, unencrypted** Track 1 + Track 2 data.
- **Track 1** = 210 bpi, 79 alphanum chars; **Track 2** = 75 bpi, 40 numeric chars.
- **Four attack components:** Skimmer, PIN-capture device, MSR encoder, blank PVC card.
- **Skimming vs. Shimming:** Skimmer = magstripe (bulky, external); Shimmer = chip (thin, internal, for CNP fraud).
- **EMV chip defence:** Generates a unique **ARQC** cryptogram per transaction using a sealed secret key → cloning is *computationally infeasible*.
- **Kill-chain (memorize the 6 stages):** Recon → Deploy → Harvest → Decode → Encode → Cash-out.
- **Defence layers (memorize as PPP–TTT):** **P**eople (user awareness), **P**rocess (SOPs), **P**hysical (anti-skim hardware), **T**okenization, **T**ransaction-monitoring, **T**wo-factor (OTP).
- **Liability Shift (2015):** Merchants without EMV-capable terminals bear the fraud cost → *incentivizes* global chip migration.
- **Exam tip:** Always mention **PAN + Track + CVV + PIN** as the four stolen data elements. Always mention **ARQC** as the EMV defence.
- **Forensic reality:** Skimming is *physical*, not *remote* — and therefore *defeatable* by simple user vigilance (the **wiggle test**).
- **Real-world impact:** Global skimming losses exceed **\$ 2 billion annually** (Nilson Report estimates) — a figure that justifies the entire Module-1 treatment of this topic in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hacking Email for Money
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hacking Email for Money

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Email Hacking for Financial Gain** (also termed as **Email-Based Cyber Fraud** or **Cyber Financial Exploitation via Email**) is a category of cyber-attack wherein adversaries compromise, impersonate, or socially engineer electronic mail communications with the explicit intent of unlawfully extracting monetary assets, sensitive financial credentials, or unauthorized transaction authorizations from victims. According to the **Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3)** and **CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team)** advisories, email-based financial fraud constitutes one of the largest reported categories of cybercrime, accounting for billions of dollars in global losses annually.

The umbrella term covers several distinct but overlapping threat vectors:

| Vector | Definition |
|--------|-----------|
| **Phishing** | Mass-mailed deceptive emails impersonating legitimate entities |
| **Spear Phishing** | Targeted phishing aimed at specific high-value individuals |
| **Whaling** | Spear phishing targeting senior executives (C-level) |
| **Business Email Compromise (BEC)** | Impersonation of executives/vendors to authorize fraudulent wire transfers |
| **Email Account Compromise (EAC)** | Direct hijacking of a legitimate email account via credential theft |
| **419 / Nigerian Prince Scam** | Advance-fee fraud promising massive future returns |
| **Email Extortion / Sextortion** | Blackmail demanding crypto payments |
| **Malspam** | Spam emails distributing malware that exfiltrates banking data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU PEEOT741 Module 1 syllabus, this topic sits at the intersection of *threats, attack vectors, and financial cyber-crime*. Examiners expect students to recognize **attack anatomy**, classify **scam typologies**, and propose **counter-measures** as part of CO1 (Understanding security threats).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you receive a beautifully crafted letter in your physical mailbox. The envelope bears the official logo of your bank, the paper is heavy and watermarked, and the letter politely asks you to "verify your PIN and OTP" by calling a number printed inside. The letter even contains a real-looking but **fake phone number** that connects to a fraudster's call center.

You dial the number. A polite voice identifies themselves as a "bank officer," asks for your credentials, and within minutes, your account is drained.

Now scale this to **billions of inboxes** sent per day, routed electronically at the speed of light, with logos copied pixel-perfect from the bank's website. **That is "Hacking Email for Money" in essence.** The email is the bait, the hook, and sometimes the weapon — all in one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** The hacker does NOT necessarily "break" the email server. Instead, they exploit the **human trust** placed in email as a communication medium. The technology is the delivery mechanism; **psychology is the actual attack surface.**

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters (Statistical Anchors)

- The **FBI IC3 2023 Annual Report** recorded losses exceeding **$12.5 billion** from cybercrime, with **BEC alone accounting for $2.9 billion**.
- The average cost of a **business email compromise** attack is approximately **$125,000** per successful incident.
- Globally, **91% of cyberattacks** begin with a phishing email (Source: **Deloitte 2023 Cyber Threat Report**).
- The typical **email fraud dwell time** (time between intrusion and detection) is approximately **$101$ days**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Anchor Metric:** `Email = #1 Initial Access Vector in Cybercrime`. Every KTU student must internalize this as the single most repeated Board Exam statistic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Email-Based Financial Attack

A successful "Hacking Email for Money" operation typically follows a structured **cyber kill chain**, which can be decomposed into the following sequential phases:

1. **Reconnaissance (Information Gathering)**
   - The attacker harvests email addresses using **OSINT (Open-Source Intelligence)** tools like theHarvester, Hunter.io, or LinkedIn scraping.
   - The attacker studies the target organization's hierarchy, vendor relationships, and executive names.

2. **Weaponization**
   - The attacker crafts a malicious email — this may contain a **malicious attachment** (e.g., a macro-enabled Word document, PDF with embedded JavaScript, or a `.lnk` shortcut) or a **phishing link**.
   - The email is designed to bypass **spam filters** using obfuscation (homoglyphs, zero-font characters, base64 encoding).

3. **Delivery**
   - The email is sent from a **spoofed** or **look-alike domain** (e.g., `support@paypa1.com` instead of `paypal.com`).
   - Common spoofing techniques: **SPF bypass**, **DKIM weak key**, **DMARC misconfiguration**, **display name spoofing**.

4. **Exploitation**
   - Victim opens attachment → macro executes → **malware installs** (keylogger, RAT, banking trojan like **Emotet**, **TrickBot**, **Dridex**, or **Agent Tesla**).
   - OR Victim clicks link → lands on **credential harvester page** → enters username/password.

5. **Installation / Credential Capture**
   - Malware establishes **persistence** (registry run keys, scheduled tasks).
   - Credentials are exfiltrated to a **C2 (Command & Control) server**.

6. **Command & Control (C2)**
   - Attacker logs into the victim's inbox using stolen credentials.
   - Attacker sets up **mail forwarding rules** to silently copy all future emails.

7. **Actions on Objectives (Monetization)**
   - Attacker initiates **fraudulent wire transfers**, requests **gift cards**, diverts **payroll**, or sells access on dark-web marketplaces.
   - Money is laundered via **cryptocurrency mixers**, **mule accounts**, or **shell companies**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this section critical for KTU?** Examiners frequently ask students to "explain the stages of a phishing attack" or "describe the kill chain." Memorize the 7-stage model above.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although "Hacking Email for Money" is largely qualitative, the following quantitative metrics and formulas appear in KTU exam questions:

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit / Notes |
|---------|------------------|--------------|
| **Email Open Rate (Phishing Campaign Success)** | $\text{Open Rate} = \dfrac{N_{\text{opened}}}{N_{\text{sent}}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **percentage** |
| **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $\text{CTR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{clicked}}}{N_{\text{opened}}} \times 100\%$ | Measures link engagement |
| **Conversion Rate (Credential Submission)** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{submitted}}}{N_{\text{clicked}}} \times 100\%$ | Most critical metric for attackers |
| **Estimated Loss per BEC Attack** | $L_{\text{BEC}} = \text{Mean Loss} \approx \$125{,}000$ | FBI IC3 baseline |
| **Annual Loss Estimation Model** | $L_{\text{annual}} = N_{\text{victims}} \times \overline{L}_{\text{per incident}}$ | $N_{\text{victims}}$ = reported victims |
| **Attack ROI** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{L_{\text{stolen}} - C_{\text{attack}}}{C_{\text{attack}}} \times 100\%$ | $C_{\text{attack}}$ = campaign cost |
| **Dwell Time (Detection Latency)** | $T_{\text{dwell}} \approx 101 \text{ days}$ | Median industry value |
| **Email Spoofing Detection Rate** | $P_{\text{detect}} = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{SPF}})(1 - P_{\text{DKIM}})(1 - P_{\text{DMARC}})$ | Assuming independent layers |
| **Spear Phishing Targeting Precision** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{high-value targets}}}{N_{\text{total targets}}}$ | $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| **Mule Account Velocity Check** | $V_{\text{mule}} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Transactions}}{\Delta t}$ | Transactions per unit time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notational Rule (for Board Exams):** When substituting absolute values inside markdown tables, ALWAYS use the LaTeX vertical bar `\vert` instead of the keyboard pipe `\vert`. This prevents table-rendering breakage.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although primarily a cybersecurity topic, "Hacking Email for Money" has direct relevance to multiple engineering branches:

- **Computer Science / IT:** Designing **anti-phishing ML classifiers**, **email authentication protocols (SPF/DKIM/DMARC)**, **anomaly detection systems**.
- **Electronics & Communication:** Understanding **email routing protocols (SMTP, POP3, IMAP)** at the packet level.
- **Mechanical/Civil (Industry Context):** Understanding **BEC attacks on industrial procurement** and **vendor impersonation in supply chain finance**.
- **Finance Engineering:** Modeling **fraud risk scores** and **transaction anomaly thresholds**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivations of Core Email-Fraud Quantitative Models

#### 3.1.1 Derivation: Attack ROI Formula for an Email Fraud Campaign

Let $L_{\text{stolen}}$ denote the total monetary value successfully extracted by the attacker, and let $C_{\text{attack}}$ denote the total cost incurred by the attacker to mount the campaign (including infrastructure, mailing-list purchase, mules, and time).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= L_{\text{stolen}} - C_{\text{attack}} \\
\text{Return on Investment (ROI)} &= \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{C_{\text{attack}}} \\
\therefore \quad \text{ROI} &= \dfrac{L_{\text{stolen}} - C_{\text{attack}}}{C_{\text{attack}}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step Logic:**
- **Line 1:** Net profit is simply the difference between what was stolen and what was spent.
- **Line 2:** ROI is normalized profit divided by the cost basis.
- **Line 3:** Substituting Line 1 into Line 2 and multiplying by $100\%$ yields percentage ROI.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose an attacker sends $50{,}000$ spear-phishing emails to corporate finance executives. Campaign cost breakdown:

- Email-list purchase: $\$500$
- VPS hosting for credential harvester: $\$50$
- Domain registration (look-alike): $\$12$
- Mule account fees: $\$200$
- Attacker's time (10 hours at $\$20$/hour): $\$200$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{attack}} &= 500 + 50 + 12 + 200 + 200 = \$962 \\
\text{Successful victim count } (N_v) &= 8 \text{ executives} \\
\text{Mean loss per victim } (\overline{L}) &= \$45{,}000 \\
L_{\text{stolen}} &= N_v \times \overline{L} = 8 \times 45{,}000 = \$360{,}000 \\
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{360{,}000 - 962}{962} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{359{,}038}{962} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 37{,}323.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This astronomical ROI explains **why email fraud remains the dominant cybercrime vector** — the asymmetric cost-benefit ratio is unmatched by any other cyberattack vector.

#### 3.1.2 Derivation: Email Spoofing Detection Probability

Email authentication uses three independent layers: **SPF (Sender Policy Framework)**, **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)**, and **DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance)**. Assuming each layer independently blocks a spoofed email with probabilities $P_{\text{SPF}}$, $P_{\text{DKIM}}$, and $P_{\text{DMARC}}$ respectively, the probability that **all three layers fail** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{all fail}} &= (1 - P_{\text{SPF}})(1 - P_{\text{DKIM}})(1 - P_{\text{DMARC}}) \\
P_{\text{detect}} &= 1 - P_{\text{all fail}} \\
\therefore \quad P_{\text{detect}} &= 1 - (1 - P_{\text{SPF}})(1 - P_{\text{DKIM}})(1 - P_{\text{DMARC}})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Assume $P_{\text{SPF}} = 0.85$, $P_{\text{DKIM}} = 0.90$, $P_{\text{DMARC}} = 0.95$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{all fail}} &= (1 - 0.85)(1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.95) \\
&= (0.15)(0.10)(0.05) \\
&= 0.00075 \\
P_{\text{detect}} &= 1 - 0.00075 = 0.99925 = 99.925\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** With all three layers correctly configured, the probability of spoofed email reaching the inbox is approximately **$0.075\%$** — which is why KTU examiners emphasize implementing **all three** protocols simultaneously.

#### 3.1.3 Derivation: Funnel Conversion Analytics for Phishing Campaigns

A phishing campaign is often modeled as a **funnel**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{sent}} &\xrightarrow{\text{Delivery}} N_{\text{delivered}} \\
N_{\text{delivered}} &\xrightarrow{\text{Open}} N_{\text{opened}} \\
N_{\text{opened}} &\xrightarrow{\text{Click}} N_{\text{clicked}} \\
N_{\text{clicked}} &\xrightarrow{\text{Submit}} N_{\text{submitted}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let the conversion probabilities be:

$$
p_1 = \dfrac{N_{\text{delivered}}}{N_{\text{sent}}}, \quad p_2 = \dfrac{N_{\text{opened}}}{N_{\text{delivered}}}, \quad p_3 = \dfrac{N_{\text{clicked}}}{N_{\text{opened}}}, \quad p_4 = \dfrac{N_{\text{submitted}}}{N_{\text{clicked}}}
$$

Then the **overall credential-capture probability** per email sent is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{success}} &= p_1 \times p_2 \times p_3 \times p_4 \\
&= \dfrac{N_{\text{submitted}}}{N_{\text{sent}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:** Assume $p_1 = 0.95$, $p_2 = 0.30$, $p_3 = 0.10$, $p_4 = 0.40$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{success}} &= 0.95 \times 0.30 \times 0.10 \times 0.40 \\
&= 0.0114 \\
\text{Expected submissions from } N_{\text{sent}} &= 0.0114 \times N_{\text{sent}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $N_{\text{sent}} = 100{,}000$ emails:

$$
N_{\text{submitted}} = 0.0114 \times 100{,}000 = 1{,}140 \text{ credential captures}
$$

This shows why even low conversion rates yield **massive scale** when attackers send bulk emails.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Attack Vectors vs. Indicators

| Attack Vector | Typical Lure | Target | Primary Detection Signal |
|---------------|--------------|--------|--------------------------|
| **Bulk Phishing** | "Your account is suspended" | General public | High volume from single IP |
| **Spear Phishing** | Fake invoice / vendor update | Finance staff | Unusual sender domain |
| **Whaling** | "Legal subpoena" / "Tax notice" | CEO, CFO | Display-name mismatch |
| **BEC** | Urgent wire transfer | Accounts payable | Out-of-band verification fails |
| **EAC** | Fake security alert | Any user | Mail-rule anomalies |
| **Malspam** | Fake shipping notice | All | Sandboxing triggers on attachment |
| **419 Scam** | "Next of kin inheritance" | Greedy / naïve | Unrealistic promise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "B-S-W-B-E-M-4":** **B**ulk, **S**pear, **W**haling, **B**EC, **E**AC, **M**alspam. Memorize the typology table for 14-mark descriptive questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Email Attack Kill-Chain (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reconnaissance"]:::stage1 --> B["Weaponization"]
    B --> C["Email Delivery"]
    C --> D["Exploitation"]
    D --> E["Installation and Credential Capture"]
    E --> F["Command and Control"]
    F --> G["Monetization and Laundering"]

    A1["OSINT Harvesting"] -.-> A
    A2["Social Engineering Recon"] -.-> A
    B1["Malicious Attachment Crafting"] -.-> B
    B2["Phishing URL Generation"] -.-> B
    C1["SMTP Spoofing"] -.-> C
    C2["Lookalike Domain Registration"] -.-> C
    D1["Victim Clicks Link or Opens Attachment"] -.-> D
    D2["Macro or Exploit Kit Executes"] -.-> D
    E1["Keylogger or Banking Trojan Installs"] -.-> E
    E2["Credentials Exfiltrated"] -.-> E
    F1["Mailbox Rule Setup"] -.-> F
    F2["Lateral Movement"] -.-> F
    G1["Fraudulent Wire Transfer"] -.-> G
    G2["Cryptocurrency Laundering"] -.-> G

    classDef stage1 fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Email Authentication Stack (SPF / DKIM / DMARC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sender["Attacker Mail Server"] -->|Spoofed HELO| MX["Receiving MX Server"]
    MX --> SPFCheck{"SPF Check Pass"}
    SPFCheck -->|Yes| DKIMCheck{"DKIM Signature Valid"}
    SPFCheck -->|No| Quarantine1["Quarantine or Reject"]
    DKIMCheck -->|Yes| DMARCCheck{"DMARC Policy Aligned"}
    DKIMCheck -->|No| Quarantine2["Quarantine or Reject"]
    DMARCCheck -->|Yes| Inbox["Delivered to Inbox"]
    DMARCCheck -->|No| Quarantine3["Per DMARC Policy"]
    Inbox --> User["User Clicks Phishing Link"]
    User --> Compromise["Credential Compromise and Financial Loss"]

    classDef good fill:#1d6f42,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef bad fill:#8b1a1a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class SPFCheck,DKIMCheck,DMARCCheck decision
    class Quarantine1,Quarantine2,Quarantine3,Compromise bad
    class Inbox,MX,Sender good
```

### 4.3 BEC Attack Flow (Business Email Compromise)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant CFO as CFO Mailbox
    participant AP as Accounts Payable Staff
    participant Bank

    Attacker->>CFO: Step 1 - Credential Phishing
    Note over CFO: CFO clicks phishing link
    Attacker->>CFO: Step 2 - Inbox Access Obtained
    Note over Attacker: Sets mail forwarding rules
    Attacker->>AP: Step 3 - Spoofed email from CFO name
    Note over AP: "Urgent wire transfer needed"
    AP->>Attacker: Step 4 - Asks for confirmation
    Attacker->>AP: Step 5 - Provides fake details
    AP->>Bank: Step 6 - Initiates wire transfer
    Bank->>Bank: Step 7 - Funds move to mule account
    Note over Attacker: Money laundered via crypto
```

### 4.4 Phishing Funnel — Email-to-Credential Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Emails Sent - 100000"] --> S2["Delivered - 95000"]
    S2 --> S3["Opened - 28500"]
    S3 --> S4["Clicked Link - 2850"]
    S4 --> S5["Credentials Submitted - 1140"]

    S1 -. Conversion 95 percent .-> S2
    S2 -. Conversion 30 percent .-> S3
    S3 -. Conversion 10 percent .-> S4
    S4 -. Conversion 40 percent .-> S5

    classDef stage fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 stage
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Defense-in-Depth Email Security

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PerimeterLayer["Perimeter Defense Layer"]
      P1["Edge Firewall"]
      P2["Mail Relay Gateway"]
      P3["Anti-Spam Engine"]
    end

    subgraph AuthenticationLayer["Authentication Layer"]
      A1["SPF Validator"]
      A2["DKIM Verifier"]
      A3["DMARC Enforcer"]
    end

    subgraph ContentLayer["Content Inspection Layer"]
      C1["Attachment Sandbox"]
      C2["URL Reputation Engine"]
      C3["NLP Phishing Classifier"]
    end

    subgraph EndpointLayer["Endpoint Protection Layer"]
      E1["EDR Anti-Malware"]
      E2["Browser Isolation"]
      E3["DLP Data Loss Prevention"]
    end

    subgraph UserLayer["Human Firewall Layer"]
      U1["Security Awareness Training"]
      U2["Phishing Simulation Drills"]
      U3["Multi-Factor Authentication"]
    end

    IncomingEmail["Incoming Phishing Email"] --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3
    A3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3
    C3 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3
    E3 --> U1 --> U2 --> U3
    U3 --> FinalCheck{"Blocked Successfully"}
    FinalCheck -->|Yes| Blocked["Email Neutralized"]
    FinalCheck -->|No| UserInbox["Reaches User Inbox - Risk Persists"]

    classDef layer fill:#1d3a6f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#8b1a1a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```
