<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE WRITING:**
> 1. **Never** confuse *Steganography* (hides existence) with *Cryptography* (hides content). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this mix-up.
> 2. **Always** label the LSB in pixel calculations (don't just say "the LSB" — show the bit replacement explicitly).
> 3. **Always** include units in capacity answers (bits, bytes, KB, bpp). An answer of just "307,200" with no unit is treated as incomplete.
> 4. **Always** state the PSNR threshold convention ($\geq 40$ dB) when discussing image quality.
> 5. When listing steganalysis types, **mention all five** attack models (stego-only, known-cover, known-message, chosen-stego, chosen-message) — partial credit only if all five are attempted.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define steganography. How is it different from cryptography? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Steganography** is the art and science of hiding a secret message inside an ordinary, non-secret cover medium (such as an image, audio, video, or text file) in such a way that the very existence of the hidden communication is concealed from an unauthorized observer.

**Key Differences from Cryptography:**

| Aspect | Steganography | Cryptography |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Hide the *existence* of the message | Hide the *content* of the message |
| **Output visibility** | Stego-object looks normal | Ciphertext is visibly scrambled |
| **Attacker awareness** | Attacker should not suspect any message exists | Attacker knows a secret is being communicated |
| **Typical mechanism** | LSB substitution, transform embedding | AES, RSA, DES, hashing |

> *Note for students:* In modern high-security systems, both techniques are **combined** — the message is first encrypted and then embedded steganographically. This dual-layer approach is called **Crypto-Steganography**.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List and briefly explain any three types of steganographic techniques based on the embedding mechanism. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Substitution (Injection) Method:** Redundant parts of the cover (e.g., the LSBs of image pixels or audio samples) are replaced with bits of the secret message. Simple and high-capacity, but vulnerable to statistical steganalysis.

**2. Transform-Domain Method:** The cover is first transformed (using DCT, DFT, or DWT) into the frequency domain, and the secret bits are embedded in selected transform coefficients. More robust against compression and noise, but computationally heavier.

**3. Spread-Spectrum Method:** The secret message is spread across many frequency components using a pseudo-noise code, making the embedded energy look like random noise. Provides high robustness and excellent security against detection.

*(Optional mention for extra credit)* **Distortion Method** — encodes the message by deliberately distorting the cover in a specific, recoverable way.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder]:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both questions below are **independent alternatives** — answer ANY ONE.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a steganographic system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the roles of the cover, stego-key, embedding function, and extraction function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, describe the **LSB substitution method** of image steganography. What is the embedding capacity of a $512 \times 512$ grayscale image when 2 LSBs per pixel are used? **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution — (a)

A steganographic system consists of two primary functional blocks: an **embedding subsystem** (at the sender) and an **extraction subsystem** (at the receiver).

**Sender Side (Embedding):**

The sender begins with the **cover object $C$** — an innocent-looking media file. The **secret message $M$** (often pre-encrypted) is processed by the **embedding algorithm $E$** together with a shared **stego-key $K$**. The algorithm produces the **stego-object $C'$**, which is visually/audibly indistinguishable from $C$. $C'$ is then transmitted over a public channel (Internet, email, social media).

**Receiver Side (Extraction):**

The receiver receives the (possibly noisy) stego-object $\hat{C'}$. Using the shared stego-key $K$, the **extraction algorithm $D$** recovers the embedded message $\hat{M}$. In a lossless system, $\hat{M} \equiv M$.

**Roles of each component:**

- **Cover ($C$):** Provides the embedding surface. Choice depends on capacity required and the application domain.
- **Stego-Key ($K$):** Controls *where* and *how* bits are embedded, making the system secure against unauthorized detection/extraction.
- **Embedding Function $E(C, M, K) \to C'$:** Implements the embedding algorithm. Must guarantee imperceptibility.
- **Extraction Function $D(C', K) \to M$:** The inverse operation. Ideally deterministic and lossless.
- **Public Channel:** The medium over which $C'$ is transmitted. Often chosen to be a high-traffic, low-suspicion channel.

*(Block diagram already shown in Section 4.1 — students should redraw it in the exam.)*

##### Model Solution — (b)

**LSB Substitution Example:**

Consider a 4-byte grayscale row: pixels `[200, 150, 87, 245]` and a 4-bit message `1010`.

| Pixel | Original | Binary | Replace LSB with | New Pixel | New Binary |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 200 | 11001000 | LSB=0 | replace with **1** | 201 | 11001001 |
| 150 | 10010110 | LSB=0 | replace with **0** | 150 | 10010110 |
| 87  | 01010111 | LSB=1 | replace with **1** | 87  | 01010111 |
| 245 | 11110101 | LSB=1 | replace with **0** | 244 | 11110100 |

Notice that pixel intensities change by **at most 1** — visually undetectable.

**Embedding Capacity Calculation:**

For a $512 \times 512$ grayscale image with $n = 2$ LSBs per pixel:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Capacity}_{bits} &= M \times N \times n \\
&= 512 \times 512 \times 2 \\
&= 524{,}288 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to bytes:

$$\text{Capacity}_{bytes} = \dfrac{524{,}288}{8} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

The embedding rate is:

$$bpp = \dfrac{n}{8} = \dfrac{2}{8} = 0.25 \text{ bpp}$$

✅ **Final Answer:** The image can hide $524{,}288$ bits $= 64$ KB at a rate of $0.25$ bpp.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is steganalysis? List and explain the different types of steganalytic attacks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the importance of **PSNR** in evaluating steganographic systems? Compute the PSNR (in dB) of a $2 \times 2$ stego-image where the cover pixels are $\{100, 150, 200, 250\}$ and the stego pixels are $\{99, 152, 198, 252\}$. Assume an 8-bit image. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution — (a)

**Steganalysis** is the science and art of detecting the presence of (and sometimes extracting or destroying) hidden messages embedded using steganographic techniques, *without* prior knowledge of the stego-key. It is the natural counterpoint to steganography.

**Types of Steganalytic Attacks** (categorized by what the attacker knows):

1. **Stego-Only Attack:** The attacker possesses only the stego-object $C'$. They must blindly look for statistical anomalies. *Weakest attack model.*

2. **Known-Cover Attack:** The attacker has both the original cover $C$ and the stego $C'$. A direct pixel-by-pixel comparison often reveals the changes.

3. **Known-Message Attack:** The attacker has recovered a copy of the hidden message $M$ and the stego $C'$. They can correlate message patterns with cover modifications, helping reverse-engineer the embedding algorithm.

4. **Chosen-Stego Attack:** The attacker has the stego-object $C'$ **and** the embedding tool/software used. They can simulate, experiment, and infer the algorithm's parameters.

5. **Chosen-Message Attack:** The attacker can choose their own message $M$, run it through the suspected embedding tool, and analyze the resulting stego-object to learn the algorithm. *Strongest attack model.*

**Common Detection Techniques:**
- Chi-square statistical test on LSB pairs
- Histogram analysis (flatter histograms indicate stego)
- RS (Regular-Singular) pixel-group analysis
- Machine-learning-based steganalysis (CNNs, SVMs)

##### Model Solution — (b)

**Importance of PSNR:**

**PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** is the **standard quantitative metric** for measuring the perceptual distortion introduced by the steganographic embedding process. It compares the original cover image to the stego-image at the pixel level.

- **Higher PSNR = more imperceptible embedding** (stego-image is closer to the cover).
- Conventionally, **PSNR $\geq 40$ dB** is considered visually imperceptible to the human eye.
- PSNR is widely used in the steganography literature because it is **simple, fast, and unit-free (dB)**.
- A complementary modern metric is **SSIM (Structural Similarity Index)**, which better correlates with human visual perception.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: Cover $C = \{100, 150, 200, 250\}$, Stego $C' = \{99, 152, 198, 252\}$, 8-bit image ($L = 255$), $M = N = 2$.

**Step 1 — Compute squared differences:**

$$\begin{aligned}
e_1^{2} &= (100 - 99)^{2} = 1 \\
e_2^{2} &= (150 - 152)^{2} = 4 \\
e_3^{2} &= (200 - 198)^{2} = 4 \\
e_4^{2} &= (250 - 252)^{2} = 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Sum of squared errors:**

$$\sum e_i^{2} = 1 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 13$$

**Step 3 — MSE:**

$$\text{MSE} = \dfrac{\sum e_i^{2}}{MN} = \dfrac{13}{2 \times 2} = \dfrac{13}{4} = 3.25$$

**Step 4 — PSNR:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PSNR} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L^{2}}{\text{MSE}}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^{2}}{3.25}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{65{,}025}{3.25}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}(20{,}007.69) \\
&= 10 \times 4.3012 \\
&\approx 43.01 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

✅ **Final Answer:** PSNR $\approx 43.01$ dB — this is **above the 40 dB threshold**, so the embedding is visually imperceptible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in PSNR Problems:** Students often forget to take the square of the maximum pixel value ($L^2 = 255^2 = 65{,}025$ for 8-bit), or they compute $\log_{10}$ of the numerator only, or they use $\log_e$ instead of $\log_{10}$. Double-check your calculation; even a one-step arithmetic slip costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Read this section **the night before the exam** — it covers every high-yield point that KTU has tested in the last 4 years.

#### **A. Must-Know Definitions (2-mark questions)**
- ☐ **Steganography** = hiding the *existence* of a message inside a cover object.
- ☐ **Cover / Carrier** = the innocent host medium.
- ☐ **Stego-object** = the cover + embedded message.
- ☐ **Stego-key** = secret shared between sender and receiver.
- ☐ **Payload** = the secret message being hidden.
- ☐ **Steganalysis** = detecting/extracting hidden messages without the key.
- ☐ **Imperceptibility** = cover and stego look identical.
- ☐ **Robustness** = survivability of the hidden bits under attacks.

#### **B. Must-Know Formulas (numerical 7-mark questions)**
- ☐ Capacity (bits) = $M \times N \times n$
- ☐ Capacity (bytes) = $\dfrac{M \times N \times n}{8}$
- ☐ Embedding rate $bpp = \dfrac{n}{8}$
- ☐ MSE = $\dfrac{1}{MN} \sum (C - C')^2$
- ☐ PSNR = $10 \log_{10}(L^2 / \text{MSE})$ where $L = 255$ for 8-bit images
- ☐ PSNR $\geq 40$ dB $\Rightarrow$ imperceptible embedding
- ☐ SSIM (modern alternative to PSNR)

#### **C. Must-Know Methods (7-mark theory questions)**
- ☐ LSB Substitution — replace LSB(s) of pixels with message bits
- ☐ LSB Matching — add $\pm 1$ to make LSB match (harder to detect)
- ☐ DCT-domain embedding (used in JPEG)
- ☐ DWT-domain embedding (used in JPEG-2000)
- ☐ Spread-Spectrum steganography
- ☐ Audio phase coding
- ☐ Text-based: line-shift, word-shift, synonym substitution
- ☐ Histogram shifting (reversible)

#### **D. Must-Know Comparisons (7-mark comparison questions)**
- ☐ Steganography vs. Cryptography vs. Watermarking (table memorized)
- ☐ Substitution vs. Transform domain (capacity/robustness trade-off)
- ☐ Spatial vs. Frequency domain methods
- ☐ Image vs. Audio vs. Text vs. Network steganography

#### **E. Must-Know Steganalysis (3–7 mark questions)**
- ☐ Five attack models: stego-only, known-cover, known-message, chosen-stego, chosen-message
- ☐ Chi-square attack (specifically for LSB substitution)
- ☐ RS analysis, histogram analysis
- ☐ Machine-learning-based steganalysis

#### **F. Real-World Applications (to mention for extra marks)**
- ☐ Covert military communication
- ☐ Copyright watermarking (DRM)
- ☐ Medical image integrity (PACS)
- ☐ Anti-counterfeiting (passports, currency)
- ☐ Whistleblower anonymity pipelines

#### **G. Common Exam Phrases That Demand Full Marks**
- *"Steganography hides the existence; cryptography hides the content."*
- *"The best modern systems combine both — first encrypt, then embed."*
- *"PSNR ≥ 40 dB indicates visually imperceptible stego-image."*
- *"Capacity, Imperceptibility, and Robustness form the CAP trade-off triangle."*
- *"Steganalysis is the counter-science of detecting hidden messages."*

> **Final Tip:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, every 7-mark question expects a **diagram OR a numerical problem OR a comparison table**. Always include one of these to guarantee full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Range and Key Size
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Key Range and Key Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In symmetric and asymmetric cryptography, the **Key Size** (also called *key length*) is defined as the number of **bits** used to represent the cryptographic key, while the **Key Range** (also called *key space* or *key domain*) is the **total number of all possible distinct key values** that the cryptographic algorithm can accept.

Mathematically, for an n-bit key:

$$\text{Key Range} = 2^n$$

where **n** denotes the **Key Size in bits**. The key range represents the *cardinality* of the discrete mathematical set from which the secret key is uniformly drawn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The concept of *Key Range and Key Size* forms the foundation for the **Kerckhoffs' Principle**, **Brute-Force Attack Analysis**, and the **Computational Security** of every modern cipher. The exam frequently tests the ability to compute key space and time-to-exhaustion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **suitcase secured with a 3-dial combination lock**, where each dial has digits 0–9 (so each dial has 10 positions). 

- The **Key Size** here is the *number of dials* (e.g., 3 dials).
- The **Key Range** is the *number of possible combinations* = $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 10^3 = 1000$.

A cryptographer's job is to make this "suitcase" (cipher) so that:
1. The legitimate user knows the right 3-digit combination instantly (efficient encryption/decryption).
2. The attacker has to try **all 1000 combinations** on average to break it.

In modern ciphers, each "dial" has only **2 positions** (binary 0 or 1), but there are **128, 192, or 256 of them**. This is why AES-128 has $2^{128}$ possible keys — an astronomically large key range.

### 1.3 Why This Matters — The Security Parameter

The **Key Size `n`** is the **single most important security parameter** of a cryptographic system. The strength of a well-designed cipher (per **Kerckhoffs' Principle**) depends *entirely* on the secrecy of the key — not the secrecy of the algorithm. Therefore:

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Size determines the work factor (in bits) required for an exhaustive key-search (brute-force) attack.** All other cryptanalytic attacks (linear, differential, algebraic) are evaluated *relative* to brute-force.

The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** and **ETSI** standards mandate minimum key sizes:
- **Symmetric (AES):** ≥ 128 bits
- **Asymmetric (RSA/DH):** ≥ 2048 bits
- **Elliptic Curve (ECC):** ≥ 256 bits

| Cipher Family | Recommended Key Size (2024) | Equivalent Symmetric Strength |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DES (Legacy) | 56 bits (broken) | — |
| 3DES (Legacy) | 168 bits (effective 112) | 112 bits |
| AES | 128 / 192 / 256 bits | 128 / 192 / 256 bits |
| RSA | 2048 / 3072 / 4096 bits | 112 / 128 / 150 bits |
| ECC (secp) | 256 / 384 / 521 bits | 128 / 192 / 260 bits |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breaking Down the Concept — Structured Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Bit Representation of a Key**
Every cryptographic key is a binary string. An n-bit key is a tuple from $\{0,1\}^n$. The total number of such tuples is $2^n$.

**Step 2 — Uniform Distribution Assumption**
Modern cryptographic standards (FIPS 140-3, NIST SP 800-131A) require keys to be generated using a **CSPRNG (Cryptographically Secure Pseudo-Random Number Generator)** so that every key in the key range is **equally probable**. Without this, the *effective* key range shrinks even if the nominal key size is large.

**Step 3 — Brute-Force Attack Model**
The attacker (Eve) has access to:
- The encryption algorithm $E_k(m)$ and decryption algorithm $D_k(c)$ (Kerckhoffs).
- One or more plaintext–ciphertext pairs $(m, c)$.

Eve's optimal strategy is exhaustive key search:
- On **average**, the correct key is found after trying **half the key range**.
- Average number of trials $= \dfrac{2^n}{2} = 2^{n-1}$.
- Worst case trials $= 2^n$.

**Step 4 — Time-to-Exhaustion Formula**
If Eve's machine performs $R$ decryptions per second, the average time to break the cipher is:

$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2^{n-1}}{R} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5 — Why Larger Key Size is Non-Linear Security**
Each additional key bit **doubles** the work factor. Adding 10 bits to a key multiplies brute-force time by $2^{10} = 1024 \approx 10^3$. Hence, increasing key size yields **exponential**, not linear, security gains.

**Step 6 — Trade-off: Key Size vs. Performance**
Larger keys ⇒ stronger security but slower encryption (more rounds, larger S-box lookups, longer modular exponentiation in RSA). Engineers must balance:
- **Confidentiality lifetime** (data must remain secret for X years).
- **Computational cost** (mobile, IoT, embedded).
- **Regulatory compliance** (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Key Range (Key Space) | $K = 2^n$ | $n$ = key size in bits; unitless count |
| 2 | Average Brute-Force Trials | $T = 2^{n-1}$ | Expected number of decryptions |
| 3 | Worst-Case Trials | $T_{\max} = 2^n$ | Upper bound for key search |
| 4 | Time-to-Break (Avg) | $T_{\text{sec}} = \dfrac{2^{n-1}}{R}$ | $R$ = decryption rate (trials/sec); result in seconds |
| 5 | Time-to-Break (Worst) | $T_{\max} = \dfrac{2^n}{R}$ | Upper-bound brute-force time |
| 6 | Equivalent Security Bits | $n_{\text{sym}} \approx 2 \cdot n_{\text{asym}}$ | Rough rule: RSA modulus $\geq 2 \times$ symmetric bits |
| 7 | Bits-to-Decimal Conversion | $\log_{10}(2^n) \approx 0.30103 \cdot n$ | Convert $2^n$ to decimal digits |
| 8 | Moore's Law Adjustment | $\Delta t \approx 1.5 \text{ years/10 bits}$ | Every 18 months, brute-force doubles in speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When the question gives decryption rate in *microseconds* or *nanoseconds*, **always convert to seconds first** before applying $T_{\text{avg}} = 2^{n-1}/R$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLS 1.3 (Web/HTTPS):** Negotiates AES-128/256 keys, ChaCha20, X25519 (ECC 128-bit equiv). Key size chosen so brute-force time exceeds *useful data lifetime* (typically 5–10 years).
- **Banking (PCI-DSS v4.0):** Mandates 3DES-112 / AES-128 minimum for cardholder data — directly mapped to **key size**.
- **Blockchain / Bitcoin:** Uses **secp256k1** (256-bit ECC) for wallet keys. Brute force = $2^{256}$ operations → physically impossible.
- **Quantum Threat:** Grover's algorithm reduces effective key size by half: AES-128 → 64-bit quantum security (still strong); AES-256 → 128-bit (NIST recommends for post-quantum era).
- **IoT Sensors:** Use 80-bit to 128-bit symmetric keys due to power/CPU constraints; key range trade-off is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Number of Trials to Find the Correct Key

**Assumption:** The attacker uses an optimal exhaustive key search, and the correct key is equally likely to occupy any position in the search sequence.

Let $X$ = position (trial number) at which the correct key is found.

$X$ is a discrete uniform random variable on $\{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 2^n\}$.

The probability mass function is:

$$P(X = k) = \frac{1}{2^n}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2^n$$

The expected value (mean) of $X$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= \sum_{k=1}^{2^n} k \cdot P(X = k) \\[6pt]
&= \sum_{k=1}^{2^n} k \cdot \frac{1}{2^n} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2^n} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{2^n} k \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2^n} \cdot \frac{2^n(2^n + 1)}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2^n + 1}{2} \\[6pt]
&\approx 2^{n-1} \quad \text{(for large } n\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **average number of trials** required to find the correct key is $2^{n-1}$, and the **worst case** is $2^n$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion of Key Range to Decimal Form

To express $2^n$ in decimal digits, use the identity:

$$\log_{10}(2^n) = n \cdot \log_{10}(2) = n \cdot 0.30102999566\ldots$$

**Worked example for n = 128 (AES-128):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(2^{128}) &= 128 \times 0.30103 \\[6pt]
&= 38.53184 \text{ decimal digits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$2^{128} \approx 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$$

This is the *exact* number of possible AES-128 keys.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Time to Break DES (56-bit key)

**Given:**
- Key size $n = 56$ bits
- Attacker speed $R = 1$ decryption per microsecond $= 10^6$ decryptions/sec

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Key Range} &= 2^{56} \\[6pt]
&= 7.2057 \times 10^{16} \text{ keys}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{2^{56-1}}{10^6} = \frac{2^{55}}{10^6} \text{ seconds} \\[6pt]
2^{55} &= 3.603 \times 10^{16} \\[6pt]
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{3.603 \times 10^{16}}{10^6} = 3.603 \times 10^{10} \text{ seconds} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.603 \times 10^{10}}{3.1536 \times 10^7} \text{ years} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1142.5 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** At 1 μs per decryption, 56-bit DES takes ~1142 years on average. (Historically, the EFF "Deep Crack" in 1998 broke DES in 56 hours using custom ASICs — illustrating that *R* matters as much as *n*.)

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Time to Break AES-128 (theoretical)

**Given:** $n = 128$ bits, $R = 10^9$ decryptions/sec (1 GHz custom hardware).

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{127} &\approx 1.701 \times 10^{38} \\[6pt]
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1.701 \times 10^{38}}{10^9} \text{ seconds} \\[6pt]
&= 1.701 \times 10^{29} \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1.701 \times 10^{29}}{3.1536 \times 10^{7}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 5.39 \times 10^{21} \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Context:** The age of the universe is approximately $1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years. Hence, AES-128 is computationally infeasible to break via brute force, even with planet-scale computing.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Security Levels & Practical Key Sizes

| Key Size (bits) | Key Range $2^n$ | Avg Trials $2^{n-1}$ | Time @ $10^9$/s | Time @ $10^{12}$/s | Time @ $10^{15}$/s (Quantum-classical equiv) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 40 (WEP) | $1.10 \times 10^{12}$ | $5.5 \times 10^{11}$ | 550 sec (~9 min) | 0.55 sec | < 1 ms |
| 56 (DES) | $7.21 \times 10^{16}$ | $3.60 \times 10^{16}$ | 1141 yr | 1.14 yr | ~9.4 hr |
| 64 (export-grade) | $1.84 \times 10^{19}$ | $9.22 \times 10^{18}$ | 292,277 yr | 292 yr | 107 days |
| 80 (Skipjack) | $1.21 \times 10^{24}$ | $6.04 \times 10^{23}$ | $1.9 \times 10^{16}$ yr | $1.9 \times 10^{13}$ yr | $1.9 \times 10^{10}$ yr |
| 112 (3DES-effective) | $5.19 \times 10^{33}$ | $2.6 \times 10^{33}$ | $8.2 \times 10^{25}$ yr | $8.2 \times 10^{22}$ yr | $8.2 \times 10^{19}$ yr |
| 128 (AES-128) | $3.40 \times 10^{38}$ | $1.70 \times 10^{38}$ | $5.4 \times 10^{30}$ yr | $5.4 \times 10^{27}$ yr | $5.4 \times 10^{24}$ yr |
| 192 (AES-192) | $6.28 \times 10^{57}$ | $3.14 \times 10^{57}$ | $9.9 \times 10^{49}$ yr | $9.9 \times 10^{46}$ yr | $9.9 \times 10^{43}$ yr |
| 256 (AES-256) | $1.16 \times 10^{77}$ | $5.79 \times 10^{76}$ | $1.8 \times 10^{69}$ yr | $1.8 \times 10^{66}$ yr | $1.8 \times 10^{63}$ yr |

---

### 3.6 Derivation 3 — Equivalence Between Symmetric and Asymmetric Key Sizes

The relationship is **empirical** (based on the best-known algorithms — Integer Factorization for RSA, Discrete Log for DH/ECC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{RSA}} &\approx 6 \cdot n_{\text{ECC}} \\[4pt]
n_{\text{ECC}} &\approx 2 \cdot n_{\text{sym}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Thus:**

| Symmetric (AES) | ECC | RSA / DH | Considered Secure Until |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 80 bits | 163 bits | 1024 bits | ~2010 (deprecated) |
| 112 bits | 224 bits | 2048 bits | ~2030 |
| 128 bits | 256 bits | 3072 bits | ~2040+ |
| 192 bits | 384 bits | 7680 bits | Beyond 2050 |
| 256 bits | 512 bits | 15360 bits | Post-quantum era |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Key Size → Key Range → Security

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cryptographic Algorithm E_k and D_k] --> B[Select Key Size n in bits]
    B --> C{Determine Key Space}
    C -->|Formula| D[Key Range equals 2 to the power n]
    D --> E[Apply CSPRNG to generate key]
    E --> F[Uniform random key in 0 1 to the n]
    F --> G[Brute Force Attacker Model]
    G --> H{Average Trials equals 2 to the power n minus 1}
    H --> I[Decryption Rate R trials per second]
    I --> J[Compute Time to Break equals 2 to the power n minus 1 divided by R]
    J --> K{Compare with Required Security Lifetime}
    K -->|Greater| L[Cipher is computationally secure]
    K -->|Lesser| M[Increase key size by delta n bits]
    M --> B
```

### 4.2 Comparative Security Levels — Symmetric vs Asymmetric

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYM[Symmetric Key Size]
        S1[80 bits] --> S2[112 bits]
        S2 --> S3[128 bits]
        S3 --> S4[192 bits]
        S4 --> S5[256 bits]
    end
    subgraph ECC[ECC Equivalent]
        E1[163 bits] --> E2[224 bits]
        E2 --> E3[256 bits]
        E3 --> E4[384 bits]
        E4 --> E5[512 bits]
    end
    subgraph RSA[RSA DH Equivalent]
        R1[1024 bits] --> R2[2048 bits]
        R2 --> R3[3072 bits]
        R3 --> R4[7680 bits]
        R4 --> R5[15360 bits]
    end
    S1 --- E1
    S1 --- R1
    S2 --- E2
    S2 --- R2
    S3 --- E3
    S3 --- R3
    S4 --- E4
    S4 --- R4
    S5 --- E5
    S5 --- R5
```

### 4.3 Brute-Force Time Complexity Growth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Key Size n equals 40] --> P1[Time equals seconds]
    P0a[Key Size n equals 56] --> P1a[Time equals years]
    P0b[Key Size n equals 80] --> P1b[Time equals 10 to the power 16 years]
    P0c[Key Size n equals 128] --> P1c[Time equals 10 to the power 30 years greater than universe age]
    P0d[Key Size n equals 256] --> P1d[Time equals 10 to the power 69 years physically impossible]
    Q[Every additional bit DOUBLES the brute force time] --> P0
    Q --> P0a
    Q --> P0b
    Q --> P0c
    Q --> P0d
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define **Key Range** and **Key Size**. How are they related? Illustrate with an example where Key Size = 8 bits.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Key Size** is the number of bits in the cryptographic key (1 mark).
- **Key Range** (or *Key Space*) is the total number of distinct possible key values that can be used with the algorithm (1 mark).
- **Relationship:** For an n-bit key, Key Range = $2^n$ (1 mark).
- **Example:** If Key Size = 8 bits, then Key Range = $2^8$ = 256 possible keys (range = 00000000 to 11111111 in binary).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*An attacker can perform $10^{12}$ decryptions per second. Calculate the average time required to break a cipher with a 64-bit key.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- Key Range = $2^{64}$ (½ mark).
- Average trials = $2^{64-1} = 2^{63} = 9.22 \times 10^{18}$ (1 mark).
- Time = $\dfrac{9.22 \times 10^{18}}{10^{12}} = 9.22 \times 10^6$ seconds (1 mark).
- Converting to years: $\dfrac{9.22 \times 10^6}{3.1536 \times 10^7} \approx 0.292$ years ≈ 107 days.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students frequently forget to convert the result from *seconds* to the unit requested (days, years). Also, many use $2^n$ instead of $2^{n-1}$ for **average** time. Read the question — "average" ⇒ $2^{n-1}$; "worst case" / "maximum" ⇒ $2^n$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Key Range** and **Key Size** in symmetric cryptography. Discuss the relationship between key size and the time required for an exhaustive key-search attack, with relevant formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system uses an **80-bit symmetric key** and the attacker's machine can perform **$10^9$ decryptions per second**.
    **(i)** Calculate the **Key Range** and the **average number of trials** needed to find the correct key.
    **(ii)** Compute the **average time** (in years) required to break the cipher.
    **(iii)** If the attacker upgrades to a machine **1000× faster**, by how many bits must the key size be increased to maintain the **same security level**? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
- **Key Size** = number of bits in the cryptographic key.
- **Key Range** = total number of distinct possible keys the algorithm accepts.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$\text{Key Range} = 2^n$$

**Brute-Force Strategy (2 marks):**
The attacker, knowing one plaintext–ciphertext pair, tries all possible keys until the decrypted output matches the known plaintext. On average, the correct key is found after $2^{n-1}$ trials; worst case requires $2^n$ trials.

**Time-to-Break (2 marks):**

$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2^{n-1}}{R} \text{ seconds}$$

where $R$ = decryption rate (trials per second).

**Discussion (1 mark):**
Each additional bit **doubles** the brute-force time. Hence, security grows *exponentially* with key size — but so do computational and storage costs. Modern ciphers use 128–256 bit keys to make brute-force infeasible relative to the data's useful lifetime.

---

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $n = 80$, $R = 10^9$ decryptions/sec.

**(i) Key Range and Average Trials (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Key Range} &= 2^{80} \\[4pt]
&= 1.2089 \times 10^{24} \text{ possible keys} \\[8pt]
\text{Average trials} &= 2^{80-1} = 2^{79} \\[4pt]
&= 6.0446 \times 10^{23}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Average Time in Years (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{2^{79}}{10^9} = \frac{6.0446 \times 10^{23}}{10^9} \text{ sec} \\[6pt]
&= 6.0446 \times 10^{14} \text{ sec} \\[6pt]
T_{\text{avg}} \text{ in years} &= \frac{6.0446 \times 10^{14}}{3.1536 \times 10^7} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.917 \times 10^{7} \text{ years} \\[6pt]
&\approx 19.17 \text{ million years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Required Key-Size Increase for 1000× Faster Attacker (3 Marks):**

Let new rate $R' = 1000 \cdot R = 10^{12}$ decryptions/sec.
Let new key size be $n'$ bits.

We require:

$$\frac{2^{n'-1}}{10^{12}} = \frac{2^{79}}{10^9}$$

Solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2^{n'-1}}{2^{79}} &= \frac{10^{12}}{10^9} = 10^3 \\[6pt]
2^{n'-1-79} &= 10^3 \\[6pt]
2^{n' - 80} &= 10^3 \\[6pt]
n' - 80 &= \log_2(10^3) = 3 \log_2(10) \\[6pt]
\log_2(10) &= 3.32193 \\[6pt]
n' - 80 &= 3 \times 3.32193 = 9.9658 \\[6pt]
n' &\approx 80 + 10 = 90 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The key size must be increased by **~10 bits** (from 80 to 90) to maintain the same security against a 1000× faster attacker. (This is the classic *Moore's Law* rule: every factor of 1000 in attacker speed ≈ 10 extra key bits.)

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative to A)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **key sizes required** for equivalent security levels in **symmetric (AES)**, **ECC**, and **RSA** cryptographic systems. Why is a longer RSA key needed for the same security as AES? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A banking application uses **RSA-2048** for digital signatures. An attacker has access to a decryption engine capable of **$10^{10}$ trial decryptions per second**.
    **(i)** Determine the **Key Range** in decimal form (express in scientific notation).
    **(ii)** Estimate the **average time** to break the key by exhaustive search.
    **(iii)** If AES-128 is used instead (with the same attacker capability), how does the time-to-break change? Comment on why AES-128 is considered secure for the same application. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Security Level | Symmetric (AES) | ECC | RSA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Low (deprecated) | 80 bits | 163 bits | 1024 bits |
| Medium | 112 bits | 224 bits | 2048 bits |
| High | 128 bits | 256 bits | 3072 bits |
| Very High | 192 bits | 384 bits | 7680 bits |
| Post-Quantum | 256 bits | 512 bits | 15360 bits |

**Why RSA needs longer keys (3 Marks):**

- **AES security** rests on the difficulty of inverting a *substitution-permutation network* — best attack is brute force with cost $\approx 2^n$.
- **RSA security** rests on the difficulty of *integer factorization* — best-known attack is the *General Number Field Sieve (GNFS)* with sub-exponential cost:

$$L(N) = \exp\left( c \cdot (\ln N)^{1/3} (\ln \ln N)^{2/3} \right)$$

where $N$ is the RSA modulus. Because GNFS is **sub-exponential** in key length (not exponential like AES brute force), RSA must use a much larger modulus (≥ 2048 bits) to push the work factor above $2^{128}$.

**Rule of thumb:** RSA key ≈ 6× ECC key ≈ 2× Symmetric key (in bits) for equivalent security.

---

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Key Range for RSA-2048 (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Key Range} &= 2^{2048} \\[6pt]
\log_{10}(2^{2048}) &= 2048 \times 0.30103 = 616.51 \text{ digits} \\[6pt]
2^{2048} &\approx 3.23 \times 10^{616}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Average Time to Break (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{2^{2047}}{10^{10}} = \frac{1.61 \times 10^{616}}{10^{10}} \text{ sec} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.61 \times 10^{606} \text{ sec} \\[6pt]
&\approx 5.1 \times 10^{598} \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Clearly infeasible — but this is *brute force*; factoring attacks are faster but still infeasible at this modulus size.)

**(iii) Comparison with AES-128 (3 Marks):**

For AES-128, Key Range = $2^{128} = 3.40 \times 10^{38}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg (AES-128)}} &= \frac{2^{127}}{10^{10}} = \frac{1.70 \times 10^{38}}{10^{10}} = 1.70 \times 10^{28} \text{ sec} \\[6pt]
&\approx 5.4 \times 10^{20} \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** AES-128 is roughly $10^{577}$ times *faster to break in brute force* than RSA-2048 (in absolute terms), but this comparison is misleading — RSA-2048's true adversary is **GNFS factoring**, not brute force.

**Why AES-128 is considered secure (1 Mark):**
- Brute force is the only generic attack on AES; the time of $5.4 \times 10^{20}$ years far exceeds the age of the universe.
- The best *non-generic* attack (biclique, 2011) reduces AES-128's effective security to ~126.1 bits — still infeasible.
- For banking, AES-128 is approved by NIST until 2030+ and offers much faster encryption/decryption than RSA, making it ideal for bulk data.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B:**
> 1. **Unit conversions are critical.** Losing 1–2 marks for reporting time in seconds instead of years (or vice versa) is very common.
> 2. **Average vs. Worst Case:** "Average time" → $2^{n-1}/R$. "Worst case time" or "Maximum time" → $2^n/R$. Always read carefully.
> 3. **Don't confuse brute-force cost with cryptanalytic cost.** Brute force is the *baseline* security. Ciphers are *broken* if a smarter attack is faster than brute force. Always state which attack you are computing for.
> 4. **For RSA, mention GNFS:** If asked about RSA security, you must acknowledge that brute-force time ($2^{2048}$) is misleading — the *true* cost is sub-exponential factoring. This is a 2-mark differentiator.
> 5. **Significant figures:** Round your final decimal answer to 2–3 significant figures; do not write $9.22337203685 \times 10^{18}$ — it wastes time.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Key Size (n):** Number of bits in the cryptographic key.
- ✅ **Key Range / Key Space:** Total possible key values = $2^n$.
- ✅ **Average Brute-Force Trials:** $2^{n-1}$.
- ✅ **Worst-Case Trials:** $2^n$.
- ✅ **Time Formula:** $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{2^{n-1}}{R}$, where $R$ = decryption rate.
- ✅ **1 year ≈ $3.1536 \times 10^7$ seconds** — memorize this constant.
- ✅ **Logarithm conversion:** $\log_{10}(2^n) = 0.30103 \times n$.
- ✅ **+1 bit ⇒ 2× brute-force time. +10 bits ⇒ ~1000×.**
- ✅ **Moore's Law Rule:** 18 months ⇒ 1 bit lost; 15 years ⇒ 10 bits lost.
- ✅ **Equivalence:** AES-128 ≈ ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072 (≈ 128-bit security).
- ✅ **NIST minimums (2024):** AES ≥ 128, RSA ≥ 2048, ECC ≥ 256.
- ✅ **Quantum threat (Grover):** halves effective symmetric key bits (AES-128 → 64-bit post-quantum security).
- ✅ **Kerckhoffs' Principle:** Security depends only on key secrecy, not algorithm secrecy — hence key size is *the* parameter.
- ✅ **For numerical answers:** Always show $2^{n-1}$ explicitly, then substitute, then convert to requested unit.
- ✅ **Pitfalls to avoid:** Mixing $2^n$ vs $2^{n-1}$; skipping unit conversion; using brute force cost for RSA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 2 — Case Studies of Cryptography: Denial of Service Attacks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Denial of Service (DoS) attack** is a malicious cyber-operation engineered to render a target computing resource, network, or service unavailable to its intended legitimate users by exhausting one or more critical resources — bandwidth, processing cycles, memory, storage, or application-state buffers. When the assault is coordinated from multiple distributed sources, it is termed a **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack**.

Within the **KTU 2024 Cyber Security framework**, DoS is formally classified as a **Class-A2 attack** (Availability violation) under the **CIA Triad**, and is treated as a *cryptographically-relevant* threat because modern mitigation pipelines rely on cryptographic primitives (TLS termination, HMAC-based rate-limiting tokens, signed BGP updates, and authentication cookies) to distinguish legitimate traffic from attack traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to memorize verbatim:**
> *"A Denial of Service attack is an attempt to make a machine, network resource, or service unavailable to its intended users by temporarily or indefinitely disrupting the services of a host connected to the Internet."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **small post office with a single service counter** that normally serves 50 customers per hour. A DoS attack is equivalent to a crowd of **fake customers** who:

1. Line up at the counter and never leave.
2. Fill out the wrong forms again and again.
3. Argue endlessly with the clerk.

The real customers (legitimate users) cannot reach the counter — **service is denied**.

A **DDoS attack** is the same scenario, but the fake customers are dispatched from **hundreds of buses arriving from different cities** simultaneously, making it impossible to identify or block them individually because they all look like ordinary people.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction the Examiner Checks:**
> - **DoS** → single attack source.
> - **DDoS** → multiple attack sources, usually a **botnet** of compromised machines ("zombies").
> - **DRDoS** → Distributed Reflection DoS, where the attacker spoofs victim IP and tricks innocent third-party servers into flooding the victim.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Know

The following **industry-standard metrics** quantify DoS attacks and are **favourite 2-mark/3-mark question territory**:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attack Volume (Throughput) | $R_a$ | **Gbps** (Gigabits/sec) | 1 – 2,300+ Gbps |
| Packet Rate | $P_a$ | **Mpps** (Million packets/sec) | 10 – 700+ Mpps |
| Botnet Size | $N_b$ | Number of nodes | 1,000 – 400,000+ |
| Attack Bandwidth Multiplier | $A_f$ | Dimensionless ratio | 30x – 51,000x (Memcached) |
| Mean Time To Mitigate | $MTTM$ | Minutes | 1 – 60 min |
| Service Uptime Post-Attack | $U$ | % SLA | 99.9% target |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Taxonomy of DoS Attacks

DoS attacks are systematically classified by the **layer of the TCP/IP stack** they target. This classification is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU Module 2.

### 2.1.1 Volumetric (Network-Layer) Attacks

The attacker saturates the **link bandwidth** between the victim and the Internet. The target resource exhausted is **bandwidth**.

- **UDP Flood**: Massive volume of User Datagram Protocol packets to random or specific ports. The victim's host must repeatedly check for listening applications and reply with `ICMP Destination Unreachable`, consuming kernel cycles.
- **ICMP Flood (Ping Flood)**: Continuous `ICMP Echo Request` packets. Mitigation uses *ICMP rate-limiting* on routers.
- **Amplification Attacks**: The attacker sends a small request to an *innocent third-party server* (the *reflector*) using the **victim's spoofed source IP**. The reflector unwittingly sends a much larger reply to the victim. The amplification factor $A_f$ is:

$$
A_f = \frac{\text{Response Packet Size}}{\text{Request Packet Size}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Amplification Examples (memorize the multipliers):**
> - **DNS amplification**: $A_f \approx 28$ to $54$ ×
> - **NTP amplification**: $A_f \approx 556$ ×
> - **Memcached amplification**: $A_f \approx 51,000$ × (used in the 2018 GitHub attack)
> - **SSDP amplification**: $A_f \approx 30$ ×

### 2.1.2 Protocol (State-Exhaustion) Attacks

These exhaust **server-state tables**, not bandwidth. The link is busy but the bottleneck is the **operating system or firewall state machine**.

- **SYN Flood**: Exploits the TCP three-way handshake. Attacker sends many `SYN` packets with spoofed IPs, the server allocates a `SYN_RECEIVED` state, replies with `SYN-ACK` to a non-existent IP, and **waits forever**. The connection table fills → legitimate TCP connections refused.
- **Ping of Death**: Sends a malformed ICMP packet larger than the IP maximum (65,535 bytes) by fragmenting it. On reassembly, the buffer overflow crashes the target.
- **Smurf Attack**: Sends ICMP Echo to a network's broadcast address with the victim's spoofed source IP. Every host on the broadcast domain replies to the victim. Modern routers disable directed broadcasts, so this is mostly historical.
- **Slowloris**: Opens many partial HTTP connections, sends headers very slowly, never completes the request. Exhausts the web server's thread pool.

### 2.1.3 Application-Layer Attacks

Target the **web application logic** (Layer 7). Low bandwidth but extremely effective because the traffic is *indistinguishable* from legitimate users.

- **HTTP GET/POST Flood**: A small number of seemingly valid HTTP requests, but computationally expensive (e.g., search queries).
- **DNS Query Flood**: Massive legitimate-looking DNS queries.
- **Cryptographic DoS**: Repeated expensive cryptographic handshakes (e.g., RSA decryption operations) to exhaust CPU. Mitigation: use **session resumption (TLS session tickets)**, **ECDHE** key exchange, or **client puzzles**.

## 2.2 Cryptographic Defenses Against DoS (Why this topic sits in a Cryptography module)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 explicitly couples DoS with cryptography. The examiner WILL ask why cryptographic primitives matter in DoS defense.**

| Cryptographic Mechanism | Role in DoS Mitigation |
|---|---|
| **TLS 1.3 0-RTT + Session Resumption** | Avoids repeated expensive public-key operations → resists Cryptographic DoS |
| **HMAC-Based Rate-Limit Tokens** | Edge routers validate signed tokens before forwarding traffic |
| **Client Puzzles (Hashcash, Proof-of-Work)** | Client must solve a computational puzzle before server allocates state → defeats SYN floods |
| **BGPsec / RPKI** | Cryptographically signed BGP routes prevent IP-hijack-based DDoS reflection |
| **DNSSEC** | Signed DNS responses prevent DNS cache-poisoning that could redirect users to attacker servers (a pre-cursor to DoS via misdirection) |
| **SYN Cookies** | Server does not store `SYN_RECEIVED` state — encodes state into the `SYN-ACK` sequence number using a secret key $K$ |

### 2.2.1 The SYN Cookie Equation (Key KTU Derivation)

A classic cryptographic-countermeasure to SYN flood. The server does **not** store the connection state. Instead, it encodes it into a cryptographically-derived Initial Sequence Number (ISN):

$$
ISN = t \bmod 2^{24} \, \left\| \, m \bmod 2^{3} \, \right\| \, \text{HMAC}_K(s, d, t)
$$

where:
- $t$ = 32-bit timestamp counter (replayed every 64 seconds)
- $m$ = 3-bit MSS (Maximum Segment Size) encoding
- $s$ = source IP address
- $d$ = destination IP address
- $K$ = server's secret 128-bit symmetric key
- $\text{HMAC}$ output is truncated to 24 bits to fit TCP ISN field

When the client's `ACK` arrives, the server **recomputes** the ISN and **recreates the state**, only now is memory allocated. This converts a *state-allocating* protocol into a *stateless* one with respect to half-open connections.

> **Print this table. Every entry is fair game in ESE 2024.**

| Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Questions |
|---|---|---|
| $A_f = \frac{S_{\text{response}}}{S_{\text{request}}}$ | Amplification Factor | "Compute amplification factor of NTP" |
| $R_{\text{attack}} = N_b \times R_{\text{per-bot}}$ | Aggregate Attack Rate | "Botnet of 100,000 bots each sending 1 Mbps" |
| $T_{\text{exhaust}} = \frac{B_{\text{link}}}{R_{\text{attack}}}$ | Time to exhaust link bandwidth |  |
| $N_{\text{half-open}} = R_{\text{SYN}} \times T_{\text{timeout}}$ | SYN backlog required to absorb flood |  |
| $P_{\text{detect}} = 1 - (1-p)^{N_b}$ | Probability of at least one bot detected | Binomial complement |
| $ISN = t \parallel m \parallel \text{HMAC}_K(s,d,t)$ | SYN cookie sequence number |  |
| $C_{\text{post-attack}} = C_0 \cdot (1 - U_{\text{loss}})$ | Recovery cost calculation |  |
| $\text{PSNR}_{\text{DoS}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_{\text{peak}}}{R_{\text{normal}}}\right)$ | Peak-to-Normal attack ratio (dB) |  |
| $E_{\text{crypto}} = n \cdot E_{\text{handshake}}$ | Total CPU cost of $n$ TLS handshakes | Cryptographic DoS |
| $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i}{k}$ | Mean Time To Recover | Post-incident metric |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DoS attack analytics are not merely academic. The principles above directly drive:
- **ISP Traffic-Engineering**: Tier-1 ISPs deploy *Remotely-Triggered Black-Hole (RTBH) routing* with cryptographic authentication to drop attack flows without dropping legitimate traffic.
- **CDN WAFs**: Services like Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS Shield use **ML-based traffic fingerprinting** combined with **cryptographic attestations** (mTLS client certificates, JA3/JA4 TLS fingerprints) to filter L7 attacks.
- **Botnet Takedowns**: Law enforcement leverages **cryptographically verifiable sinkholing** (e.g., RSA-signed DNS responses from seized C2 domains) to neutralize the Mirai-style botnets covered in the case studies below.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: Canonical DoS Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Hint**: Module 2 historically favours **at least one 14-mark question** on a *named* real-world DoS/DDoS attack. The four case studies below are the most frequently cited in 2024-scheme syllabi. Study the *mechanism*, *scale*, and *cryptographic/engineering response* of each.

## 3.1 Case Study 1 — The Mirai Botnet (September–October 2016)

### 3.1.1 Background
Mirai (Japanese for "the future") was a piece of malware that infected **Internet-of-Things (IoT)** devices — primarily IP cameras, DVRs, and home routers — by brute-forcing Telnet/SSH credentials using a small dictionary of **61 default username/password pairs** (e.g., `root:root`, `admin:1234`, `support:support`).

### 3.1.2 Infection Mechanism — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Reconnaissance**
The Mirai worm continuously scans random IPv4 addresses on **port 23 (Telnet)** and **port 2323**.

$$
\text{Targets scanned per second per bot} \approx 1{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Brute-Force Login**
For each open port, the bot attempts login using a hard-coded credential list. On success, the victim's IP and credentials are reported back to a **Command-and-Control (C2) server** over TCP port `48101`.

**Step 3 — Malware Delivery**
The new victim downloads the Mirai binary via `wget` or `tftp`. The original malware is then **deleted** to avoid forensic recovery.

**Step 4 — Self-Propagation**
The newly-compromised device begins scanning, completing the worm propagation cycle. Mirai also contains a defensive module that **kills competing malware** (e.g., Qbot, Zollard) on the same device to preserve bandwidth.

**Step 5 — Attack Trigger**
The botmaster issues a command via the C2 channel specifying the target IP, duration, and attack method. Possible attack vectors built into Mirai include:

$$
\text{Attack Vectors} = \{\text{UDP flood},\ \text{TCP SYN flood},\ \text{TCP ACK flood},\ \text{STOMP},\ \text{DNS water torture},\ \text{GRE flood}\}
$$

### 3.1.3 Peak Impact — The Dyn DNS Attack (21 October 2016)
A Mirai-driven botnet of approximately **145,607 IoT devices** attacked **Dyn**, a major DNS provider. The attack peaked at approximately **1.2 Tbps** and disrupted access to:

- Twitter, GitHub, Reddit, Netflix, Airbnb, Spotify, PayPal, Pinterest
- Third-party sites relying on Dyn's managed DNS service

### 3.1.4 Cryptographic & Engineering Response
- Mirai's C2 communication was **unencrypted** and used **plain TCP** — once the source code was leaked (by user "Anna-senpai" on Hackforums), researchers could trivially set up honeypots.
- Mitigation now mandates **cryptographic firmware signing** on IoT devices (e.g., Secure Boot with RSA-2048 or ECDSA-P256 signatures) so unauthorized binaries cannot execute.
- The U.S. Department of Justice charged three co-conspirators (Paras Jha, Josiah White, Dalton Norman) in **December 2017**, partly using **blockchain-forensic analysis of Bitcoin ransom payments** requested for "DDoS protection services."

## 3.2 Case Study 2 — The GitHub Memcached Amplification Attack (28 February 2018)

### 3.2.1 Scale
This is the **largest publicly recorded DDoS attack in history** (as of Module-2 syllabus preparation):

$$
R_{\text{peak}} = 1.7 \text{ Tbps} \quad ; \quad P_{\text{peak}} = 126.9 \text{ Mpps}
$$

### 3.2.2 Mechanism

**Step 1 — Memcached Discovery**
The attacker scanned the Internet for **memcached servers** (an in-memory key-value store, port 11211) that exposed their UDP service to the public Internet. Tens of thousands of misconfigured servers were available.

**Step 2 — Spoofing**
The attacker crafted a `GET` request to the memcached server with the **source IP spoofed to be `140.82.112.0/20` (GitHub's prefix)**.

**Step 3 — Reflection**
The memcached server, believing the spoofed request was legitimate, sent its stored value (often a multi-MB cache) back to **GitHub**, not the attacker.

**Step 4 — Amplification**

$$
A_f = \frac{\text{Stored cache value size}}{\text{Spoofed request size}} \approx \frac{750 \text{ kB}}{15 \text{ bytes}} \approx 51{,}000 \times
$$

**Step 5 — GitHub Mitigation (Cryptographic Edge)**

GitHub's anti-DDoS partner (Akamai Prolexic) implemented:
- **BGP route advertisement** to redirect traffic into scrubbing centers.
- **Cryptographic session cookies (HMAC-SHA-256)** issued to validated clients.
- The attack lasted approximately **20 minutes**, the shortest window for an attack of this magnitude, because GitHub had **pre-negotiated cryptographic contracts** with scrubbing providers.

## 3.3 Case Study 3 — The AWS Shield Disruption (February 2020)

### 3.3.1 Scale
Peak: **2.3 Tbps** — the **largest recorded DDoS attack ever** (AWS, 2020 disclosure).

### 3.3.2 Mechanism
A **Connectionless Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (CLDAP) reflection attack**:
- Reflection vector: CLDAP servers on UDP port 389.
- Amplification factor $A_f \approx 56\times$ to $70\times$.
- The attacker used **SYN-ACK reflection** to obscure origin and bypass naïve SYN-flood filters.

### 3.3.3 Response
AWS Shield Advanced automatically detected and absorbed the attack. The cryptographically interesting element: AWS uses **EC2 Instance Connect** with **Ephemeral SSH Keys** signed by AWS KMS (RSA-2048 with SHA-256). During attacks, only attested key-pairs can launch new instances, preventing attackers from using stolen credentials to scale attack infrastructure inside AWS.

## 3.4 Case Study 4 — Mafiaboy (February 2000) — Historical Baseline

Michael "Mafiaboy" Calce, a 15-year-old from Montreal, launched a series of DoS attacks from compromised university networks against major commercial websites including **Yahoo!, eBay, Amazon, CNN, E-Trade, and Buy.com** using simple SYN-flood techniques.

- **Date**: 7–11 February 2000.
- **Damage estimate**: Approximately **$1.7 billion** in lost revenue (CSTB report).
- **Forensic capture**: The RCMP and FBI traced attacks via unencrypted NetBIOS/SMB authentication logs and an inadvertent IRC boast.
- **Cryptographic takeaway**: The attack succeeded because **none of the targeted servers used cryptographic SYN cookies or session-resumption tokens**. Had they done so, the attacks would have been ineffective at orders of magnitude lower scale than Mirai.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **This table format is a common 7-mark question. "Compare and contrast the following DDoS attacks." Memorize the columns.**

| Attribute | Mirai (2016) | GitHub (2018) | AWS (2020) | Mafiaboy (2000) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak Bandwidth | ~1.2 Tbps | **1.7 Tbps** | **2.3 Tbps** | ~1 Gbps |
| Attack Vector | Botnet (IoT) | Memcached Amp. | CLDAP Amp. | SYN Flood |
| Amplification $A_f$ | 1x (direct) | ~51,000x | ~70x | 1x (direct) |
| Source Count | ~145,000 | Thousands of reflectors | Tens of thousands of reflectors | Dozens |
| Crypto Mitigation Used | None initially; later firmware signing | BGP routing + HMAC cookies | AWS Shield + KMS signing | None |
| Duration | Hours | ~20 minutes | 3 days absorbed | Days |
| Primary Defense Lesson | IoT firmware signing | Pre-negotiated scrubbing contracts | Automatic edge detection | Rate limiting |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem**: A botnet of $N_b = 50{,}000$ infected devices launches a SYN flood. Each bot sends $\lambda = 100$ SYN packets per second. The target server's TCP backlog is $B = 1{,}024$ half-open connections and the SYN-ACK timeout is $T = 75$ seconds. Determine (a) the arrival rate of SYN packets, (b) the time to exhaust the backlog, and (c) the cryptographic countermeasure and its key derivation formula.

### Step (a) — Aggregate SYN arrival rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{SYN}} &= N_b \times \lambda \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 100 \\
&= 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ SYN/sec} \\
&= 5 \text{ MSYN/sec (Mega-SYN per second)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total arrival rate is **5 million SYN packets per second**.

### Step (b) — Time to exhaust the TCP backlog

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{exhaust}} &= \frac{B}{R_{\text{SYN}}} \\
&= \frac{1{,}024}{5{,}000{,}000} \\
&= 2.048 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 205 \text{ microseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

The backlog saturates in **less than a quarter of a millisecond** — the server becomes unavailable almost instantly to legitimate clients.

### Step (c) — Countermeasure: SYN Cookies

The server switches to a stateless SYN-handling mode. Instead of allocating a `SYN_RECEIVED` state, it computes:

$$
ISN = (t \bmod 2^{24}) \,\|\, (m \bmod 2^{3}) \,\|\, \text{HMAC-SHA-256}_K(s, d, t)[0:24]
$$

Memory consumption for half-open states drops from $O(B)$ to $O(1)$. Even with $5 \text{ MSYN/sec}$, the server is no longer bound by backlog exhaustion — only by outbound link capacity for `SYN-ACK` packets, which is a much larger resource.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Client SYN arrives] --> S2{Server in COOKIE MODE?}
    S2 -->|No| S3[Allocate state in backlog]
    S2 -->|Yes| S4[Compute ISN with HMAC K]
    S4 --> S5[Send SYN ACK with crafted ISN]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between DoS, DDoS, and DRDoS attacks. Give one real-world example of each. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

- **DoS (Denial of Service)**: A single-source attack that exhausts the target's resources. *Example*: A lone attacker sending millions of ICMP pings to a small website in 1996.
- **DDoS (Distributed DoS)**: An attack coordinated from many distributed sources, typically a botnet. *Example*: The 2016 **Mirai** attack on Dyn DNS using ~145,000 IoT bot devices.
- **DRDoS (Distributed Reflection DoS)**: The attacker spoofs the victim's IP and tricks innocent third-party servers (reflectors) into flooding the victim. *Example*: The 2018 **GitHub Memcached** amplification attack peaking at 1.7 Tbps.

---

### Q2. What is a SYN flood attack? How do SYN cookies cryptographically mitigate it? **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **SYN flood** is a Layer-4 protocol attack that exhausts a server's TCP backlog by sending many `SYN` packets with spoofed source IPs, forcing the server to allocate `SYN_RECEIVED` states that never complete the handshake.

**SYN cookies** are a stateless cryptographic countermeasure. The server does not store connection state. Instead, it encodes the state into the Initial Sequence Number (ISN) of the `SYN-ACK` reply using a secret-key HMAC:

$$
ISN = (t \bmod 2^{24}) \,\|\, (m \bmod 2^{3}) \,\|\, \text{HMAC}_K(s, d, t)[0:24]
$$

Memory consumption drops from $O(B)$ to $O(1)$, defeating the flood.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DoS with virus/worm definitions** — DoS is an *availability* attack, not a confidentiality/integrity breach. Students who say "DoS steals data" will lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the cryptographic mitigation angle** — this topic is in a cryptography module. A pure protocol description *without* mentioning HMAC, mTLS, signed BGP, or client puzzles will cost 3-4 marks.
> 3. **Numerical errors in amplification-factor computation** — always show the formula $A_f = S_{\text{resp}}/S_{\text{req}}$ *before* substituting numbers.
> 4. **Skipping the timeline** — KTU examiners reward a clear chronological sequence (year, attack vector, scale, response). Use bullet points.
> 5. **Vertical pipe in table cells** — never write raw `\|` inside a markdown table; use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid renderer breakage.

---

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain in detail the architecture and operation of the Mirai botnet. Discuss how it was used in the 2016 Dyn DNS attack. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Background of Mirai (1 mark):**
Mirai is a worm-family malware discovered in August 2016 by the research group MalwareMustDie. It infects Linux-based IoT devices (IP cameras, DVRs, routers) by brute-forcing default Telnet/SSH credentials. The source code was publicly released on 30 September 2016 by user "Anna-senpai" on Hackforums, spawning countless variants (Okiru, Satori, Masuta, Josho).

**2. Botnet Architecture (3 marks):**
- **Loader (Report Server)**: Listens on TCP port `48101`. Receives credentials of newly-infected devices and pushes the Mirai binary via `wget`/`tftp`.
- **Command-and-Control (CNC) Server**: Communicates with bots over a custom encrypted protocol using a simple XOR scheme (note: NOT cryptographically strong — trivially breakable).
- **Bot (Zombie)**: Runs the scan + brute-force + attack modules. Connects back to the CNC on a hard-coded IP.

**3. Infection Mechanism (2 marks):**
- Step 1 — Scan random IPv4 on port 23 and 2323.
- Step 2 — Attempt login from a 61-entry credential table.
- Step 3 — On success, report to loader.
- Step 4 — Download binary, delete original to prevent forensic recovery.
- Step 5 — Begin scanning and propagating.

**4. Dyn DNS Attack — 21 October 2016 (1 mark):**
- Botnet size: ~145,607 active devices.
- Peak throughput: ~1.2 Tbps.
- Target: Dyn's managed DNS infrastructure.
- Impact: Major sites (Twitter, GitHub, Reddit, Netflix, Spotify, PayPal) became unreachable.
- Mitigations: Dyn implemented BGP routing changes, traffic filtering, and rate-limiting.

---

#### (b) Discuss cryptographic countermeasures against application-layer DoS attacks. Include the role of HMAC, mTLS, and client puzzles. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Application-Layer DoS Definition (1 mark):**
A Layer-7 attack where malicious traffic is *indistinguishable* from legitimate users (e.g., a low-volume HTTPS POST flood). Volume-based filtering fails; semantic analysis is required.

**2. HMAC-Based Rate-Limit Tokens (2 marks):**
The CDN edge issues each validated client an HMAC-SHA-256 signed token bound to the client's IP and a timestamp:

$$
\text{token} = \text{HMAC-SHA-256}_{K_{\text{edge}}}(\text{IP}_{\text{client}} \,\|\, t \,\|\, \text{nonce})
$$

Subsequent requests must present this token. The origin server verifies the HMAC in constant time. An attacker forging tokens without $K_{\text{edge}}$ is computationally infeasible.

**3. Mutual TLS (mTLS) Client Authentication (2 marks):**
- All clients must present a valid X.509 client certificate signed by a trusted CA.
- Even if an attacker controls a million bots, they need legitimate client certificates to complete the handshake.
- Cryptographic handshake uses ECDHE for forward secrecy — the per-session key never leaves memory, preventing key-extraction attacks.

**4. Client Puzzles (Proof-of-Work) (2 marks):**
- Before the server allocates a session state, the client must solve a hash puzzle:

$$
\text{Find } x \text{ such that } \text{SHA-256}(\text{challenge} \,\|\, x) \text{ has } k \text{ leading zero bits}
$$

- Cost for a legitimate user: $\sim 2^k$ hash operations (cheap).
- Cost for a botnet: $\sim 2^k \times N_b$ (prohibitive when $k$ is dynamically raised under load).
- This is the same idea as Bitcoin mining and Hashcash (Adam Back, 1997).

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a memcached-based amplification DDoS attack. Compute the amplification factor for a request of 15 bytes producing a 750 kB response. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of Amplification (1 mark):**
An amplification attack exploits a protocol where a small request triggers a much larger response. The attacker spoofs the victim's source IP, so the amplified response floods the victim.

**2. Memcached Protocol (1 mark):**
Memcached is a distributed in-memory key-value cache. It supports both TCP and UDP. Misconfigured servers exposed the UDP service to the public Internet, with no authentication — the "get" request returns the stored value at line-rate.

**3. Step-by-Step Attack Operation (3 marks):**
- **Step 1**: Attacker scans for memcached servers on UDP port 11211.
- **Step 2**: Attacker loads the memcached server with a large payload (e.g., a 750 kB blob) under a chosen key.
- **Step 3**: Attacker sends a 15-byte `GET <key>` request to the memcached server with the source IP spoofed to the victim's address.
- **Step 4**: The memcached server sends the 750 kB response to the victim.
- **Step 5**: Thousands of memcached servers do this simultaneously, overwhelming the victim.

**Diagram (1 mark):** (Refer to SECTION 4.2)

**4. Amplification Factor Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_f &= \frac{S_{\text{response}}}{S_{\text{request}}} \\
&= \frac{750 \times 1024 \text{ bytes}}{15 \text{ bytes}} \\
&= \frac{768{,}000 \text{ bytes}}{15 \text{ bytes}} \\
&= 51{,}200 \times
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why a 1.7 Tbps attack was launched from a relatively small set of reflectors.

---

#### (b) Describe three real-world case studies of DDoS attacks. For each, identify the cryptographic or engineering lesson learned. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Case Study | Date | Peak | Lesson (with cryptographic angle) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mafiaboy** | Feb 2000 | ~1 Gbps | Lack of rate-limiting and SYN cookies on major web servers allowed trivial SYN floods. **Lesson**: Even at low scale, stateless cryptographic handshake defenses (SYN cookies) are essential. |
| **Mirai / Dyn** | Oct 2016 | ~1.2 Tbps | Unencrypted default credentials on IoT devices. **Lesson**: **Cryptographic firmware signing** (Secure Boot with ECDSA/RSA) at the manufacturing level is now an industry baseline. |
| **GitHub Memcached** | Feb 2018 | **1.7 Tbps** | Misconfigured UDP services with no authentication. **Lesson**: **Pre-negotiated cryptographic contracts** between origin and scrubbing providers (e.g., HMAC-signed BGP announcements) reduce mitigation time from hours to minutes. |
| **AWS Shield** | Feb 2020 | **2.3 Tbps** | Even hyperscalers face attack. **Lesson**: **Cloud KMS-based ephemeral credentials** (e.g., EC2 Instance Connect) prevent attackers from using stolen long-term keys to scale botnet infrastructure inside the cloud. |

**Final remark (1 mark):** The trend shows that attack volumes grow roughly in step with **Shannon's channel capacity** of global Internet backbones, while the cryptographic defense surface (signed firmware, attested sessions, signed routing) must scale in parallel.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master these before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **DoS** = single-source, **DDoS** = multi-source (botnet), **DRDoS** = uses reflectors with spoofed IP.
- ☐ Attack is a **CIA-Triad Availability** violation; classified as Class-A2 in KTU 2024.
- ☐ Three attack layers: **Volumetric (Layer 3/4)**, **Protocol state-exhaustion (Layer 4)**, **Application (Layer 7)**.
- ☐ Top volumetric case studies: **Mirai (1.2 Tbps)**, **GitHub Memcached (1.7 Tbps)**, **AWS CLDAP (2.3 Tbps)**.
- ☐ Amplification factor formula: $A_f = S_{\text{response}} / S_{\text{request}}$.
- ☐ Memcached amplification $\approx 51{,}200\times$ — memorize the order of magnitude.
- ☐ SYN cookies encode state into ISN via $\text{HMAC}_K(s, d, t)$ — stateless defense.
- ☐ Cryptographic DoS defenses: **HMAC tokens, mTLS, client puzzles, DNSSEC, BGPsec, Secure Boot**.
- ☐ Mirai scanned port 23 (Telnet) and 2323; used a 61-entry default-credential table.
- ☐ The 2016 Dyn attack affected Twitter, GitHub, Reddit, Netflix, Spotify, PayPal.
- ☐ The 2018 GitHub attack lasted only ~20 minutes due to pre-positioned cryptographic scrubbing contracts.
- ☐ Mafiaboy (2000) is the historical baseline; caused ~$1.7 B in damages.
- ☐ **Never** use raw `\|` inside a markdown table; use `\vert` or `\mid`.
- ☐ Every 14-mark answer must mention **cryptographic mitigation**, not just protocol description.
- ☐ KTU loves timelines: always state *date → vector → scale → mitigation → lesson*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IP Spoofing Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** IP Spoofing Attacks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **IP Spoofing** is a malicious network-level attack technique in which an attacker forges the **Source IP Address** field in the header of an Internet Protocol (IP) packet so that the packet appears to originate from a trusted host, a legitimate network, or a different geographical identity than its true origin. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEOT741)**, IP Spoofing is classified under *Network-Layer Security Threats* (Module 2). It exploits the fundamental design weakness of the **IPv4** protocol suite — namely, that IP itself is a *connectionless*, *best-effort* protocol with **no built-in source-address authentication mechanism**. The technique abuses the **Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP)**, **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)**, and **User Datagram Protocol (UDP)** by tampering with the 32-bit source address field in the IPv4 header (or the 128-bit source address in IPv6). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a postal system where anyone can drop a letter into a mailbox and write **any return address they wish** on the back of the envelope — even the President's official address. The post office (router) will dutifully deliver the letter, and if the recipient (victim server) trusts letters bearing the President's seal (firewall rule based on source IP), the letter is given VIP treatment. > **IP Spoofing = Forging the "return address" on a network packet.** A simpler real-world parallel is **caller-ID spoofing** on telephones — the number displayed on the victim's phone is fake, but the victim picks up the call trusting the displayed identity. ### 1.3 Core Characteristics (Syllabus Highlights) > [!NOTE] > **Key Identifiers of an IP Spoofing Attack:** > - **Spoofed Source:** Source IP address in the IP header does **not** match the actual sender's IP. > - **Trust Exploitation:** Many perimeter firewalls, ACLs, and authentication systems historically relied solely on source IP for trust decisions. > - **One-Way Attack Vector:** Spoofing is typically a **prelude** to larger attacks (DoS, MITM, session hijacking), not a standalone breach. > - **RFC Reference:** The underlying weakness was formally documented in **RFC 2827 / RFC 3704** — *Ingress Filtering* (Network Ingress Filtering: Defeating Denial-of-Service Attacks which employ IP Source Address Spoofing). > - **No Native Detection:** Standard TCP/IP stack has no checksum for source authenticity — it only checks header integrity, not authorship. ### 1.4 Standards and Constants | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | IPv4 Source Address Length | **32 bits** (4 octets) | | IPv6 Source Address Length | **128 bits** (8 hextets) | | IP Header Checksum | **16-bit one's complement sum** of the 16-bit words in the header | | Recommended Countermeasure Standard | **RFC 2827 / BCP 38** (Ingress Filtering) | | Related Cryptographic Mitigation | **RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure)** — RFC 6480 | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of an IPv4 Header (Attack Surface) The IPv4 header is the primary target of any spoofing operation. The attacker must recompute the **Header Checksum** after modifying any field — failing to do so causes the packet to be silently dropped by the destination. The minimum relevant fields for understanding IP spoofing are: - **Version (VER):** 4 bits — must remain `0100` for IPv4. - **Internet Header Length (IHL):** 4 bits — header length in 32-bit words. - **Type of Service (TOS):** 8 bits — packet priority. - **Total Length:** 16 bits — entire packet size. - **Identification (ID):** 16 bits — used for fragmentation reassembly. - **Flags (3 bits):** DF, MF, Reserved. - **Fragment Offset:** 13 bits. - **Time To Live (TTL):** 8 bits — decremented at each hop. - **Protocol:** 8 bits — 6 (TCP), 17 (UDP), 1 (ICMP). - **Header Checksum:** 16 bits — recomputed by the attacker. - **Source Address:** **32 bits** — the **attack vector**. - **Destination Address:** 32 bits. - **Options + Padding:** variable. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic > [!NOTE] > **The Four-Phase Spoofing Lifecycle (as per KTU Module-2 analytical framework):** **Phase 1 — Reconnaissance & Target Selection** - Attacker identifies the victim's IP address, open ports (via port scanning), and trust relationships (e.g., a server that accepts commands from a privileged internal IP). - Tools commonly referenced in KTU syllabus: `nmap`, `hping3`, `Scapy`. **Phase 2 — Trust-Relationship Mapping** - Attacker enumerates systems that have ACL rules permitting traffic from a specific source IP (often a router, printer, or another server). - Classic scenario: *rlogin / rsh trust* between UNIX machines — historical but still a syllabus example. **Phase 3 — Packet Forgery** - Attacker crafts raw packets using raw sockets (requires root/admin privileges) and replaces the source IP field with the spoofed address. - **TTL must be guessed** or estimated to match the trust path — a wrong TTL causes the packet to die before reaching the destination. - Header Checksum and TCP/UDP Checksum are recomputed over the modified header. **Phase 4 — Launch & Cover** - The forged packet is transmitted; replies (if any) are routed to the spoofed address, often masking the attacker's true location. - For blind spoofing (no reply interception), the attacker disables or suppresses return traffic. ### 2.3 Categories of IP Spoofing | Category | Description | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Blind Spoofing** | Attacker cannot see the response; relies on prediction | Remote DoS, blind TCP connection hijack (deprecated due to SYN cookies) | | **Non-Blind Spoofing** | Attacker is on the same subnet and can sniff replies (e.g., ARP poisoning) | Session hijack, MITM, Kerberos attacks | | **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** | Attacker intercepts and forwards traffic using spoofed ARP/IP bindings | Active eavesdropping, credential theft | | **Denial-of-Service (DoS / DDoS)** | Forged source IPs amplify reflection (e.g., Smurf Attack via ICMP) | Bandwidth exhaustion, NTP/DNS amplification | | **DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning** | Spoofed UDP packets inject fake DNS records | Redirection of users to malicious sites | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use in Cyber Security | |---|---|---| | **IP Header Checksum** (RFC 1071) | $\text{Checksum} = \overline{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \text{word}_i}$ (one's complement sum, then one's complement) | Must be recomputed after source IP forgery or packet is dropped | | **IP Header Word Count** | $N = \text{IHL} \times 2$ (since IHL = number of 32-bit words, each is 2 bytes) | Determines total header size in 16-bit words | | **Total Packet Size** | $\text{Total Length} = \text{Header Length} + \text{Data Length}$ | Validates packet bounds during crafting | | **TTL Decrement Law** | $\text{TTL}_{\text{dest}} = \text{TTL}_{\text{orig}} - (\text{number of hops traversed})$ | Used to estimate attacker-to-victim hop distance via traceroute | | **SYN Cookie Initial Sequence Number** | $\text{ISN} = H(K, s\_addr, d\_addr, sport, dport, t\_sec)$ | Defense against blind TCP spoofing / SYN floods | | **IPv4 Address Format** | $a.b.c.d$ where $a,b,c,d \in [0, 255]$ | Forging constrained to valid unicast addresses | | **CIDR Subnet Size** | $\text{Hosts} = 2^{(32 - n)} - 2$ where $n$ = prefix length | Determines broadcast/usable range during Smurf attack amplification | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pitfall to Memorize:** The IP header checksum covers **only the header**, not the payload. Therefore, modifying the source IP forces a checksum recalculation — a common mistake is to modify only the source address and forget to update the checksum, resulting in silent packet drops by the victim's kernel. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Recomputing the IP Header Checksum After Source Forgery The IPv4 header checksum is a **16-bit one's complement sum** of all 16-bit words in the header. The official algorithm is defined in **RFC 1071** and is mandatory to understand for any KTU numerical or analytical question involving packet inspection. **Given:** A 20-byte IPv4 header (IHL = 5, so $N = 5 \times 2 = 10$ 16-bit words). The original Source Address is `192.168.1.50`. The attacker wishes to spoof it to `10.0.0.5`. **Original 16-bit words of the header (excluding checksum field):** $W_0, W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4, W_5, W_6, W_7, W_8, W_9$ Note that the checksum field itself is replaced with `0x0000` during the calculation (standard practice). **Step 1 — Compute the one's complement sum of all 16-bit header words:** Let the sum (with carry-around) be $S$: $$ S = \sum_{i=0}^{9} W_i \pmod{0xFFFF} $$ where the addition wraps around any overflow back into the low 16 bits (one's complement carry-add). **Step 2 — Add the carry back to the low word:** If during the sum the result exceeds 16 bits, the overflow bits are wrapped: $$ S_{\text{final}} = (S \ \& \ 0xFFFF) + (S \gg 16) $$ This loop may iterate if a carry is generated again. **Step 3 — Take the one's complement:** $$ \text{Checksum} = \overline{S_{\text{final}}} $$ where $\overline{x}$ denotes the bitwise NOT operation on a 16-bit value. **Step 4 — The forgery delta:** When the attacker changes the source IP, only the two 16-bit words corresponding to the source address change. Let: - $W_8^{\text{old}}$ = original source address upper 16 bits (`192.168` $\Rightarrow$ `0xC0A8`) - $W_9^{\text{old}}$ = original source address lower 16 bits (`1.50` $\Rightarrow$ `0x0132`) - $W_8^{\text{new}}$ = new source address upper 16 bits (`10.0` $\Rightarrow$ `0x0A00`) - $W_9^{\text{new}}$ = new source address lower 16 bits (`0.5` $\Rightarrow$ `0x0005`) The delta in the sum is: $$ \Delta = (W_8^{\text{new}} + W_9^{\text{new}}) - (W_8^{\text{old}} + W_9^{\text{old}}) $$ The new checksum is therefore: $$ \text{Checksum}_{\text{new}} = \text{Checksum}_{\text{old}} + \Delta \pmod{0xFFFF} $$ followed by the same one's complement and carry-around step. **Worked Numerical Example:** Let the **original checksum** = `0xB82E`. Original source word pair: $0xC0A8 + 0x0132 = 0xC1DA$.
