Subtracting from 1 yields the probability that **at least one** is breached. This is a **probabilistic union** under the assumption of independence — standard practice in CVSS.

---

### Step 3: Compute the Impact Value (Scope Unchanged Branch)

Since $S = \text{Unchanged}$, we use the linear branch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 6.42 \times ISS \\
&= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\
&= 5.87631 \quad (\text{retain full precision for chaining})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4: Compute the Exploitability Sub-Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 \quad \text{(stepwise multiplication, no rounding yet)} \\
&= 6.987 \times 0.77 \\
&= 5.37999 \times 0.85 \\
&= 4.57299 \times 0.85 \\
&= 3.88704
\end{aligned}
$$

> Logic: Exploitability is the geometric product of the four attack-surface factors. The constant 8.22 is the CVSS specification's calibration constant that maps the multiplicative range to a 0–10 band.

---

### Step 5: Final Base Score Assembly

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseScore} &= \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min[(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}),\ 10]\bigr) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min[(5.87631 + 3.88704),\ 10]\bigr) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min[9.76335,\ 10]\bigr) \\
&= \text{roundUp}(9.76335) \\
&= 9.8
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6: Severity Mapping

Since $9.8 \ge 9.0$, this vulnerability is classified as **CRITICAL**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world interpretation:** This vector string describes a classic unauthenticated remote code execution or sensitive data breach vulnerability (e.g., Log4Shell `CVE-2021-44228` was scored 10.0 because Scope was Changed; this example scores 9.8 because Scope is Unchanged).

---

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Apply Each Tool

| Scenario | Recommended Tool | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Internal corporate LAN, 5000+ hosts | **Nessus / Qualys** | Agent-based, low network noise, authenticated accuracy |
| Internet-facing web app | **Burp Suite + OWASP ZAP** | Application-layer inspection, OWASP Top 10 mapping |
| Quick port discovery, large IP range | **Masscan** | Asynchronous, extremely fast, banner-light |
| Source code review | **SonarQube, Checkmarx** | Static Application Security Testing (SAST) |
| Running container images | **Trivy, Clair** | CVE scanning inside Docker layers |
| Wireless network audit | **Aircrack-ng, Kismet** | 802.11 protocol-level vulnerability discovery |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Vulnerability Analysis Lifecycle (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1["PHASE 1: ASSET DISCOVERY"]:::phaseNode
    phase2["PHASE 2: VULNERABILITY SCANNING"]:::phaseNode
    phase3["PHASE 3: TRIAGE AND ANALYSIS"]:::phaseNode
    phase4["PHASE 4: CVSS SCORING AND PRIORITIZATION"]:::phaseNode
    phase5["PHASE 5: REMEDIATION AND RE-SCAN"]:::phaseNode
    repoDB[("CVE / NVD / CWE DATABASE")]:::dataStore
    outputR["VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT REPORT VAR"]:::outputNode

    phase1 -->|enumerates hosts and services| phase2
    repoDB -->|provides signature feed| phase2
    phase2 -->|raw findings with false positives| phase3
    phase3 -->|confirmed true positives| phase4
    phase4 -->|prioritized patch queue| phase5
    phase5 -->|verify fixes| phase1
    phase5 -->|publish findings| outputR

    classDef phaseNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffd166,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef dataStore fill:#5a189a,stroke:#ffd166,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#d62828,stroke:#ffd166,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 CVSS v3.1 Metric Group Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    base["BASE METRICS"]:::baseGroup
    temp["TEMPORAL METRICS"]:::tempGroup
    env["ENVIRONMENTAL METRICS"]:::envGroup
    final[("FINAL CVSS SCORE 0 to 10")]:::finalNode

    base -->|AV AC PR UI C I A| final
    temp -->|E X RL| final
    env -->|CR IR AR CDP TD CRMA VC| final

    classDef baseGroup fill:#003049,stroke:#fcbf49,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef tempGroup fill:#669bbc,stroke:#fcbf49,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef envGroup fill:#fdf0d5,stroke:#fcbf49,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef finalNode fill:#c1121f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Vulnerability vs. Threat vs. Risk — Conceptual Venn

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph weaknesses["WEAKNESS DOMAIN"]
        v["VULNERABILITY: A FLAW IN THE SYSTEM"]:::vulnNode
    end
    subgraph adversary["ADVERSARY DOMAIN"]
        t["THREAT: AN AGENT CAPABLE OF EXPLOITING"]:::threatNode
    end
    subgraph business["BUSINESS IMPACT DOMAIN"]
        i["IMPACT: HARM IF EXPLOIT SUCCEEDS"]:::impactNode
    end
    risk["RISK: THE OVERLAP OF ALL THREE"]:::riskNode

    v -->|exposed to| risk
    t -->|targets| risk
    i -->|materializes via| risk

    classDef vulnNode fill:#e63946,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef threatNode fill:#457b9d,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef impactNode fill:#f4a261,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef riskNode fill:#1d3557,stroke:#f1faee,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Venn:** Risk only exists in the **triple intersection**. A vulnerability with no threat actor = theoretical risk only. A threat with no vulnerability = no entry path. A vulnerability + threat but no business asset = no impact, hence no real risk. KTU essay questions often test this exact logic.

## 4.4 Active vs. Passive Scanning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    target["TARGET SYSTEM"]:::targetNode
    passive["PASSIVE SCANNER: sniffs traffic, no packets sent"]:::passiveNode
    active["ACTIVE SCANNER: injects probe packets"]:::activeNode
    pcap["TRAFFIC CAPTURE / PCAP"]:::dataNode
    vulnLog["VULNERABILITY LOG"]:::dataNode

    target -->|broadcasts normal traffic| passive
    target -->|responds to probes| active
    passive -->|analyzes headers and protocols| pcap
    active -->|compares responses to signatures| vulnLog

    classDef targetNode fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef passiveNode fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef activeNode fill:#f4a261,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef dataNode fill:#264653,stroke:#e9c46a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Vulnerability Analysis. Differentiate it from Penetration Testing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Vulnerability Analysis** is the systematic, non-destructive process of identifying, classifying, and scoring security weaknesses in an information system using automated scanning tools, manual techniques, and standardized frameworks such as CVSS, against vulnerability databases like NVD and CVE.

| Parameter | Vulnerability Analysis | Penetration Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Find and catalog weaknesses | Exploit weaknesses to prove impact |
| **Destructiveness** | Non-invasive | Invasive / may crash systems |
| **Output** | Scanned report with CVSS scores | Proof-of-concept exploit + risk narrative |
| **Frequency** | Continuous / weekly | Quarterly / annually |
| **Skill Required** | Tool operation + CVE knowledge | Advanced exploit development + scripting |
| **Scope in KTU Module 3** | **Core focus** | Mentioned only as escalation |

*(1 mark for correct definition, 2 marks for the 4-row comparison table — 3 marks total)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is CVSS? List and briefly explain the three CVSS metric groups.**

**Model Answer:**

**CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** is an open, industry-standard framework maintained by **FIRST.org** that provides a numerical score (0.0 to 10.0) reflecting the severity of a software vulnerability. It is the de-facto standard for vulnerability prioritization mandated by NIST.

The three metric groups are:

1. **Base Metrics** — Intrinsic, unchangeable properties of the vulnerability (AV, AC, PR, UI, C, I, A, S). These are scored by the **vulnerability analyst** at the time of disclosure.
2. **Temporal Metrics** — Properties that change over time (Exploit Code Maturity `E`, Remediation Level `RL`, Report Confidence `RC`). These reflect the **current state of the threat landscape**.
3. **Environmental Metrics** — Customized scores reflecting the specific organization's asset value and security controls (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability Requirements, Modified Base metrics).

*(1 mark for CVSS definition + standard body, 2 marks for the three metric groups with examples — 3 marks total)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** confuse Vulnerability Analysis with Risk Assessment. The former is a *technical* activity; the latter is a *business* activity. Mixing them loses 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Always** mention the database (NVD/CVE) and the standard (CVSS) when describing scanning. A scanner name alone is incomplete.
> 3. In CVSS calculations, students often **forget the Scope branch** or the `roundUp` step. This causes a 1-2 mark deduction.
> 4. Do **not** write Vulnerability Management where the question asks for Analysis — they are **different concepts** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the five phases of the **Vulnerability Analysis Lifecycle** as defined by NIST. For each phase, name **at least one tool** and **one output deliverable**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A newly disclosed vulnerability in Apache Log4j has the following CVSS v3.1 vector string:
`CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H`
Compute the **Base Score** step-by-step and state the severity rating. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — The Five Phases (7 marks)**

**Phase 1 — Asset Discovery & Inventory:**
- **Activities:** Identify all IP-enabled devices, software versions, open ports, and running services within scope.
- **Tools:** Nmap, Advanced IP Scanner, NetBox.
- **Output:** Asset inventory spreadsheet (IP, MAC, OS, hostname, open ports, services).
- *(1.4 marks — split across activities, tools, output)*

**Phase 2 — Vulnerability Scanning:**
- **Activities:** Match discovered services against known vulnerability signatures.
- **Tools:** Nessus, Qualys VMDR, OpenVAS.
- **Output:** Raw scan report listing thousands of potential findings.
- *(1.4 marks)*

**Phase 3 — Vulnerability Analysis & Triage:**
- **Activities:** Manually verify findings, eliminate false positives, map to CVE/CWE IDs.
- **Tools:** Manual review, search NVD database, exploit-db verification.
- **Output:** Validated vulnerability list with CVE-ID, CWE-ID, affected versions.
- *(1.4 marks)*

**Phase 4 — Risk Scoring & Prioritization:**
- **Activities:** Apply CVSS v3.1 to compute Base Score; map to severity bands.
- **Tools:** CVSS calculator (FIRST.org official), Nessus built-in scorer.
- **Output:** Prioritized remediation list sorted by Base Score.
- *(1.4 marks)*

**Phase 5 — Remediation, Reporting & Re-scanning:**
- **Activities:** Apply patches, reconfigure services, document exceptions, re-scan to verify.
- **Tools:** WSUS, SCCM, Ansible for patch deployment.
- **Output:** Vulnerability Assessment Report (VAR) with executive summary and verification proof.
- *(1.4 marks)*

**Conclusion (synthesis statement for full marks):** The five phases form a closed feedback loop; Phase 5 re-enters Phase 1, ensuring continuous security posture improvement in line with the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle of ISO 27001.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — CVSS Base Score Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Decode Vector (1 mark):**

- $AV:N = 0.85$, $AC:L = 0.77$, $PR:N = 0.85$, $UI:N = 0.85$
- $S:C$ (Scope Changed), $C:H = 0.56$, $I:H = 0.56$, $A:H = 0.56$

**Step 2 — Compute ISS (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISS &= 1 - [(1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56)] \\
&= 1 - (0.44)^3 \\
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Impact (Scope Changed branch) (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 7.52 \times (ISS - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (ISS \times 0.9731 - 0.02)^{13} \\
&= 7.52 \times (0.914816 - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (0.914816 \times 0.9731 - 0.02)^{13} \\
&= 7.52 \times 0.885816 - 3.25 \times (0.89019 - 0.02)^{13} \\
&= 6.66134 - 3.25 \times (0.87019)^{13} \\
&= 6.66134 - 3.25 \times 0.14070 \\
&= 6.66134 - 0.45729 \\
&= 6.20405
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Exploitability (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
&= 3.88704 \quad \text{(as derived in Section 3 example)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Base Score with Scope Changed branch (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseScore} &= \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min[1.08 \times (6.20405 + 3.88704),\ 10]\bigr) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min[1.08 \times 10.09109,\ 10]\bigr) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\bigl(\min[10.89838,\ 10]\bigr) \\
&= \text{roundUp}(10) \\
&= 10.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Severity Rating (0.5 mark):**
Score = **10.0 → CRITICAL** severity.

This matches the actual real-world scoring of **Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228)** which received a CVSS 10.0 Critical rating and triggered global emergency patching.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **CVE, CWE, and NVD** repositories. Explain how they interlink to form the global vulnerability knowledge ecosystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Authenticated vs Unauthenticated vulnerability scanning**. In which scenario is each recommended? Provide **two pros and two cons** of each approach. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — The CVE / CWE / NVD Ecosystem (7 marks)**

**CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) (2 marks):**
Maintained by the **MITRE Corporation** (US Federally funded R&D center). A CVE is a unique, persistent identifier assigned to a **specific, single vulnerability** in a specific product. Example: `CVE-2024-3094` (XZ Utils backdoor). Format: `CVE-YEAR-NNNNN`. CVE records contain only a brief description and reference links — they do **not** contain severity scores.

**CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration) (2 marks):**
Also maintained by **MITRE**, but CWE is a **hierarchical taxonomy of weakness types** (root causes), not specific instances. Example: `CWE-79` = Cross-Site Scripting, `CWE-89` = SQL Injection, `CWE-120` = Buffer Overflow. A single CVE can map to **multiple** CWEs. CWE enables engineers to fix classes of bugs, not just one instance.

**NVD (National Vulnerability Database) (1.5 marks):**
Maintained by the **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology), USA**. NVD is the **enriched superset** of CVE data. For every CVE, NVD adds: CVSS v3.1 Base Score, CPE (affected products), references, and patch status. NVD synchronizes with the CVE feed within minutes of publication.

**Interlinkage (1.5 marks):**
- A **vendor** discovers a flaw → assigns/requests **CVE-ID** → publishes advisory.
- **NVD** automatically ingests the CVE → adds **CVSS score, CPE mapping, CWE classification, references**.
- Engineers search NVD to find their affected product (matched by CPE) → trace to the underlying **CWE class** → apply fix pattern (not just patch).
- This **CVE → NVD enrichment → CWE classification** flow forms a closed knowledge loop that powers every commercial scanner (Nessus, Qualys) and academic research.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Authenticated vs Unauthenticated Scanning (7 marks)**

**Definition Block (2 marks):**
- **Authenticated Scan:** Scanner is provided with valid read-only credentials (SSH, SMB, WMI) to log into target systems and inspect patch levels, registry keys, file versions, and configuration files.
- **Unauthenticated Scan:** Scanner interacts with the target only as an external attacker would — sending probe packets and observing responses, with no internal visibility.

**Authenticated Scanning — Pros and Cons (2.5 marks):**

*Pros:*
1. **High accuracy** — Can detect missing patches at the exact KB level (e.g., "KB5034441 not installed on Windows Server 2019").
2. **Low false-positive rate** — Direct inspection of configuration eliminates guesswork.

*Cons:*
1. **Credential management overhead** — Storing, rotating, and securing scan credentials is operationally complex.
2. **Privileged access risk** — Compromised scanner account could become a pivot point for lateral movement.

**Unauthenticated Scanning — Pros and Cons (2.5 marks):**

*Pros:*
1. **True attacker perspective** — Reveals exactly what an external adversary would see.
2. **No credentials required** — Easier to deploy on perimeter devices, OT/ICS systems, or third-party appliances.

*Cons:*
1. **High false-positive rate** — Banner-based detection often misidentifies versions.
2. **Limited depth** — Cannot see patch levels, registry misconfigurations, or local privilege escalation flaws.

**Scenario Recommendation Table (extra synthesis marks):**
- **Authenticated** → Internal corporate network, PCI-DSS regulated assets, post-deployment baseline.
- **Unauthenticated** → External perimeter, DMZ, web applications, OT/SCADA environments where installing agents is unsafe.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Vulnerability Analysis (Module 3)**

- [x] **Definition:** Systematic identification, classification, scoring, and reporting of weaknesses in an information system, aligned with NIST SP 800-115.
- [x] **Risk Equation:** $R = T \times V \times I$ — Risk is the product, not the sum.
- [x] **5 Lifecycle Phases:** Discovery → Scanning → Triage → Scoring → Remediation (cyclic, PDCA-aligned).
- [x] **CVSS Severity Bands:** None (0.0), Low (0.1-3.9), Medium (4.0-6.9), High (7.0-8.9), Critical (9.0-10.0).
- [x] **CVSS Formula Order:** Decode → ISS → Impact → Exploitability → BaseScore → `roundUp` → Severity.
- [x] **Scope Unchanged vs Changed:** Use the simpler `6.42 × ISS` branch for Unchanged; the complex polynomial branch is **only** for Changed.
- [x] **Impact Encoding Triplet:** None=0.00, Low=0.22, High=0.56.
- [x] **Exploitability Constants to Memorize:** $AV:N=0.85$, $AC:L=0.77$, $PR:N=0.85$, $UI:N=0.85$, Base Constant = 8.22.
- [x] **Key Repositories:** CVE (MITRE — unique IDs), CWE (MITRE — root cause classes), NVD (NIST — enriched + CVSS).
- [x] **Authenticated vs Unauthenticated:** Authenticated = internal, accurate, credential-dependent; Unauthenticated = external, attacker-perspective, higher false positives.
- [x] **Vulnerability Analysis ≠ Penetration Testing:** Analysis is non-destructive scanning; Pentest is invasive exploitation.
- [x] **Zero-Day:** Vulnerability with no patch available; highest threat-to-defender ratio.
- [x] **Vulnerability Management** is the parent discipline that includes Analysis + Remediation + Tracking + Reporting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Hacking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** System Hacking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**System Hacking** is the post-exploitation phase of ethical hacking wherein an attacker, having gained initial foothold access to a target computing system, attempts to escalate privileges, crack authentication credentials, execute arbitrary applications, conceal malicious presence, and erase forensic evidence of intrusion. According to the **EC-Council Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH)** framework, system hacking represents the controlled compromise of an operating system to evaluate its defensive posture against unauthorized privilege escalation and persistent unauthorized access.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, system hacking is classified as the **third tactical phase** of the broader penetration testing lifecycle, following footprinting/reconnaissance and scanning/enumeration, and preceding the maintenance of access and clearing tracks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** System hacking encompasses password cracking, privilege escalation, executing applications, hiding files, and covering tracks. These five sub-topics are **high-yield** for 14-mark essay questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thief who has managed to pick the front-door lock of a house. The thief is *inside* but only has access to the **living room** (low privileges). The thief's next steps are intuitive:

- 🔑 **Find the safe** (locate the SAM database or `/etc/shadow` file) and crack it (password cracking).
- 🪜 **Find a higher floor with a master key** (privilege escalation to root/administrator).
- 💻 **Plant a hidden camera** (execute a backdoor or trojan).
- 🖼️ **Hang a painting over the safe** to hide the evidence (rootkits and steganography).
- 🧹 **Wipe fingerprints and disable the alarm** (covering tracks by clearing logs).

This mirrors exactly what a system hacker performs on a compromised endpoint.

### 1.3 The Five Pillars of System Hacking

> [!NOTE]
> The **CEH/ECC** methodology divides system hacking into **5 distinct phases**, each corresponding to a specific KTU Module 3 sub-topic:

| Pillar | Sub-Topic | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **P1** | Cracking Passwords | High (3-mark + 7-mark) |
| **P2** | Privilege Escalation | Very High (frequently tested) |
| **P3** | Executing Applications | Medium (definition-style) |
| **P4** | Hiding Files & Covering Tracks | High (essay favorite) |
| **P5** | Rootkits & Keyloggers | Very High (frequently tested) |

The attacker’s ultimate objective is **full administrative control** — gaining **NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM** on Windows or **root (UID 0)** on Linux/Unix.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The System Hacking Methodology — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Logic

The ECCouncil-defined system hacking lifecycle consists of **six sequential stages**. Each stage is non-optional; skipping one dramatically reduces the success probability of subsequent stages.

**Stage 1 — Cracking Passwords**

Passwords are the **first line of defensive authentication** on virtually all computing systems. The attacker must convert the stored password representation into its plaintext form to gain legitimate credentials.

> [!NOTE]
> **How passwords are stored:**
> - **Windows:** Stored in the **SAM (Security Account Manager) database** at `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM`. Modern systems additionally use the **NTDS.dit** file on Domain Controllers. SAM is encrypted with the **SYSKEY** boot key.
> - **Linux/Unix:** Stored in `/etc/shadow` (hashed) and `/etc/passwd` (user info, often contains hash placeholder `x`).

**The Six Password Cracking Techniques:**

1. **Passive Online Attack (Wire Sniffing/Packet Analysis)**
   - Attacker monitors network traffic using a sniffer (e.g., **Wireshark**, **Ettercap**, **Cain & Abel**).
   - Extracts credentials transmitted in plaintext (e.g., FTP, Telnet, HTTP Basic Auth).
   - Counter-defense: **Encryption** (SSH, HTTPS, SFTP).

2. **Active Online Attack (Direct Guessing)**
   - **Dictionary Attack:** Tries every word from a wordlist (e.g., `rockyou.txt` — 14 million entries).
   - **Brute-Force Attack:** Tries every possible character combination up to a defined keyspace.
   - **Rule-Based Attack:** Applies mutation rules (e.g., `password` → `P@ssw0rd!2024`).
   - **Default Password Attack:** Exploits vendor-shipped default credentials.
   - Tools: **Hydra**, **Medusa**, **Ncrack**, **L0phtCrack** (Windows), **John the Ripper** (offline).

3. **Offline Attack (Hash Extraction & Cracking)**
   - Attacker dumps the SAM database using tools like **pwdump7**, **fgdump**, **Mimikatz**, or `/etc/shadow` on Linux.
   - Cracks hashes offline using **Rainbow Tables** or CPU/GPU crackers.
   - Speed: **Hashcat** with NVIDIA GPUs can compute **billions of MD5 hashes/second**.

4. **Non-Technical Attack (Social Engineering)**
   - Shoulder surfing, dumpster diving, phishing, tailgating.
   - Bypasses all technical controls.

5. **Hybrid Attack**
   - Combines dictionary + brute-force. Uses the dictionary as the base and appends/prepends brute-force characters.
   - Example: `summer` + `[0-9]{4}` → `summer2024`.

6. **Pre-Computed Hash / Rainbow Table Attack**
   - Pre-computed lookup tables mapping plaintext → hash.
   - **Trade-off:** Fast cracking vs. massive storage (e.g., LM-hash rainbow tables for all alphanumeric 14-char passwords = ~14 GB).
   - **Counter-defense:** Use **salted hashes** (defeats rainbow tables since each user's salt is unique).

**The Password Hashing Algorithms — A Critical Comparison:**

| Algorithm | Type | Output Length | Vulnerable to Rainbow Table? | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LM (LAN Manager)** | Legacy, DES-based | 16 hex chars | **Yes — trivially** | Very High (must mention) |
| **NTLM / NTLMv2** | MD4-based | 32 hex chars | Partially | High |
| **MD5** | Message-Digest 5 | 128 bits (32 hex) | Yes (without salt) | High |
| **SHA-1** | Secure Hash Algorithm 1 | 160 bits (40 hex) | Partially | Medium |
| **SHA-256** | SHA-2 family | 256 bits (64 hex) | No (with salt) | Medium |
| **bcrypt** | Adaptive, salted | Variable | No | High (modern best practice) |
| **Argon2** | Memory-hard | Configurable | No | High (modern best practice) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Mnemonic:** **"L-N-M-S-S"** for hash algorithms in chronological order of attack vulnerability:
> **L**M → **N**TLM → **M**D5 → **S**HA-1 → **S**HA-256/bcrypt/Argon2.
> Students must mention the **LM hash** is case-insensitive, splits passwords into two **7-byte chunks**, and is the most commonly tested historical weakness.

**Stage 2 — Privilege Escalation**

Privilege escalation is the act of exploiting a vulnerability, design flaw, or configuration oversight in an operating system or application to gain elevated access to resources that are normally protected from an application or user.

Two categories exist:

- **Vertical Privilege Escalation (Privilege Elevation):** A lower-privilege user gains the privileges of a higher-privilege user (e.g., standard user → Administrator → SYSTEM/root). This is the **most commonly tested** in KTU.

- **Horizontal Privilege Escalation:** A user gains the privileges of another user of *similar* privilege level (e.g., User A accessing User B's files). Less commonly tested.

**Six Methods of Privilege Escalation:**

1. **Cracking Passwords** (covered above).
2. **Exploiting Vulnerabilities:** Using unpatched OS/application bugs (e.g., **EternalBlue** — MS17-010, **Dirty COW** — CVE-2016-5195 on Linux, **PrintNightmare** — Windows Print Spooler).
3. **Exploiting Misconfigured Services/Permissions:**
   - Weak ACLs (Access Control Lists).
   - Unquoted service paths on Windows (`C:\Program Files\App\app.exe` vs. `C:\Program.exe`).
   - SUID/SGID bits on Linux (files executable with owner's permissions).
   - World-writable `/etc/passwd` or `/etc/shadow`.
4. **DLL Hijacking / Injection:** Replacing a legitimate DLL with a malicious one loaded by a privileged process.
5. **Token Impersonation:** Stealing access tokens from privileged processes (e.g., **Incognito** module in Metasploit, **Mimikatz `token::elevate`**).
6. **Keyloggers & Spyware:** Capturing credentials of privileged users when they log in.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *vertical* and *horizontal* escalation. **Vertical = different privilege levels; Horizontal = same level, different user.** Examiners award 1 mark for this distinction.

**Stage 3 — Executing Applications**

After gaining elevated privileges, the attacker must execute malicious code to maintain access. Key concepts:

- **Backdoors:** Hidden methods of bypassing normal authentication. Examples: **Netcat** reverse shells, **Trojans** (e.g., **SubSeven**, **Back Orifice**), **webshells** on web servers.
- **Malware Components:**
  - **Virus:** Self-replicating, requires host file.
  - **Worm:** Self-replicating, standalone (network-propagating).
  - **Trojan:** Disguised as legitimate software; non-replicating.
  - **Ransomware:** Encrypts user data, demands payment.
  - **Rootkit:** Hides itself and other malware.
- **Tool of choice in KTU labs:** **Metasploit Framework's `msfvenom`** for payload generation, **nc.exe** (Netcat) for simple reverse shells.

**Stage 4 — Hiding Files**

Techniques to conceal malicious artifacts from system administrators and anti-virus:

1. **Alternate Data Streams (ADS) — Windows NTFS only:**
   - Allows hiding a file "behind" another file. The host file appears unchanged.
   - Command: `type payload.exe > normal.txt:payload.exe`
   - The hidden file **does not appear in `dir` output** but appears in `dir /R`.
   - To execute: `start normal.txt:payload.exe`

2. **Steganography:**
   - Embedding data within image, audio, or video files.
   - Tools: **OpenStego**, **Steghide**, **Snow** (whitespace steganography).
   - LSB (Least Significant Bit) encoding is the most common technique.

3. **NTFS File Attributes:**
   - Setting `+h` (hidden) and `+s` (system) attributes: `attrib +h +s malware.exe`
   - Hides from default `dir` listing.

4. **Linux Hiding Techniques:**
   - **Dot-prefixed directories:** `~/.cache/.evil/`
   - **Rootkit-based file hiding:** Hooking kernel syscalls (`getdents`, `readdir`).

5. **Registry Hiding (Windows):**
   - `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` — auto-start location.
   - Attacker uses obscure, plausible names (e.g., `WindowsUpdateHelper`).

**Stage 5 — Covering Tracks**

The final stage — removing evidence of intrusion:

1. **Disable Auditing:**
   - Windows: `auditpol /set /category:"Logon/Logoff" /success:disable /failure:disable`
   - Or modify registry: `HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\PolAdtEv` (limited user write access).

2. **Clear Logs:**
   - **Windows Event Viewer:** Clear Security, System, Application logs.
   - Tools: **WinZapper**, **Evidence Eliminator**, **Metasploit's `clearev` command**.
   - **Linux:** `> /var/log/auth.log`, `> /var/log/syslog`, `history -c`, then `ln -sf /dev/null ~/.bash_history`.

3. **Manipulate Timestamps (Timestomping):**
   - Modify the MACE timestamps (Modified, Accessed, Changed/Entry, Birth) of files to match legitimate files.
   - Tool: **timestomp** (from Metasploit), `touch -t` on Linux.

4. **Hide Network Evidence:**
   - Use **reverse shells** (inbound firewall rules block attacker-initiated connections, but victim initiates outbound).
   - Use **HTTPS/TLS-encrypted C2 channels**.
   - Tunnel through **DNS** (e.g., **iodine**, **dnscat2**) or **ICMP** (e.g., **icmpsh**).

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Since System Hacking is **qualitative** in nature, the "formulas" below are conceptual formulae, decision rules, and metric definitions that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce verbatim.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No literal mathematical equations dominate this topic** — but the following decision matrices, metric definitions, and KTU-defining principles are **high-yield memorization items**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Boundary | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Keyspace size** for brute-force | $K = C^n$ | $K$ = total combinations, $C$ = character set size, $n$ = password length | Determines cracking time: $T = K / R$ where $R$ = hash rate (hashes/sec) |
| 2 | **Cracking Time** | $T = \frac{C^n}{R \cdot P}$ | $P$ = parallelism factor (CPU/GPU count) | Estimates feasibility of brute-force attack |
| 3 | **Password Entropy** | $E = n \cdot \log_2(C)$ | bits | Higher $E$ = stronger password. $\geq 80$ bits is considered strong (NIST SP 800-63B) |
| 4 | **LM Hash Split** | $P = P_1 \vert P_2$, each $\leq 7$ bytes | Padded with nulls if shorter | Each half cracked independently — 7-char passwords crack in seconds |
| 5 | **Information Density (Steganography)** | $D = \frac{\text{payload bits}}{\text{cover bits}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Measures concealment efficiency; typical LSB = 0.4%–12.5% |
| 6 | **Privilege Level Hierarchy (Windows)** | $Guest < User < Power\ User < Admin < SYSTEM$ | Discrete ladder | Determines attack path selection |
| 7 | **Privilege Level Hierarchy (Linux)** | $UID\ 0\ (root) > UID\ 1\text{-}999\ (system) > UID\ \geq 1000\ (users)$ | Integer UID | Determines exploit target priority |
| 8 | **Adversary Dwell Time** | $T_{dwell} = T_{detect} - T_{entry}$ | Days / hours | Lower = better for defender (MITRE ATT&CK benchmark) |
| 9 | **Hashcat Speed** | $R_{MD5} \approx 50\ GH/s$ (8× RTX 4090) | Hashes/second | Capacity planning for password policy enforcement |
| 10 | **Rainbow Table Reduction** | $R = T_{\text{crack}} / T_{\text{compute}} = O(1)$ | Constant-time lookup | Salting invalidates pre-computed tables |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept for KTU:** Password entropy formula is frequently asked. **Memorize:** $E = n \log_2 C$. Example: 8-character password from 95 printable ASCII chars → $E = 8 \cdot \log_2 95 \approx 52.4$ bits. **Weak.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cracking Time for a Brute-Force Attack

The following derivation shows the exhaustive mathematical reasoning expected in a KTU 14-mark question on password cracking feasibility.

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> An organization enforces an 8-character password policy using all 95 printable ASCII characters. An attacker possesses an offline hash-cracking rig capable of $R = 10^{10}$ (10 billion) MD5 hashes per second. Calculate:
> (a) the total keyspace $K$,
> (b) the average cracking time $T_{avg}$,
> (c) the maximum cracking time $T_{max}$, and
> (d) the password entropy $E$.

#### Step 1 — Establish the Keyspace Formula

The total number of possible password combinations (keyspace) is:

$$
K = C^n
$$

where:
- $C = 95$ (printable ASCII: 26 lowercase + 26 uppercase + 10 digits + 33 special characters)
- $n = 8$ (password length)

#### Step 2 — Compute the Keyspace

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= 95^8 \\
&= 6.634 \times 10^{15} \\
&\approx 6.6\ \text{quadrillion combinations}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Average Cracking Time

The average case assumes the target password lies at the **midpoint** of the keyspace, so on average the cracker examines half the candidates before success:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= \frac{K}{2 \cdot R} \\
&= \frac{6.634 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= \frac{6.634 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= 3.317 \times 10^{5}\ \text{seconds} \\
&\approx 3.84\ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute Maximum Cracking Time

The worst case requires examining **every** candidate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{K}{R} \\
&= \frac{6.634 \times 10^{15}}{10^{10}} \\
&= 6.634 \times 10^{5}\ \text{seconds} \\
&\approx 7.68\ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute Password Entropy

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= n \cdot \log_2 C \\
&= 8 \cdot \log_2 95 \\
&= 8 \cdot 6.5699 \\
&\approx 52.56\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 52.56-bit password can be brute-forced in **under 8 days** by a single modern GPU. NIST SP 800-63B requires $\geq 80$ bits for human-chosen passwords, which would require either **12+ characters** from a 95-character set OR the use of **passphrases** with longer length.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Attack vs. Defense Mapping

This matrix is **critical** for 14-mark KTU questions that ask "Explain the countermeasures for system hacking."

| Attack Stage | Attacker Tool / Technique | Defender Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| **Password storage extraction** | `pwdump7`, `Mimikatz`, `fgdump` | **Credential Guard** (Windows), **LSA Protection**, restrict `SeDebugPrivilege` |
| **Offline hash cracking** | Hashcat, John the Ripper, Rainbow Tables | **Salted + adaptive hashing** (bcrypt, Argon2), enforce password length $\geq 12$ |
| **Brute force online** | Hydra, Medusa, Ncrack | **Account lockout policy** (e.g., 5 attempts → 30 min lockout), **CAPTCHA**, rate limiting |
| **LM hash exploitation** | Extraction + crack | Disable LM hash storage: `gpedit.msc → Computer Config → Windows Settings → Security Settings → Local Policies → Security Options → Network security: Do not store LAN Manager hash value on next password change = Enabled` |
| **Privilege escalation (Windows)** | UAC bypass, token impersonation | **Enable UAC at highest level**, **Patch Tuesday** discipline, **LAPS** (Local Admin Password Solution) |
| **Privilege escalation (Linux)** | SUID exploitation, Dirty COW | Regular patching, **auditd** for SUID file monitoring, **SELinux/AppArmor** |
| **Backdoor installation** | Netcat, Metasploit `meterpreter`, Trojans | **Application whitelisting** (AppLocker, WDAC), **EDR** (Endpoint Detection & Response) |
| **Hiding files (ADS)** | `type evil.exe > good.txt:evil.exe` | PowerShell auditing: `Get-Item .\* -Stream *` to enumerate streams, FSRM (File Server Resource Manager) |
| **Steganography exfiltration** | Steghide, OpenStego | **DLP** (Data Loss Prevention) with content inspection, egress filtering |
| **Log clearing** | `clearev`, `> /var/log/syslog` | **Centralized log forwarding** (SIEM — Splunk, ELK, Wazuh) so attacker cannot delete remote copies |
| **Timestamp manipulation** | `timestomp`, `touch -t` | **File integrity monitoring** (Tripwire, OSSEC, AIDE) with cryptographic hash baselines |
| **Covering tracks via tunneling** | DNS tunneling, ICMP tunneling | **Egress monitoring**, deep packet inspection, restrict outbound to known-good destinations |

### 3.3 Detailed Procedural Walkthrough: Mimikatz-Based Credential Extraction (Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Relevant Conceptual Walkthrough** (No actual exploitation code, only the conceptual steps a KTU essay question would expect):

**Scenario:** Attacker has local administrator access on a Windows 10 endpoint and wishes to extract credentials from the **LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service)** process memory for lateral movement.

**Conceptual Step-by-Step:**

1. **Attacker drops Mimikatz** (or uses Metasploit's `load kiwi`) onto the target. *(Tool deployment — gaining code execution.)*

2. **Attacker elevates to SYSTEM** using `token::elevate` or `privilege::debug`. *(Privilege escalation — obtaining SeDebugPrivilege required to read LSASS memory.)*

3. **Attacker dumps credentials** via `sekurlsa::logonpasswords` or `lsadump::sam`. *(Credential extraction from volatile memory.)*

4. **Output reveals** plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets (TGTs), and WDigest credentials. *(Hash extraction for offline cracking or Pass-the-Hash attack.)*

5. **Attacker uses extracted NTLM hash** for **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** lateral movement to other systems where the same account is trusted, **without ever cracking the password**. *(Credential reuse exploitation.)*

6. **Defender's response** would be: deploy **Credential Guard** (virtualization-based security isolating LSASS), enable **Protected Process Light (PPL)** for LSASS, restrict `SeDebugPrivilege` to SYSTEM only, and use **EDR rules** to alert on Mimikatz signatures.

### 3.4 Rootkit Deep-Dive Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Topic:** Rootkits are tested in nearly every KTU Cyber Security paper. Use this matrix for a guaranteed 7 marks.

| Rootkit Type | Privilege Level | Persistence | Detection Difficulty | Famous Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User-Mode Rootkit** | Ring 3 (User) | Limited | Easy (AV/anti-rootkit) | **FU**, **Hacker Defender** |
| **Kernel-Mode Rootkit** | Ring 0 (Kernel) | High | Hard (requires kernel-level AV) | **ZeroAccess**, **TDSS/TDL4** |
| **Bootkit** | MBR/EFI firmware | **Very High** (survives OS reinstall) | Very Hard | **Stuxnet**, **Mebromi** |
| **Firmware Rootkit** | BIOS/UEFI/Hardware | **Extremely High** | Nearly Impossible | **LoJax** (UEFI), **Equation** |
| **Hypervisor Rootkit** | Ring -1 (below OS) | High | Extremely Hard | **Blue Pill** (research) |
| **Memory Rootkit** | RAM-only, no disk | None (reboots wipe) | Hard (memory forensics) | **Stuxnet's memory payload** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Defense Hierarchy (KTU-Expected Answer):**
> 1. **Boot integrity:** **Secure Boot** (UEFI) prevents unsigned bootkits.
> 2. **Kernel integrity:** **Kernel Patch Protection (PatchGuard)** on 64-bit Windows.
> 3. **Code signing:** Driver Signature Enforcement.
> 4. **Behavioral detection:** EDR watches for SSDT hooking, IRP hooking, and direct kernel object manipulation (DKOM).
> 5. **Memory forensics:** **Volatility Framework**, **Rekall** for offline RAM analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The System Hacking Lifecycle — Master Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Foothold Obtained] --> B[Stage 1: Password Cracking]
    B --> C{Passwords Cracked?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Stage 2: Privilege Escalation]
    C -- No --> E[Try Dictionary / Brute Force / Rainbow Table]
    E --> C
    D --> F{Admin / Root Achieved?}
    F -- No --> G[Exploit Vulnerabilities / Token Impersonation / SUID]
    G --> F
    F -- Yes --> H[Stage 3: Execute Applications]
    H --> I[Install Backdoors / Trojans / Reverse Shells]
    I --> J[Stage 4: Hide Files]
    J --> K[ADS / Steganography / Registry Hiding / Rootkits]
    K --> L[Stage 5: Cover Tracks]
    L --> M[Clear Logs / Timestomp / Disable Auditing]
    M --> N[Persistent Access Established]
```

### 4.2 Password Cracking Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Choose Attack Vector] --> P1{Has Network<br/>Access?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2[Active Online Attack]
    P1 -- No --> P3[Offline Attack]
    P2 --> P4[Dictionary]
    P2 --> P5[Brute Force]
    P2 --> P6[Rule-Based]
    P2 --> P7[Default Passwords]
    P2 --> P8[Hybrid]
    P3 --> P9[Extract Hashes<br/>SAM / etc-shadow]
    P9 --> P10{Has Rainbow Table?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Lookup in Table]
    P10 -- No --> P12[Brute Force Offline<br/>Hashcat / John]
    P9 --> P13[LM Hash Specific Attack]
    P9 --> P14[NTLM / NTLMv2]
    P4 --> P15[Cracked?]
    P5 --> P15
    P6 --> P15
    P7 --> P15
    P8 --> P15
    P11 --> P15
    P12 --> P15
    P13 --> P15
    P14 --> P15
    P15 -- Yes --> P16[Plaintext Credentials Obtained]
    P15 -- No --> P17[Try Next Technique]
```

### 4.3 Privilege Escalation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOWER[Lower Privilege Zone]
        U1[Standard User<br/>UID 1000+]
        U2[Guest Account]
    end
    subgraph MID[Mid Privilege Zone]
        U3[Power User]
        U4[Service Account]
    end
    subgraph HIGH[High Privilege Zone]
        U5[Local Administrator]
        U6[NT AUTHORITY SYSTEM]
        U7[Linux Root UID 0]
        U8[Domain Admin]
    end
    U1 -->|Exploit Vulnerability| U5
    U1 -->|Token Impersonation| U6
    U1 -->|SUID Bit Abuse| U7
    U1 -->|Credential Theft| U8
    U3 -->|UAC Bypass| U6
    U4 -->|Misconfigured Service| U5
    U5 -->|Pass-the-Hash| U8
    U6 -->|Golden Ticket| U8
```

### 4.4 Rootkit Layer Architecture (Ring-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R3[Ring 3 - User Mode<br/>Applications and User-Mode Rootkits] --> R0
    R0[Ring 0 - Kernel Mode<br/>OS Kernel and Kernel-Mode Rootkits] --> RN1
    RN1[Ring -1 - Hypervisor<br/>Hypervisor Rootkits] --> RN2
    RN2[Ring -2 - System Management Mode<br/>SMM Rootkits] --> RN3
    RN3[Ring -3 - Firmware<br/>UEFI/BIOS/Hardware Rootkits]
    R3 -. Detected by .-> AV1[Antivirus / EDR]
    R0 -. Harder to detect .-> AV2[Kernel Integrity Monitors]
    RN1 -. Very Hard .-> AV3[Memory Forensics]
    RN2 -. Nearly Impossible .-> AV4[Hardware Anomaly Detection]
    RN3 -. Requires Physical .-> AV5[UEFI Validation Tools]
```

### 4.5 Covering Tracks — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CT1[Compromise Complete] --> CT2[Step 1: Disable Auditing]
    CT2 --> CT3[Step 2: Clear Event Logs]
    CT3 --> CT4[Step 3: Modify Timestamps]
    CT4 --> CT5[Step 4: Delete Uploaded Tools]
    CT5 --> CT6[Step 5: Tunnel Outbound Traffic]
    CT6 --> CT7[Step 6: Reverse Shell Persistence]
    CT7 --> CT8[Step 7: Install Rootkit]
    CT8 --> CT9[Stealthy Persistent Access]
    CT2 -.auditpol disable.-> CT3
    CT3 -.clearev / history -c.-> CT4
    CT4 -.timestomp / touch -t.-> CT5
    CT6 -.DNS / ICMP tunnel.-> CT7
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "System Hacking" and list its five phases as per the CEH methodology.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**System Hacking** is the post-exploitation phase of ethical hacking where an attacker, after gaining initial access to a system, attempts to escalate privileges, crack passwords, execute applications, hide files, and cover tracks to maintain unauthorized persistent access.

**Five Phases:**
1. **Cracking Passwords** — extracting and converting stored credentials to plaintext.
2. **Escalating Privileges** — gaining higher-level access (admin/SYSTEM/root).
3. **Executing Applications** — running backdoors, Trojans, or remote-access tools.
4. **Hiding Files** — concealing malicious artifacts using ADS, steganography, or rootkits.
5. **Covering Tracks** — clearing logs, disabling auditing, and timestomping to remove evidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Five phases listed (0.4 marks each = 2 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the difference between a User-Mode rootkit and a Kernel-Mode rootkit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | User-Mode Rootkit | Kernel-Mode Rootkit |
|---|---|---|
| **Privilege Ring** | Ring 3 (User space) | Ring 0 (Kernel space) |
| **Operation Level** | Modifies user-level APIs and system calls (hooking) | Modifies kernel data structures and system call tables |
| **Detection** | Easy (standard AV, anti-rootkit tools) | Hard (requires kernel-level integrity monitors) |
| **Privilege** | Limited to user permissions | Full system/kernel privileges |
| **Examples** | **FU Rootkit**, **Hacker Defender** | **ZeroAccess**, **TDSS/TDL4**, **Stuxnet** |
| **Persistence** | Lower | Higher (often survives reboots) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Distribution:** 1.5 marks for clear distinction + 1.5 marks for examples and detection difficulty.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice** — the student must answer EITHER OR the two sub-options. Each question is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a) Explain the different techniques used for password cracking in detail.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Passive Online Attack (1 mark)**
The attacker passively monitors network traffic using packet sniffers such as **Wireshark**, **Tcpdump**, or **Cain & Abel** to capture credentials transmitted in plaintext. Protocols vulnerable to this attack include FTP, Telnet, SMTP, and HTTP Basic Authentication. The defense is to use encryption (SSH, HTTPS, SFTP, TLS).

**2. Active Online Attack (2 marks)**
The attacker directly attempts to log in to the target system using automated tools. Sub-techniques include:
- **Dictionary Attack:** Tries every word from a precompiled wordlist (`rockyou.txt` — 14M entries, `crackstation.txt`).
- **Brute-Force Attack:** Systematically tries every possible combination up to a defined keyspace.
- **Rule-Based Attack:** Applies mutation rules to dictionary words (e.g., leetspeak, appending years).
- **Default Password Attack:** Exploits vendor-shipped credentials (admin/admin, root/toor).
- Tools: **Hydra**, **Medusa**, **Ncrack**, **L0phtCrack**, **Brutus**.

**3. Offline Attack (2 marks)**
The attacker first extracts password hashes from the system — `pwdump7`, `fgdump`, **Mimikatz** for Windows SAM; reading `/etc/shadow` on Linux. The hashes are then cracked offline using:
- **Rainbow Tables** (precomputed lookup tables for unsalted hashes).
- **CPU/GPU-accelerated brute force** using **Hashcat** or **John the Ripper**.
Offline attacks are faster than online attacks because they bypass account lockout policies.

**4. Non-Technical / Social Engineering Attack (1 mark)**
- **Shoulder Surfing:** Observing the user typing the password.
- **Dumpster Diving:** Searching discarded documents for credentials.
- **Phishing:** Deceiving users into revealing credentials via fake login pages.
- **Tailgating/Piggybacking:** Physically following an authorized user into a secured area.

**5. Hybrid Attack (1 mark)**
Combines dictionary and brute-force methods — uses a dictionary word as the base and appends/prepends brute-force character combinations. Example: `Summer` + 4-digit year → `Summer2024`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Distribution:** 1 mark per technique × 5 = 5 marks. The remaining 2 marks are awarded for proper examples (tools, file paths) and clear classification.

---

**Q. (b) What is Privilege Escalation? Explain the various methods of privilege escalation with examples.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Privilege Escalation** is the process of exploiting a vulnerability, design flaw, or configuration error in an operating system or software application to gain elevated access rights beyond those originally granted. The two main types are:
- **Vertical Privilege Escalation:** Lower-privilege user gains higher-privilege access (e.g., user → admin).
- **Horizontal Privilege Escalation:** User gains the privileges of another user of the *same* level.

**Methods of Privilege Escalation (6 marks — 1.2 marks each):**

**1. Password Cracking**
Cracking the password of a privileged account (e.g., local Administrator, root). Tools: John the Ripper, Hashcat.

**2. Exploiting Software Vulnerabilities**
- **EternalBlue (MS17-010):** SMBv1 RCE → SYSTEM on Windows 7/Server 2008.
- **Dirty COW (CVE-2016-5195):** Linux kernel race condition → root.
- **PrintNightmare (CVE-2021-1675):** Windows Print Spooler RCE → SYSTEM.

**3. Exploiting Misconfigured Permissions/Services**
- **SUID/SGID bits** on Linux: A binary with SUID root can be exploited to spawn a root shell.
- **Unquoted service paths** on Windows: `C:\Program Files\My App\service.exe` — if `C:\Program.exe` exists, Windows runs it as SYSTEM.
- **Weak ACLs** on system binaries or service executables.

**4. DLL Hijacking / Injection**
Replacing a legitimate DLL with a malicious one. When a privileged process loads the DLL (e.g., a service running as SYSTEM), the attacker's code executes with elevated privileges. Tools: **DLLInjector**, **Process Hacker**.

**5. Token Impersonation**
Stealing access tokens from privileged processes. Tools: **Incognito** (Metasploit), **Mimikatz** (`token::elevate`, `token::steal`). The Windows token model allows a thread to impersonate the security context of another logged-in user.

**6. Keyloggers & Credential Theft**
Spyware/keyloggers capturing passwords of privileged users as they authenticate. Hardware keyloggers (e.g., USB keystroke recorders) bypass software controls entirely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Distribution:** Definition + 2 types (1 mark) + 6 methods with examples (1 mark each = 6 marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently omit the **vertical vs. horizontal distinction** and lose 1 mark. Examiners also expect **specific CVE numbers or tool names** — generic answers like "exploiting vulnerabilities" without examples score 0.5/1.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a) Explain the concept of Rootkits. Discuss its types and how to detect and defend against rootkits.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **Rootkit** is a collection of malicious software (often stealthy) that enables unauthorized users to gain administrator-level (root) access to a computer system while actively hiding its presence. Rootkits modify the OS's behavior to conceal files, processes, registry entries, and network connections.

**Types of Rootkits (3.5 marks — 0.7 each):**

**1. User-Mode (Application) Rootkit**
Operates at Ring 3. Hooks user-level API functions (`CreateProcess`, `FindFirstFile`) to hide malicious artifacts. Example: **Hacker Defender**, **FU Rootkit**. Detection: standard AV, anti-rootkit tools (GMER, Malwarebytes Anti-Rootkit).

**2. Kernel-Mode Rootkit**
Operates at Ring 0. Modifies the kernel — hooks the System Service Descriptor Table (SSDT), Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT), or inline-patches kernel functions. Example: **ZeroAccess**, **TDSS/TDL4**. Detection: **GMER**, **RootkitRevealer**, kernel integrity monitors.

**3. Bootkit (MBR/VBR Rootkit)**
Infects the **Master Boot Record** or **Volume Boot Record** so it loads *before* the OS, evading OS-level security. Example: **Stuxnet** (2010), **Mebromi** (BIOS-infecting). Defense: **Secure Boot** (UEFI), trusted boot chain.

**4. Firmware Rootkit**
Embeds in hardware firmware (BIOS, UEFI, network card, hard drive controller). Example: **LoJax** (UEFI), **Equation Group HDD firmware**. Defense: firmware integrity verification, vendor patches.

**5. Memory Rootkit**
Loads only in RAM; no disk footprint. Wiped on reboot but extremely difficult to detect while running. Detection: **memory forensics** using **Volatility Framework**.

**6. Hypervisor Rootkit**
Theoretically, the rootkit runs as a thin hypervisor beneath the OS, intercepting all hardware operations. Example: **Blue Pill** (research PoC). Detection: integrity checks of virtualization extensions (VT-x, AMD-V).

**Detection and Defense (2.5 marks):**

- **Integrity Checking:** Hash-based file integrity monitoring (Tripwire, AIDE, OSSEC).
- **Behavioral Analysis:** EDR solutions that detect SSDT/IDT hooking, IRP hooking, and DKOM.
- **Boot Integrity:** UEFI **Secure Boot** to prevent unsigned bootkits.
- **Kernel Protection:** **Kernel Patch Protection (PatchGuard)** on 64-bit Windows.
- **Code Signing:** **Driver Signature Enforcement** prevents unsigned kernel drivers.
- **Memory Forensics:** **Volatility**, **Rekall** for offline RAM analysis.
- **Network Anomaly Detection:** C2 traffic, beaconing patterns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Distribution:** Definition (1) + 6 types with examples (3.5) + 6 defenses (2.5).

---

**Q. (b) What is meant by "Covering Tracks"? Explain the various techniques used by attackers to cover their tracks after a system compromise.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Covering Tracks** is the final stage of system hacking in which the attacker removes or obscures all evidence of intrusion to avoid detection, attribution, and legal prosecution. It includes disabling auditing, clearing logs, manipulating timestamps, and erasing artifacts.

**Techniques (6 marks — 1.2 marks each):**

**1. Disable Auditing (Windows)**
The attacker disables security auditing so that subsequent malicious actions are not logged.
- Command: `auditpol /set /category:"Logon/Logoff" /success:disable /failure:disable`
- Registry: `HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\PolAdtEv` (requires SYSTEM).
- On Linux: stop `auditd` service: `service auditd stop`.

**2. Clearing Event Logs**
- **Windows:** Clear Security, System, and Application logs via Event Viewer, `wevtutil cl Security`, or tools like **WinZapper**, **Metasploit's `clearev`**.
- **Linux/Unix:** Truncate log files: `> /var/log/syslog`, `> /var/log/auth.log`, or use `logrotate` to force rotation.
- Bash history: `history -c` then `ln -sf /dev/null ~/.bash_history` to prevent future logging.

**3. Manipulating Timestamps (Timestomping)**
Modify the MACE (Modified, Accessed, Changed/Created, Birth) timestamps of files to match legitimate files on the system, defeating manual forensic analysis.
- Windows: **timestomp** (Metasploit).
- Linux: `touch -t YYYYMMDDhhmm filename`.

**4. Hiding Network Traces**
- Use **reverse shells** (outbound connections) instead of inbound (avoid triggering firewall alerts on attacker side).
- **Tunneling:** DNS tunneling (**iodine**, **dnscat2**), ICMP tunneling (**icmpsh**, **ptunnel**), HTTPS C2.
- Delete uploaded tools after use: `rm mimikatz.exe`.

**5. Rootkit Installation**
A persistent rootkit conceals processes, files, registry entries, and network connections from the OS itself, making the intruder's presence invisible to the admin. (Types discussed in Q5.A.)

**6. Disabling Security Software**
Stop or disable antivirus, EDR agents, or host-based firewalls (e.g., `net stop WinDefend`, `sc config WinDefend start= disabled`). Modern EDRs detect and alert on such actions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Distribution:** Definition (1) + 6 techniques with tools and commands (6 marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe covering tracks generically ("delete logs") without specifying **tools**, **commands**, or **file paths**. Examiners demand specific tool names (`clearev`, `auditpol`, `timestomp`) and paths (`/var/log/syslog`, `%SystemRoot%\System32\winevt\Logs\`). Generic answers are capped at 50% of marks for this question.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's High-Frequency Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ⭐ **System Hacking = 5 stages:** Password Cracking → Privilege Escalation → Execute Apps → Hide Files → Cover Tracks. **Always list in this order.**

- ⭐ **Windows SAM location:** `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM` (encrypted with SYSKEY).
- ⭐ **Linux password file:** `/etc/shadow` (hashed), `/etc/passwd` (user metadata).
- ⭐ **LM Hash:** DES-based, splits into two 7-byte chunks, case-insensitive, **must be disabled** via Group Policy.
- ⭐ **NTLM Hash:** MD4-based, 32 hex chars, still vulnerable to Pass-the-Hash.
- ⭐ **Tools to remember:** **John the Ripper**, **Hashcat**, **Hydra**, **L0phtCrack**, **Mimikatz**, **Metasploit**, **pwdump7**, **Nmap**, **Wireshark**, **Cain & Abel**, **Steghide**, **WinZapper**, **timestomp**, **Volatility**.

- ⭐ **Privilege Escalation Types:** Vertical (different levels) vs. Horizontal (same level). Six methods: password cracking, vulnerability exploit, misconfig, DLL hijack, token impersonation, keyloggers.

- ⭐ **Privilege Hierarchy Windows:** Guest < User < Power User < Admin < SYSTEM.
- ⭐ **Privilege Hierarchy Linux:** UID 0 (root) > System (1-999) > Users (≥1000).
- ⭐ **Token Impersonation Tools:** Incognito (Metasploit), Mimikatz `token::steal`/`token::elevate`.

- ⭐ **Hiding Files Techniques:** ADS (`type x > y:x`), steganography (Steghide), NTFS `+h +s` attributes, registry `Run` keys, rootkits.
- ⭐ **ADS Detection:** `dir /R` (Windows), `Get-Item .\* -Stream *` (PowerShell).

- ⭐ **Rootkit Rings:** Ring 3 (user), Ring 0 (kernel), Ring -1 (hypervisor), Ring -2 (SMM), Ring -3 (firmware/UEFI).
- ⭐ **Famous Rootkits:** Stuxnet (bootkit), ZeroAccess (kernel), LoJax (UEFI), Blue Pill (hypervisor PoC).

- ⭐ **Covering Tracks Tools:** `clearev`, `auditpol`, `timestomp`, `history -c`, `> /var/log/syslog`, `WinZapper`.
- ⭐ **Tunneling Protocols:** DNS (iodine, dnscat2), ICMP (icmpsh, ptunnel), HTTPS reverse shells.

- ⭐ **Keyspace Formula:** $K = C^n$. **Cracking Time:** $T = K / R$. **Password Entropy:** $E = n \log_2 C$.
- ⭐ **Defense Triad:** **Centralized Logging (SIEM)** + **File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)** + **Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR)**.

- ⭐ **KTU Magic Number:** Brute-force an 8-char alphanumeric password (~52 bits entropy) cracks in **days on modern GPU**. Length ≥ 12 is the modern minimum.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Malware** (short for **Malicious Software**) is any program, code, script, active content, or software intentionally designed to cause damage to computer systems, networks, servers, or users, gain unauthorized access, exfiltrate sensitive data, or disrupt normal operations without the informed consent of the system owner.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEOT741)** syllabus framing, malware threats constitute the broadest class of cyber-attack vectors that leverage hostile code to compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA) triad** of information systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad Impact of Malware:**
> - **Confidentiality** → breached by Spyware, Keyloggers, Trojans
> - **Integrity** → breached by Viruses, Worms, Rootkits
> - **Availability** → breached by Ransomware, Logic Bombs, Botnets (DDoS)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **contagious biological virus** spreading in a crowded city:
- The **virus** (malware) hides inside a **sneeze droplet** (infected file/email attachment).
- An **unaware healthy person** (the victim user) inhales it (opens the file).
- The virus then **uses the host's cells** (system resources, CPU, RAM) to replicate itself and infect more people.
- Sometimes it causes a **mild cold** (adware pop-ups), sometimes **organ failure** (data wipe/ransomware encryption), and sometimes lies **dormant for months** (logic bomb, fileless malware) before striking.

This is exactly how malware behaves in cyberspace — it propagates through vectors (email, USB, network), infects hosts, replicates, and activates payloads based on triggers.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition (in one line) |
|---|---|
| **Payload** | The destructive portion of malware that executes the attacker's objective. |
| **Vector** | The pathway used by malware to reach the victim (email, web, USB). |
| **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)** | Forensic artifact (file hash, IP, domain) confirming a malware infection. |
| **Zero-Day** | A vulnerability unknown to the vendor, hence no patch exists. |
| **Dropper** | A small malware whose only job is to download/install the real payload. |
| **C2 Server** | Command & Control server used by attackers to remotely command infected hosts. |
| **Polymorphic Code** | Malware that changes its signature each time it replicates to evade AV. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Malware Classification Metric (Symantec Internet Security Threat Report taxonomy):** Malware is classified primarily on **(a) Propagation Mechanism** (how it spreads) and **(b) Payload Action** (what it does after activation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Taxonomy of Malware

Malware is broadly bifurcated based on **how it propagates** and **how it acts**. Below is the canonical KTU-aligned breakdown.

### 2.1.1 Propagation-Based Classification

1. **Virus** — Requires **user interaction** (opening a file/executing a program) to spread. Attaches itself to a *host file* (.exe, .doc, .vbs).
2. **Worm** — **Self-replicating**; spreads autonomously over networks without any human trigger. Exploits OS/protocol vulnerabilities.
3. **Trojan Horse** — Disguised as **legitimate/benign software** but carries a hidden malicious payload. Does *not* self-replicate.
4. **Fileless Malware** — Operates entirely in **volatile memory (RAM)**; writes nothing to disk, making it invisible to traditional signature-based AV.

### 2.1.2 Action-Based Classification

1. **Ransomware** — Encrypts user files and demands cryptocurrency ransom.
2. **Spyware** — Silently monitors user activity and exfiltrates data.
3. **Adware** — Displays unwanted advertisements; sometimes a gateway to spyware.
4. **Rootkit** — Gains **privileged (root/admin) access** and hides itself deep in the OS kernel.
5. **Keylogger** — Records every keystroke to steal credentials.
6. **Botnet Agent (Zombie)** — Turns the host into a remotely controlled "bot" for DDoS/spam.
7. **Logic Bomb** — Malicious code that **triggers on a specific event** (date, file deletion, user login).
8. **Backdoor** — Creates a hidden remote entry point, bypassing normal authentication.
9. **RAT (Remote Access Trojan)** — Provides the attacker full GUI/CLI control of the victim machine.

## 2.2 Operational Anatomy of a Malware Attack

Every malware attack progresses through **five canonical phases**:

| Phase | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Infiltration** | Entry via email, web, USB, supply chain | Phishing attachment |
| **2. Persistence** | Achieves foothold via registry keys, scheduled tasks | Run-key modification |
| **3. Privilege Escalation** | Elevates to admin/root | Exploiting CVE-2021-34527 (PrintNightmare) |
| **4. Lateral Movement** | Spreads across the network | Pass-the-Hash, SMB exploitation |
| **5. Exfiltration / Payload** | Data theft, encryption, DDoS | Cobalt Strike beacon, file encryption |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Reference Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" below are **analytical models** and **risk equations** used to quantify malware impact — these are high-yield for KTU numerical/analytical questions.

### 2.3.1 Core Detection & Risk Equations

| # | Equation | Meaning of Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(\text{Infection}) = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$ | $p$ = per-vector attack probability, $n$ = number of attack vectors | Probability of at least one infection succeeding |
| 2 | $R = T \times V \times C$ | $R$ = Risk, $T$ = Threat, $V$ = Vulnerability, $C$ = Consequence | Classical NIST risk assessment model |
| 3 | $D(t) = D_0 \cdot e^{\lambda t}$ | $D_0$ = initial damage, $\lambda$ = propagation rate, $t$ = time | Worm damage growth (exponential) |
| 4 | $T_{\text{detect}} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | $\mu$ = detection rate (scans/sec) | Mean time to detect malware by AV |
| 5 | $\text{Signature Match} = H(f) \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{known}}$ | $H$ = cryptographic hash (SHA-256/MD5) | AV signature-based detection logic |
| 6 | $E[\text{Loss}] = P(\text{breach}) \times \text{ALE}$ | $ALE$ = Annual Loss Expectancy | Expected annual malware loss |
| 7 | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | $SLE$ = Single Loss Expectancy, $ARO$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Risk quantification |

### 2.3.2 Propagation Thresholds (Epidemiological Model)

The **simple epidemic model** for worm spread inside a network of $N$ nodes is:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot (N - I(t))$$

Where:
- $I(t)$ = number of infected nodes at time $t$
- $N$ = total vulnerable nodes
- $\beta$ = infection rate (pair-wise contact rate × success probability)
- $(N - I(t))$ = number of susceptible (not yet infected) nodes

The **epidemic threshold** is:

$$\beta_{c} = \frac{1}{N}$$

If $\beta < \beta_{c}$, the worm **dies out**. If $\beta > \beta_{c}$, it becomes a **network-wide epidemic** (this is the principle behind Slammer, Blaster, and WannaCry).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step: Worm Propagation Derivation (KTU-Favorite Analytical Problem)

### Problem Context
A corporate LAN has $N = 500$ vulnerable unpatched Windows XP hosts. A worm begins infecting hosts. The pair-wise scan rate is $\eta = 5$ scans/sec and the probability that a scanned host is successfully compromised is $s = 0.1$. Find:
(a) The infection rate $\beta$.
(b) The epidemic threshold $\beta_c$.
(c) Whether an outbreak will occur.
(d) The number of infected hosts after $t = 60$ seconds (use the analytical solution, assume $I(0)=1$).

---

### Step 1 — Express the infection rate $\beta$

By definition:

$$\beta = \eta \cdot s$$

Substituting values:

$$\beta = 5 \cdot 0.1 = 0.5 \;\; \text{(scans/sec per infected host)}$$

**Logic:** Each infected host scans $\eta$ other hosts per second, and each scan succeeds with probability $s$. Thus the effective infection rate is the product.

---

### Step 2 — Compute the epidemic threshold $\beta_c$

Using the threshold formula:

$$\beta_{c} = \frac{1}{N} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002$$

**Logic:** If $\beta$ exceeds this value, the worm can sustain exponential growth.

---

### Step 3 — Outbreak Decision

Compare:

$$\beta = 0.5 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \beta_{c} = 0.002$$

Since:

$$\beta \gg \beta_{c}$$

An **outbreak is guaranteed** — this is precisely why Slammer and WannaCry were so devastating.

---

### Step 4 — Solve the Epidemic ODE

The differential equation is:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I)$$

This is a **logistic differential equation**. Standard form:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = r \cdot I \left(1 - \frac{I}{K}\right)$$

where $r = \beta N$ and $K = N$.

The analytical solution with $I(0) = I_0$ is:

$$I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + \left(\dfrac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right) e^{-\beta N t}}$$

**Derivation of the solution (exhaustive, step-by-step):**

**Step 4.1** — Separate variables:

$$\frac{dI}{I(N - I)} = \beta \, dt$$

**Step 4.2** — Use partial fractions:

$$\frac{1}{I(N - I)} = \frac{1}{N}\left(\frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I}\right)$$

**Step 4.3** — Integrate both sides:

$$\int \frac{1}{N}\left(\frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I}\right) dI = \int \beta \, dt$$

$$\frac{1}{N}\bigl[\ln I - \ln(N - I)\bigr] = \beta t + C$$

**Step 4.4** — Simplify:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{N - I}\right) = N \beta t + C'$$

**Step 4.5** — Exponentiate:

$$\frac{I}{N - I} = A \cdot e^{N \beta t}$$

where $A = e^{C'}$.

**Step 4.6** — Apply initial condition $I(0) = I_0$:

$$A = \frac{I_0}{N - I_0}$$

**Step 4.7** — Solve for $I(t)$:

$$\frac{I}{N - I} = \frac{I_0}{N - I_0} \cdot e^{N \beta t}$$

$$I = (N - I) \cdot \frac{I_0}{N - I_0} \cdot e^{N \beta t}$$

$$I \left[1 + \frac{I_0}{N - I_0} e^{N \beta t}\right] = N \cdot \frac{I_0}{N - I_0} \cdot e^{N \beta t}$$

$$\boxed{I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + \left(\dfrac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right) e^{-N \beta t}}}$$

---

### Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation

Substitute: $N = 500$, $I_0 = 1$, $\beta = 0.5$, $t = 60$ s.

**Compute $N \beta t$:**

$$N \beta t = 500 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 60 = 15000$$

**Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{N - I_0}{I_0} = \frac{500 - 1}{1} = 499$$

**Compute the exponential term:**

$$e^{-15000} \approx 0 \;\; (\text{underflows to } \sim 0)$$

**Final answer:**

$$I(60) \approx \frac{500}{1 + 499 \cdot 0} = \frac{500}{1} = 500$$

**Interpretation:** Within 60 seconds, the worm infects **all 500 hosts** — confirming the catastrophic exponential nature of worm propagation. This is why **patching latency** is the single most important defensive metric in real-world cybersecurity.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Malware Type vs. CIA Triad

| Malware Type | Propagation | Primary CIA Violation | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Virus | File infection (user-triggered) | Integrity, Availability | **ILOVEYOU** (2000) — $15B damage |
| Worm | Network (autonomous) | Availability, Integrity | **Code Red** (2001), **WannaCry** (2017) |
| Trojan | Social engineering | Confidentiality | **Zeus** (banking trojan) |
| Ransomware | Phishing / RDP brute-force | Availability | **WannaCry**, **NotPetya**, **LockBit** |
| Spyware | Bundled software | Confidentiality | **DarkHotel**, **Pegasus** |
| Rootkit | Exploit / supply chain | Integrity, Confidentiality | **Sony BMG** (2005), **Stuxnet** (2010) |
| Keylogger | Trojan / phishing | Confidentiality | Agent Tesla, Hawkeye |
| Logic Bomb | Insider / dormant code | Integrity, Availability | **Siemens Logic Bomb** (disgruntled contractor) |
| Botnet | Worm + C2 | Availability | **Mirai** (Dyn DNS takedown, 2016) |
| Fileless | PowerShell / WMI | All three | **Poweliks**, **Cobalt Strike Beacon** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
