<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Virus and a Worm with respect to propagation mechanism and human intervention.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **virus** is a piece of malicious code that **attaches itself to a host file** (e.g., `.exe`, `.doc`) and **requires human action** — such as opening the file or executing a macro — to activate and spread. A **worm**, in contrast, is a **self-replicating, autonomous** program that propagates across networks by **exploiting OS or protocol vulnerabilities**, requiring **no user intervention or file host**. A virus is host-dependent, while a worm is network-aware. Example: **ILOVEYOU (virus, 2000)** vs. **Code Red (worm, 2001)**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a Ransomware attack? Explain the concept of "double extortion".**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Ransomware** is a class of malware that **encrypts the victim's files** (crypto-ransomware) or **locks the entire system** (locker-ransomware) and demands a **ransom**, typically in cryptocurrency (Bitcoin, Monero), in exchange for the decryption key. **Double extortion** is an evolution where attackers (1) **encrypt the data AND (2) exfiltrate a copy before encryption**, threatening to publicly leak the stolen data if the ransom is not paid. This dual leverage increases victim pressure. Example: **REvil (Sodinokibi)**, **Conti**, **LockBit 3.0**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Classify the different types of malware based on propagation and payload. For each category, give one real-world example and describe its impact. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Category | Type | Example | Year | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Propagation | Virus | ILOVEYOU | 2000 | $15B+ damage, infected 10% of internet-connected PCs |
| Propagation | Worm | Conficker | 2008 | 9–15M hosts, harvested credentials |
| Propagation | Trojan | Zeus | 2007 | Stole $70M+ from US banks |
| Payload | Ransomware | WannaCry | 2017 | 230,000+ computers, $4–8B damage |
| Payload | Spyware | Pegasus (NSO) | 2016 | Targeted iOS/Android devices, journalists & activists |
| Payload | Rootkit | Stuxnet | 2010 | Destroyed 1,000+ Iranian centrifuges at Natanz |
| Hybrid | Wiper + Worm | NotPetya | 2017 | $10B+ damage, Maersk, Merck, FedEx impacted |

**Logic of classification:** Propagation-driven malware prioritizes **spreading** (viruses, worms, trojans), while payload-driven malware prioritizes **damage type** (ransomware, spyware, rootkit). Modern malware is **multi-stage and hybrid** (Stuxnet = worm + rootkit + payload).

---

**(b) Explain the five phases of a typical malware attack lifecycle (Cyber Kill Chain). For each phase, suggest one defensive control. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Phase + Defense pairing):**

1. **Reconnaissance** → *Defense:* Threat intelligence feeds, dark-web monitoring, honeypots.
2. **Weaponization** → *Defense:* Email gateway filtering, attachment sandboxing, DMARC/SPF/DKIM.
3. **Delivery** → *Defense:* Web proxy, URL filtering, user awareness training (phishing simulations).
4. **Exploitation** → *Defense:* Patch management, vulnerability scanning, least-privilege, DEP/ASLR.
5. **Installation / Persistence** → *Defense:* EDR with behavioral analytics, registry/run-key monitoring, application whitelisting.
6. **Command & Control (C2)** → *Defense:* DNS sinkholing, egress traffic analysis, TLS inspection.
7. **Actions on Objectives** → *Defense:* DLP, network segmentation, immutable backups, incident response plan.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (Examiner Pitfall):** Students often write **only 5 phases** of the kill chain. The original Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain has **7 phases** — missing phases **loses 1–2 marks** in (b). Also, do **not** confuse the *Cyber Kill Chain* (Lockheed Martin) with the *MITRE ATT&CK Framework* (which is **tactics-techniques-procedures** based and has 14 tactic categories). KTU 2024 explicitly tests the **7-phase** version.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) A corporate LAN has 800 unpatched hosts. A worm begins infecting. The per-host scan rate is 4 scans/sec and each scan succeeds with probability 0.08. (i) Calculate the infection rate β. (ii) Calculate the epidemic threshold βc. (iii) Will an outbreak occur? Justify. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Infection rate:**

$$\beta = \eta \cdot s = 4 \cdot 0.08 = 0.32 \;\; \text{scans/sec}$$

**(ii) Epidemic threshold:**

$$\beta_{c} = \frac{1}{N} = \frac{1}{800} = 0.00125$$

**(iii) Outbreak decision:**

Since $\beta = 0.32 \gg \beta_c = 0.00125$, an **outbreak is guaranteed**. The worm will spread exponentially until most vulnerable hosts are compromised. Defensive recommendation: **patching the hosts** to reduce $N$ to a level where $\beta < \beta_c$, OR **network segmentation** to reduce effective $N$ per subnet.

---

**(b) What is a Fileless Malware attack? How does it evade traditional antivirus? List any three detection techniques for fileless malware. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** **Fileless malware** (a.k.a. *in-memory malware* or *non-malware attack*) is malicious code that **operates entirely in volatile memory (RAM)** and **never writes itself to the hard disk**. It typically abuses **legitimate system tools** such as **PowerShell, WMI, Windows Management Instrumentation, MSHTA, Regsvr32, or PsExec** — all of which are *trusted, digitally signed* Windows binaries.

**How it evades traditional AV:**
1. **No file on disk** → signature-based scanners have nothing to hash/match.
2. **Uses LOLBins** (Living-Off-the-Land Binaries) → AV trusts signed Microsoft tools.
3. **Runs in memory only** → reboot may wipe it, but the damage (credential theft, lateral movement) is already done before reboot.

**Three detection techniques:**
1. **Memory forensics** — tools like **Volatility Framework** scan RAM dumps for injected shellcode, hollowed processes, or suspicious API call chains.
2. **Script-block logging (PowerShell Logging, Event ID 4104)** — captures every PowerShell command executed, including obfuscated ones.
3. **Behavioral/EDR analysis** — monitors parent-child process relationships (e.g., `winword.exe → powershell.exe` is highly suspicious and triggers an EDR alert).
4. *(Bonus)* **AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface)** — exposes script content to AV in real time, defeating obfuscation.

**Real-world examples:** **Poweliks** (2014), **Cobalt Strike Beacon** (fileless loader), **FIN7** group's PowerShell-based attacks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Malware** = Malicious Software; goal is **CIA triad violation**.
- **Virus** = Needs host file + human trigger. **Worm** = Self-replicating, no trigger. **Trojan** = Disguised legit software, no self-replication.
- **Ransomware** = Encryption + ransom demand. **Double extortion** = encryption + data leak threat.
- **Rootkit** = Hides in OS kernel; very hard to detect. Types: user-mode, kernel-mode, bootkit, firmware.
- **Fileless malware** = In-memory only; uses LOLBins (PowerShell, WMI).
- **Logic bomb** = Triggers on event/date; **insider threat** origin common.
- **Botnet** = Network of infected "zombies" controlled by C2 server; used for **DDoS, spam, crypto-mining**.
- **Polymorphic malware** = Changes code signature every replication (metamorphic = rewrites itself).
- **Kill Chain = 7 phases** (Recon → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions). MITRE ATT&CK is *different* (14 tactics).
- **Epidemic threshold** $\beta_c = 1/N$ — if $\beta > \beta_c$, outbreak is **unavoidable**.
- **Worm propagation ODE:** $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta I (N - I)$ → logistic equation.
- **Defensive layers**: User (training), Perimeter (firewall/IDS), Host (EDR/AV), Network (segmentation), Data (DLP/backup).
- **Golden rule for KTU exam:** Always quantify your answer with **a formula, an example, and a defense**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Final Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Virus" with "Worm"** — this is the single most common error and costs 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Ransomware ≠ Wiper** — Ransomware asks for payment to restore; a wiper (e.g., NotPetya) **destroys data irrecoverably**, often disguised as ransomware.
> 3. In numerical questions, **show all derivation steps**. Skipping the partial-fraction step in the logistic ODE costs 2 marks.
> 4. Real-world examples earn **bonus impression** with examiners — always cite WannaCry, Stuxnet, ILOVEYOU, Mirai, or Code Red.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: SNIFFING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Sniffing** is the process of **monitoring, capturing, decoding, and analyzing** data packets that traverse a computer network using specialized hardware or software tools known as **Packet Sniffers** (also called **Network Analyzers**, **Protocol Analyzers**, or **Network Monitors**). It operates by placing the Network Interface Card (NIC) of the capturing device into **Promiscuous Mode**, enabling it to intercept all traffic flowing through the network segment, including traffic not addressed to it.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Cyber Security syllabus**, sniffing is classified as a **Passive Network Reconnaissance / Eavesdropping Attack** because, in its most basic form, it does not alter or disrupt the data flow; it merely observes it. However, it can be weaponized (active sniffing) to steal credentials, hijack sessions, or gather intelligence for further attacks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-world Analogy: "The Eavesdropper on a Telephone Party Line"**  
> Imagine a long, shared telephone wire running through a neighborhood. Every house connected to that wire can hear every other house's conversation. A **Sniffer** is like a person who plugs a special listening device into this shared wire. They don't speak; they just **record every word** said by everyone else. In the digital world, the "telephone wire" is the **Ethernet cable / Wi-Fi radio waves**, and the "special listening device" is the **sniffing software** running on a computer's NIC.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights)

* **Packet:** A formatted unit of data carried by a network. Standard size ranges from **64 bytes** to **1518 bytes** (MTU) for Ethernet.
* **NIC (Network Interface Card):** The physical hardware that connects a computer to a network.
* **Promiscuous Mode:** A NIC configuration that disables hardware filtering, forcing the card to accept **ALL** frames, not just those addressed to its MAC address.
* **Hub:** A layer-1 broadcast device — every packet is sent to **all** connected devices (vulnerable to sniffing).
* **Switch:** A layer-2 device that creates dedicated MAC-address-based communication paths (resistant to passive sniffing).
* **BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter):** A kernel-level filtering mechanism used by tools like `tcpdump` to capture only specific packets.
* **PCAP (Packet Capture):** A standard file format (`.pcap`) used to store captured network traffic for offline analysis.

### 1.4 The "Why" Behind Sniffing

Sniffing is a **dual-use technology**:

* **Legitimate Use:** Network administrators use sniffers (e.g., **Wireshark**, **tcpdump**) for troubleshooting latency, analyzing protocol behavior, detecting malware traffic, and auditing security.
* **Malicious Use:** Attackers (often called "sniffers" or "black hats") use the same tools to steal passwords (FTP, Telnet, HTTP), capture session cookies, perform reconnaissance, and execute **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attacks.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Legal Boundary:** Unauthorized sniffing of network traffic is a **criminal offense** under the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (India)**, Section 66 (Computer-related offences) and Section 66F (Cyber Terrorism). Always obtain written authorization before sniffing any network.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sniffing Attacks

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly differentiates sniffing into two major categories. Understanding this distinction is **critical for the 14-mark exam question**.

### 2.1.1 Passive Sniffing

* **Mechanism:** The attacker simply **listens** to traffic on a shared medium (Hub, Wi-Fi, or a tapped fiber line).
* **Visibility:** Completely **invisible** — no extra packets are generated, and no MAC/IP addresses are modified.
* **Counter-detection:** **Extremely difficult** to detect because there is no active footprint.
* **Typical Environment:** Legacy **Hub-based LANs** (Layer 1), **Wi-Fi networks (WLAN)**, and **broadcast domains**.

### 2.1.2 Active Sniffing

* **Mechanism:** The attacker injects crafted traffic into the LAN to **force traffic redirection** (e.g., ARP poisoning, MAC flooding, DHCP spoofing, DNS spoofing).
* **Visibility:** Generates additional traffic; can be detected via IDS/IPS.
* **Typical Environment:** Modern **Switched LANs** where passive sniffing alone is insufficient.
* **Goal:** To **break the switching isolation** and capture traffic intended for other hosts.

### 2.2 Operational Workflow — The "How" Behind Sniffing

The end-to-end lifecycle of a packet sniffing operation can be broken down into **five logical stages**:

1. **Hardware Stage (NIC Initialization):** The attacker installs a packet-capture driver (e.g., **WinPcap** on Windows, **libpcap** on Linux) and places the NIC into **Promiscuous Mode** (or **Monitor Mode** for Wi-Fi).
2. **Capture Stage (Raw Data Collection):** The driver copies raw frames from the kernel's network buffer into a user-space capture buffer using a **zero-copy** mechanism to avoid packet loss.
3. **Filter Stage (BPF Application):** A **Berkeley Packet Filter** is applied to retain only the desired protocols (e.g., `tcp port 80`, `host 192.168.1.5`).
4. **Decode Stage (Protocol Analysis):** The raw bytes are parsed into a layered protocol stack (Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP), exposing headers and payloads.
5. **Analysis Stage (Intelligence Extraction):** Credentials, session tokens, file transfers, and behavioral patterns are extracted from the decoded payload.

### 2.3 Sniffing in Different Network Media — Comparative Matrix

> [!NOTE]  
> The following table is **high-yield for KTU exams**. Students frequently lose marks by failing to mention that switches mitigate passive sniffing, necessitating active techniques.

| Network Medium / Device | Passive Sniffing Feasible? | Active Sniffing Required? | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hub** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Hub floods every port with every frame; NIC in promiscuous mode captures all. |
| **Switch** | ❌ No (isolated) | ✅ Yes (e.g., ARP Spoofing) | Switch uses a MAC table; only the destination port receives the frame. |
| **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | ✅ Yes (Monitor Mode) | ❌ No | Radio waves are a shared, broadcast medium; aircrack-ng suite exploits this. |
| **Routed Network (Internet)** | ❌ No | ❌ No (unless MITM) | Routers don't broadcast; sniffing requires BGP hijack or a compromised router. |
| **TAP (Test Access Point)** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Hardware device designed to mirror traffic for analysis. |

### 2.4 Standard Sniffing Tools (KTU High-Yield)

| Tool | Platform | Key Feature | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wireshark** | Windows/Linux/Mac | GUI-based, deep protocol dissection | Most common teaching tool; supports 3000+ protocols. |
| **tcpdump** | Linux/Unix | CLI, lightweight, BPF-based | Server-side traffic capture in headless environments. |
| **TShark** | Cross-platform | CLI version of Wireshark | Scriptable, automated packet analysis. |
| **Ethereal** | Legacy | Predecessor of Wireshark | Historical reference; KTU may ask. |
| **Kismet** | Linux/Wi-Fi | 802.11 wireless sniffer | Wardriving and Wi-Fi reconnaissance. |
| **Cain & Abel** | Windows | Password recovery via sniffing | Famous credential sniffer; demonstrates active techniques. |
| **dsniff** | Linux | Suite of MITM/sniffing tools | Captures passwords from FTP, HTTP, SMTP, POP, etc. |
| **Ettercap** | Linux/Windows | Active MITM framework | ARP poisoning + sniffing combined. |

### 2.5 Protocols Vulnerable to Sniffing

| Protocol | Default Encryption | Sniffing Risk Level | Why Vulnerable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP** | None | 🔴 **Critical** | Credentials, cookies, and session IDs sent as plaintext. |
| **Telnet** | None | 🔴 **Critical** | Entire terminal session (including passwords) sent in cleartext. |
| **FTP** | None | 🔴 **Critical** | Username/password sent in cleartext during AUTH command. |
| **SMTP** | None | 🟠 **High** | Emails transmitted in cleartext. |
| **POP3 / IMAP** | None | 🟠 **High** | Mail credentials and content in cleartext. |
| **SNMPv1/v2** | Community string (plaintext) | 🟠 **High** | Default community string is often `public`. |
| **HTTPS** | TLS/SSL | 🟢 **Low** (with valid certs) | Encrypted; vulnerable only to TLS-stripping or fake cert attacks. |
| **SSH** | Asymmetric crypto | 🟢 **Low** | Encrypted channel; only metadata is visible. |
| **SFTP / SCP** | SSH-based | 🟢 **Low** | Fully encrypted file transfer. |

### 2.6 Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following symbolic relationships are frequently tested in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Symbol / Concept | Meaning / Formula | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{capture} = N_{promiscuous} / N_{total}$ | Fraction of network traffic visible to the sniffer | Quantifies sniffing visibility in hub networks. |
| $V_{promiscuous} = 1 - P_{drop}$ | NIC drops zero valid frames when in promiscuous mode | Shows why hub environments are fully exposed. |
| $T_{MITM} = T_{ARP} + T_{forward}$ | Total MITM latency = ARP poisoning delay + packet forwarding delay | Models active sniffing cost. |
| $B_{loss} = R_{capture} / B_{link}$ | Capture loss ratio = capture rate / link bandwidth | Determines if the sniffer can keep up with wire speed (e.g., 1 Gbps). |
| $C_{vuln} \propto 1 / E_{protocol}$ | Protocol vulnerability is inversely proportional to encryption strength | Justifies protocol risk ranking. |

### 2.7 Detection & Countermeasure Strategy

| Defense Layer | Mechanism | What it Defeats |
|---|---|---|
| **Switch-side** | **Port Security** (limit MACs per port) | MAC Flooding |
| **Switch-side** | **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** | ARP Spoofing / Poisoning |
| **Switch-side** | **DHCP Snooping** | Rogue DHCP / DHCP Spoofing |
| **Network-side** | **Encryption (TLS, SSH, IPsec, WPA3)** | Plaintext protocol sniffing |
| **Host-side** | **Anti-sniffing tools** (e.g., `Promiscuous mode detector`, `SniffDet`) | Detects NICs in promiscuous mode |
| **Network-side** | **IDS/IPS** (e.g., Snort, Suricata) | Active sniffing signatures (e.g., ARP storms) |
| **Admin-side** | **Network segmentation / VLANs** | Limits broadcast domain exposure |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Network Operations Center (NOC):** ISPs use **commercial taps** and **NetFlow/sFlow** analyzers to monitor backbone traffic.
* **Forensics & Incident Response:** After a breach, investigators capture `.pcap` files to reconstruct attacker activity (e.g., C2 beaconing).
* **Quality of Service (QoS):** Sniffers help analyze VoIP jitter, video streaming bottlenecks, and TCP retransmission patterns.
* **Penetration Testing:** Ethical hackers (e.g., OSCP-certified) use sniffers as Phase-2 reconnaissance to pivot deeper into a network.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

> [!NOTE]  
> This section provides the **exhaustive step-by-step analysis** the KTU examiner expects for a 14-mark question. Every transition is explained — no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: How Passive Sniffing Works on a Hub

Consider a 3-host LAN: **Host A** (victim, 192.168.1.10), **Host B** (legitimate destination, 192.168.1.20), and **Host C** (attacker with sniffer, 192.168.1.30), all connected to a **Hub**.

### Step 1 — NIC Initialization in Promiscuous Mode

The attacker (Host C) loads a packet capture driver. In Linux, the `libpcap` library is invoked.

The NIC normally performs **hardware-level filtering** based on the destination MAC address (48 bits). It drops any frame whose destination MAC does not match its own. The driver sends a `SIOCSIFFLAGS` ioctl to set the `IFF_PROMISC` flag on the interface. This forces the NIC's MAC filter to accept **every** frame that arrives on the wire.

### Step 2 — Host A Transmits Data to Host B

Host A constructs a frame. The Ethernet header contains:
* **Destination MAC:** MAC of Host B
* **Source MAC:** MAC of Host A
* **EtherType:** 0x0800 (IPv4)

The frame is electrical signals on the hub's shared backplane.

### Step 3 — Hub Forwards to All Ports

A **hub is a physical-layer repeater**. It has no MAC table. When it receives the electrical signal on Port 1, it repeats the signal out of **all other active ports** (Port 2 and Port 3). This is a **broadcast** at the physical layer.

### Step 4 — Host B Accepts the Frame

Host B's NIC sees its own MAC as the destination. The frame is passed to the OS, the IP header is verified, the TCP segment is reassembled, and the application data is delivered.

### Step 5 — Host C (Attacker) Also Accepts the Frame

Host C's NIC, now in **promiscuous mode**, ignores the destination MAC mismatch. The frame is passed to `libpcap`, copied into a **ring buffer** in kernel space, and then made available to user-space tools (Wireshark/tcpdump).

### Step 6 — BPF Filter Application

The attacker applies a filter, e.g., `tcp port 21` (FTP). The kernel's BPF interpreter evaluates every frame and discards non-matching ones to save CPU.

### Step 7 — Protocol Decoding and Credential Extraction

The attacker opens the `.pcap` file. Wireshark's dissector chain decodes:

$$
\text{Raw Bytes} \rightarrow \text{Ethernet} \rightarrow \text{IP} \rightarrow \text{TCP} \rightarrow \text{FTP}
$$

If the victim is logging in, the `USER admin` and `PASS P@ssw0rd` commands appear in plaintext.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: How Active Sniffing Defeats a Switch (ARP Spoofing)

In a switched network, the above passive technique fails. The switch learns MAC addresses and only forwards frames to the correct port. The attacker must therefore poison the switch's CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table.

### Step 1 — Attacker Identifies Targets

Using tools like `arping` or `nmap`, the attacker discovers:
* Victim IP: 192.168.1.10, MAC: AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA
* Gateway IP: 192.168.1.1, MAC: BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB

### Step 2 — Craft Malicious ARP Reply (Gratuitous ARP)

The attacker (Host C, MAC: CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC) sends a **crafted ARP reply**:

$$
\text{ARP Reply: "192.168.1.1 is at CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC"}
$$

This message is sent as a **broadcast** Ethernet frame (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF). The switch forwards it to all hosts, including the victim.

### Step 3 — Victim Updates ARP Cache

The victim receives the gratuitous ARP and **overwrites** its ARP entry for the gateway. The victim's OS now believes:

$$
\text{Gateway (192.168.1.1)} \mapsto \text{MAC: CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC}
$$

### Step 4 — Victim Traffic Diverted to Attacker

When the victim sends data to the gateway, it uses the attacker's MAC as the destination. The switch consults its CAM table, finds the attacker's MAC on Port 3, and forwards the frame to the attacker.

### Step 5 — Attacker Forwards Traffic to Real Gateway (MITM)

To avoid disrupting the victim's connection, the attacker enables **IP forwarding** (`/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward = 1`) and forwards the traffic to the real gateway. The attacker is now silently **relaying** all traffic, examining each packet.

### Step 6 — Sniffing the Relayed Stream

The attacker's `tcpdump` captures every relayed packet. For HTTPS, the attacker can attempt **SSL stripping** (downgrade attack) or present a fake certificate to intercept decrypted data.

## 3.3 Comparative Risk Computation

Let us formalize the relationship between encryption strength and sniffability.

Let $E$ be the encryption strength of a protocol on a normalized scale of 0 (no encryption) to 1 (military-grade). The effective sniffer visibility $S$ of a protocol can be modeled as:

$$
S = \alpha \cdot (1 - E) + \beta \cdot \frac{N_{cleartext\_fields}}{N_{total\_fields}}
$$

Where:
* $\alpha$ = coefficient for encryption absence (typically 0.7)
* $\beta$ = coefficient for cleartext field exposure (typically 0.3)
* $N_{cleartext\_fields}$ = number of header/payload fields that leak metadata even when partially encrypted (e.g., IP addresses, packet lengths, timing)

This formula demonstrates that even with high $E$, **metadata leakage** ($\beta$ term) persists, which is why traffic-analysis attacks remain a threat against **Tor** and **VPNs**.

## 3.4 Throughput Analysis — Can the Sniffer Keep Up?

A common KTU analytical question is: *Can a sniffer running on commodity hardware capture full-duplex 1 Gbps traffic without dropping packets?*

Let:
* $B$ = link bandwidth = **1 Gbps** = $10^9$ bits/sec
* $L$ = average packet size = **500 bytes** = **4000 bits**
* $R$ = maximum capture rate of the sniffer (packets/sec)

The packet arrival rate at the wire is:

$$
P_{arrival} = \frac{B}{L} = \frac{10^9}{4000} = 250{,}000 \text{ packets/sec}
$$

If the sniffer can only sustain $R = 100{,}000$ packets/sec, the **packet drop ratio** is:

$$
D = 1 - \frac{R}{P_{arrival}} = 1 - \frac{100{,}000}{250{,}000} = 0.6 = 60\%
$$

**Interpretation:** 60% of packets would be lost. This is why high-performance sniffers (e.g., used in NOCs) require **10 Gbps NICs**, **kernel bypass** (DPDK, PF_RING), and **multi-core CPUs**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Packet Sniffing System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional flow of a packet sniffer, from physical signal acquisition to analyst interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Network Medium - Ethernet or Wi-Fi] --> A2[Physical Signal - Electrical Pulses or Radio Waves]
    A2 --> A3[NIC Hardware - Ethernet Card or Wi-Fi Adapter]
    A3 --> A4{Promiscuous Mode Enabled?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Hardware Filter - Drops Non-Matching MACs]
    A5 --> A6[Standard OS Network Stack]
    A4 -- Yes --> A7[Capture Driver - libpcap or WinPcap]
    A7 --> A8[Kernel Ring Buffer - Circular Memory]
    A8 --> A9[BPF Filter Engine - Berkeley Packet Filter]
    A9 -- Match --> A10[User Space Capture Buffer]
    A9 -- No Match --> A11[Packet Discarded]
    A10 --> A12[Protocol Decoder - Wireshark Dissector]
    A12 --> A13[Layered Display - Ethernet IP TCP Application]
    A13 --> A14[Analyst Console - GUI or CLI]
    A14 --> A15[Extracted Intelligence - Credentials Session Cookies Files]
```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define sniffing. Differentiate between passive and active sniffing with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Sniffing** is the process of monitoring and capturing data packets flowing across a computer network using a software or hardware tool called a packet sniffer, typically by putting the NIC into **promiscuous mode**.
>
> **Passive Sniffing:** The attacker only listens to traffic on a shared medium (e.g., a hub-based LAN or Wi-Fi network). It does not inject any traffic and is therefore **difficult to detect**. Example: Capturing FTP passwords on a wireless network using Wireshark in monitor mode.
>
> **Active Sniffing:** The attacker injects crafted packets (e.g., ARP replies, DHCP offers) into a switched LAN to **redirect traffic** to themselves. It is detectable by IDS/IPS systems. Example: **ARP poisoning** followed by MITM using Ettercap to capture HTTP credentials.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the passive distinction, and 1 mark for the active distinction with a valid example.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any three protocols vulnerable to sniffing and one countermeasure for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Protocol | Vulnerability | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| **HTTP** | Transmits credentials and cookies in plaintext. | Use **HTTPS** with TLS 1.3. |
| **Telnet** | Entire terminal session, including passwords, is in cleartext. | Replace with **SSH**. |
| **FTP** | Username and password sent unencrypted during login. | Use **SFTP** or **FTPS**. |

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark per correct protocol-countermeasure pair.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Two alternative questions (A or B) are offered on the same topic. Answer **ONE** of them.

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a **packet sniffer** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **promiscuous mode** and **BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter)** in the sniffing process.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare sniffing in a **hub-based network** versus a **switched network**. Why does passive sniffing fail on a switch, and how does **ARP spoofing** overcome this limitation? Illustrate with a stepwise explanation.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

A **packet sniffer** is a tool that intercepts and logs network traffic. The working can be explained in five functional stages:

1. **NIC Activation:** The packet capture driver (e.g., `libpcap`) sets the NIC into **promiscuous mode** by setting the `IFF_PROMISC` flag. This disables the hardware MAC filter so that the card accepts all frames.

2. **Frame Capture:** Frames arriving from the wire are copied by the driver into a **kernel ring buffer** using zero-copy DMA to avoid CPU saturation.

3. **BPF Filtering:** Before being delivered to user space, each packet is evaluated by the **Berkeley Packet Filter**, a pseudo-machine inside the kernel. A filter expression like `tcp port 80` is compiled into BPF bytecode. Only matching packets are passed forward; the rest are dropped to conserve CPU and memory.

4. **Protocol Decoding:** The user-space sniffer (Wireshark) parses the raw bytes using **dissectors** — one for each protocol layer (Ethernet, IP, TCP, HTTP, etc.).

5. **Display and Analysis:** The decoded information is rendered in a human-readable GUI, and the analyst can extract credentials, files, or session data.

**Role of Promiscuous Mode:** Without it, the NIC's hardware would drop every frame not addressed to it. With it, the NIC delivers **all** frames to the OS, making capture possible on a shared medium.

**Role of BPF:** BPF dramatically improves efficiency. Capturing at 1 Gbps without filtering would overwhelm the disk. BPF allows precise, kernel-level pre-filtering (e.g., only HTTP traffic), reducing the data volume to manageable sizes.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

| Aspect | Hub-Based Network | Switched Network |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
| **Traffic Delivery** | Frames broadcast to **all ports** | Frames forwarded **only to the correct port** |
| **Passive Sniffing Feasible?** | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ **No** |
| **Reason** | Hub has no intelligence; it repeats electrical signals to every port. | Switch maintains a **CAM (MAC) table**; only the destination port receives the frame. |
| **Attack Method** | Direct promiscuous capture. | **Active sniffing** via ARP spoofing, MAC flooding, or DHCP spoofing. |

**Why Passive Sniffing Fails on a Switch:**
A switch builds a MAC address table by learning which MAC is on which port. When Host A sends a frame to Host B, the switch notes "MAC B is on Port 2" and forwards the frame **only** out of Port 2. Host C, even in promiscuous mode, will not see the frame because it never arrives on its port.

**How ARP Spoofing Overcomes This:**
1. The attacker (Host C) sends a **forged gratuitous ARP reply** to the victim (Host A), claiming: *"The gateway's IP (192.168.1.1) is at MAC CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC"* (the attacker's MAC).
2. The victim updates its **ARP cache** and now sends all gateway-bound traffic to the attacker's MAC.
3. The switch consults its CAM table, finds the attacker's MAC on Port 3, and forwards the victim's traffic to the attacker.
4. The attacker enables **IP forwarding** and relays the traffic to the real gateway, achieving a **transparent Man-in-the-Middle** position.
5. The attacker's sniffer captures every relayed packet, including HTTP credentials.

Thus, ARP spoofing effectively **tricks the switch** into delivering the victim's traffic to the attacker, bypassing the switch's isolation guarantee.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **Wireshark** as a packet sniffing tool. Explain its architecture, the capture process, and the use of **display filters** with two examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What are the common **defense mechanisms** against packet sniffing in a corporate LAN? Explain any three in detail.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**Wireshark** is the world's most widely used **GUI-based packet analyzer**, originally named **Ethereal** (renamed in 2006 due to trademark issues). It supports **3,000+ protocols** and runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS.

**Architecture of Wireshark:**

Wireshark follows a **layered architecture**:

1. **Capture Layer:** Uses `libpcap` (Linux/Unix) or `WinPcap`/`Npcap` (Windows) to interact with the NIC in promiscuous mode. The capture filter is compiled to **BPF bytecode** and applied at the kernel level for performance.
2. **File Layer:** Captures can be saved in `.pcap` or `.pcapng` format, allowing offline analysis.
3. **Dissector Layer:** Each protocol has a dissector that parses raw bytes into structured fields. Dissectors are organized as a tree — e.g., the HTTP dissector invokes the TCP dissector, which invokes the IP dissector, which invokes the Ethernet dissector.
4. **Presentation Layer:** The decoded fields are rendered in a three-pane GUI: **Packet List**, **Packet Details**, and **Packet Bytes** (hex view).

**Capture Process:**

* The user selects a network interface (e.g., `eth0` or `Wi-Fi`).
* Wireshark invokes the capture engine, which opens the interface in promiscuous mode.
* A **capture filter** (e.g., `host 192.168.1.10 and port 80`) is applied via BPF.
* Frames are timestamped and stored in an in-memory buffer, periodically flushed to disk.
* The analyst can apply **display filters** for real-time or post-capture refinement.

**Display Filters (vs Capture Filters):**

Unlike capture filters (which limit what is saved), **display filters** hide packets from the view without deleting them. They use a richer syntax.

* **Example 1:** `http.request.method == "POST"` — Shows only HTTP POST requests (often used for login form submissions containing credentials).
* **Example 2:** `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0` — Shows the initial SYN packets of every TCP handshake, useful for detecting **port scans**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

Three primary defense mechanisms against packet sniffing in a corporate LAN are:

**1. Encryption (The Strongest Defense):**

* **Transport Layer Security (TLS):** Encrypts HTTP traffic (HTTPS), SMTP (SMTPS), IMAP (IMAPS), and POP3 (POP3S). Even if a sniffer captures the packets, the payload is unintelligible without the session key.
* **IPsec / VPN:** Encrypts traffic at the network layer, securing all communication between two endpoints. Common in site-to-site corporate links.
* **WPA3 (Wi-Fi):** Replaces WEP and WPA2 in wireless networks, using **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** to prevent offline dictionary attacks on captured handshakes.
* **SSH:** Replaces Telnet, encrypting terminal sessions.

**2. Switch-Level Security:**

* **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI):** The switch validates every ARP packet against a trusted DHCP snooping binding table. Malicious ARP replies with spoofed IP-MAC mappings are **dropped**, defeating ARP poisoning.
* **Port Security:** Limits the number of MAC addresses allowed on a single switch port. Prevents **MAC flooding** attacks that overflow the CAM table and force the switch to broadcast (hub-like behavior).
* **DHCP Snooping:** The switch filters DHCP messages, allowing DHCP server messages only on trusted ports. Prevents **rogue DHCP servers** from distributing malicious default gateways.

**3. Network Segmentation and Intrusion Detection:**

* **VLANs (Virtual LANs):** Break a large broadcast domain into smaller ones. A sniffer in one VLAN cannot see traffic in another. This limits lateral exposure.
* **IDS/IPS (e.g., Snort, Suricata):** Pattern-match network traffic against signatures of known sniffing tools and techniques. Anomalies such as **ARP storms**, **gratuitous ARPs**, and **unusual port mirroring** generate alerts.

> [!TIP]  
> **Bonus defense for completeness:** *Host-based promiscuous mode detectors* (e.g., `Promqry`, `SniffDet`) send crafted ARP requests; a NIC in promiscuous mode responds differently from a NIC in normal mode, allowing detection of rogue sniffers.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks on Sniffing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Hub with Switch:** Students often incorrectly state that "a sniffer can capture all traffic on a switch." This is **false** for passive sniffing; explain that the switch isolates traffic, and active techniques are needed.
> 2. **Forgetting Promiscuous Mode:** Failing to mention that the NIC must be in **promiscuous mode** (or **monitor mode** for Wi-Fi) costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing Sniffing with Spoofing:** Sniffing is **passive observation**. Spoofing (ARP, IP, DNS) is an **active injection** of forged packets. The two are related (spoofing enables sniffing on switches) but are distinct concepts.
> 4. **Ignoring Legality:** Failing to mention the **legal and ethical boundaries** of sniffing (IT Act 2000, Section 66) in a 14-mark question shows a lack of professional maturity and may cost a half-mark for "holistic understanding."
> 5. **No Diagram in 14-Mark Questions:** KTU examiners frequently award 1–2 marks for a "neat labeled diagram." Always include a block diagram of the sniffer or the network topology.
> 6. **Mentioning Tools Without Context:** Listing Wireshark or tcpdump without explaining **how** they work is shallow. Always describe the **filter mechanism** and **dissector chain**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Exam-Day Cram Sheet):**

* ✅ **Definition:** Sniffing = capturing and analyzing network packets using a sniffer tool with the NIC in **promiscuous mode**.
* ✅ **Two Types:** **Passive** (listens on hubs/Wi-Fi, undetectable) vs **Active** (injects packets to bypass switches, detectable).
* ✅ **Why Hubs Leak:** Hub is a Layer-1 repeater — it broadcasts to **all ports**.
* ✅ **Why Switches Resist:** Switch uses a **CAM/MAC table** for unicast forwarding.
* ✅ **ARP Spoofing:** Sends forged gratuitous ARP replies to poison victim cache → enables MITM.
* ✅ **MAC Flooding:** Overwhelms CAM table → switch fails open and behaves like a hub.
* ✅ **DHCP Spoofing:** Attacker runs rogue DHCP server → assigns malicious default gateway.
* ✅ **Tools:** Wireshark (GUI), tcpdump (CLI), TShark, Ettercap, Cain & Abel, dsniff, Kismet.
* ✅ **Vulnerable Protocols:** HTTP, Telnet, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, SNMPv1/v2.
* ✅ **Safe Protocols:** HTTPS, SSH, SFTP, SCP, SNMPv3.
* ✅ **Countermeasures:** TLS/SSH/WPA3 encryption; DAI, Port Security, DHCP Snooping; VLAN segmentation; IDS/IPS.
* ✅ **Legal Status:** Unauthorized sniffing is a **criminal offense** under IT Act 2000.
* ✅ **Detection Tools:** Promqry, SniffDet, IDS signature analysis.
* ✅ **Key Files:** `.pcap` / `.pcapng` for stored captures; BPF for kernel-level filters.
* ✅ **Memorize Formulas:** $S = \alpha(1-E) + \beta \frac{N_{cleartext}}{N_{total}}$ and $D = 1 - R/P_{arrival}$.
* ✅ **Always Include** a diagram in 14-mark answers (block diagram of sniffer or ARP-spoofing flow).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Social Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Social Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Official Definition**
> **Social Engineering** is the art and science of manipulating people into performing actions or divulging confidential information, rather than breaking into systems by brute-force or technical exploitation. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PEEOT741), it is formally defined as the *non-technical intrusion vector* that exploits **human psychology** — including trust, fear, urgency, curiosity, and authority bias — to bypass an organization's physical, technical, and procedural security controls.

In the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) framework, social engineering primarily threatens **Confidentiality** (data leakage) and **Integrity** (unauthorized actions), and indirectly threatens **Availability** through techniques like ransomware delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Social Engineering is the #1 Cyber Threat in 2024–2025**
> Industry reports consistently show that **~98% of cyber attacks involve some form of social engineering**, because humans — unlike firewalls or encryption — can be *persuaded*, *deceived*, or *pressured* into becoming the weakest link in the security chain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Trojan Horse" of the Digital Age

Imagine a fortress surrounded by a deep moat, thick walls, and armed guards (this is your firewall, antivirus, and intrusion detection system). The gates are sealed shut, the drawbridge is up, and no arrow can penetrate the walls. Now imagine a smooth-talking merchant dressed in royal robes arrives at the gate, smiles, and says: *"I am a friend of the King. Open the gate — he is expecting me."*

The guards, believing him, lower the drawbridge and open the gates. The merchant walks in — not as an enemy, but as a **welcome guest**. Once inside, the fortress is compromised from within.

That merchant is the **social engineer**. The fortress is your **computer network**. The guards are your **employees**. The drawbridge is your **trust**.

> [!TIP] **The Three Pillars of Social Engineering Exploitation**
> 1. **Authority** — Pretending to be a person of power (CEO, IT admin, police).
> 2. **Urgency** — Creating pressure ("your account will be deleted in 1 hour!").
> 3. **Trust** — Building a fake relationship over days or weeks before striking.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Cyber Security

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mean Time to Compromise (MTTC)** | $\leq 1$ click | Average time for a phishing email to be clicked |
| **Human Error Rate** | **~95%** of breaches | Verizon DBIR — human factor in breaches |
| **Phishing Success Rate** | **~3% to 33%** | Emails clicked ÷ emails sent |
| **Cost of a Social Engineering Attack** | **\$4.76M** (avg) | IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2023 |
| **Spear-Phishing Open Rate** | **~70%** | Targeted vs mass phishing |


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Psychological Principles Exploited

Robert Cialdini's **6 Principles of Persuasion** form the academic backbone of social engineering theory. KTU examiners frequently test this directly.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Cialdini's 6 Principles — Must Memorize for KTU**
> 1. **Reciprocity** — *"I gave you a gift, now you owe me."*
> 2. **Commitment & Consistency** — *"You said yes once, say yes again."*
> 3. **Social Proof** — *"Everyone else is doing it, so should you."*
> 4. **Authority** — *"I am the manager/CEO/police officer."*
> 5. **Liking** — *"We are friends, help me out."*
> 6. **Scarcity / Urgency** — *"Only 2 minutes left before the offer expires!"*

## 2.2 The 6 Main Types of Social Engineering Attacks (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Attack Type | Medium | Core Trick |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Phishing** | Email / Web | Mass-mailed deceptive link or attachment |
| 2 | **Spear Phishing** | Email | Targeted phishing using OSINT on the victim |
| 3 | **Whaling** | Email | Spear phishing aimed at CEOs / CFOs |
| 4 | **Vishing** | Voice / Phone | Caller pretends to be bank, IT, or tax official |
| 5 | **Smishing** | SMS / WhatsApp | Text messages with malicious links |
| 6 | **Pretexting** | Conversation | Fabricated scenario (e.g., "I'm new here, help me") |
| 7 | **Baiting** | Physical / USB | Drop infected USB drives in parking lots |
| 8 | **Quid Pro Quo** | Service | Fake IT help in exchange for credentials |
| 9 | **Tailgating / Piggybacking** | Physical | Following an authorized person through a door |
| 10 | **Shoulder Surfing** | Physical | Watching someone enter their password |
| 11 | **Dumpster Diving** | Physical | Searching trash for unshredded documents |

## 2.3 The Social Engineering Attack Lifecycle (4-Phase Model)

### Phase 1: **Information Gathering (OSINT — Open Source Intelligence)**
The attacker uses **public sources** to build a profile of the victim:
- Social media (LinkedIn, Facebook, Instagram, X/Twitter)
- Company websites, press releases, "About Us" pages
- Data brokers (Spokeo, Pipl, BeenVerified)
- Google Dorking techniques

### Phase 2: **Establishing Trust & Rapport**
The attacker contacts the victim and builds a believable pretext:
- Uses the victim's name, job title, and interests
- Mimics the communication style of the impersonated authority
- Sends a benign first message to lower defenses

### Phase 3: **Exploitation (The Hook)**
The attacker triggers the attack using the **3 A's**:
- **Authority** (fake CEO email)
- **Anxiety** ("your account is compromised")
- **Action** (click this link / open this file / send money)

### Phase 4: **Execution & Exit**
The attacker achieves the goal (data theft, malware install, wire transfer) and disappears:
- Wipes logs
- Uses disposable infrastructure (burner SIMs, temporary email)
- Leverages the foothold for lateral movement

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

Although Social Engineering is qualitative, the following **risk-score formula** is frequently asked in KTU exams:

$$
R_{SE} = \frac{P \times V \times I}{C}
$$

Where:
- $R_{SE}$ = Social Engineering Risk Score
- $P$ = Probability of human error ($\approx 0.3$ to $0.9$)
- $V$ = Value of the asset being targeted (in USD)
- $I$ = Impact severity (1 = Low, 5 = Critical)
- $C$ = Cost of the control countermeasure (in USD)

> [!TIP] **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> This formula is used by **GRC (Governance, Risk, and Compliance)** teams to justify budget for **security awareness training** programs. A high $R_{SE}$ score mandates immediate user-training interventions.

## 2.5 Comparison: Phishing vs Spear Phishing vs Whaling

| Feature | Phishing | Spear Phishing | Whaling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Targeting** | Mass (thousands) | Specific individual or group | C-suite executives |
| **Personalization** | None | Heavy (OSINT-based) | Extremely detailed |
| **OSINT Required** | None | Moderate | Extensive |
| **Effort to Deploy** | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Success Rate** | ~3% | ~20–40% | ~70% |
| **Example** | "PayPal — verify your account" | "Hi [Name], saw your LinkedIn post on Project X..." | Fake legal subpoena to CFO |


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown — The Social Engineering Kill Chain

Since this topic is non-mathematical, the "derivation" here is the **logical deconstruction of the attack methodology** as a sequential chain. Every step is explicitly laid out, as KTU valuation requires.

## 3.1 The 7-Stage Kill Chain (Exhaustive)

### **Step 1 — Reconnaissance (Passive)**

The attacker collects **passive intelligence** about the target without directly interacting with the target's systems. This avoids triggering any security alerts.

> [!NOTE] **Sub-techniques:**
> - **OSINT gathering** from LinkedIn, Facebook, Instagram
> - **Google Dorking**: `site:company.com filetype:pdf "confidential"`
> - **Shodan.io** search for company IP ranges
> - **Wayback Machine** to view old company pages
> - **WHOIS lookup** for domain registration details
> - **Email harvesting** tools like theHarvester

> **Conversion logic:** Passive recon = no direct contact, so no logs are generated on the victim's side. The attacker becomes an "invisible observer" before any malicious action is taken.

---

### **Step 2 — Weaponization**

The attacker prepares the malicious payload and the **delivery vehicle**.

> [!NOTE] **Common Payloads:**
> - **Malicious Office documents** with VBA macros (e.g., fake invoice `.docm`)
> - **PDF exploits** (CVE-based)
> - **HTML smuggling** (JavaScript-encoded payloads that evade email gateways)
> - **Weaponized USB drives** (USB Rubber Ducky, O.MG Cable)
> - **Fake login portals** (cloned from real sites using SET — Social Engineering Toolkit)

> **Conversion logic:** The weapon is tailored based on Step 1's recon — e.g., if the target's role mentions "Finance," the payload will be disguised as an invoice.

---

### **Step 3 — Delivery**

The payload is transmitted to the victim via a chosen channel.

> [!NOTE] **Delivery Vectors:**
> - **Email** (most common — 91% of phishing starts here)
> - **SMS / WhatsApp** (smishing)
> - **Voice call** (vishing)
> - **Physical drop** (USBs in parking lots, fake CDs in mail)
> - **Social media DMs** (LinkedIn impersonation, Instagram DM scams)
> - **QR codes** (quishing — phishing via QR codes)

> **Conversion logic:** The vector is chosen to maximize the **Cialdini principle** being exploited. A phone call exploits *authority*; an email exploits *urgency*; a USB drop exploits *curiosity*.

---

### **Step 4 — Exploitation (Triggering the User)**

This is the **psychological attack** — the moment the human makes the mistake.

> [!NOTE] **Exploitation Triggers (KTU high-yield):**
> - **Curiosity** — *"See who viewed your profile"*
> - **Fear** — *"Your account will be suspended"*
> - **Greed** — *"You've won a \$1000 Amazon gift card"*
> - **Helpfulness** — *"URGENT: Are you at your desk? Need you to wire \$50K to this vendor ASAP — CEO"*
> - **Trust** — *"Hi, this is John from IT, we need to verify your password"*

> **Conversion logic:** The attacker uses **emotional hijacking** to disable the victim's rational thinking (prefrontal cortex) and replace it with an amygdala-driven impulsive response.

---

### **Step 5 — Installation / Compromise**

The malicious payload achieves its goal on the victim's system or in the victim's mind.

> [!NOTE] **Outcomes:**
> - **Malware installation** (Trojan, RAT — Remote Access Trojan, keylogger)
> - **Credential capture** (fake login page collects username/password)
> - **Session hijacking** (stolen cookies)
> - **Wire transfer fraud** (BEC — Business Email Compromise)
> - **Data exfiltration** (customer PII, trade secrets, financial records)

> **Conversion logic:** Once the user clicks or reveals, the human is now the **delivery mechanism for the technical intrusion** — the technical attack becomes possible only because the human enabled it.

---

### **Step 6 — Command & Control (C2)**

The malware establishes a **C2 channel** to the attacker's server.

> [!NOTE] **C2 Channels:**
> - HTTPS (encrypted, looks like normal traffic)
> - DNS tunneling (data smuggled in DNS queries)
> - Tor hidden services (.onion)
> - Cloud services (legitimate — Google Drive, Dropbox, Telegram)

> **Conversion logic:** The C2 channel is the **lifeline** of the attack. Without it, the malware is useless. Attackers use **domain fronting** and **fast flux DNS** to make C2 resilient.

---

### **Step 7 — Actions on Objectives & Exfiltration**

The attacker performs the final, damaging action.

> [!NOTE] **Final Objectives:**
> - **Data theft** → sold on dark web or held for ransom
> - **Ransomware deployment** → encrypt files, demand payment
> - **Lateral movement** → pivot to other systems using stolen creds
> - **Persistence** → install backdoors for re-entry
> - **Cover-up** → wipe logs, clear timestamps

> **Conversion logic:** The attacker exits cleanly to **avoid attribution**, leaving behind a backdoor for **future exploitation** (APT — Advanced Persistent Threat behavior).

---

## 3.2 Worked Case Study — The "CEO Fraud / BEC" Attack

> [!IMPORTANT] **Business Email Compromise (BEC) — Step-by-Step**

A mid-sized company receives an email that appears to be from the CEO:

$$
\text{Email: } \text{ceo@company.com (spoofed)} \rightarrow \text{finance@company.com}
$$

$$
\text{Subject: } \text{URGENT: Confidential Wire Transfer — Do Not Notify Anyone}
$$

$$
\text{Body: } \textit{"I'm in a closed-door board meeting. Wire \$250,000 to vendor X immediately. I'll explain later."}
$$

**Logical deconstruction of the kill chain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1 Recon:} &\quad \text{Attacker scrapes LinkedIn for CEO name, company finance head, recent deals} \\
\textbf{Step 2 Weapon:} &\quad \text{Email spoofed to mimic CEO's writing style and signature} \\
\textbf{Step 3 Deliver:} &\quad \text{Sent on a Friday afternoon, when finance staff is rushed} \\
\textbf{Step 4 Exploit:} &\quad \text{Urgency + Authority + Secrecy ("do not notify anyone")} \\
\textbf{Step 5 Install:} &\quad \text{Finance officer wires money to attacker's account} \\
\textbf{Step 6 C2:} &\quad \text{N/A — pure social engineering, no malware needed} \\
\textbf{Step 7 Exit:} &\quad \text{Money laundered through shell companies in 24 hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Notice that **no technical vulnerability was exploited** — the entire attack was a **psychological exploit of human trust and authority bias**. This is why **user awareness training** is the single most cost-effective security control.


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Social Engineering Attack Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[attackerStart] --> B[reconPhase]
    B --> B1[osintLinkedIn]
    B --> B2[osintWeb]
    B --> B3[osintEmail]
    B1 --> C[weaponPhase]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[maliciousEmail]
    C --> C2[fakeLoginPage]
    C --> C3[infectedUSB]
    C1 --> D[deliveryPhase]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[phishEmail]
    D --> D2[smishSMS]
    D --> D3[vishingCall]
    D --> D4[physicalDrop]
    D1 --> E[exploitPhase]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> E1[userClicks]
    E --> E2[userReveals]
    E --> E3[userGrants]
    E1 --> F[compromisePhase]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[malwareInstall]
    F --> F2[credsStolen]
    F --> F3[wireTransfer]
    F1 --> G[c2Channel]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[exfilExit]
    H --> H1[dataStolen]
    H --> H2[logsWiped]
    H1 --> I[attackComplete]
    H2 --> I
```

## 4.2 Cialdini's 6 Principles — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph se [Social Engineering Psychological Exploits]
        P1[recPrinciple] --> X1[giftThenAsk]
        P2[commitPrinciple] --> X2[smallYesBigYes]
        P3[socialPrinciple] --> X3[everyoneIsDoing]
        P4[authorityPrinciple] --> X4[iAmTheBoss]
        P5[likingPrinciple] --> X5[weAreFriends]
        P6[scarcityPrinciple] --> X6[actNowOrLose]
    end
    X1 --> Y[userComplies]
    X2 --> Y
    X3 --> Y
    X4 --> Y
    X5 --> Y
    X6 --> Y
    Y --> Z[securityBreach]
```

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Strategy Against Social Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[defenseStart] --> B[humanLayer]
    A --> C[processLayer]
    A --> D[technicalLayer]
    B --> B1[awarenessTraining]
    B --> B2[phishSimulation]
    B --> B3[redTeamExercises]
    C --> C1[dualApproval]
    C --> C2[callbackVerification]
    C --> C3[noSharedCreds]
    D --> D1[emailFiltering]
    D --> D2[dmarcSpfDkim]
    D --> D3[multiFactorAuth]
    D --> D4[endpointEDR]
    B1 --> E[secureOrganization]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define *Social Engineering* in the context of cyber security. List any **four (4)** types of social engineering attacks.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Social engineering is a non-technical attack technique that exploits human psychology — such as trust, fear, urgency, authority, and curiosity — to manipulate individuals into divulging confidential information, performing unauthorized actions, or bypassing security controls.

**Four Types (0.375 × 4 = 1.5 marks):**
1. **Phishing** — Fraudulent emails disguised as legitimate communications.
2. **Vishing** — Voice-based phishing via phone calls.
3. **Baiting** — Leaving infected USB drives in public places.
4. **Tailgating** — Physically following an authorized person into a restricted area.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Q2.** Explain the concept of **"Pretexting"** with a suitable real-world example. Why is it difficult to detect?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept (1.5 marks):** Pretexting is a social engineering technique where the attacker fabricates a *false scenario (pretext)* to trick a victim into revealing information or granting access. The attacker plays a specific role — a co-worker, IT technician, bank official, or auditor.

**Example (1 mark):** An attacker calls an employee, pretends to be a new IT support hire, and asks the employee to "test the system" by sharing their login credentials, claiming the password database is being migrated.

**Why difficult to detect (0.5 mark):** The pretext is **plausible, consistent, and tailored** using OSINT, making it psychologically believable and bypassing technical security controls entirely.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 3 Internal Choice]**

### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **7-stage Social Engineering Kill Chain**. Discuss in detail the **"Exploitation"** phase and the **3 psychological triggers** attackers use. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Differentiate between **Phishing, Spear Phishing, and Whaling** with suitable examples. Explain why **Whaling has a higher success rate** than generic phishing. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks**

The **Social Engineering Kill Chain** is a 7-stage sequential model adapted from the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain, focusing on the *human* attack surface:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Stage 1: Reconnaissance} &\rightarrow \text{OSINT gathering, social media scraping, Google dorking} \\
\textbf{Stage 2: Weaponization} &\rightarrow \text{Crafting malicious email, fake page, or USB payload} \\
\textbf{Stage 3: Delivery} &\rightarrow \text{Email, SMS, voice, physical drop} \\
\textbf{Stage 4: Exploitation} &\rightarrow \text{Psychological manipulation — the hook} \\
\textbf{Stage 5: Installation} &\rightarrow \text{Malware install, credential capture, or wire transfer} \\
\textbf{Stage 6: Command and Control} &\rightarrow \text{C2 channel for remote access} \\
\textbf{Stage 7: Actions on Objectives} &\rightarrow \text{Data theft, ransomware, lateral movement, exit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The Exploitation Phase (4 marks):**

The exploitation stage is the **psychological trigger** moment. Attackers use **3 A's**:

1. **Authority** — Impersonating a CEO, IT admin, or government official.
2. **Anxiety** — Inducing fear, panic, or urgency ("account locked!").
3. **Action** — Forcing the victim to **do something now** (click, share, transfer).

The diagram in SECTION 4.1 above illustrates the complete flow. **(1.5 marks for the diagram reference + 1.5 marks for the 3 A's description).**

#### **Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks**

| Parameter | Phishing | Spear Phishing | Whaling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Target** | Mass audience | Specific individual/group | C-suite executives |
| **Personalization** | None (generic) | High (OSINT-based) | Extreme (deep OSINT) |
| **Example** | "PayPal — verify your account" | "Hi Alex, noticed your LinkedIn post on Q3 sales..." | Fake legal subpoena to CFO from "CBI" |
| **Effort** | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Open Rate** | ~3% | ~20–40% | ~70% |

**Why Whaling has higher success rate (2 marks):**
- **Extremely high personalization** — every detail (name, company, recent deals) is accurate.
- **Targets have high authority** — employees comply immediately with "CEO's request" without questioning.
- **Attackers research for weeks** — making the email indistinguishable from a real one.
- **Exploits hierarchy bias** — junior staff do not challenge senior executives.

---

### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)**

> **Q (a)** Discuss **Cialdini's 6 Principles of Persuasion** and explain how each is exploited in social engineering attacks. Give an example for each principle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** What is **Phishing**? Explain its **types, lifecycle, and preventive measures** in detail. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks**

**Cialdini's 6 Principles (5 marks for explanations + 2 marks for examples):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Reciprocity} &\rightarrow \text{Attacker sends a free gift / favor first} \rightarrow \text{Victim feels obligated} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Ex: "I shared this report with you, can you share your login details?"} \\
\\
\textbf{2. Commitment \& Consistency} &\rightarrow \text{Small yes $\rightarrow$ bigger yes} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Ex: "Confirm your email first" then later "now confirm password"} \\
\\
\textbf{3. Social Proof} &\rightarrow \text{Everyone else is doing it} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Ex: "500 employees have already updated their info via this link"} \\
\\
\textbf{4. Authority} &\rightarrow \text{Impersonate a powerful person} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Ex: Fake email from CEO / IT admin / CBI officer} \\
\\
\textbf{5. Liking} &\rightarrow \text{Build rapport first} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Ex: Attacker pretends to share a hobby with victim before asking for favors} \\
\\
\textbf{6. Scarcity} &\rightarrow \text{Urgency / limited-time offer} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Ex: "Your account will be deleted in 30 minutes unless you verify now"}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks**

**Phishing (1 mark):** Phishing is a social engineering attack in which the attacker masquerades as a **legitimate and trusted entity** (bank, e-commerce site, employer) to trick victims into revealing sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, or credit card numbers.

**Types (2 marks):**
- **Email phishing** — Generic bulk emails.
- **Spear phishing** — Targeted at specific individuals.
- **Whaling** — Targeted at high-level executives.
- **Smishing** — SMS-based phishing.
- **Vishing** — Voice-call-based phishing.
- **Clone phishing** — Replicating a legitimate, previously delivered email with malicious links.

**Lifecycle (2 marks):**
1. Reconnaissance of victims
2. Crafting the fake email/page
3. Sending the bait
4. Victim clicks/responds
5. Credential/data capture
6. Fraudulent use of credentials

**Preventive Measures (2 marks):**
- User awareness training
- Email filtering (DMARC, SPF, DKIM)
- Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
- Anti-phishing browser extensions
- Reporting mechanisms (e.g., Google's Report Phishing)

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do NOT confuse "Phishing" and "Spear Phishing"** in definitions — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for vague answers like *"phishing is sending fake emails"* without specifying the **impersonation** aspect.
> - In Cialdini's principles, students often **list only 4–5 principles** instead of 6 — full mark requires **all 6 with at least 4 examples**.
> - When drawing the kill chain diagram, **label every stage with its output** (e.g., "Reconnaissance → Victim Profile"). A diagram with only boxes and no flow labels loses 1–2 marks.
> - For Whaling, always mention **C-suite / high-net-worth targets** — generic answers like "phishing for big people" score zero.
> - The 3 A's (**Authority, Anxiety, Action**) must be explicitly stated — do not paraphrase into "scare the user."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- [ ] **Definition:** Social engineering = psychological manipulation of humans (not technical hacking).
- [ ] **The 3 A's of Exploitation:** **Authority, Anxiety, Action** — must be memorized.
- [ ] **Cialdini's 6 Principles:** Reciprocity, Commitment, Social Proof, Authority, Liking, Scarcity.
- [ ] **Top 5 Attacks to Memorize:** Phishing, Spear Phishing, Vishing, Smishing, Pretexting.
- [ ] **Physical Attacks:** Tailgating, Baiting, Dumpster Diving, Shoulder Surfing.
- [ ] **7-Stage Kill Chain:** Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Exfil.
- [ ] **Business Email Compromise (BEC):** Pure social engineering, no malware — financial fraud.
- [ ] **Risk Formula:** $R_{SE} = \dfrac{P \times V \times I}{C}$ — use sparingly, only if asked.
- [ ] **Countermeasures (Defense-in-Depth):** Human layer (training), Process layer (dual approval), Technical layer (MFA, DMARC, EDR).
- [ ] **Key Industry Stats:** ~98% of attacks involve social engineering; human error is in ~95% of breaches.
- [ ] **Diagram must-have:** A labeled 4–7 stage kill chain — practice drawing it before the exam.
- [ ] **One-liner to remember:** *"You can patch a server, but you cannot patch a human brain."*

> [!TIP] **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as: **Definition (1 mark) → Diagram (2 marks) → Detailed explanation (4 marks) → Examples (2 marks) → Countermeasures (2 marks) → Conclusion (1 mark) → 2 spare marks for neatness and keyword usage.** This template guarantees full marks if content is accurate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Denial-of-Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Denial-of-Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 - Denial-of-Service (DoS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Denial-of-Service (DoS)** attack is a malicious cyber-attack engineered to render a targeted computer system, network resource, or online service **unavailable** to its intended legitimate users by temporarily or indefinitely disrupting the services of the host connected to the Internet. The attacker achieves this by flooding the target with superfluous requests, exploiting protocol vulnerabilities, or exhausting critical computational resources (CPU cycles, memory, bandwidth, or socket descriptors).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> DoS attacks target **availability** — one of the three pillars of the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). They do NOT primarily seek data theft but rather service disruption.

When the assault is launched simultaneously from **multiple distributed sources** (often a network of compromised machines called a **botnet**), the attack is reclassified as a **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)** attack.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Traffic Jam"

Imagine a **4-lane highway** that normally carries **4,000 cars per minute** smoothly to a city center (the server). Now, a malicious actor deliberately sends **1,000,000 fake cars** (attack packets) onto the same highway all at once:

- Legitimate commuters (real users) cannot reach the city because the road is choked.
- The city's entrance gates (server CPU/network sockets) are overwhelmed.
- Emergency services (authentication, transactions) time out and fail.
- The city does NOT collapse — it just stops accepting new visitors.

This is precisely how a DoS attack works in the digital realm: the attacker does not "break into" the server; they simply **block the door** so nobody else can get in.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Terminology

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Network Bandwidth consumed | $B_{attack}$ | **Gbps** (Gigabits per second) |
| Packet Rate | $R_p$ | **Mpps** (Million packets per second) |
| Requests per Second | $R_{req}$ | **RPS** (Requests/second) |
| Server CPU Load | $L_{cpu}$ | **% utilization** |
| Connection Half-Opens | $N_{syn}$ | **count** |
| Mean Time Between Failures (pre-attack) | $MTBF$ | **hours/days** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Availability Equation (KTU High-Yield):**
> $$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$
> where **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair. A successful DoS attack artificially inflates the **MTTR**, thereby plummeting the system's **Availability (A)** toward zero.

### 1.4 The CIA Triad Placement

The DoS attack uniquely targets the **Availability** leg:

| Pillar | Compromised by DoS? | Example Attack |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | No (data is NOT stolen) | Phishing (different topic) |
| Integrity | Indirect (logs may be corrupted) | MITM (different topic) |
| **Availability** | **YES — Primary target** | **DoS / DDoS** |
</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of DoS Attacks (KTU Syllabus-Mandated Classification)

DoS attacks are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis 1 — By Attack Volume (Capacity-Based)

- **Volumetric Attacks**: Saturate the link bandwidth (e.g., UDP Flood, ICMP Flood). Measured in **Gbps**.
- **Protocol Attacks**: Exhaust server-state resources by abusing layer 3/4 protocol handshakes (e.g., SYN Flood, Ping of Death). Measured in **pps** (packets per second).
- **Application Layer Attacks**: Target layer 7 services with legitimate-looking but expensive requests (e.g., HTTP GET/POST Flood, Slowloris). Measured in **RPS**.

### Axis 2 — By Source Architecture

- **Single-Source DoS**: One attacker machine vs. one victim. Easy to block via IP filtering.
- **Distributed DoS (DDoS)**: Hundreds–millions of compromised "zombie" hosts (botnet) simultaneously flood the target. Hard to mitigate.

### Axis 3 — By Exploited Vulnerability

- **Logic / Software Exploits**: Target bugs in OS / application code (e.g., Ping of Death buffer overflow, Teardrop fragment overlap).
- **Resource Exhaustion**: Legitimate-looking requests consuming finite resources (sockets, memory, CPU cycles).

## 2.2 Layer-wise Attack Mapping (OSI Model)

| OSI Layer | Attack Type | Resource Exhausted |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 7 — Application | HTTP Flood, Slowloris, DNS Query Flood | Thread pool, sockets, DB queries |
| Layer 6 — Presentation | SSL/TLS Renegotiation Attack | CPU cycles (asymmetric crypto) |
| Layer 4 — Transport | SYN Flood, UDP Flood, TCP Reset | Connection table, bandwidth |
| Layer 3 — Network | ICMP Flood (Smurf), Ping of Death | Router buffers, bandwidth |
| Layer 2 — Data Link | ARP Flood | CAM table overflow |
| Layer 1 — Physical | Cable cut, EMP, Wi-Fi jamming | Physical medium |

## 2.3 The TCP Three-Way Handshake — Foundation of SYN Flood

The Transmission Control Protocol establishes connections via a **3-step handshake**:

1. **SYN** — Client sends synchronization packet with initial sequence number $ISN_c$.
2. **SYN-ACK** — Server allocates buffer, replies with $ISN_s$ and acknowledges $ISN_c + 1$.
3. **ACK** — Client confirms $ISN_s + 1$. Connection becomes **ESTABLISHED**.

Between step 2 and step 3, the connection sits in a **SYN_RECEIVED** half-open state. The server maintains state in its **Transmission Control Block (TCB)** table. Each half-open entry consumes roughly **~1 KB to 16 KB** of kernel memory.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — DoS Quick Reference

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Reliability metric; DoS increases MTTR |
| 2 | SYN Backlog Capacity | $B_{syn} = \dfrac{M_{kernel}}{S_{tcb}}$ | Max half-open sockets = kernel memory / per-entry size |
| 3 | Effective Attack Rate | $R_{eff} = N_{bots} \times R_{per\_bot}$ | Total DDoS throughput |
| 4 | Amplification Factor | $F_{amp} = \dfrac{Bytes_{reply}}{Bytes_{request}}$ | Ratio of attacker payload to victim load (e.g., DNS ~50x) |
| 5 | Botnet Size Threshold | $N_{crit} = \dfrac{B_{link}}{R_{per\_bot}}$ | Bots required to saturate a link |
| 6 | Slowloris Window | $T_{timeout} = \dfrac{W_{win}}{B_{dl}}$ | Time to exhaust idle HTTP threads |
| 7 | ICMP Smurf Multiplier | $M_{smurf} = N_{hosts\_on\_net}$ | Hosts replying to spoofed broadcast |
| 8 | CPU Exhaustion Rate | $L_{cpu}(t) = L_0 + R_{req} \times C_{cost}$ | Load as a function of incoming request cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Table-Break Rule:** All vertical bars rendered with the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` command to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding DoS mechanics is foundational for designing:
- **Web server farms** with rate-limiting and load-balancing.
- **ISP traffic scrubbing centers** (e.g., Akamai, Cloudflare scrubbing).
- **Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS)** signature databases.
- **Firewalls** with stateful inspection and SYN cookies.
- **High-availability (HA) clusters** with failover timers tuned to MTTR budgets.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SYN Flood Backlog Exhaustion

**Problem Statement:** A Linux web server has **128 MB** of kernel memory reserved for the TCP backlog, and each half-open SYN entry consumes an average of **8 KB**. The server's SYN-ACK retry timeout is **75 seconds**. If an attacker sends **SYN packets** at a rate of $R_{syn}$ packets/sec, at what rate will the backlog fill completely? How long until legitimate users get locked out?

### Step 1 — Calculate Backlog Capacity

We begin by determining the maximum number of half-open sockets the server can hold.

$$B_{syn} = \frac{M_{kernel}}{S_{tcb}}$$

Substituting $M_{kernel} = 128 \text{ MB} = 128 \times 1024 \text{ KB} = 131072 \text{ KB}$ and $S_{tcb} = 8 \text{ KB}$:

$$B_{syn} = \frac{131072 \text{ KB}}{8 \text{ KB}} = 16384 \text{ half-open connections}$$

This means the server can queue at most **16,384 half-open sockets** before it begins dropping new SYNs.

### Step 2 — Compute Time-to-Fill at Given Attack Rate

Assume the attacker fires $R_{syn} = 5000$ SYNs/sec and none complete the handshake (spoofed source IPs).

$$T_{fill} = \frac{B_{syn}}{R_{syn}}$$

$$T_{fill} = \frac{16384}{5000} \approx 3.28 \text{ seconds}$$

**Result:** In just **3.28 seconds** the attacker has filled the entire backlog.

### Step 3 — Verify Against Retry Timeout

The backlog entries are released only after the SYN-ACK retry expires (typically 75 s) OR after a backlog overflow. The attacker need only sustain:

$$R_{sustain} = \frac{B_{syn}}{T_{retry}} = \frac{16384}{75} \approx 218.5 \text{ SYNs/sec}$$

So an attacker firing only **~219 SYNs/sec** can perpetually lock out the server. This is trivially achievable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DDoS Botnet Bandwidth Saturation

**Problem Statement:** A victim's web server has a **10 Gbps** Internet link. Each bot in the attacker's botnet is a residential DSL line capable of **2 Mbps** upstream. Assuming 100% uplink utilization, how many bots are required to saturate the victim's link?

### Step 1 — Normalize Units

$$B_{link} = 10 \text{ Gbps} = 10 \times 1024 \text{ Mbps} = 10240 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Bot Count Threshold

$$N_{crit} = \frac{B_{link}}{R_{per\_bot}}$$

$$N_{crit} = \frac{10240 \text{ Mbps}}{2 \text{ Mbps}} = 5120 \text{ bots}$$

### Step 3 — Factor in Amplification (DNS Reflected DDoS)

If the attacker leverages DNS amplification with $F_{amp} = 50\times$, the effective per-bot throughput becomes:

$$R_{per\_bot}^{eff} = R_{per\_bot} \times F_{amp} = 2 \text{ Mbps} \times 50 = 100 \text{ Mbps}$$

$$N_{crit}^{amp} = \frac{10240}{100} = 102.4 \approx 103 \text{ bots}$$

**Result:** With DNS amplification, only **~103 bots** can saturate a **10 Gbps** link. This is why modern DDoS attacks routinely exceed **1 Tbps** (e.g., the 2018 GitHub attack peaked at 1.35 Tbps).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Slowloris Application-Layer Exhaustion

**Problem Statement:** Apache 2.2 defaults to **150 max threads** and a **300-second** keep-alive timeout. An attacker uses Slowloris, opening a partial HTTP header every **10 seconds** to keep sockets alive. How many concurrent sockets are needed to exhaust the thread pool?

### Step 1 — Identify Thread Pool Capacity

$$N_{threads} = 150$$

### Step 2 — Confirm Sustained Rate Requirement

Slowloris maintains an open socket with a slow trickle. To keep all threads busy permanently:

$$N_{sockets} \geq N_{threads} = 150$$

Since Slowloris opens one socket per thread and renews headers every 10 s, with a timeout of 300 s, an attacker needs only **150 simultaneous connections** — a remarkably low bar.

### Step 3 — Time-to-Exhaustion

$$T_{exhaust} = N_{threads} \times \Delta t_{open} = 150 \times 0.1 \text{ s} = 15 \text{ seconds}$$

**Result:** A single laptop with negligible bandwidth can cripple an Apache server in **15 seconds**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. Mitigation Quick-Map

| Attack | OSI Layer | Resource Targeted | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| SYN Flood | 4 | TCB table | SYN Cookies, Reducing SYN-RECEIVED timer |
| UDP Flood | 4 | Bandwidth | Rate-limiting, Upstream filtering |
| ICMP Smurf | 3 | Bandwidth + reflector net | Disable IP-directed broadcast |
| Ping of Death | 3 | Fragment reassembly buffer | Patch OS, drop oversized fragments |
| HTTP Flood | 7 | Thread pool / DB | CAPTCHA, JS challenge, WAF |
| Slowloris | 7 | Open sockets | Reverse proxy (nginx), tighten timeout |
| DNS Amp | 7 | Bandwidth | Response Rate Limiting (RRL) at resolvers |
| ARP Flood | 2 | CAM table | Dynamic ARP Inspection, port security |
| Botnet DDoS | 3–7 | Everything | Anycast scrubbing, BGP blackholing |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Botnet ROI

> **Problem:** A "DDoS-for-hire" service charges **₹500/hour** to deliver **50 Gbps** of attack traffic. A botnet master rents **10,000** bots at **₹2/bot/month**. Compute the cost per Gbps-hour.

**Step 1:** Monthly botnet cost.

$$C_{bot} = 10000 \times 2 = \text{₹}20000 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 2:** Assume 30-day month, 24-hour operation:

$$C_{hour} = \frac{20000}{30 \times 24} = \frac{20000}{720} \approx \text{₹}27.78 \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 3:** Revenue per hour at 50 Gbps:

$$R_{hour} = 500 \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 4:** Gross profit margin:

$$P = \frac{R_{hour} - C_{hour}}{R_{hour}} = \frac{500 - 27.78}{500} \approx 0.944 = 94.4\%$$
