- **Mechanism**: Send a single packet with a non-standard flag combination:
  - `FIN` only → FIN scan
  - **no flags set** → NULL scan
  - `FIN + PSH + URG` → XMAS scan (the packet is "lit up like a Christmas tree")
- **Why it works**: Per **RFC 793**, a `CLOSED` port must respond with `RST`. An `OPEN` port silently drops the packet. A `FILTERED` port gives no response.
- **Trade-off**: **Useless against Windows** (Microsoft's TCP/IP stack does *not* follow RFC 793 — it always replies with `RST`). Only works against *RFC-compliant* Unix/Linux.

### 2.2.4 UDP Scan (`-sU`)
- **Mechanism**: UDP is connectionless. The scanner sends a UDP datagram. If the port is `CLOSED`, the target replies with **ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3, Code 3)**. If `OPEN`, no response (or a service-specific reply).
- **Trade-off**: Extremely slow (rate-limited by ICMP error responses). Often combined with TCP scans via `-sSU`.

### 2.2.5 ACK Scan / Firewall Detection (`-sA`)
- **Mechanism**: Sends an `ACK` packet. Determines *firewall rule sets*, not port state:
  - `RST` response → port is `UNFILTERED` (no firewall, or firewall passes it)
  - No response (or ICMP unreachable) → port is `FILTERED`
- **Why it matters**: Maps the **firewall's Access Control List (ACL)** — a critical step before launching a stealth SYN scan.

### 2.2.6 Service/Version Detection (`-sV`)
- **Mechanism**: After discovering an `OPEN` port, the scanner sends a database of **probes** (specialized payload strings) to elicit a response. The response is matched against a `nmap-service-probes` signature database.
- **Why it works**: Services like Apache vs. nginx, or MySQL 5.7 vs. 8.0, return subtly different banners and error messages. The scanner fingerprints these deltas.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Concept:** The `-A` flag in Nmap is an *aggressive* meta-option that enables **OS detection (`-O`) + version detection (`-sV`) + script scanning (`--script=default`) + traceroute (`--traceroute`)**. It is the "one-shot" choice for a KTU lab examination.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Open Port/Service Identification is a **qualitative + procedural** domain (not heavily mathematical), the "formulas" below are **decision matrices and heuristic rules** that map directly to KTU exam marks.

### Table 2.1 — Port State Decision Matrix

| Sent by Scanner | Response from Target | Inferred State | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| TCP `SYN` | `SYN-ACK` | **OPEN** | Target wants to complete handshake |
| TCP `SYN` | `RST` / `RST-ACK` | **CLOSED** | Target actively rejects connection |
| TCP `SYN` | No response (or ICMP unreachable) | **FILTERED** | Firewall dropped packet silently |
| TCP `SYN` → `RST` | `RST` from target to *our* `RST` | **OPEN` (in `-sS`)** | Handshake was half-completed |
| TCP `FIN` / `NULL` / `XMAS` | No response | **OPEN** or `FILTERED` (Linux: OPEN) | RFC 793 silent drop rule |
| TCP `FIN` / `NULL` / `XMAS` | `RST` | **CLOSED** | Target forces reset |
| UDP datagram | No response | **OPEN` or `FILTERED`** | UDP is unreliable; cannot distinguish |
| UDP datagram | ICMP Type 3, Code 3 | **CLOSED** | Port unreachable error |
| UDP datagram | UDP response | **OPEN** | Service-specific reply received |
| TCP `ACK` | `RST` | **UNFILTERED** | Firewall allows the packet through |
| TCP `ACK` | No response / ICMP error | **FILTERED** | Firewall blocks ACK packets |

### Table 2.2 — Scan Technique Quick Reference

| Scan Type | Flag | Privilege Required | Stealth | Detects |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TCP Connect | `-sT` | User | **Low** | Open/closed TCP |
| SYN (Half-Open) | `-sS` | Root | **High** | Open/closed TCP |
| FIN | `-sF` | Root | **High** | Open TCP (RFC-compliant OS) |
| NULL | `-sN` | Root | **High** | Open TCP (RFC-compliant OS) |
| XMAS | `-sX` | Root | **High** | Open TCP (RFC-compliant OS) |
| ACK | `-sA` | Root | Medium | Firewall rules |
| UDP | `-sU` | Root | Medium | Open/closed UDP |
| Version | `-sV` | Root | Low | Service + version |
| OS Detect | `-O` | Root | Low | Target OS family |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark questions, examiners expect you to **draw and label** the TCP header showing flag bits (`URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN`) for at least one scan type. Always include the **control bits bitmap** in your diagram.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Open Port/Service Identification is not a "hacker toy" — it is the foundation of:
- **Vulnerability Management** (Tenable Nessus, Qualys scan targets before auditing)
- **Penetration Testing** (every PTES / OSSTMM engagement begins with port mapping)
- **Network Asset Inventory** (CMDB population)
- **Compliance Auditing** (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001 require regular port/service reviews)
- **Firewall Rule Verification** (ACK scan validates ACLs)
- **IoT Security** (identifying default ports like `Telnet:23`, `HTTP:80` on smart devices)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following sections model the **analytical reasoning** a scanner applies. They are not algebraic derivations in the calculus sense, but rather **logical decision-tree derivations** that KTU examiners reward with full marks when written out step-by-step.

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a SYN-ACK Response Implies an OPEN Port

We model a target TCP stack as a finite state machine. Let $S_t \in \{\text{LISTEN}, \text{SYN\_RCVD}, \text{CLOSED}\}$ be the target's state on port $p$ at time $t$.

**Step 1 — Initial Condition.** The target service is bound to port $p$ and is in the `LISTEN` state, with an open file descriptor and an accepting backlog queue.

**Step 2 — Scanner Action.** The scanner sends a TCP segment with `SYN=1, ACK=0, seq=x`.

**Step 3 — Transition.** Per RFC 793 §3.9, a `LISTEN` state receiving a `SYN` MUST transition to `SYN_RCVD` and emit a `SYN-ACK` with `seq=y, ack=x+1`.

**Step 4 — Inference.** Observation of `SYN-ACK` by the scanner implies the transition `LISTEN → SYN_RCVD` occurred, which logically requires the port to be in `LISTEN` at the moment of contact.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** Therefore, the port is **OPEN**.

$$
\text{Observation: } (\text{SYN-ACK received}) \;\Rightarrow\; S_t = \text{LISTEN} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{State}(p) = \text{OPEN}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: The FIN Scan Logic Chain

We derive the *necessary and sufficient conditions* for the FIN scan to distinguish open vs. closed on an RFC-compliant host.

**Step 1 — Premise.** RFC 793 §3.9 mandates: *"A CLOSED port MUST respond to a non-SYN segment with a RST."*

**Step 2 — Scanner Action.** A FIN packet (`FIN=1, all other flags=0`) is sent to port $p$. This is a non-SYN segment.

**Step 3 — Case A: Port is CLOSED.** The TCP stack matches the RFC rule and emits `RST`.

**Step 4 — Case B: Port is OPEN and LISTEN.** The TCP stack has *no rule* in the LISTEN state to handle a stray FIN (the connection does not exist). The packet is **silently dropped**.

**Step 5 — Decision Rule.**

$$
\text{State}(p) = \begin{cases} \text{CLOSED} & \text{if } R \text{ is received} \\ \text{OPEN} \lor \text{FILTERED} & \text{if } R = \varnothing \text{ (no response)} \end{cases}
$$

where $R$ is the set of received response segments within the timeout window $\tau$.

**Step 6 — Ambiguity Note.** The "no response" outcome cannot distinguish OPEN from FILTERED. This is an inherent limitation — to resolve, run a parallel SYN scan or ACK scan.

**Step 7 — Why Windows Fails.** Microsoft's `tcpip.sys` deviates from RFC 793 and emits `RST` for FIN packets on `OPEN` ports, breaking Case B's silent-drop assumption. The scan reports a Linux server as entirely `CLOSED`, which is a false negative.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Nmap Command Synthesis

A KTU practical exam often asks: *"Write the Nmap command to scan a target 192.168.1.10, perform a SYN scan on ports 1–1024, detect service versions, and use aggressive timing."*

**Step 1 — Identify required flags.** From Table 2.2, we need:
- `-sS` → SYN scan
- `-p 1-1024` → port range
- `-sV` → version detection
- `-T4` → aggressive timing template (T0=sneaky, T5=insane)

**Step 2 — Compose command.**

```bash
nmap -sS -p 1-1024 -sV -T4 192.168.1.10
```

**Step 3 — Optional intensification** (for a 14-mark descriptive answer, add):
- `-O` → OS detection
- `-A` → aggressive mode (combines -O, -sV, scripts, traceroute)
- `-v` → verbose output for the report

**Step 4 — Final expert command.**

```bash
nmap -sS -sV -O -A -T4 -p 1-1024 -v 192.168.1.10
```

> [!TIP]
> Always explain *why* you chose each flag in the answer. Examiners award marks for **justification**, not just syntax.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Scan Output to Exploitation Path

| Discovered Service | Default Port | Common Vulnerabilities | Recommended Follow-up Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| `vsftpd 2.3.4` | 21 | Backdoor command execution (CVE-2011-2523) | Metasploit |
| `OpenSSH 7.2` | 22 | Username enumeration, weak keys | Hydra, ssh-audit |
| `Apache 2.4.49` | 80 | Path traversal (CVE-2021-41773) | Nikto, dirb |
| `MySQL 5.7` | 3306 | Weak credentials, UDF exploit | Hydra, sqlmap |
| `RDP (mstsc)` | 3389 | BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) | Metasploit |
| `SMBv1` | 445 | EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144) | Metasploit |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **bridge** between Module 4 (Scanning) and Module 5 (Vulnerability & Exploitation). Examiners love questions that span modules.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Open Port/Service Identification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Target IP Acquired] --> B[Phase 1: Host Discovery]
    B --> B1{Ping Sweep / ARP Scan}
    B1 -->|Host Alive| C[Phase 2: Port Scanning]
    B1 -->|No Response| B2[Host Down - Stop]
    C --> C1{Select Scan Type}
    C1 -->|TCP SYN| D1[SYN Scan -sS]
    C1 -->|TCP Connect| D2[Connect Scan -sT]
    C1 -->|UDP| D3[UDP Scan -sU]
    C1 -->|Stealth Flags| D4[FIN/NULL/XMAS]
    C1 -->|Firewall Map| D5[ACK Scan -sA]
    D1 --> E[Collect Raw Responses]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Phase 3: State Classification]
    F --> F1{Response Analysis}
    F1 -->|SYN-ACK received| G1[State: OPEN]
    F1 -->|RST received| G2[State: CLOSED]
    F1 -->|No Response| G3[State: FILTERED]
    F1 -->|ICMP Type3 Code3| G4[State: CLOSED UDP]
    G1 --> H[Phase 4: Service Version Detection]
    G2 --> I[Skip - No Service]
    G3 --> I
    G4 --> I
    H --> H1[Send Service Probes]
    H1 --> H2[Match nmap-service-probes DB]
    H2 --> J[Phase 5: Output Report]
    J --> J1[Service Name + Version + OS Guess]
    J1 --> K[End: Hand Off to Vulnerability Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: TCP SYN Scan Mechanics

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sc as Scanner
    participant Fw as Firewall
    participant Tg as Target Host
    Sc->>Fw: SYN packet to port 80
    Fw->>Tg: Forward SYN
    Tg-->>Fw: SYN-ACK (port 80 is LISTEN)
    Fw-->>Sc: Forward SYN-ACK
    Note over Sc: Port marked OPEN
    Sc->>Fw: RST (tear down half-open)
    Fw->>Tg: Forward RST
    Tg-->>Sc: Connection aborted silently
    Sc->>Fw: SYN packet to port 9999
    Fw->>Tg: Forward SYN
    Tg-->>Fw: RST-ACK (no service)
    Fw-->>Sc: Forward RST-ACK
    Note over Sc: Port marked CLOSED
    Sc->>Fw: SYN packet to port 443
    Fw--xTg: DROP (firewall rule)
    Note over Sc: Timeout, no response
    Note over Sc: Port marked FILTERED
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: TCP Header & Flag Bits (Flag-Map for XMAS vs NULL vs FIN)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TCP_Header
        H1[Source Port 16 bits] --> H2[Destination Port 16 bits]
        H2 --> H3[Sequence Number 32 bits]
        H3 --> H4[Acknowledgment Number 32 bits]
        H4 --> H5[Data Offset + Reserved 4 bits]
        H5 --> F[Control Flags 6 bits]
        F --> F1[URG]
        F --> F2[ACK]
        F --> F3[PSH]
        F --> F4[RST]
        F --> F5[SYN]
        F --> F6[FIN]
    end
    F1 --> M1[XMAS Scan: URG=1 ACK=0 PSH=1 RST=0 SYN=0 FIN=1]
    F2 --> M1
    F3 --> M1
    F4 --> M1
    F5 --> M1
    F6 --> M1
    F1 --> M2[NULL Scan: All flags = 0]
    F2 --> M2
    F3 --> M2
    F4 --> M2
    F5 --> M2
    F6 --> M2
    F1 --> M3[FIN Scan: FIN=1 only]
    F2 --> M3
    F3 --> M3
    F4 --> M3
    F5 --> M3
    F6 --> M3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree: Port State Inference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Send Probe to Target Port P] --> Q{Protocol Type?}
    Q -->|TCP| T1{Which TCP Scan?}
    Q -->|UDP| U1[Send UDP datagram]
    T1 -->|SYN| T2{SYN-ACK back?}
    T1 -->|FIN| T3{RST back?}
    T1 -->|ACK| T4{RST back?}
    T2 -->|Yes| R1[State: OPEN]
    T2 -->|No RST| R2[State: FILTERED]
    T2 -->|RST| R3[State: CLOSED]
    T3 -->|Yes| R3
    T3 -->|No| R4[State: OPEN or FILTERED]
    T4 -->|Yes| R5[State: UNFILTERED]
    T4 -->|No| R6[State: FILTERED]
    U1 --> U2{Response received?}
    U2 -->|UDP Reply| R1
    U2 -->|ICMP Type3 Code3| R3
    U2 -->|No Reply| R7[State: OPEN or FILTERED]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**Differentiate between Open, Closed, and Filtered port states in TCP scanning. How does a scanner infer each state?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **OPEN** — Port has an active service in `LISTEN` state, accepts connections. Scanners infer it by receiving `SYN-ACK` (SYN scan) or completing the 3-way handshake (Connect scan). *(1 mark)*
2. **CLOSED** — Port has no service, but the host is reachable. Inferred by receiving `RST` or `RST-ACK` in response to a `SYN`. *(1 mark)*
3. **FILTERED** — A firewall/ACL is blocking probe packets. Inferred by *no response* within the timeout window, or by ICMP unreachable codes (Type 3, Codes 1/2/9/10/13). *(1 mark)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional Exam]`
**What is the purpose of the XMAS scan in Nmap? Why is it called "XMAS" and what is its key limitation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Purpose:** To stealthily identify open TCP ports on RFC-compliant hosts by exploiting the silent-drop rule. *(1 mark)*
2. **"XMAS" Naming:** The packet sets the `FIN`, `PSH`, and `URG` flags simultaneously, lighting up the flag bits like a Christmas tree. *(1 mark)*
3. **Key Limitation:** It does **not work against Windows** (and certain other non-RFC-compliant stacks) because Microsoft's TCP/IP always replies with `RST`, producing false negatives (all ports appear closed). *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the TCP three-way handshake mechanism. With suitable diagrams, describe how a SYN scan (`-sS`) exploits this handshake to identify open ports. List the responses and their meanings. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The TCP three-way handshake establishes a reliable connection between client and server using three segments:

1. **Step 1 — SYN:** The client sends a segment with the `SYN` flag set to `1` and an initial sequence number `seq = x`. This requests synchronization.
2. **Step 2 — SYN-ACK:** The server, if accepting connections, replies with `SYN=1, ACK=1`, `seq = y`, `ack = x + 1`. This acknowledges the client's sequence and offers its own.
3. **Step 3 — ACK:** The client sends `ACK=1`, `seq = x + 1`, `ack = y + 1`. The connection is now `ESTABLISHED`.

**SYN Scan Mechanism:**
A SYN (half-open) scan is a *stealth variant* that does not complete the handshake:
- Scanner sends **SYN** to target port.
- **If OPEN:** Target responds with **SYN-ACK**. Scanner immediately sends **RST** to abort the connection — the handshake is *half-completed*. The application never sees the connection.
- **If CLOSED:** Target responds with **RST-ACK**.
- **If FILTERED:** No response (firewall drops the packet).

**Responses Summary Table:**

| Response | Port State |
|---|---|
| SYN-ACK | **OPEN** |
| RST / RST-ACK | **CLOSED** |
| No response (timeout) | **FILTERED** |

**Advantages:** Faster, stealthier than Connect scan; requires root for raw sockets. *(7 marks total — 3 for handshake, 2 for scan mechanism, 1 for state table, 1 for advantages)*

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss Service Version Detection (`-sV`) and OS Fingerprinting (`-O`) in Nmap. Explain the probing mechanism and the use of signature databases. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Service Version Detection (`-sV`):**
After a port is found OPEN, Nmap enters a deeper probe phase. The stages are:
1. **Null probe** — Connect to the port without sending data. Some services (e.g., SSH) greet the scanner with a banner.
2. **Probe transmission** — Nmap sends packets from its `nmap-service-probes` database. Each probe is a string designed to elicit a unique response from a specific service (e.g., `HELP\r\n` for FTP, `GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n` for HTTP).
3. **Signature matching** — The response is parsed and matched against regex patterns in the signature DB. A match yields the service name and version (e.g., `Apache httpd 2.4.41`).
4. **Fallback:** If no probe matches, Nmap reports the service based on port number alone (e.g., `http` on port 80).

**OS Fingerprinting (`-O`):**
- Sends a suite of **probe packets** (SYN, ACK, FIN, ICMP echo, etc.) and analyzes subtle differences in the **TCP/IP stack behavior** (ISN generation, DF bit, window size, TTL).
- These values are compared against the `nmap-os-db` signature database, which contains thousands of known OS fingerprints.
- Output: OS family, vendor, version, and a confidence percentage (e.g., `Linux 5.x (96%)`).

**Why It Matters:** Knowing the *exact* service version (e.g., `OpenSSL 1.0.1`) allows the attacker to query CVE databases for matching vulnerabilities — the critical input to Module 5 (Exploitation). *(7 marks total — 3 for -sV mechanism, 2 for -O mechanism, 1 for DB role, 1 for exploitation context)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the following Nmap scan techniques: FIN scan, NULL scan, and XMAS scan. Compare their advantages and limitations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

All three scans are **stealth scans** that send a single, non-SYN TCP segment to a target port. They rely on **RFC 793 §3.9**, which states that a `CLOSED` port must respond with `RST` to any non-SYN segment, while an `OPEN` port silently drops the packet.

**1. FIN Scan (`-sF`):**
- Sends a TCP packet with **only the FIN flag set**.
- **OPEN port → No response.** **CLOSED port → RST.** **FILTERED port → No response (or ICMP unreachable).**

**2. NULL Scan (`-sN`):**
- Sends a TCP packet with **no flags set at all** (all six flag bits = 0).
- Same response logic as FIN. The "blank" packet is the "null" probe.

**3. XMAS Scan (`-sX`):**
- Sends a TCP packet with **FIN + PSH + URG all set to 1** (the packet's flag byte looks like a Christmas tree lit up: 0 1 1 0 0 0 1 0 1 in binary = 0x29).
- Same response logic as FIN and NULL.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | FIN | NULL | XMAS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flags Set | FIN | None | FIN, PSH, URG |
| Stealth | High | High | High |
| Works on Linux | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Works on Windows | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| IDS Detection | Moderate | Moderate | Higher (unusual flags) |

**Common Limitation:** All three fail against Windows (Microsoft violates RFC 793). They also cannot reliably distinguish `OPEN` from `FILTERED`. *(7 marks total — 1.5 per scan + 1 for common RFC rule + 1.5 for comparison table + 1 for limitations)*

---

**Q. (b)** What is the role of Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) in service identification? Explain with examples of at least three NSE scripts from the `default` and `vuln` categories. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Role of NSE:**
The Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) is a **Lua-based scripting framework** that extends Nmap's capabilities beyond simple port scanning. It allows automated, customized probes for vulnerability detection, brute-force attacks, and advanced enumeration. Scripts are stored in `/usr/share/nmap/scripts/`.

**Script Selection Categories:**
- `default` — Run automatically with `-sC` or `-A`.
- `vuln` — Checks for known CVEs.
- `auth` — Authentication bypass / credential testing.
- `brute` — Brute-force attacks.
- `discovery` — Deeper enumeration of services.

**Three Example Scripts:**

1. **`smb-vuln-ms17-010`** (vuln category)
   - Command: `nmap --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 -p 445 <target>`
   - Purpose: Detects whether the target is vulnerable to **EternalBlue** (CVE-2017-0144), the SMBv1 exploit used by WannaCry.

2. **`http-title`** (default category)
   - Command: `nmap --script http-title -p 80,443 <target>`
   - Purpose: Grabs the HTML `<title>` tag from HTTP responses, often revealing the application name (e.g., "Login - WordPress" or "Apache2 Ubuntu Default Page").

3. **`ssh-brute`** (brute category)
   - Command: `nmap --script ssh-brute -p 22 <target>`
   - Purpose: Attempts SSH login using credential lists from `nselib/data/passwords.lst`. Useful for testing weak passwords on `OpenSSH`.

**Why NSE Matters:** It transforms Nmap from a *scanner* into a *lightweight vulnerability assessment tool*, bridging Module 4 (Scanning) and Module 5 (Exploitation). *(7 marks total — 2 for NSE overview, 1.5 per script, 1 for bridging to exploitation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse "no response" with "open" universally.** In a SYN scan, no response = FILTERED. In a FIN scan on Linux, no response = OPEN. Students lose 2–3 marks by misapplying this rule.
> 2. **Do not write "XMAS means Christmas."** Examiners want the flag combination (`FIN+PSH+URG`) explained. Generic definitions score zero.
> 3. **SYN scan is NOT "stealthy against everything."** Modern stateful firewalls and IDS (Snort, Suricata) easily detect SYN scans by tracking half-open connections. Use phrases like "stealthier than Connect scan" — not "undetectable."
> 4. **Always mention root privileges** when discussing `-sS`, `-sU`, `-sV`, `-O`. Examiners mark this as a sign of practical knowledge.
> 5. **Do not confuse Service Detection with OS Fingerprinting.** They use *different* signature databases (`nmap-service-probes` vs. `nmap-os-db`).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Open Port/Service Identification**

- **Definition:** The 2nd phase of ethical hacking (post-footprinting) that maps open/closed/filtered ports and identifies the services running on them.
- **Port Ranges:** 0–1023 (Well-Known), 1024–49151 (Registered), 49152–65535 (Dynamic). IANA governs; OS does not enforce.
- **TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`):** Full handshake, no root needed, easily logged.
- **SYN Scan (`-sS`):** Half-open, requires root, **default and preferred** technique.
- **FIN/NULL/XMAS:** Stealth scans; rely on RFC 793 silent-drop rule; **fail on Windows**.
- **UDP Scan (`-sU`):** Uses ICMP Port Unreachable to find closed ports; slow.
- **ACK Scan (`-sA`):** Maps firewall ACLs; not for port state.
- **Service Version (`-sV`):** Uses `nmap-service-probes` DB to match response signatures.
- **OS Detection (`-O`):** Uses `nmap-os-db`; based on TCP/IP stack fingerprinting (TTL, window size, DF bit).
- **Aggressive Mode (`-A`):** Combines `-O`, `-sV`, `-sC`, `--traceroute`.
- **NSE:** Lua-based scripting engine; categories include `default`, `vuln`, `auth`, `brute`, `discovery`.
- **Three Port States:** OPEN (SYN-ACK), CLOSED (RST), FILTERED (no response / ICMP unreachable).
- **UDP Ambiguity:** No response = OPEN or FILTERED; can only confirm CLOSED via ICMP Type 3, Code 3.
- **Real-World Output:** Service name + version is the direct input to CVE lookup → vulnerability analysis → exploitation.
- **Tools Beyond Nmap:** Masscan (Internet-scale), Zmap, Unicornscan, Angry IP Scanner (GUI), Netcat (manual).
- **Defensive Counterparts:** Port knocking, single-packet authorization (SPA), fail2ban, port-triggered firewalls.
- **Legal Note (KTU Ethics Section):** Scanning without written authorization is a criminal offense under the **IT Act 2000 §43, §66** of India. Always obtain a *Rules of Engagement* document.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Banner/Version Check
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Banner/Version Check

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Banner Grabbing (Banner/Version Check)** is a *passive or active reconnaissance technique* used in the **Scanning & Enumeration** phase of ethical hacking, in which an attacker connects to a remote host and reads the **service identification banner** (also called the *greeting message* or *version string*) returned by a network service on an open TCP or UDP port. The goal is to identify the **service type**, **vendor**, and **software version** running on the target system, so that known vulnerabilities associated with that specific version can be exploited later.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, this falls under the **Information Gathering / Footprinting → Scanning → Enumeration** pipeline, and is formally classified as a **Service Enumeration** activity under the **OSINT (Open Source Intelligence)** framework.

> [!NOTE] > **Service Banner** = a small text string automatically transmitted by a network daemon (e.g., web server, mail server, SSH daemon) the moment a client connects. It typically contains the **application name, version number, module name, and sometimes OS details**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you walk into an unfamiliar shop. Before you even ask anything, you notice the **name plate on the door** ("Sharma Electronics — Est. 2010"), the **model stickers on the window display** ("Sony Bravia X90L"), and the **uniform of the security guard** ("Securitas Pvt. Ltd."). All of this is *passive information* — you didn't ask, yet you already know the brand, age, and supplier of the shop.

**Banner/Version Check works exactly the same way on the Internet.** When a server is online, it *voluntarily* announces:

- Who it is (e.g., `Apache`)
- What version it is (e.g., `2.4.57`)
- What OS it sits on (e.g., `(Ubuntu)`)
- Sometimes even extra modules (e.g., `OpenSSL/1.1.1f`, `PHP/8.1.2`)

To an attacker, this is *gold* — because if the server proudly says "I am Apache 2.4.49", the attacker simply looks up *known exploits for Apache 2.4.49* and tries them.

> [!NOTE] > **One-line intuition:** Banner grabbing is the digital equivalent of reading a stranger's name badge from across the room — the information is *handed to you* if you know where to look.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Port** | A 16-bit logical endpoint (0–65535) | Universal identifier for a service |
| **TCP/23 (Telnet)** | Unencrypted remote login | Classic banner-grab target |
| **TCP/21 (FTP)** | File Transfer Protocol | Returns `220` greeting with version |
| **TCP/22 (SSH)** | Secure Shell | Sends `SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.2p1` on connect |
| **TCP/80/443 (HTTP/S)** | Web server | `Server:` header in HTTP response |
| **TCP/25 (SMTP)** | Mail server | `220 mail.example.com ESMTP Postfix` |
| **Fingerprinting** | Broader term — banner is *one* sub-technique | Distinguish OS-level vs service-level |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference:** Banner grabbing is formally catalogued as **MITRE ATT&CK Technique T1592.002 — Gather Victim Network Information: Software** and is the first practical step inside the **OWASP Testing Guide v4 — Section 4.2 (Information Gathering)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification

Banner/Version Check techniques are broadly split into **two categories**, which the KTU examiner frequently tests in Part-A:

### A) Active Banner Grabbing
The attacker **directly initiates a connection** to the target's open port. The target *sends back* the banner as a normal part of the TCP handshake or as a service greeting. This is **loud** — it is **logged** in the target's logs.

| Example Tool | Mechanism |
|--------------|-----------|
| `netcat -v target 80` | Raw TCP connect via Netcat |
| `telnet target 25` | Handshakes SMTP, reads `220` greeting |
| `curl -I http://target` | Reads HTTP `Server:` header |
| `nmap -sV target` | Probes + matches against `nmap-service-probes` DB |
| `openssl s_client -connect target:443` | Reads SSL/TLS certificate + banner |

### B) Passive Banner Grabbing
The attacker **observes traffic already flowing** on the network (e.g., via packet capture / IDS tap) **without ever sending a packet to the target**. This is **silent** — the target has *no idea* it was observed.

| Example Tool | Mechanism |
|--------------|-----------|
| **Wireshark / tcpdump** | Captures banners from existing sessions |
| **P0f** | Passive OS fingerprinting |
| **Search engines (Shodan, Censys)** | Reads banners from prior Internet-wide scans |
| **NetFlow analysis** | Indirect version inference |

> [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Examiners love asking *"Distinguish between active and passive banner grabbing."* Memorize the **one-line contrast**: *Active = attacker sends a packet; Passive = attacker only watches.*

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Banner Grabbing — Attacker's Motivation

The flow is logical and must be memorized for KTU ESE:

1. **Reconnaissance phase** — attacker enumerates live hosts (ping sweep).
2. **Port scanning phase** — attacker identifies *open ports* (e.g., 22, 80, 3306).
3. **Service enumeration phase** — attacker reads the banner to know *what software + version* is listening on that port.
4. **Vulnerability mapping phase** — attacker cross-references the version with databases like **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)**, **NVD**, or **Exploit-DB**.
5. **Exploitation phase** — attacker runs a matching exploit.

> **Example real-world chain:**
> Banner says → `Apache/2.4.49` → Attacker searches → **CVE-2021-41773** (Path Traversal RCE) → Compromise.

## 2.3 The "How" — Service-by-Service Banner Mechanics

### HTTP (Port 80) & HTTPS (Port 443)

The **HTTP response header** `Server:` and sometimes `X-Powered-By:` leak the banner:

$$\text{HTTP/1.1 200 OK} \\ \text{Server: Apache/2.4.57 (Ubuntu)} \\ \text{X-Powered-By: PHP/8.1.2}$$

Mechanism: server *sends* these headers *unsolicited* in every response. Therefore **passive** Wireshark capture reveals them.

### SSH (Port 22)

$$\text{SSH-2.0-OpenSSH\_8.2p1\ Ubuntu-4ubuntu0.5}$$

The SSH daemon sends this line **immediately** after the TCP handshake — before any authentication. There is **no encryption** on the protocol-version string itself.

### FTP (Port 21)

$$\text{220\ (vsFTPd\ 3.0.3)}$$

The server prints `220` (positive completion) followed by a human-readable string identifying itself.

### SMTP (Port 25)

$$\text{220\ mail.example.com\ ESMTP\ Postfix\ (Ubuntu)}$$

Sent on connection; a **rich fingerprint** of the mail transfer agent (MTA) + OS.

### SMB / NetBIOS (Ports 139, 445)

SMB *negotiation* response includes `NT LM 0.12 (SMBv1) [Windows 10 Pro 19045]` — leaks **OS build number**.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters to KTU students:** Every service *wants* to identify itself for legitimate protocol reasons (client compatibility, debugging). The vulnerability is that *attackers weaponize* this helpful identification.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — Service ↔ Port ↔ Banner Tool

| Service | Default Port | Protocol | Banner Source | Best Grab Tool |
|---------|--------------|----------|---------------|----------------|
| FTP | **21/TCP** | TCP | `220` greeting | `nc -v`, `nmap -sV` |
| SSH | **22/TCP** | TCP | Protocol version line | `nc -v`, `ssh -v` |
| Telnet | **23/TCP** | TCP | Login prompt header | `telnet` |
| SMTP | **25/TCP** | TCP | `220 ESMTP` line | `nc -v 25` |
| DNS | **53/TCP+UDP** | Both | Version.bind CHAOS class | `dig version.bind TXT CH` |
| HTTP | **80/TCP** | TCP | `Server:` header | `curl -I`, `nc` |
| POP3 | **110/TCP** | TCP | `+OK` greeting | `nc` |
| IMAP | **143/TCP** | TCP | `* OK` IMAP banner | `nc` |
| HTTPS | **443/TCP** | TCP | TLS cert + HTTP header | `openssl s_client`, `curl -Ik` |
| SMB | **445/TCP** | TCP | Negotiate Protocol Response | `nmap --script smb-os-discovery` |
| RDP | **3389/TCP** | TCP | TPKT + Cookie | `nmap -sV --script rdp-enum-encryption` |
| MySQL | **3306/TCP** | TCP | Greeting packet | `nc -v 3306` |
| PostgreSQL | **5432/TCP** | TCP | Startup message | `nc -v 5432` |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real-World Domains

| Domain | Why Banner Check Matters |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **Penetration Testing** | First step in any engagement to scope vulnerabilities |
| **Network Asset Management** | CMDB population, license auditing |
| **Compliance Auditing** | Detecting *unauthorized* or *outdated* software (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001) |
| **Threat Intelligence** | Shodan/Censys index *billions* of banners for global exposure mapping |
| **Incident Response** | Rapidly confirm if a service is patched against a known CVE |
| **DevSecOps** | Blocking deployment of containers exposing version info in headers |

## 2.6 Countermeasures (Defensive Side — KTU Loves This)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Hardening Rules (must memorize):**
> 1. **Suppress / modify banners** — change config files to remove version strings (e.g., `ServerTokens Prod` in Apache; `sshd_config → DebianBanner no`).
> 2. **Use wrappers** — front-end servers (reverse proxies like Nginx) that *strip* the backend version.
> 3. **Restrict port exposure** — firewall rules; bind services to `127.0.0.1`.
> 4. **Patch discipline** — keep software updated so banner version has *no known exploit*.
> 5. **Honeypot/Honeypot banners** — feed *fake* version strings to mislead attackers.
> 6. **Network-level encryption** — e.g., DoH (DNS over HTTPS) prevents DNS banner leak.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Analytical Matrix

> *Banner/Version Check is procedural, not algebraic. The "derivation" here is therefore the **logical step-by-step methodology** that an attacker (or pentester) follows. The KTU 2024 scheme examiner rewards procedure-explanation questions for **7–14 marks**.*

## 3.1 The Five-Stage Analytical Procedure

### **STAGE 1 — Host Discovery (Pre-Recon)**
Before reading a banner, the attacker must confirm a host is alive.

**Step 1.1:** Identify target IP range (e.g., `192.168.1.0/24`).

**Step 1.2:** Perform a **ping sweep** to find live hosts.

$$\text{Live Hosts} = \{h \in \text{Network} \mid \text{ICMP Echo Reply received from } h\}$$

Common tools: `nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24`, `fping -g 192.168.1.0/24`.

**Step 1.3:** Build a candidate list of IPs. *Banner grabbing cannot occur on a host whose existence is unconfirmed.*

### **STAGE 2 — Port Scanning**
For every live host, identify *open* ports.

**Step 2.1:** Initiate a **TCP SYN scan** (half-open scan) using `nmap -sS target`.

**Step 2.2:** The attacker classifies port state based on the response packet:

| Response | Port State | Banner Possible? |
|----------|------------|------------------|
| `SYN-ACK` | **Open** | ✅ Yes |
| `RST` | **Closed** | ❌ No |
| No response / ICMP unreachable | **Filtered** | ❌ No |

**Step 2.3:** Output: a list of `(IP, open_port)` tuples.

### **STAGE 3 — Service Detection (The Core Banner Step)**

For each open port, the attacker attempts to retrieve the banner. This is done via **three sub-techniques**, in increasing order of sophistication:

#### Sub-Technique 3A — Direct TCP Connection (Manual Banner Grab)

**Step 3A.1:** Open a raw TCP socket to `(target_ip, open_port)`.

**Step 3A.2:** Wait for the server to transmit its greeting.

**Step 3A.3:** Read and parse the ASCII response for version string.

**Example** — grabbing SSH banner via Netcat:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Command:} \quad & \texttt{nc\ -v\ 192.168.1.10\ 22} \\ \text{Output:} \quad & \texttt{SSH-2.0-OpenSSH\_8.2p1\ Ubuntu-4ubuntu0.5} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3A.4:** Cross-reference the parsed string with known CVE databases.

#### Sub-Technique 3B — Protocol-Specific Probe

Some services do **not** send an unsolicited banner. The attacker must *trigger* a specific protocol exchange.

**Example** — HTTP requires a valid `GET` request:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Command:} \quad & \texttt{curl\ -I\ http://192.168.1.10} \\ \text{Output:} \quad & \texttt{HTTP/1.1\ 200\ OK} \\ & \texttt{Server:\ Apache/2.4.57\ (Ubuntu)} \\ & \texttt{X-Powered-By:\ PHP/8.1.2} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3B.1:** Send protocol-appropriate handshake (e.g., `EHLO test` for SMTP).
**Step 3B.2:** Capture response.
**Step 3B.3:** Parse for `Server:`, `X-Powered-By:`, `User-Agent:` of responding daemons.

#### Sub-Technique 3C — Nmap Service/Version Detection (Automated)

**Step 3C.1:** Invoke `nmap -sV target`. The `-sV` flag enables version probing.

**Step 3C.2:** Nmap consults its built-in database `nmap-service-probes` (≈ 16,000 signatures).

**Step 3C.3:** For each open port, Nmap sends one or more probe packets and matches the response against the signature database.

$$\text{Confidence Score} = \frac{\text{Matched Probe Patterns}}{\text{Total Patterns in Signature}}$$

**Step 3C.4:** Output includes a confidence percentage.

**Example output:**

$$\begin{aligned} \texttt{PORT\ \ \ STATE\ SERVICE\ VERSION} \\ \texttt{22/tcp\ open\ ssh\ \ \ \ OpenSSH\ 8.2p1\ Ubuntu\ 4\ (protocol\ 2.0)} \\ \texttt{80/tcp\ open\ http\ \ \ Apache\ httpd\ 2.4.57\ ((Ubuntu))} \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Insight:** Nmap's `-sV` is essentially **automated signature-based banner grabbing**. It is the *single most exam-relevant* command for this topic.

### **STAGE 4 — Vulnerability Mapping**

**Step 4.1:** The extracted `service:version` tuple is queried against:

- **NVD** (nvd.nist.gov)
- **Exploit-DB** (exploit-db.com)
- **CVE Mitre** (cve.mitre.org)
- **searchsploit** (offline mirror in Kali Linux)

**Step 4.2:** The attacker identifies CVEs whose *vulnerable_version_range* includes the detected version.

**Step 4.3:** Prioritize by **CVSS score** (Common Vulnerability Scoring System — 0 to 10).

### **STAGE 5 — Reporting (Defensive Side)**

In a *defensive* context (audit, blue team), the same procedure produces a **compliance report**:
- List of services
- Detected versions
- Whether they are *patched* / *end-of-life* / *vulnerable*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Active vs Passive Comparison

| Dimension | Active Banner Grab | Passive Banner Grab |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Source of packet** | Attacker initiates | Third-party / existing flow |
| **Logged on target?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Stealth** | Low | High |
| **Tools** | `nc`, `telnet`, `nmap -sV`, `curl` | `Wireshark`, `p0f`, `Shodan` |
| **Network position required** | Anywhere (Internet) | Usually *on-path* / same segment |
| **Data freshness** | Real-time | Stale (Shodan lags days/weeks) |
| **Detection difficulty** | Easy (IDS sees new connection) | Hard |
| **KTU marker keyword** | "Connect then read" | "Capture and analyze" |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Nmap Confidence Scoring)

Suppose `nmap -sV` matches **3 out of 4** probe patterns for a given signature. The confidence is:

$$\text{Confidence} = \frac{3}{4} \times 100\% = 75\%$$

This is reported in the `VERSION` column of the Nmap output. A confidence of **≥ 90%** is considered *high certainty*; **< 50%** is often flagged for manual verification.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** A common 7-mark question is: *"Explain the procedure of banner grabbing using Nmap, with an example output."* Structure the answer as: **Discover → Scan → Probe → Match → Report**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Banner Grabbing Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Machine] --> B[Step1 Host Discovery Ping Sweep]
    B --> C[Step2 TCP SYN Scan Identify Open Ports]
    C --> D{Is Port Open?}
    D -- No --> E[Mark as Closed or Filtered Skip]
    D -- Yes --> F[Step3 Initiate TCP Connect to Open Port]
    F --> G[Step3A Service Responds with Banner]
    G --> H[Step4 Parse Banner Extract Service and Version]
    H --> I[Step5 Query CVE Database NVD ExploitDB]
    I --> J{Vulnerable Version Found?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Step6 Launch Matching Exploit]
    J -- No --> L[Step7 Log and Move to Next Port]
    L --> M[Step8 Report Findings to Attacker Dashboard]
    E --> N[End of Scan]
    K --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Active vs Passive Banner Grabbing — Side-by-Side Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ActiveFlow["ACTIVE BANNER GRABBING - Attacker Sends Packets"]
        A1[Attacker] -- TCP SYN --> T1[Target Server]
        T1 -- SYN ACK --> A1
        A1 -- ACK --> T1
        T1 -- Service Banner --> A1
        A1 -- Logged on Target --> A1
    end

    subgraph PassiveFlow["PASSIVE BANNER GRABBING - Attacker Only Observes"]
        C1[Legitimate Client] -- Normal Traffic --> C2[Target Server]
        C2 -- Banner in Response --> C1
        Att[Attacker via Wireshark] -. Reads .-> C2
    end

    style ActiveFlow fill:#fff4e6
    style PassiveFlow fill:#e6f4ff
```

## 4.3 Defensive Countermeasure Stack (Layered Defense)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer1 Network Firewall] --> L2[Layer2 Reverse Proxy Nginx Strips Server Header]
    L2 --> L3[Layer3 Application Hardening ServerTokens Prod]
    L3 --> L4[Layer4 Patch Management Keep Software Updated]
    L4 --> L5[Layer5 IDS IPS Alerts on Service Probes]
    L5 --> L6[Result Minimized Banner Leakage]

    style L6 fill:#d4f7d4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define banner grabbing. Name any two tools used for it.** *(3 Marks)*
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 4 — PEEOT741]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Banner Grabbing** is a reconnaissance technique in which an attacker connects to an open port on a target system and reads the **service identification banner** automatically transmitted by the service. The banner typically reveals the **application name, version number, and sometimes the underlying operating system**. The collected information is then used to identify known vulnerabilities specific to that software version.
>
> **Two tools:**
> 1. **Netcat (`nc`)** — establishes a raw TCP connection and displays the banner.
> 2. **Nmap with `-sV` flag** — performs automated signature-based service/version detection.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between active and passive banner grabbing.** *(3 Marks)*
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 4 — PEEOT741]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Active Banner Grabbing | Passive Banner Grabbing |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Mechanism | Attacker directly connects to the target's open port and triggers the banner response | Attacker only observes/captures traffic already in the network |
| Detection | Target's logs will show the connection (easily detected by IDS) | No new traffic generated; target is unaware (stealthy) |
| Example tools | `netcat`, `telnet`, `nmap -sV`, `curl` | `Wireshark`, `p0f`, `Shodan` |
| Network position | Anywhere (just needs IP reachability) | Requires on-path / same broadcast domain / Internet-wide scanner |

> **One-liner:** *Active = connect and read; Passive = capture and analyze.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either (A)** *or* **(B)**. Each carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Methodological Question

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Module 4]`**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure of banner grabbing** in ethical hacking. Mention the role of Nmap's `-sV` flag. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** Describe **five common services** whose banners leak version information, and for each, show the **typical banner format** and a **hardening technique** to suppress the leak. *(7 Marks)*

### **Model Answer for Q-A (a):**

The banner grabbing procedure consists of **five sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Host Discovery:** The attacker first identifies live hosts in the target network using a ping sweep. Tools used: `nmap -sn`, `fping -g`.

**Stage 2 — Port Scanning:** Open ports on each live host are detected using a **TCP SYN scan** (`nmap -sS`). Only ports in the `open` state can yield a banner; `closed` and `filtered` ports cannot.

**Stage 3 — Service Detection / Banner Retrieval:** This is the core stage. Three sub-techniques are used:

- **3A — Direct connection (Netcat / Telnet):** Connect manually and read the service greeting.
- **3B — Protocol-specific probe (curl / openssl):** Send a protocol-appropriate handshake (e.g., `HEAD / HTTP/1.0`) and parse the response headers.
- **3C — Automated fingerprinting (Nmap `-sV`):** Nmap sends a series of probes from its built-in `nmap-service-probes` database and matches the responses against known signatures, returning a confidence score.

**Stage 4 — Vulnerability Mapping:** The extracted `service:version` is cross-referenced with **CVE / NVD / Exploit-DB** to find matching vulnerabilities.

**Stage 5 — Reporting / Exploitation:** Findings are documented. In a *defensive* audit, this becomes a compliance report; in an *offensive* test, the next step is running the matching exploit.

**Role of Nmap `-sV`:** The `-sV` flag enables **service and version detection**. Nmap probes each open port, sends a variety of protocol-specific packets, and matches the response against a database of over 16,000 service signatures. It outputs the service name, version string, and a confidence percentage — making it the most efficient and exam-relevant banner-grabbing tool.

### **Model Answer for Q-A (b):**

| # | Service (Port) | Typical Banner Format | Hardening Technique |
|---|----------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **HTTP (80)** | `Server: Apache/2.4.57 (Ubuntu)` | Apache: set `ServerTokens Prod` and `ServerSignature Off` in `httpd.conf` |
| 2 | **SSH (22)** | `SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.2p1 Ubuntu-4ubuntu0.5` | Edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` → add `DebianBanner no`; recompile without version string |
| 3 | **FTP (21)** | `220 (vsFTPd 3.0.3)` | vsFTPd: set `ftpd_banner=Welcome` to replace version; recompile without version macros |
| 4 | **SMTP (25)** | `220 mail.example.com ESMTP Postfix (Ubuntu)` | Postfix: set `smtpd_banner = $myhostname ESMTP` (drop version) in `main.cf` |
| 5 | **SMB (445)** | `NT LM 0.12 (SMBv1) [Windows 10 Pro 19045]` | Disable SMBv1 via Group Policy; enable SMB signing; restrict via firewall |

> The general hardening principle: **deploy a reverse proxy (Nginx)** in front of backend servers so the proxy strips the `Server:` header before the response leaves the network.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Module 4]`**

> **(a)** What is **banner grabbing**? Explain **how HTTP, FTP, and SSH services leak version information** through their banners, with examples. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **risks of version information exposure** and elaborate on **six countermeasures** that a system administrator can deploy to mitigate banner-grabbing attacks. *(7 Marks)*

### **Model Answer for Q-B (a):**

**Banner Grabbing** is the process of retrieving the identification string (banner) automatically sent by a network service when a client connects. The banner typically reveals the service software name and version number, which can be cross-referenced with vulnerability databases.

**How the three services leak version info:**

**1. HTTP (Port 80):** Every HTTP response includes a `Server:` header containing the web server software and version. For example, accessing `http://target` returns a response such as `Server: Apache/2.4.57 (Ubuntu)`. Additionally, the `X-Powered-By:` header may reveal scripting engines (e.g., `PHP/8.1.2`).

**2. FTP (Port 21):** Upon TCP connection, the FTP daemon immediately transmits a `220` status code followed by a human-readable identification string. For example: `220 (vsFTPd 3.0.3)`. This is sent *before* any authentication, so any unauthenticated user can read it.

**3. SSH (Port 22):** The SSH daemon sends its protocol version line as the very first message after the TCP handshake — *before encryption begins*. For example: `SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.2p1 Ubuntu-4ubuntu0.5`. The format `SSH-<protoversion>-<softwareversion><comments>` is fixed by the SSH standard, making it impossible to omit without recompiling the daemon.

### **Model Answer for Q-B (b):**

**Risks of version information exposure:**

- **Targeted exploitation:** Attackers look up *known CVEs* matching the version, then launch specific exploits.
- **OS fingerprinting:** Banners often reveal the underlying OS, allowing OS-specific attacks.
- **Compliance violation:** Regulatory standards (PCI-DSS, HIPAA) require hiding such information.
- **Reconnaissance enabler:** A leaked version is the first domino in the *kill chain* — without it, the attacker must run blind, expensive probes.

**Six countermeasures:**

1. **Modify configuration files** to remove version strings (e.g., `ServerTokens Prod` in Apache; `DebianBanner no` in SSH).
2. **Deploy a reverse proxy (Nginx/HAProxy)** in front of backend servers to strip the `Server:` header.
3. **Use firewall rules** to restrict access to management ports (22, 3306, 5432) — only allow trusted IPs.
4. **Maintain a strict patch management policy** — keep all software updated so any leaked version has *no public exploit*.
5. **Deploy an IDS/IPS** (e.g., Snort, Suricata) to detect and alert on version-probing traffic patterns.
6. **Use a deceptive banner** — feed attackers fake version strings to mislead them into wasting effort on non-existent exploits.

> [!WARNING] > **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Reasons:**
> 1. *Confusing banner grabbing with port scanning.* — Port scanning finds open ports; banner grabbing identifies the **software** behind those ports. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if these are conflated.
> 2. *Forgetting that the banner is sent **before** authentication on most services (FTP, SSH, SMTP).* — Many students incorrectly say "only authenticated users can see it." **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 3. *Writing only tool names (`nmap`, `netcat`) without explaining **how** they extract the banner.* — Examiners want the *mechanism*, not just the tool. **−2 marks.**
> 4. *In countermeasures, listing only "use a firewall" without service-level config changes (e.g., `ServerTokens Prod`).* — Mark deduction **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 5. *Skipping the CVE cross-reference step* in the procedure — this is what *connects* banner grabbing to exploitation. **−1 mark** if omitted.
> 6. *Calling `nmap -sV` "active" while also writing that it is "passive"* — pick one; it is **active** (it sends packets to the target).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> 📌 **Memorize before the KTU exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Banner grabbing = reading the service identification string from a target's open port.
- ✅ **Two flavours:** **Active** (attacker sends packets — logged) vs **Passive** (attacker observes — silent).
- ✅ **Pipeline:** Host Discovery → Port Scan → Service/Version Detection → CVE Lookup → Exploit.
- ✅ **Core commands:** `nc -v host port`, `nmap -sV host`, `curl -I http://host`, `openssl s_client -connect host:443`.
- ✅ **Nmap `-sV`** uses the `nmap-service-probes` signature DB and outputs a **confidence score**.
- ✅ **Banners leak in:** HTTP `Server:` header, SSH protocol-version line, FTP `220` greeting, SMTP `220 ESMTP`, SMB negotiate response.
- ✅ **Banners are sent BEFORE authentication** on most services — this is the key attack surface.
- ✅ **Hardening shortcuts to memorize:** `ServerTokens Prod` (Apache), `DebianBanner no` (SSH), reverse proxy with header stripping.
- ✅ **Legal/ethical note:** Unauthorized banner grabbing is a cybercrime under **IT Act 2000 §66** (India) / equivalent statutes — only perform in authorized pentests.
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK link:** Technique **T1592.002** — Gather Victim Software Information.
- ✅ **One-line KTU definition (memorize verbatim):** *"Banner grabbing is the act of retrieving the service identification string broadcast by a network daemon on an open port to determine the software name and version for vulnerability assessment."*

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — Banner/Version Check**
> **PEEOT741 — Fundamentals of Cyber Security — KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traffic Probe
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Traffic Probe

# Traffic Probe — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the KTU **2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Fundamentals of Cyber Security*, a **Traffic Probe** is formally defined as:

> A **traffic probe** is a dedicated hardware device, software application, or hybrid mechanism strategically deployed within a computer network to **intercept, capture, mirror, copy, and analyze** data packets traversing the communication medium, without actively altering the original data flow.

The probe operates by attaching itself to a network segment (or by being configured virtually on a switch/router) and then exporting the observed traffic to a centralized analyzer, intrusion detection system (IDS), or forensic repository. The defining characteristics of a traffic probe are:

- **Non-intrusive observation** — does not normally modify payload.
- **Protocol-aware dissection** — understands Layers 2 through 7 of the OSI/TCP-IP stack.
- **Continuous or triggered monitoring** — operates either in streaming mode or on rule-based triggers.
- **Metadata + payload extraction** — captures both headers and content (subject to policy).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (The "CCTV over a Highway" Model)**
>
> Imagine a multi-lane highway carrying thousands of cars (data packets) every second. You, as a traffic analyst, want to know **what kind of vehicles are passing, how fast they are moving, where they came from, and where they are going** — but you are **not allowed to stop, redirect, or modify** any vehicle. What do you do?
>
> You build a **camera gantry (probe)** above the highway that **silently photographs and logs** every vehicle's number plate, color, weight, and route. This is exactly what a network traffic probe does — it sits quietly on the wire (or virtually on a switch port) and **watches the digital traffic flow**, recording everything for later analysis.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized metrics are used to characterize a traffic probe's performance (highlighted per KTU 2024 board expectations):

- **Bandwidth Capacity:** typically **1 Gbps**, **10 Gbps**, **40 Gbps**, or **100 Gbps** line-rate support.
- **Packet Capture Rate (PCR):** measured in **packets per second (pps)**.
- **Bit Error Rate (BER) of probe channel:** typically **< 10⁻¹²** for fiber-based TAPs.
- **Buffer Size:** **64 MB to 4 GB** depending on burst capture requirements.
- **Latency Introduced:** **< 1 microsecond (µs)** for passive optical TAPs.
- **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit):** standard **1500 bytes** (Ethernet), with **jumbo frames** up to **9000 bytes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4):**
> Students must be able to **distinguish between passive and active probing**, explain **SPAN port mirroring versus network TAP architectures**, and identify the **role of probes in Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), Network Forensics, and Performance Monitoring**.

---

## 2. Classification of Traffic Probes

A traffic probe can be classified along multiple axes:

### 2.1 By Mode of Operation

| Probe Type | Description | Visibility to Network | Example |
|------------|-------------|----------------------|---------|
| **Passive Probe** | Listens silently to traffic without injecting packets | **Invisible** to endpoints | Tcpdump, Wireshark in promiscuous mode, optical TAP |
| **Active Probe** | Sends crafted packets (e.g., ping, traceroute, ICMP echo) to elicit responses | **Detectable** by endpoints | Nmap, ping sweep, SNMP poller |

### 2.2 By Physical Implementation

| Implementation | Description | Use Case |
|---------------|-------------|----------|
| **Hardware TAP (Test Access Point)** | Physical splitter that copies optical/electrical signals | High-fidelity data centers |
| **SPAN Port (Switched Port Analyzer)** | Software-configured mirror of switch ports | Cisco-style enterprise switches |
| **Software Probe** | Runs on a host NIC in promiscuous mode | Endpoint monitoring, debugging |
| **Virtual TAP (vTAP)** | Operates in hypervisor/virtual switch layer | Cloud, SDN, VMware NSX |

### 2.3 By Deployment Location

- **Inline Probe** — placed directly in the data path (introduces latency but sees all traffic).
- **Tap/Tee Probe** — splitter off the main line (true passive).
- **Out-of-Band Probe** — receives mirrored traffic from a SPAN port.
- **Cloud-Native Probe** — agent-based, installed on cloud workloads (e.g., AWS VPC Traffic Mirroring).
]<]minimax[>[</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 3. Operational Architecture of a Traffic Probe

The end-to-end operational flow of a traffic probe can be broken into **six sequential phases**, each of which a KTU examiner can frame as a 2-mark sub-question:

### Phase 1 — Link Acquisition
The probe attaches to a physical or logical network segment. This can be:
- A copper RJ-45 tap inserted between two endpoints.
- A fiber LC/SC splitter on an optical link.
- A SPAN session on a managed switch.

### Phase 2 — Signal Copying / Mirroring
- **Optical TAP** uses a **passive beam-splitter** (typically 70/30 or 50/50 split ratio) to forward a copy of the light signal to the probe port while letting the original continue uninterrupted.
- **SPAN port** uses the switch's ASIC to copy frames from one or more source ports to a designated destination (monitor) port.

### Phase 3 — Frame Reception & Decapsulation
The probe's NIC receives raw Ethernet frames and strips the Layer-2 encapsulation to reveal the IP header, TCP/UDP header, and payload.

### Phase 4 — Protocol Parsing & Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)
Each frame is parsed against known protocol signatures (HTTP, DNS, TLS, SMB, etc.). DPI may extend up to Layer 7.

### Phase 5 — Storage / Forwarding
Captured frames are either:
- Stored locally in **PCAP (Packet Capture)** format, or
- Streamed in real time via **IPFIX / sFlow / NetFlow** to a collector.

### Phase 6 — Analysis & Alerting
An IDS engine (Snort, Suricata, Zeek) or a SIEM correlates captured events and raises alerts.

> [!TIP]
> **Why and How — The Core Intuition**
>
> - **Why a SPAN port drops packets under load?** Because the SPAN destination port shares the same backplane as data ports, so during congestion the switch ASIC **prioritizes forwarding over mirroring** (called *SPAN port oversubscription*).
> - **Why a hardware TAP never drops packets?** Because it is **passive and line-rate** — copying is done at the physical layer with zero software buffering.
> - **How does promiscuous mode help?** It disables MAC-address filtering on the NIC so the probe sees **all** frames on the segment, not just those destined to it.

---

## 4. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes `|` are used inside table cells to avoid markdown breakage — vertical bars in formulas are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` instead.**

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|--------------------|----------------------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Packet Capture Rate (PCR)** | $\text{PCR} = \dfrac{N_{pkt}}{T_{window}}$ | packets per second (pps) | $N_{pkt}$ = packets captured in $T_{window}$ seconds |
| 2 | **Average Packet Size** | $\bar{L} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i}{N}$ | bytes | Used to estimate storage requirements |
| 3 | **Required Storage for Capture** | $S = N_{pkt} \times \bar{L}$ | bytes | Plus ~24 B per-packet header overhead |
| 4 | **Effective Throughput** | $T_{eff} = \dfrac{\text{Total Useful Bytes}}{T_{capture}}$ | bits per second (bps) | $T_{eff} \le T_{link}$ always holds |
| 5 | **Drop Probability (SPAN)** | $P_{drop} = 1 - \dfrac{R_{span}}{R_{in}} \quad \text{if } R_{in} > R_{span}$ | dimensionless | $R_{span}$ = mirror port rate, $R_{in}$ = input rate |
| 6 | **SNR of Probe Channel** | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | decibels (dB) | For optical TAPs, $\text{SNR} \ge 20 \text{ dB}$ recommended |
| 7 | **TAP Split Ratio (Optical)** | $P_{out} = P_{in} \times \left(1 - \alpha\right)$ | milliwatts (mW) | $\alpha$ = split loss factor; 50:50 → 3 dB loss |
| 8 | **Capture Time Resolution** | $\Delta t = \dfrac{1}{f_{sample}}$ | seconds | $f_{sample}$ = NIC sampling clock |
| 9 | **Bit Error Rate Requirement** | $\text{BER} < 10^{-12}$ | dimensionless | Fiber TAPs typically satisfy this |
| 10 | **Bezek Log Format Fields** | $(ts, uid, orig\_ip, orig\_p, resp\_ip, resp\_p, proto, service, duration, orig\_bytes, resp\_bytes, conn\_state)$ | — | Standard Zeek/Bro schema |

---

## 5. Real-World Engineering Utility

Traffic probes are the **backbone of every modern Network Operations Center (NOC)** and **Security Operations Center (SOC)**. Their engineering utility spans:

- **Network Performance Monitoring (NPM):** Latency, jitter, packet loss analysis (Cisco NAM, NetFlow, sFlow).
- **Intrusion Detection / Prevention (IDS/IPS):** Suricata, Snort, Zeek (Bro) consume probe feeds.
- **Digital Forensics & Incident Response (DFIR):** PCAP files become courtroom-grade evidence.
- **Application Performance Management (APM):** Transaction tracing, TCP retransmission analysis.
- **Compliance & Auditing:** GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS mandate traffic logging of sensitive flows.
- **Threat Hunting:** Behavioral baselining of east-west traffic in data centers.
- **QoS Engineering:** DiffServ marking validation, SLA verification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Snapshot (Kerala / India Context):** KTU-affiliated companies such as **Tata Elxsi (Trivandrum), Infosys (Kochi), UST Global, and IBS Software** routinely use traffic probes for both product development and SOC operations.
]<]minimax[>[</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 6. Worked Derivation 1 — Estimating PCAP Storage for a Full-Duplex 1 Gbps Link

### Problem Statement
A KTU-lab network administrator wants to capture traffic from a **1 Gbps full-duplex Ethernet link** continuously for **24 hours** using a hardware TAP feeding Wireshark/Tcpdump. Assuming an **average frame size of 800 bytes** (including preamble, IPG, and Ethernet overhead) and that **~40 % of link capacity is utilized** during business hours but **~5 %** during off-hours, estimate the **total PCAP file size in gigabytes (GB)**.

### Given Data
- Link rate: $R = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bits/s}$ (per direction).
- Full-duplex → both directions carry traffic simultaneously.
- Average frame size: $L_{avg} = 800 \text{ bytes} = 800 \times 8 = 6400 \text{ bits}$.
- Capture duration: $T = 24 \text{ h} = 86\,400 \text{ s}$.
- Utilization: business hours = 12 h at 40 %; off-hours = 12 h at 5 %.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Effective throughput per direction**

For business hours:
$$
T_{biz} = R \times U_{biz} = 10^9 \times 0.40 = 4 \times 10^8 \text{ bps}
$$

For off-hours:
$$
T_{off} = R \times U_{off} = 10^9 \times 0.05 = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 2 — Packets per second (PCR) in each window**

Business hours:
$$
\text{PCR}_{biz} = \dfrac{T_{biz}}{L_{avg}} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^8}{6400} = 62\,500 \text{ pps}
$$

Off-hours:
$$
\text{PCR}_{off} = \dfrac{T_{off}}{L_{avg}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^7}{6400} \approx 7\,812.5 \text{ pps}
$$

**Step 3 — Total packets captured in each window**

Business window (12 h = 43 200 s):
$$
N_{biz} = \text{PCR}_{biz} \times T_{biz,h} = 62\,500 \times 43\,200 = 2.7 \times 10^9 \text{ packets}
$$

Off-hours window (12 h = 43 200 s):
$$
N_{off} = \text{PCR}_{off} \times T_{off,h} = 7\,812.5 \times 43\,200 \approx 3.375 \times 10^8 \text{ packets}
$$

**Step 4 — Total bytes of raw payload per direction**

$$
B_{raw} = (N_{biz} + N_{off}) \times L_{avg}
$$
$$
B_{raw} = (2.7 \times 10^9 + 3.375 \times 10^8) \times 800
$$
$$
B_{raw} = 3.0375 \times 10^9 \times 800 = 2.43 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Full-duplex adjustment (Tx + Rx captured)**

$$
B_{duplex} = 2 \times B_{raw} = 4.86 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6 — Add per-packet PCAP header overhead (typically 24 bytes per packet)**

$$
B_{overhead} = (N_{biz} + N_{off}) \times 24 = 3.0375 \times 10^9 \times 24 = 7.29 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 7 — Total PCAP storage**

$$
B_{total} = B_{duplex} + B_{overhead} = 4.86 \times 10^{12} + 7.29 \times 10^{10} \approx 4.933 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to GB (decimal, 1 GB = 10⁹ B) and GiB (binary, 1 GiB = 2³⁰ B)**

$$
B_{total,GB} = \dfrac{4.933 \times 10^{12}}{10^9} \approx 4\,933 \text{ GB} \quad (\text{decimal})
$$
$$
B_{total,GiB} = \dfrac{4.933 \times 10^{12}}{1.0737 \times 10^9} \approx 4\,594 \text{ GiB} \quad (\text{binary})
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A 1 Gbps link at moderate utilization can produce **~5 TB of PCAP per day**, which is why production SOCs use **flow records (NetFlow/sFlow) as a lossy first-line filter** and store full PCAPs only for flagged flows.

---

## 7. Worked Derivation 2 — Verifying That a SPAN Port Will Drop Packets

### Problem Statement
A 24-port Gigabit Cisco switch has a SPAN session mirroring **four full-duplex 1 Gbps ports** to a single 1 Gbps destination monitor port. Determine the **drop probability** and the **percentage of frames lost** if all four source ports are simultaneously saturated at **900 Mbps each**.

### Given Data
- Number of mirrored source ports: $n = 4$.
- Each source: $R_{in} = 900 \text{ Mbps} = 9 \times 10^8 \text{ bps}$ (per direction, but mirroring captures both directions, so effectively $2 \times 900 = 1800$ Mbps per port observed by SPAN).
- Monitor port capacity: $R_{span} = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bps}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total traffic offered to the SPAN destination**

The SPAN engine must forward all ingress + egress frames from all four ports, so:
$$
R_{offered} = n \times 2 \times R_{in} = 4 \times 2 \times 900 = 7\,200 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with monitor port capacity**

Since $R_{offered} = 7\,200 \text{ Mbps} > R_{span} = 1\,000 \text{ Mbps}$, **the monitor port is oversubscribed by a factor of 7.2×**.

**Step 3 — Compute drop probability (using the simple loss model)**

$$
P_{drop} = 1 - \dfrac{R_{span}}{R_{offered}} = 1 - \dfrac{1\,000}{7\,200} \approx 0.8611
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage of frames lost**

$$
\%\text{Loss} = P_{drop} \times 100\% \approx 86.11\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Many students forget that SPAN mirroring captures **both ingress and egress** traffic. Writing $R_{offered} = 4 \times 900$ instead of $4 \times 2 \times 900$ is a common 2-mark deduction. Always state **"ingress + egress"** explicitly.

---

## 8. Worked Derivation 3 — Loss Budget for an Optical TAP

### Problem Statement
An optical TAP uses a **50:50 beam-splitter** to tap a single-mode fiber link carrying 1310 nm laser light at **0 dBm input power**. Compute (a) the power delivered to the probe port and (b) the SNR assuming the detector has a noise floor of **−45 dBm**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recognize the 50:50 split**

A 50:50 splitter introduces a **3 dB insertion loss** on each output branch:
$$
L_{split} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -3 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Power at probe port**

$$
P_{probe,dBm} = P_{in,dBm} + L_{split} = 0 + (-3) = -3 \text{ dBm}
$$

In linear units:
$$
P_{probe,mW} = 10^{\frac{-3}{10}} = 0.5012 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 3 — SNR calculation**

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = P_{probe,dBm} - P_{noise,dBm} = -3 - (-45) = 42 \text{ dB}
$$

In linear ratio:
$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{\frac{42}{10}} \approx 15\,849
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** An SNR of **42 dB** is well above the typical **20 dB** threshold required for clean packet reconstruction, confirming that a 50:50 optical TAP is more than adequate for full-duplex 10 Gbps links.
]<]minimax[>[</br>

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 9. Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Traffic Probe System

The diagram below illustrates the full functional pipeline of a traffic probe deployment, from physical signal acquisition to SOC alerting. Node IDs follow the alpha-prefix rule (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHY["Physical Network Segment"]
        node1["Source Host A"] -->|Ethernet Frame| node2["Switch / Router Core"]
        node2 -->|Forwarded Frame| node3["Destination Host B"]
    end

    subgraph TAP["Probe Acquisition Layer"]
        node4["Hardware Optical TAP or SPAN Port"]
        node5["Signal Splitter 50:50"]
    end

    subgraph CAPT["Capture & Decode Layer"]
        node6["Probe NIC in Promiscuous Mode"]
        node7["Frame Decapsulation Engine"]
        node8["Protocol Parser DPI up to Layer 7"]
    end

    subgraph STORE["Storage & Export Layer"]
        node9["PCAP Ring Buffer in RAM or Disk"]
        node10["NetFlow sFlow IPFIX Exporter"]
    end

    subgraph ANALYZE["Analysis & Detection Layer"]
        stepA["Signature Engine Suricata Snort"]
        stepB["Anomaly Engine Zeek Machine Learning"]
        stepC["Flow Correlator SIEM Splunk ELK"]
    end

    subgraph RESP["Response & Forensics Layer"]
        stepD["SOC Alerting Dashboard"]
        stepE["Forensic PCAP Archive Cold Storage"]
        stepF["Active Response Firewall Block"]
    end

    node2 -. Mirror Copy .-> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> stepA
    node9 --> stepB
    node10 --> stepC
    stepA --> stepD
    stepB --> stepD
    stepC --> stepD
    stepA --> stepE
    stepC --> stepF
    stepD --> stepF
```

### 9.1 SPAN vs TAP — Comparative Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA["Need full line-rate capture without drops?"] -->|Yes| branchA["Use Hardware Optical TAP"]
    startA -->|No| startB["Switch already supports SPAN?"]
    startB -->|Yes| branchB["Use SPAN Port Monitor"]
    startB -->|No| startC["Virtualization or cloud environment?"]
    startC -->|Yes| branchC["Deploy vTAP or VPC Traffic Mirroring"]
    startC -->|No| branchD["Endpoint-based software probe Tcpdump on host"]
```

### 9.2 Probe Data Reduction Pipeline (PCAP to Flow Records)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    step1["Raw PCAP Stream from Probe"] --> step2["L3 L4 Header Extraction"]
    step2 --> step3["5 Tuple Hash srcIP dstIP srcPort dstPort proto"]
    step3 --> step4["Aggregate Counters Packets Bytes Duration"]
    step4 --> step5["Export as NetFlow v9 or IPFIX"]
    step5 --> step6["Collector Splunk or ELK Stack"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three diagrams?** KTU 2024 scheme board questions often include a "draw and label" sub-part. These three diagrams collectively cover (i) full architecture, (ii) deployment decision, and (iii) data-reduction pipeline — the three most likely drawing prompts.
