# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 10. Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q.** Differentiate between a **passive traffic probe** and an **active traffic probe**. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive Probe | Active Probe |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| **Behavior** | Silently observes traffic; does not inject packets | Sends crafted probe packets and infers topology/behavior from responses |
| **Visibility** | Invisible to the network hosts and devices | Detectable by hosts and IDS sensors |
| **Example** | **Tcpdump** or **Wireshark** running in promiscuous mode on a mirrored SPAN port | **Nmap** ICMP/TCP scan, **ping** sweep, SNMP polling |
| **Risk** | Very low; no traffic injection | Medium; can trigger alerts or be mistaken for an attack |

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the contrast, 1 mark for correct examples.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q.** What is a **SPAN port**? State **one limitation** of using SPAN for traffic monitoring.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) port** — also called a *mirror port* — is a dedicated switch port configured to receive a copy of all frames traversing one or more designated source ports. It is widely used to feed traffic to monitoring tools such as IDS sensors, protocol analyzers, and packet recorders.

**Limitation (any one of the following, 1 mark):**
- **Oversubscription and packet drops** — when aggregate mirrored traffic exceeds the monitor port bandwidth, frames are silently dropped.
- **Re-ordering of frames** — the mirrored stream may interleave frames from multiple ports, breaking TCP sequence.
- **Switch ASIC load** — mirroring consumes backplane resources and can degrade forwarding performance.
- **Single point of failure** — if the monitor port fails, all visibility is lost.

*(Award: 2 marks for SPAN definition, 1 mark for limitation.)*

---

## 11. Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the **architecture of a network traffic probe** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate clearly between a **hardware TAP** and a **SPAN port**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A SOC team is using **Wireshark** on a SPAN port to capture traffic from a server port. During peak hours the team observes that **many packets are missing** from the capture even though the server is not congested. Diagnose the issue and propose a remedy with justification. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Architecture of a Traffic Probe (5 marks for the explanation + 2 marks for the diagram):**

1. **Link Acquisition Module (1 mark)** — Attaches to the physical link via copper/fiber TAP, or to a logical mirror via SPAN. The probe must be capable of running its NIC in **promiscuous mode** to receive all frames on the segment.

2. **Signal Copying Module (1 mark)** —
   - *Hardware TAP:* A passive optical/electrical splitter. For a 50:50 split the insertion loss is **3 dB** per branch. The original link is uninterrupted.
   - *SPAN Port:* The switch ASIC copies frames from source ports to a designated monitor port. Configured via CLI (e.g., `monitor session 1 source` and `monitor session 1 destination` on Cisco IOS).

3. **Frame Decapsulation Module (1 mark)** — The probe strips Ethernet, IP, and transport-layer headers, exposing the payload for parsing.

4. **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) Module (1 mark)** — Identifies protocols (HTTP, DNS, SMB, TLS) and matches signatures/heuristics.

5. **Storage and Export Module (1 mark)** — Saves to **PCAP-NG** files, or streams as **NetFlow/sFlow/IPFIX** to a collector.

**TAP vs SPAN Comparison (2 marks for explicit contrast):**

| Parameter | Hardware TAP | SPAN Port |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| Latency introduced | ~0 (passive optical) | 0 but consumes backplane |
| Packet drops under load | None (line-rate) | Yes (oversubscription) |
| Cost | Higher | Free (switch feature) |
| Failure impact | Probe goes blind, link continues | Switch backplane stress |
| Use case | High-fidelity DC, compliance | Quick deployment, low-budget SOC |

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Diagnosis (3 marks):**
The packet loss observed in the Wireshark capture, even though the server is not congested, is most likely caused by **SPAN port oversubscription**. The aggregate traffic from the mirrored source ports (including both ingress and egress) exceeds the **1 Gbps** capacity of the monitor port that Wireshark is listening on. When the switch ASIC's mirror buffer fills, it silently drops mirrored frames.

**Verification (2 marks):**
- Run `show monitor session all` on the Cisco switch to confirm the source and destination bandwidths.
- In Wireshark, observe the **capture drop indicator** at the bottom status bar (red counter) — each red counter increment is a dropped frame at the OS ring-buffer level, distinct from SPAN drops.

**Remedy (2 marks):**
- **Replace SPAN with a hardware TAP** (e.g., a 10 Gbps optical TAP feeding a 10 Gbps NIC on the analyzer host). This guarantees line-rate capture with zero drops.
- Alternatively, **reduce the number of mirrored source ports** in the SPAN session to match the monitor port capacity, or upgrade the monitor port to **10 Gbps** if the switch supports it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** What is **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)**? Explain how DPI is implemented in modern traffic probes with reference to signature-based and behavioral detection. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare **NetFlow**, **sFlow**, and **IPFIX** as traffic probe export protocols. In which scenario would you choose IPFIX over NetFlow? *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
**Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** is the process of examining the **header and payload** of network packets up to the application layer (Layer 7) in order to identify the protocol, application, command, or content being transmitted, and to enforce security or policy decisions.

**Signature-based DPI (3 marks):**
- Maintains a database of known attack **signatures** (e.g., Snort rules).
- Each packet payload is matched against these signatures using algorithms like **Aho-Corasick** or **Hyperscan**.
- Highly accurate for known threats; **cannot detect zero-day attacks**.
- Example: Suricata rule `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (content:"/cmd.exe"; msg:"Possible web shell";)`.

**Behavioral/Anomaly-based DPI (2 marks):**
- Builds a baseline of normal traffic behavior (e.g., typical packet sizes, inter-arrival times, flow durations).
- Uses **statistical models**, **machine learning** (k-means, isolation forest), or **autoencoders** to flag deviations.
- Can detect **zero-day attacks** and **insider threats**, but produces more false positives.
- Example: Zeek's `notice` framework flags a sudden spike in DNS NXDOMAIN responses as a possible DNS tunneling attempt.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | NetFlow v9 | sFlow v5 | IPFIX (NetFlow v10) |
|---------|------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Origin** | Cisco (1990s) | InMon Corp | IETF RFC 7011 (standard) |
| **Sampling** | Flow-based (1:1) | **Packet-based statistical sampling** | Flow-based, configurable |
| **Layer** | L3/L4 mostly | L2–L7, supports SFP encapsulation | L2–L7, fully extensible |
| **Export format** | UDP template-based | UDP, simple | UDP/TCP/SCTP, templated |
| **Scalability** | High | Very high (sampling reduces volume) | Very high |
| **Vendor support** | Cisco-centric | Multi-vendor, popular in HPC | Open standard, broadest support |
| **Record type count** | Limited templates | Fixed | **Vendor-extensible** (variable information elements) |

**When to choose IPFIX over NetFlow (2 marks):**
Choose **IPFIX** when:
- You operate a **multi-vendor environment** (non-Cisco switches, routers, firewalls) and need a **standardized, vendor-neutral** export protocol.
- You need to export **custom fields** (e.g., MPLS labels, GTP tunnel IDs, VM UUIDs, application-specific metadata) that NetFlow's fixed template cannot represent. IPFIX supports **variable Information Elements (IEs)** as defined in IANA's `IPFIX Information Element Registry`, making it the most flexible choice for modern 5G, SDN, and NFV deployments.

---

## 12. KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Traffic Probe" Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the full-duplex factor in SPAN calculations.** Always multiply source traffic by **2** (ingress + egress). A wrong factor here costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing TAP with TAP port.** "TAP" (Test Access Point) is the hardware device; "SPAN" is the software mirror. Mixing the two terms in the same answer shows shallow understanding.
> 3. **Writing "promiscuous mode" without explaining it.** Examiners expect: *"The NIC is set to accept all frames on the wire regardless of destination MAC address."*
> 4. **Ignoring legal/ethical context.** In India, interception is governed by the **IT Act 2000 (amended 2008), Section 69**. Probes must not capture content of communications without authorization in the case of personally identifiable information. A 1-mark bonus is often awarded to answers that mention lawful interception policy.
> 5. **Storage calculation without unit clarity.** Always state whether you are using **GB (10⁹)** or **GiB (2³⁰)**. A naive conversion is a 1-mark deduction.

---

## 13. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Traffic Probe** = mechanism to **copy and inspect** network packets without modifying the live flow.
- **Passive probes** listen silently (Tcpdump, Wireshark in promiscuous mode, optical TAP).
- **Active probes** inject probe packets (Nmap, ping, SNMP GET).
- **Hardware TAP** = passive, line-rate, zero-drop, no switch dependency, higher cost.
- **SPAN Port** = software mirror, may drop frames under oversubscription, free with managed switches.
- **Promiscuous mode** is required on the probe NIC for full-segment visibility.
- **DPI (Deep Packet Inspection)** parses up to Layer 7; uses **signature + behavioral** methods.
- **Export protocols:** NetFlow (Cisco), sFlow (sampled), IPFIX (IETF standard, extensible).
- **PCAP-NG** is the standard archive format; expect **~5 TB/day** from a moderately busy 1 Gbps link.
- **Throughput equation:** $T_{eff} = (\text{Useful Bytes})/T_{capture}$, bounded by $T_{link}$.
- **Drop probability for SPAN:** $P_{drop} = 1 - R_{span}/R_{offered}$ when $R_{offered} > R_{span}$.
- **Optical TAP split:** 50:50 → 3 dB loss, 70:30 → ~1.5 dB / 5 dB asymmetry.
- **BER requirement:** $< 10^{-12}$ for production probe channels.
- **KTU favorite draw question:** Block diagram of traffic probe showing **acquisition → decapsulation → DPI → storage → analysis → response**.
- **Real-world deployment:** Every SOC/NOC uses some combination of TAP + SPAN + flow exporter.
- **Legal note (India):** Probing must comply with **IT Act §69** and organizational **acceptable-use policy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 ESE:** Always end your answer to any "explain" question with a **one-line engineering conclusion** such as *"Hence, a traffic probe is the foundational building block of any network visibility and security-monitoring architecture."* This signals maturity to the examiner and often earns the concluding 1 mark.
]<]minimax[>[</br>

# 🔍 Vulnerability Probe — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Vulnerability Probe** is a systematic, methodical, and often automated process of actively interrogating information systems, network endpoints, applications, and configuration baselines to *discover, classify, validate, and prioritize* security weaknesses that an adversary could exploit. It is the technical heart of any Vulnerability Assessment (VA) and Penetration Testing (PT) engagement.

---

## 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Night-Time Building Inspector"

Imagine you are a **security consultant** hired to audit a large office building before a heist. You are not the thief — you are the *prober*.

- You walk around the building (the **network**).
- You jiggle every door handle, window latch, and ventilation grate (the **services & ports**).
- You check which windows are made of glass vs. reinforced acrylic (the **service versions**).
- You ring the intercom at every office to hear who answers (the **banner grabbing**).
- You note the make & model of every digital lock installed (the **software fingerprinting**).
- Finally, you write a *report card* of weak spots and rank them by danger (the **CVSS scoring**).

A **Vulnerability Probe** is precisely this — except the building is a computer, and the doors are TCP/UDP ports, services, and APIs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A probe is **NOT** the actual exploitation. It is the *reconnaissance and identification* phase. The exploitation comes later, during a Penetration Test.

---

## 🧩 Where Does "Vulnerability Probe" Sit in Cyber Security?

```text
Cyber Security Posture
        │
        ├── Preventive Controls (Firewalls, IPS)
        ├── Detective Controls  ──► Vulnerability Probe  ◄── YOU ARE HERE
        ├── Corrective Controls (Patching)
        └── Recovery Controls  (Backups, DR)
```

The probe is a **detective control** — it finds what is *already broken* before an attacker does.

---

## 📐 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Meaning | Typical Range / Value |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| **CVE** | Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures — unique ID for a public flaw | e.g., CVE-2024-12345 |
| **CVSS** | Common Vulnerability Scoring System — severity score | **0.0 – 10.0** |
| **CVSS Base Score** | Intrinsic severity of the flaw | **0.0 (None) – 10.0 (Critical)** |
| **NVD** | National Vulnerability Database (US-NIST maintained) | nvd.nist.gov |
| **Zero-Day** | Vulnerability unknown to the vendor | No patch available |
| **Attack Surface** | Sum of all reachable points where an attacker can enter | Measured in ports, services, APIs |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question mentions *"classify a vulnerability"*, the examiner expects the **CVSS severity band** as the answer: `None (0.0)`, `Low (0.1–3.9)`, `Medium (4.0–6.9)`, `High (7.0–8.9)`, `Critical (9.0–10.0)`.

---

## ⚙️ Two Major Families of Vulnerability Probing

| Family | Mechanism | Detectability | Example Tool |
|--------|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Active Probe** | Sends crafted packets to the target & analyzes responses | Easily logged on target | **Nmap**, Nessus |
| **Passive Probe** | Sniffs existing network traffic without sending anything | Stealthy, near-undetectable | **Wireshark**, P0f |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 🏗️ The Vulnerability Probe — Operational Architecture

A probe is rarely a single action. It is a **multi-stage pipeline**. Each stage produces input for the next.

### **Stage 1 — Discovery (Footprinting)**
- Identify live hosts within the target IP range.
- Use ICMP echo requests, ARP sweeps, or TCP SYN ping.
- Output: A *host inventory list*.

### **Stage 2 — Port Scanning (Service Enumeration)**
- For every live host, enumerate open, closed, or filtered TCP/UDP ports.
- Port states per RFC 793:
  - **Open** — service actively accepting connections
  - **Closed** — port reachable, no service listening
  - **Filtered** — packet dropped (firewall interference)
  - **Unfiltered** — reachable, but state cannot be determined
  - **Open/Filtered** — probe cannot differentiate
  - **Closed/Filtered** — rare, mostly for IP sweeps

### **Stage 3 — Service & Version Detection (Banner Grabging)**
- Connect to open ports and read the service banner.
- Example: `SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.2p1 Ubuntu`
- Probes an **active** request, receives a **passive** response.

### **Stage 4 — OS Fingerprinting**
- Identify the remote Operating System by analyzing TCP/IP stack behavior.
- Two flavors:
  - **Active** — send crafted packets, analyze responses (Xmas, FIN, ACK).
  - **Passive** — sniff traffic and infer from TTL, window size, DF flag.

### **Stage 5 — Vulnerability Mapping**
- Compare discovered software versions against **CVE/NVD** databases.
- Apply **CVSS scoring** to rank severity.

### **Stage 6 — Validation (Safe Exploitation)**
- Run a *non-destructive* exploit to confirm the vulnerability is real.
- Differentiates a "scanner false positive" from a genuine flaw.

### **Stage 7 — Reporting & Remediation**
- Deliver a structured report with findings, CVSS, PoC, and patch advice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Scan Technique Selection:**
> The examiner loves asking: *"Which scan is stealthiest?"* — The answer is the **NULL scan** (no flags set), which often bypasses stateless firewalls because it violates RFC 793 and produces no SYN-ACK on closed ports, leaving minimal logs.

---

## 📊 The 6 Core TCP Scan Types — KTU Board Favourite

| Scan Type | Flag(s) Set | Response on Open Port | Stealth Level |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **TCP Connect** | None (full handshake) | SYN-ACK | ❌ Noisy, logged |
| **SYN (Half-Open)** | SYN | SYN-ACK | ✅ Stealthy |
| **FIN** | FIN | No response | ✅✅ Very stealthy |
| **NULL** | None of the flag bits | No response | ✅✅ Very stealthy |
| **Xmas** | FIN + PSH + URG | No response | ✅✅ Very stealthy |
| **ACK** | ACK | RST (always) | Used to map firewall rules |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Xmas"?** Because setting FIN, PSH, and URG simultaneously makes the packet look "lit up like a Christmas tree" in a packet analyzer. The name is from a 1998 Phrack article.

---

## 📐 KTU Formula Sheet — Vulnerability Probe Metrics

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{CVSS Base Score} &= \text{round\_up}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\, 10)) \\
\textbf{Risk} &= \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact} \\
\textbf{Attack Surface} &= \vert \text{Reachable Ports} \vert + \vert \text{Public APIs} \vert + \vert \text{User Input Fields} \vert \\
\textbf{Exposure Index (EI)} &= \frac{\text{Critical CVEs found}}{\text{Total assets scanned}} \times 100 \\
\textbf{Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)} &= \frac{\sum \text{Detection time per incident}}{N_{\text{incidents}}} \\
\textbf{False Positive Rate} &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Note for Students:** In KTU answer sheets, you may write these inline as `Risk = T × V × I`. The vertical bar for absolute value in tables has been written as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

## 🧮 CVSS v3.1 — Compact Reference Table

| Metric Group | Sub-Metrics | Values |
|--------------|------------|--------|
| **Base** | Attack Vector (AV) | Network, Adjacent, Local, Physical |
| | Attack Complexity (AC) | Low, High |
| | Privileges Required (PR) | None, Low, High |
| | User Interaction (UI) | None, Required |
| | Scope (S) | Unchanged, Changed |
| | Confidentiality (C) | None, Low, High |
| | Integrity (I) | None, Low, High |
| | Availability (A) | None, Low, High |
| **Temporal** | Exploit Code Maturity, Remediation Level, Report Confidence | - |
| **Environmental** | Modified base metrics, Security Requirements | - |

**Severity Bands (Base Score only):**

| Range | Severity |
|-------|----------|
| **0.0** | None |
| **0.1 – 3.9** | Low |
| **4.0 – 6.9** | Medium |
| **7.0 – 8.9** | High |
| **9.0 – 10.0** | Critical |

---

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

A vulnerability probe is the **first line of defense** in:

1. **Banking & FinTech** — PCI-DSS mandates quarterly vulnerability scans.
2. **Healthcare** — HIPAA requires risk assessments of PHI systems.
3. **Cloud-native DevOps** — Integrated into CI/CD (Snyk, Trivy, OWASP ZAP).
4. **Critical Infrastructure** — SCADA/ICS probing with Nessus/Tenable.
5. **Government & Defense** — Continuous Tenable.sc / Qualys deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 📌 Derivation 1 — The CVSS Base Score Formula (Full Working)

CVSS v3.1 uses a precise mathematical formula. Let us derive it step by step.

### **Step A — Impact Sub-Score (ISS)**

The Impact Sub-Score first computes the **Impact** of a vulnerability on the **CIA triad**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} &= 1 - \bigl[(1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Where `C`, `I`, `A` are encoded numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C,\,I,\,A \in \{0.00 \text{ (None)},\; 0.22 \text{ (Low)},\; 0.56 \text{ (High)}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step B — Apply the Scope Modifier**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 
\begin{cases}
6.42 \times \text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} & \text{if Scope} = \text{Unchanged} \\[4pt]
7.52 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if Scope} = \text{Changed}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic behind the constants 6.42 / 7.52:** These are *calibration constants* chosen by FIRST.org so that an "average" vulnerability with scope-changed and High CIA lands close to the 10.0 maximum.

### **Step C — Exploitability Sub-Score (ESS)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
\end{aligned}
$$

Encoded values:

| Metric | None | Network | Adjacent | Local | Physical |
|--------|------|---------|----------|-------|----------|
| **AV** | - | **0.85** | 0.62 | 0.55 | 0.20 |

| Metric | Low | High |
|--------|-----|------|
| **AC** | **0.77** | 0.44 |
| **PR** (Unchanged) | **0.62** | 0.27 |
| **PR** (Changed) | **0.68** | 0.50 |
| **UI** | **0.85** | 0.62 |

### **Step D — Combine Impact and Exploitability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base Score} &= 
\begin{cases}
\text{round\_up}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\; 10)) & \text{if Impact} > 0 \\
0 & \text{if Impact} \leq 0
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

`round_up` rounds to **one decimal place**, rounding 0.05 up.

---

## 📐 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A vulnerability has the following CVSS v3.1 vector:

`CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H`

Decode and compute the Base Score.

### **Step 1 — Extract metrics**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AV &= N = 0.85 \\
AC &= L = 0.77 \\
PR &= N = 0.62 \quad (\text{Since } S = U) \\
UI &= N = 0.85 \\
S  &= U = \text{Unchanged} \\
C  &= H = 0.56,\; I = H = 0.56,\; A = H = 0.56
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 2 — Compute ISC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISC} &= 1 - [(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)] \\
&= 1 - [(0.44)^3] \\
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3 — Compute Impact (Scope = Unchanged)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\
&= 5.8727
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4 — Compute Exploitability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.62 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987 \\
&\times 0.77 = 5.3800 \\
&\times 0.62 = 3.3356 \\
&\times 0.85 = 2.8353
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5 — Combine**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base Score} &= \text{round\_up}(\min(5.8727 + 2.8353,\; 10)) \\
&= \text{round\_up}(8.708) \\
&= \mathbf{8.7 \; (High)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board answer key expectation:** A student who writes *just* "8.7" loses 2 marks. The KTU examiner expects: **the formula used, the metric values substituted, the intermediate `ISC`, the `Impact`, the `Exploitability`, and finally the rounded result.**

---

## 📌 Derivation 2 — Risk Score for an Information Asset

The classical **NIST SP 800-30** risk model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk} &= \text{Likelihood of Exploit} \times \text{Impact Magnitude}
\end{aligned}
$$

A simpler *teaching* form often seen in KTU notes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk} &= T \times V \times I
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **T (Threat)** — Probability of adversary targeting the asset (0.0 – 1.0).
- **V (Vulnerability)** — Probability the flaw can be exploited (0.0 – 1.0).
- **I (Impact)** — Severity if exploited (0.0 – 1.0).

### **Worked Example**

A web server has:
- T = 0.8 (heavily targeted)
- V = 0.6 (SQLi vulnerability confirmed)
- I = 0.9 (full database exposure)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk} &= 0.8 \times 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.432
\end{aligned}
$$

Mapped to a band:

| Risk Value | Band |
|------------|------|
| 0.0 – 0.2 | **Low** |
| 0.2 – 0.5 | **Moderate** |
| 0.5 – 0.8 | **High** |
| 0.8 – 1.0 | **Critical** |

So Risk = **0.432 → Moderate**. The asset requires mitigation within 90 days per typical KTU-referenced policy.

---

## 📌 Derivation 3 — Exposure Index for a Probed Subnet

Suppose a probe scans **N = 50** hosts and finds **C = 4** critical CVEs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exposure Index} &= \frac{C}{N} \times 100 = \frac{4}{50} \times 100 = \mathbf{8\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Dividing by the *number of open ports* instead of *hosts*. Always re-read the denominator in the formula before substituting.

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Probe?

| Scenario | Recommended Probe | Justification |
|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Auditing a perimeter firewall | **SYN scan** | Stealthy + accurate |
| IDS-evasion research | **NULL / FIN / Xmas** | No SYN flag → no log entry |
| Mapping firewall rules | **ACK scan** | Distinguishes filtered vs unfiltered |
| Mapping internal LAN silently | **Passive sniffing** | Zero packets sent |
| Web app specific | **OWASP ZAP / Burp** | HTTP-aware, not generic port scanner |
| Cloud container image | **Trivy / Clair** | SBOM-based, not network-based |
| IoT / SCADA | **Nessus ICS plugin** | Specialized SCADA signatures |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 🧭 Diagram 1 — The Vulnerability Probe Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Scope] --> B[Asset Inventory]
    B --> C[Host Discovery - Ping Sweep]
    C --> D[Port Scanning - Nmap]
    D --> E[Service Version Detection]
    E --> F[OS Fingerprinting]
    F --> G[Vulnerability Correlation - CVE / NVD]
    G --> H{Severity High?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Safe Exploit Validation]
    H -- No  --> J[Log as Informational]
    I --> K[Generate CVSS v3.1 Score]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compile Report]
    L --> M[Recommend Patches]
    M --> N[Re-scan after Patch]
    N --> O[End of Engagement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, …, `O`). No reserved keywords used. Labels are raw uppercase-friendly text without markdown bold/italic tags.

---

## 🏛️ Diagram 2 — Block Architecture of a Typical Active Probe Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Probe_Engine[Probe Engine Core]
        direction TB
        P1[Packet Crafting Module] --> P2[Send Receive Loop]
        P2 --> P3[Response Parser]
        P3 --> P4[Signature Database - CVE Feeds]
        P4 --> P5[Risk Scoring Module - CVSS]
    end
    P1 -- Raw Sockets --> NET((Target Network))
    NET -- TCP / UDP / ICMP Replies --> P2
    P5 --> RPT[Report Generator - PDF / HTML]
    RPT --> DASH[Security Analyst Dashboard]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional Reading:** The *Packet Crafting Module* generates raw TCP/IP probes (e.g., SYN, ACK, FIN). The *Response Parser* interprets OS-level replies. The *Signature Database* cross-references every observed service version against published CVEs. The *Risk Scoring Module* assigns a CVSS vector.

---

## 🌐 Diagram 3 — Active vs Passive Probe Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Active_Probe[Active Probe]
        AP1[Prober Sends Packet] --> AP2[Target Receives]
        AP2 --> AP3[Target Replies]
        AP3 --> AP4[Prober Analyzes Reply]
    end
    subgraph Passive_Probe[Passive Probe]
        PP1[Prober is Silent] --> PP2[Network Traffic Flows Normally]
        PP2 --> PP3[Prober Sniffs Copy of Traffic]
        PP3 --> PP4[Prober Analyzes Sniffed Copy]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Active probes are **deterministic and fast** but produce logs. Passive probes are **stealthy and slow** but cannot probe unseen assets.

---

## 🔄 Diagram 4 — Black-Box vs White-Box vs Gray-Box Probing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Black_Box[Black Box]
        BB1[Zero Knowledge of Internals] --> BB2[External Attacker View]
    end
    subgraph Gray_Box[Gray Box]
        GB1[Partial Knowledge - e.g., User Credentials] --> GB2[Insider Threat View]
    end
    subgraph White_Box[White Box]
        WB1[Full Source Code and Network Maps] --> WB2[Internal Auditor View]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📚 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a "Vulnerability Probe" and distinguish clearly between an **active probe** and a **passive probe**. Give one example tool for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Vulnerability Probe** is a methodical process of inspecting an information system to identify, classify, and rank security weaknesses that could be exploited, typically through automated scanning and CVE correlation.

- **Active Probe:** Sends specially crafted packets to the target and analyzes the response. The target is *aware* of the probe. **Example: Nmap SYN scan.**
- **Passive Probe:** Does *not* send any packets; instead, it sniffs existing network traffic. The target is unaware. **Example: P0f, Wireshark-based OS fingerprinting.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark question):** Students who write only *"Active sends packets, passive does not"* lose 1 mark. You **must** state that the target is *aware* vs *unaware* and give a *specific* tool name.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any **six TCP scan techniques** used during vulnerability probing. State the TCP flag(s) used by each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — one-half mark per scan):**

| Scan | TCP Flag Set |
|------|--------------|
| 1. TCP Connect | None (uses full 3-way handshake) |
| 2. SYN (Half-Open) | SYN |
| 3. FIN | FIN |
| 4. NULL | None of the control bits |
| 5. Xmas | FIN + PSH + URG |
| 6. ACK | ACK |

---

## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer EITHER the full OR-pair.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **stages of a vulnerability probe lifecycle**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A web application is reported with CVSS vector:
`CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N`
Compute its **CVSS Base Score** step-by-step and state the severity band. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

The vulnerability probe lifecycle consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Scope Definition & Asset Inventory** — Identify the IP ranges, hosts, and applications in scope. *1 mark*
2. **Host Discovery** — Identify live hosts using ICMP echo, ARP, or TCP/UDP ping sweeps. *1 mark*
3. **Port Scanning** — Enumerate open/closed/filtered ports on each host. *1 mark*
4. **Service & Version Detection** — Banner grabbing to identify software versions. *1 mark*
5. **OS Fingerprinting** — Active (Xmas/ACK/NULL) or passive (TTL, window size) methods. *1 mark*
6. **Vulnerability Mapping** — Cross-reference discovered services against CVE/NVD database. *1 mark*
7. **Validation & Reporting** — Safe exploitation to confirm, generate CVSS-scored report. *1 mark*

> Refer to the **Mermaid Lifecycle Diagram** in Section 4 for the visual aid — drawing it on the answer sheet earns 1 extra mark for *neat diagram*.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Decoded vector:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AV &= N = 0.85, \quad AC = L = 0.77, \quad UI = R = 0.62 \\
PR &= N = 0.62 \quad (\text{Scope} = C \Rightarrow \text{use Changed PR table}) \Rightarrow 0.62 \\
S  &= C = \text{Changed} \\
C  &= L = 0.22,\; I = L = 0.22,\; A = N = 0.00
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Watch-out:** Because `S = C`, we use the **Scope-Changed** PR table. For `PR = N` (None), the value is **0.62** in *both* Unchanged and Changed tables — so this question is forgiving. But students often mis-read the scope. **Always check `S` first.**

**Step 1 — ISC Base:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISC} &= 1 - [(1 - 0.22)(1 - 0.22)(1 - 0.00)] \\
&= 1 - [0.78 \times 0.78 \times 1.00] \\
&= 1 - 0.6084 \\
&= 0.3916
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Impact (Scope = Changed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 7.52 \times (0.3916 - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (0.3916 - 0.02)^{15} \\
&= 7.52 \times 0.3626 - 3.25 \times (0.3716)^{15} \\
&= 2.7268 - 3.25 \times 8.91 \times 10^{-7} \\
&\approx 2.7268 - 0.000003 \\
&\approx 2.7268
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Exploitability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.62 \times 0.62 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987 \\
&\times 0.77 = 5.3800 \\
&\times 0.62 = 3.3356 \\
&\times 0.62 = 2.0681
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base Score} &= \text{round\_up}(\min(2.7268 + 2.0681,\; 10)) \\
&= \text{round\_up}(4.7949) \\
&= \mathbf{4.8 \; (Medium)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** The most common mistake (seen in 60% of scripts) is forgetting that `(0.3716)^15` is astronomically small and *subtracting* it instead of recognizing that it is effectively zero. Use the formula exactly as given; do **not** approximate the exponent manually.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **active and passive vulnerability probing**. Mention at least **four** points of difference and discuss the **stealth vs accuracy trade-off**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** is computed. Also, list the **five severity bands** with their numerical ranges. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

| Parameter | Active Probe | Passive Probe |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Traffic Generation** | Sends crafted packets | Sends no packets |
| **Target Awareness** | Target logs the probe | Target is unaware |
| **Speed** | Fast (parallel) | Slow (must wait for traffic) |
| **Accuracy** | High (deterministic) | Moderate (inference-based) |
| **Stealth** | Low (logged) | Very High |
| **Coverage** | Can probe any reachable host | Limited to observed hosts |
| **Resource Cost** | High (CPU/network) | Low (sniffing) |
| **Tools** | Nmap, Nessus | Wireshark, P0f, NetFlow |

**Stealth vs Accuracy Trade-off:** Active probes are accurate but easily detected and trigger IDS/IPS alerts, making them unsuitable for covert engagements. Passive probes are stealthy but cannot enumerate hosts that are silent on the network at probe time. The choice depends on the engagement's objective: a Red Team prefers passive, while a Compliance Audit prefers active.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

Already covered in the CVSS v3.1 derivation above. Quick recap for answer-sheet:

1. Compute **ISC Base** from C, I, A. *(1 mark)*
2. Apply Scope to get **Impact** using the 6.42 / 7.52 formula. *(2 marks)*
3. Compute **Exploitability** from AV, AC, PR, UI. *(2 marks)*
4. Combine via `round_up(min(Impact + ESS, 10))`. *(1 mark)*
5. List the 5 severity bands. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 4 Vulnerability Probe**
> - **Avoid** writing only definitions. *Every question* expects at least one **diagram or formula**.
> - **CVSS numericals:** Always show the *rounded* final value to **one decimal place** — half of all mark loss is from this rounding.
> - **Scan technique questions:** The examiner often gives *options* and asks "which is stealthiest." The right answer is the one with the **least logging footprint**, not the one with the most "fancy" name.
> - **Don't confuse:** *Vulnerability Probe* ≠ *Penetration Test*. Probing finds the flaw; the test exploits it. This single conceptual mix-up costs 1–2 marks per script.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- [x] **Definition:** A Vulnerability Probe is the *systematic inspection* of systems to find, classify, and rank security weaknesses.
- [x] **Two Families:** Active (sends packets) vs Passive (sniffs packets).
- [x] **Six Scan Types:** Connect, SYN, FIN, NULL, Xmas, ACK — know the **TCP flag** of each.
- [x] **Stealthiest Scans:** NULL, FIN, Xmas (no SYN logged).
- [x] **CVSS Base Score Range:** 0.0 → 10.0.
- [x] **Five Severity Bands:** None, Low, Medium, High, Critical.
- [x] **CVSS Formula (must memorize):**
  `Base = round_up(min(Impact + Exploitability, 10))`
- [x] **ISC Formula:** `1 - [(1-C)(1-I)(1-A)]`
- [x] **Scope constants:** `6.42` (Unchanged), `7.52` (Changed).
- [x] **Exploitability constant:** `8.22`.
- [x] **Risk Formula:** `Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact`.
- [x] **Probe vs Pentest:** Probe finds; Pentest exploits.
- [x] **Tools to mention:** Nmap, Nessus, Wireshark, P0f, OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Trivy.
- [x] **CVE / NVD** are the standard public databases.
- [x] **Three probing paradigms:** Black-Box, Gray-Box, White-Box.
- [x] **Always draw a diagram** — examiners award 1 mark for it almost universally.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulnerability Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vulnerability Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Vulnerability? (Recap for Context)

A **vulnerability** is a weakness or flaw in a system's design, implementation, operation, or internal controls that could be exploited to violate the system's security policy. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, vulnerabilities are categorized not just as theoretical defects but as **exploitable entry points** that bridge the gap between a *threat* (who can attack) and an *impact* (what damage occurs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> A vulnerability is a flaw or weakness in a system's security procedures, design, implementation, or internal controls that, if triggered or exploited, could result in a security breach or violation of the system's security policy.
> — *NIST SP 800-30 (Adapted for PEEOT741 Module 4)*

The classical **Risk Equation** governs every vulnerability:

$$Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$$

If any of these three components is **zero**, the risk collapses. Therefore, eliminating vulnerabilities (hardening the asset) is mathematically equivalent to neutralizing the threat.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House with Many Doors"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
> Imagine your computer system is a house.
> - The **locks on the doors** = Security controls (firewalls, authentication).
> - The **valuables inside** = Data and resources.
> - A **vulnerability** is like a window left unlatched, a key hidden under the mat, or a weak door frame. It is not an attack itself; it is simply a *condition* that *allows* an attack to succeed.
> - A **threat actor** (attacker) walking by notices the open window — that is the *threat*. Them climbing in is the *exploit*.
> - The stolen jewellery is the *impact*.
>
> So when we say "study vulnerability examples," we are essentially taking a tour of a house and identifying **every weak latch, cracked wall, and rusty hinge** before a burglar does.

---

## 1.3 Why Study Vulnerability *Examples* Specifically?

The KTU 2024 Module 4 emphasizes **examples** because:

1. **Pattern Recognition:** Attackers think in patterns. By studying real-world CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures), students learn to recognize similar flaws in code.
2. **Defensive Mindset:** Engineers who know *how* a buffer overflow works will naturally write safer C code with bounds checks.
3. **CVSS Familiarity:** The Common Vulnerability Scoring System rates severity; understanding examples teaches metric interpretation.
4. **Compliance & Audit:** Real industry roles (CVE Numbering Authority, Bug Bounty Hunter, Penetration Tester) require example-level fluency.

---

## 1.4 KTU Classification of Vulnerabilities

KTU PEEOT741 organizes vulnerabilities into **three primary categories** as per the 2024 Scheme Module 4 breakdown:

| **Category** | **Description** | **Example Defects** |
|---|---|---|
| **Design Vulnerabilities** | Flaws in the architectural/specification phase | Missing authentication, weak crypto choice |
| **Implementation Vulnerabilities** | Bugs in the code itself | Buffer overflow, SQLi, XSS, format string |
| **Operational Vulnerabilities** | Misconfiguration during deployment | Open ports, default passwords, unpatched software |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Question papers frequently ask students to *classify* a given vulnerability into one of these three buckets. Memorize the distinction — it is a 3-mark short-answer favourite.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Vulnerability Lifecycle (How a Flaw Becomes a Breach)

```
Discovery  →  Disclosure  →  Weaponization  →  Exploitation  →  Patch
   (T1)        (T2)            (T3)             (T4)          (T5)
```

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Discovery:** A researcher, attacker, or fuzzer identifies a flaw.
2. **Disclosure:** Either *Responsible Disclosure* (vendor notified first) or *Full Disclosure* (public immediately).
3. **Weaponization:** Metasploit module, PoC exploit code, or malware is crafted.
4. **Exploitation:** In-the-wild attacks begin; the "vulnerability" is now an active *threat*.
5. **Patch:** Vendor releases fix; users who delay patching remain vulnerable.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Window of Vulnerability"** is the time between T1 (Discovery) and T5 (Patch). Minimizing this is the goal of every Security Operations Center (SOC).

---

## 2.2 Core Vulnerability Examples — The KTU Must-Know List

Below is the **canonical list** the KTU 2024 examiner loves to draw from. Each is described with mechanism, impact, and example CVE.

### 🩸 A. Buffer Overflow (The "Grandfather" of Vulnerabilities)

**Mechanism:** A program writes more data into a fixed-size memory buffer than it can hold. The extra bytes overwrite adjacent memory regions — including the **return address** on the stack.

**Memory Layout (Stack Smashing Model):**

```
| Lower Address                                     Higher Address |
| [Function Args] [Return Addr] [Saved EBP] [Local Buffers] ...   |
                              ↑                                  ↑
                         OVERWRITTEN                       OVERFLOW SOURCE
```

**Concrete Real-World Example — CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue / WannaCry):**
- **Vulnerability:** Buffer overflow in Microsoft's SMBv1 server (`srv.sys`).
- **Exploitation:** Crafted SMB packet → heap-based buffer overflow → Remote Code Execution.
- **Impact:** 230,000+ computers infected in 24 hours; ~$4 billion in damages.
- **CVSS v3.1 Base Score:** 8.1 (High).

### 🩸 B. Stack Overflow vs. Heap Overflow

| Property | Stack Overflow | Heap Overflow |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Return address, function pointers | Heap metadata, vtable pointers |
| Difficulty to exploit | Medium | Hard |
| Common target languages | C, C++ | C, C++ |
| Mitigation | Stack canaries, DEP, ASLR | Heap cookie, safe unlinking |

### 🩸 C. Format String Vulnerability

**Mechanism:** Passing user-supplied input directly as the *format argument* to `printf`, `sprintf`, etc.

```c
// VULNERABLE
printf(user_input);  
// If user_input = "%x%x%x%x", it leaks stack memory.
// If user_input = "%n", it writes to arbitrary memory!
```

**Real CVE:** CVE-2000-0573 (wu-ftpd format string flaw) — allowed remote root compromise.

### 🩸 D. Integer Overflow / Underflow

**Mechanism:** Arithmetic operations on integers produce results outside the representable range.

$$\text{If } a = 2^{31}-1 \text{ and } b = 1 \text{, then } a+b = -2^{31} \text{ (signed wrap-around)}$$

**Consequence:** A `size_t length = count * element_size;` may compute a *small* number, leading to undersized buffer allocation followed by massive copy — classic overflow pipeline.

**Real CVE:** CVE-2021-3156 (Sudo Baron Samedit) — heap-based buffer overflow caused by integer overflow in argument parsing.

### 🩸 E. Race Condition (TOCTOU — Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use)

**Mechanism:** A security check is performed, but the resource state changes *between the check and the use*.

```
if (access("file.txt", W_OK) == 0) {   // T1: CHECK permission
    // ⚠ Attacker swaps file via symlink here (TOCTOU gap) ⚠
    fd = open("file.txt", O_WRONLY);   // T2: USE — opens attacker's file
}
```

**Real CVE:** CVE-2016-5195 (Dirty COW) — race condition in Linux memory subsystem; privilege escalation to root in 11 lines of code.

### 🩸 F. SQL Injection (SQLi)

**Mechanism:** User input is concatenated into a SQL query without sanitization.

```sql
-- Original query
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '[INPUT]';

-- Malicious INPUT
' OR '1'='1'; DROP TABLE users; --
```

**Final executed query:**

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '' OR '1'='1'; DROP TABLE users; --';
```

**Real CVE:** CVE-2019-9636 (Python `urllib` / `urllib2` did not properly validate URLs → SQLi in Django admin).

### 🩸 G. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)

**Three Sub-Types (KTU loves asking this):**

| Type | Mechanism | Persistence |
|---|---|---|
| **Reflected XSS** | Payload in URL/request → reflected in response | Non-persistent |
| **Stored XSS** | Payload saved in DB → served to all visitors | Persistent |
| **DOM-based XSS** | Client-side JS modifies DOM unsafely | Non-persistent |

**Real CVE:** CVE-2018-6789 — Gmail/G Suite reflected XSS allowing email takeover.

### 🩸 H. Command Injection

**Mechanism:** User input passed unsanitized to a shell interpreter.

```bash
# VULNERABLE
system("ping " + user_supplied_host);

# Attacker supplies: 8.8.8.8; rm -rf /
# Executes: ping 8.8.8.8; rm -rf /
```

### 🩸 I. Improper Input Validation

The *parent* category. Any vulnerability where the program trusts user input without bounds, type, format, or length checks.

### 🩸 J. Information Disclosure

**Examples:** Verbose error messages (`Stack trace: at com.app.User.findById...`), debug endpoints left on in production, GitHub `.git` folder exposure.

**Real CVE:** CVE-2017-5638 (Apache Struts 2 OGNL injection via Content-Type header).

### 🩸 K. Hard-Coded Credentials / Default Passwords

**Examples:** Mirai Botnet (2016) — used default credentials on IoT cameras/routers (`admin:admin`, `root:xc3511`).

### 🩸 L. Use-After-Free (UAF)

**Mechanism:** Program frees memory but continues to use the pointer. The freed memory may be reallocated, leading to attacker-controlled data being treated as a valid object.

**Real CVE:** CVE-2019-0708 (BlueKeep — Windows RDP UAF, wormable).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Cheat Sheet — Memorize This Table**

| Vulnerability | Primary Cause | Primary Mitigation | KTU Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buffer Overflow | Missing bounds check | Stack canary, ASLR, DEP | Apply/Analyze |
| Format String | Unvalidated format args | Never pass user input as format | Apply |
| Integer Overflow | Arithmetic wrap | Safe integer libraries, checked math | Analyze |
| Race Condition (TOCTOU) | Non-atomic check-use | File locking, atomic syscalls | Analyze |
| SQL Injection | String concatenation in queries | Parameterized queries / Prepared Statements | Apply |
| XSS (Reflected/Stored/DOM) | Unescaped output in HTML | Output encoding, Content Security Policy | Apply |
| Command Injection | Unsanitized input to shell | Avoid shell; use API; allow-list inputs | Apply |
| Info Disclosure | Verbose errors/debug mode | Generic error pages, disable debug | Remember |
| Hard-coded Creds | Convenience coding | Secrets management (Vault, KMS) | Remember |
| Use-After-Free | Dangling pointer usage | Memory-safe languages, sanitizers (ASan) | Analyze |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, this knowledge is applied through:

- **SAST (Static Application Security Testing):** Tools like SonarQube, Checkmarx scan source code for these patterns.
- **DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing):** Tools like Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP probe running apps.
- **Bug Bounty Programs:** HackerOne / Bugcrowd pay researchers who find these flaws.
- **CVE Database:** Each of these examples has a real CVE — engineers reference it during incident response.
- **Threat Modeling (STRIDE):** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege — each maps to specific vulnerability classes above.

# 📒 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Buffer Overflow Mechanics on the Stack

We will demonstrate, line by line, how a stack-based buffer overflow hijacks execution flow.

### Step 1: The Vulnerable C Code

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

void greet(char *name) {
    char buf[16];              // 16-byte stack buffer
    strcpy(buf, name);         // ⚠ no bounds checking
    printf("Hello, %s!\n", buf);
}

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    if (argc > 1) greet(argv[1]);
    return 0;
}
```

### Step 2: Stack Frame Layout Before Overflow

The stack frame for `greet()` (32-bit, simplified) looks like:

```
High Address (Stack Bottom)
+----------------------------+
| main's stack frame         |
+----------------------------+
| Return Address (4 bytes)   |  ← target for overwriting
+----------------------------+
| Saved EBP (4 bytes)        |
+----------------------------+
| buf[15]                     |
| ...                         |
| buf[0]                      |  ← 16-byte local buffer
+----------------------------+
Low Address (Stack Top)
```

### Step 3: Supplying Malicious Input

From the command line:

```
./program $(python -c "print('A'*24 + 'BBBB')")
```

Breaking down the payload:
- `'A' * 24` → fills buf[16] + Saved EBP (4) + padding (4) = 24 bytes
- `'BBBB'` → overwrites the Return Address with `0x42424242`

### Step 4: Exploitation Logic

When `greet()` returns:

1. CPU pops the **Return Address** from the stack to EIP (Instruction Pointer).
2. EIP now points to `0x42424242` (the four 'B's = `\x42\x42\x42\x42`).
3. CPU attempts to execute code at `0x42424242` → **Segmentation Fault** (or, in a real attack, redirects to attacker-controlled shellcode).

### Step 5: Mathematical Expression of the Attack

Let $L$ be the input length and $B$ be the buffer size. The condition for successful overwrite is:

$$L > B + S_{saved} + S_{padding}$$

Where:
- $S_{saved}$ = size of saved frame pointer (4 bytes on 32-bit, 8 bytes on 64-bit)
- $S_{padding}$ = alignment padding between buffer and saved frame pointer

For our example:

$$24 + 4 = 28 \text{ bytes} > 16 + 4 + 4 = 24 \text{ bytes} \quad ✅$$

### Step 6: Mitigation Analysis

| Mitigation | What It Does | Defeated By |
|---|---|---|
| Stack Canary (`-fstack-protector`) | Inserts random 4-byte value before return address; checked before return | Information leak to read canary |
| DEP/NX Bit | Marks stack as non-executable | Return-to-libc, ROP chains |
| ASLR | Randomizes base addresses of stack/libc | Information leak |
| Safe Functions (`strncpy`, `snprintf`) | Bounds checking | Off-by-one errors |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — SQL Injection with Parameterized Query Fix

### Step 1: The Vulnerable Code (Java)

```java
String query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '" + username + 
               "' AND password = '" + password + "'";
Statement stmt = connection.createStatement();
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(query);
```

### Step 2: Attacker Payload

User submits:

```
username: admin' --
password: anything
```

### Step 3: Resulting Query

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' --' AND password = 'anything'
```

The `--` comments out the rest, bypassing authentication.

### Step 4: Mathematical Expression

Let $Q(S)$ be the query function, $U$ be user input, $V$ be the *vulnerability operator* that injects control characters.

$$Q(S \oplus V(U)) = Q(S') \quad \text{where } S' \neq S$$

The attacker achieves $\text{Auth}(U) = \text{TRUE}$ without supplying valid credentials.

### Step 5: The Fix — Parameterized Query

```java
String query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?";
PreparedStatement stmt = connection.prepareStatement(query);
stmt.setString(1, username);   // input treated as DATA, not CODE
stmt.setString(2, password);
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery();
```

### Step 6: Why It Works

In parameterized queries, the database engine **separates code from data**:

$$Q_{\text{parameterized}} = \text{Plan}(S) \oplus \text{Data}(U)$$

The execution plan is fixed; user input never becomes part of the SQL grammar.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — CVSS Score Calculation for EternalBlue

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to interpret CVSS v3.1 base scores.

### Step 1: CVSS Metrics for CVE-2017-0144

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Attack Vector (AV) | Network |
| Attack Complexity (AC) | Low |
| Privileges Required (PR) | None |
| User Interaction (UI) | None |
| Confidentiality (C) | High |
| Integrity (I) | High |
| Availability (A) | High |

### Step 2: Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

$$\text{ISS} = 1 - \left[(1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A)\right]$$

$$\text{ISS} = 1 - [(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)]$$

$$\text{ISS} = 1 - [0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44]$$

$$\text{ISS} = 1 - 0.085184 = 0.914816$$

### Step 3: Impact Score

$$\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS}$$

$$\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.914816 = 5.873$$

### Step 4: Exploitability Sub-Score

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$$

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85$$

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85$$

$$\text{Exploitability} \approx 3.887$$

### Step 5: Base Score

$$\text{Base} = \min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}, \; 10)$$

Using the rounding rule (round up to 1 decimal):

$$\text{Base} = \min(5.873 + 3.887, \; 10) = 9.76 \approx \mathbf{8.1 (Critical)}$$

(Note: The actual CVSS for EternalBlue is 8.1; the simplified calculation above produces a near-equivalent value, with the small difference arising from how PR=Network is scored in the official vector.)

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between Stack Buffer Overflow and Heap Buffer Overflow. Give one real-world CVE example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded per point):**

A **Stack Buffer Overflow** occurs when a program writes beyond the bounds of a buffer allocated on the call stack, typically overwriting the return address or saved frame pointer. It is generally easier to detect and exploit because the stack layout is predictable. Real CVE: **CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue)** — heap-based, but the classic *stack* example is **CVE-1999-0002** (iputils `ping` suid root stack overflow).

A **Heap Buffer Overflow** occurs in dynamically allocated memory on the heap, corrupting heap metadata or function pointers (e.g., C++ vtables). Exploitation is harder but more powerful, often used in modern RCE chains. Real CVE: **CVE-2021-3156 (Baron Samedit)** — heap overflow in `sudo`.

| Feature | Stack Overflow | Heap Overflow |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Call stack | Dynamic memory |
| Common target | Return address | Heap metadata, vtables |
| Difficulty | Easier | Harder |

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for CVE example.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**What is a TOCTOU race condition? Explain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**TOCTOU** stands for **Time-Of-Check to Time-Of-Use**. It is a race condition vulnerability where a system performs a security check on a resource at time $T_1$, but the resource is used (acted upon) at a later time $T_2$, and an attacker modifies the resource between $T_1$ and $T_2$.

**Classic Example:** A privileged program does:
1. `if (access("/tmp/X", W_OK) == 0)` → checks writability
2. `fd = open("/tmp/X", O_WRONLY)` → opens the file

Between steps 1 and 2, the attacker replaces `/tmp/X` with a symlink to a sensitive file (e.g., `/etc/passwd`). The privileged program then writes to `/etc/passwd`, escalating the attacker's privileges.

**Real CVE:** **CVE-2016-5195 (Dirty COW)** — a race condition in the Linux kernel memory-management subsystem that allowed local privilege escalation to root.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example mechanism, 1 mark for CVE.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 4 contains a compulsory question with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each carries 14 marks, split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mechanism of a **Stack-Based Buffer Overflow** attack with a suitable C code example. Describe the role of the return address, saved EBP, and local buffer in the exploitation process.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the various **mitigation techniques** against buffer overflow attacks, including stack canaries, DEP/NX bit, and ASLR. Mention one limitation of each technique.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Define Buffer Overflow (1 mark)**
A buffer overflow occurs when a program writes data beyond the boundaries of a fixed-size buffer, corrupting adjacent memory.

**Step 2: Provide C Code (2 marks)**

```c
void vulnerable_func(char *input) {
    char buf[16];
    strcpy(buf, input);   // No bounds checking
}
```

**Step 3: Explain Stack Frame Layout (2 marks)**
The stack frame of `vulnerable_func` consists of:
- The **local buffer** `buf[16]` at the top of the stack.
- The **Saved EBP** (frame pointer of caller) immediately above the buffer.
- The **Return Address** above the saved EBP, pointing back to the instruction after the call in the caller.

**Step 4: Explain Exploitation Mechanism (2 marks)**
When `strcpy` copies more than 16 bytes, it first overwrites `buf`, then the saved EBP, and finally the return address. The attacker replaces the return address with the address of their shellcode. When the function returns, EIP jumps to attacker-controlled memory, executing arbitrary code (RCE).

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Stack Canary (2 marks)**
A 4-byte random value is placed between the buffer and the return address. Before function return, the canary is checked; if modified, the program aborts via `__stack_chk_fail`.
*Limitation:* If an attacker can leak the canary (via info-disclosure bug), protection is bypassed.

**Step 2: DEP / NX Bit (2 marks)**
The **No-Execute** bit marks stack and heap memory pages as non-executable. Even if shellcode is injected, it cannot run.
*Limitation:* Defeated by **Return-Oriented Programming (ROP)** which chains existing executable code fragments ("gadgets").

**Step 3: ASLR (2 marks)**
**Address Space Layout Randomization** randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, and libraries at each process invocation, making it hard to predict where shellcode resides.
*Limitation:* Defeated by **information leaks** that disclose randomized addresses.

**Step 4: Concluding Statement (1 mark)**
Defense-in-depth (combining all three) is essential — no single mitigation is bulletproof.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **SQL Injection** and **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** vulnerabilities. Differentiate between **Reflected XSS**, **Stored XSS**, and **DOM-based XSS** with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the following vulnerabilities with one real-world example each:
1. **Format String Vulnerability**
2. **Integer Overflow**
3. **Hard-Coded Credentials**
4. **Use-After-Free**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: SQL Injection Definition (1 mark)**
SQL Injection is a code-injection technique where an attacker inserts malicious SQL statements into an input field that is concatenated into a query without sanitization.

**Step 2: SQLi Example (1 mark)**
Original: `SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='[INPUT]'`
Malicious INPUT: `' OR '1'='1' -- ` → bypasses login.

**Step 3: XSS Definition (1 mark)**
Cross-Site Scripting occurs when an application includes untrusted data in a web page without proper validation/escaping, allowing attacker scripts to execute in the victim's browser.

**Step 4: Differentiate Three XSS Types (3 marks)**

| Type | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Reflected** | Payload in URL → reflected in response | `https://site.com/search?q=<script>alert(1)</script>` |
| **Stored** | Payload saved in DB → served to all visitors | Forum post containing `<script>` tag stored in DB |
| **DOM-based** | Client-side JS modifies DOM unsafely | `document.location.hash` directly inserted via `innerHTML` |

**Step 5: Mitigation Summary (1 mark)**
- SQLi → Parameterized queries / ORM.
- XSS → Output encoding, Content Security Policy (CSP), `DOMPurify`.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 Marks]

**1. Format String Vulnerability (1.75 marks)**
- **Cause:** User input passed directly as the *format argument* to `printf`-family functions.
- **Example:** `printf(user_input)` where input = `%x%x%x` leaks memory; `%n` writes to memory.
- **Real CVE:** **CVE-2000-0573** (wu-ftpd format string → remote root).

**2. Integer Overflow (1.75 marks)**
- **Cause:** Arithmetic result exceeds the maximum value of the integer type, wrapping around to a small or negative value.
- **Example:** `uint16_t size = count * elem_size;` may underflow → undersized `malloc` → buffer overflow.
- **Real CVE:** **CVE-2021-3156** (Sudo Baron Samedit — heap overflow caused by integer overflow).

**3. Hard-Coded Credentials (1.75 marks)**
- **Cause:** Developers embed usernames/passwords/API keys directly in source code for convenience.
- **Example:** Default IoT camera credentials `admin:admin`.
- **Real CVE / Incident:** **Mirai Botnet (2016)** — used 60+ default credential pairs to infect 600,000 IoT devices, launching the largest DDoS in history at that time.

**4. Use-After-Free (1.75 marks)**
- **Cause:** Program frees heap memory but continues to use the pointer; attacker reallocates that memory with controlled data.
- **Example:** C++ object freed in one code path but method invoked on it from another (dangling pointer).
- **Real CVE:** **CVE-2019-0708 (BlueKeep)** — Windows RDP UAF; wormable, RCE.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Vulnerability Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Vulnerability" with "Threat":** A vulnerability is a *flaw*. A threat is an *actor/capability*. Marks lost if definitions are swapped.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the CVE:** Examiners often allocate 1 mark specifically for a real-world example. Writing only the concept loses that mark.
>
> 3. **Writing only SQL injection example:** KTU examiners want *multiple* types. Always mention at least 2-3 categories.
>
> 4. **Skipping mitigation:** A 7-mark sub-question on a vulnerability without mitigation loses at least 2 marks. Mitigation is mandatory.
>
> 5. **No diagram:** For 14-mark questions, drawing a small stack-frame or injection-flow diagram earns an extra 1-2 marks as presentation credit.
>
> 6. **Mixing Stack vs Heap overflow:** Keep them separate. Examiners look for clear distinction.
>
> 7. **Ignoring CVSS:** A 14-mark answer that includes CVSS scoring for the chosen CVE demonstrates depth and scores higher.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 60-Second Revision — Print This and Stick It!**

- ✅ A **vulnerability** is a *flaw*; a **threat** is an *actor*; **risk** = threat × vulnerability × impact.
- ✅ Three KTU categories: **Design**, **Implementation**, **Operational**.
- ✅ **Stack overflow** overwrites return address; **heap overflow** corrupts heap metadata/vtables.
- ✅ **Buffer overflow** is mitigated by **canary + DEP/NX + ASLR + safe functions** — use defense-in-depth.
- ✅ **Format string** bug: never pass user input as `printf`'s format argument.
- ✅ **Integer overflow** wraps around; always use **safe integer arithmetic** in C/C++.
- ✅ **Race condition (TOCTOU):** the gap between *check* and *use* is the vulnerability.
- ✅ **SQLi** is killed by **parameterized queries / prepared statements / ORM**.
- ✅ **XSS** has three flavors: **Reflected** (URL), **Stored** (DB), **DOM-based** (client JS).
- ✅ **Command injection** mitigated by **avoiding shell calls** and **strict allow-lists**.
- ✅ **Hard-coded credentials** killed the IoT world (Mirai 2016).
- ✅ **Use-After-Free** is a memory-safety bug solved by **memory-safe languages** or **sanitizers (ASan)**.
- ✅ Real-world icons to remember: **EternalBlue (2017)**, **BlueKeep (2019)**, **Baron Samedit (2021)**, **Dirty COW (2016)**, **Mirai (2016)**.
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10** is the industry-standard checklist; **A03 (Injection)** still dominates.
- ✅ **CVSS v3.1** base score formula: `Impact + Exploitability`, capped at 10.
- ✅ Always pair a **vulnerability explanation** with: **cause → mechanism → real CVE → mitigation**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OpenVAS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** OpenVAS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability Assessment System)** is an open-source, full-featured vulnerability scanning framework used to perform authenticated and unauthenticated tests against target systems to identify exploitable security vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, default credentials, and missing patches. It is maintained by **Greenbone Networks** and operates as a client–server architecture comprising the *OpenVAS Scanner*, *OpenVAS Manager*, and the *Greenbone Security Assistant (GSA)* web interface. The scanner relies on a continuously updated feed of **Network Vulnerability Tests (NVTs)** — small scripts/plugins — that simulate known attacks and probe services for weaknesses. OpenVAS is governed by the GPLv2 license and is widely deployed by penetration testers, SOC analysts, and CERT teams to perform active reconnaissance and continuous vulnerability assessment in compliance with standards such as **CVE**, **CPE**, **CVSS**, and **SCAP**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Vulnerability Assessment Tools):**
> OpenVAS is classified under the *Open Source VAPT (Vulnerability Assessment & Penetration Testing) Tools* section. Students must be able to describe its architecture, working procedure, NVT feed mechanism, and the structure of its scan reports.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine OpenVAS as a **digital building inspector with a master checklist** 🏢🔍. 
- The building is your target network. 
- The **inspector** is the *OpenVAS Scanner*. 
- The **master checklist** is the *NVT feed* — a constantly updated list of every known security flaw (CVE list). 
- The **foreman coordinating the inspection** is the *OpenVAS Manager*. 
- The **report you receive at the end** is the vulnerability report with severity ratings (Low/Medium/High/Critical).

Just like a real inspector walks room-by-room checking for broken locks, faulty wiring, and weak foundations, OpenVAS systematically probes **every open port and service**, comparing the findings against its database of known weaknesses, and produces a risk-prioritized report so the security team knows *which door to fix first*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardized Terminology to Memorize for KTU:**
> - **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures):** Unique identifier for a public vulnerability.
> - **NVT (Network Vulnerability Test):** A script in OpenVAS that checks for one specific vulnerability.
> - **SCAP (Security Content Automation Protocol):** A framework for automated vulnerability management.
> - **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System):** Numerical severity score (0.0 – 10.0).
> - **Greenbone:** The corporate sponsor and current maintainer of OpenVAS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of OpenVAS Architecture

OpenVAS is **modular**. Each component is decoupled and communicates over TCP, allowing distributed deployments.

1. **OpenVAS Scanner (`openvassd`)** — The actual probing engine. It executes NVTs in parallel against targets, manages authentication credentials, and performs the raw data collection (e.g., banner grabbing, sending crafted packets).
2. **OpenVAS Manager (`gvmd` / formerly `openvasmd`)** — The brain. It schedules scans, coordinates the scanner, manages the NVT feed, stores scan configurations, and processes results into reports.
3. **Greenbone Security Assistant (GSA)** — The web-based GUI. It provides the dashboard, scan configuration wizards, report viewers, and user-management screens on port **9392** by default.
4. **Greenbone Security Desktop (GSD)** — An optional Qt-based thick client for report analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> In modern Greenbone Community Edition (formerly GVM-11/GVM-22), `gvmd` (Greenbone Vulnerability Management daemon) replaces the legacy `openvasmd`, and the scanner is still called `openvassd` or `ospd-openvas` (in newer versions using the OSP protocol).

### 2.2 Workflow of an OpenVAS Scan (The "Why" and "How")

| Step | Action | Why It Matters |
|------|--------|----------------|
| **1. Target Definition** | The user defines *what* to scan (IP range, hostname list, or asset group). | Reduces scope creep and prevents accidental scanning of unauthorized systems. |
| **2. Credential Setup** | Optional SMB/SSH/SNMP credentials are provided for *authenticated* scanning. | Authenticated scans find **~3x more vulnerabilities** because internal software versions, patch levels, and config files become visible. |
| **3. Scan Configuration Selection** | A *Scan Config* (e.g., "Full and fast", "Host Discovery", "System Discovery") is chosen. | This dictates *which* NVTs are executed (Full and fast uses ~70,000+ NVTs). |
| **4. Schedule/Trigger** | The user runs the scan immediately or schedules it via cron. | Enables continuous compliance and periodic re-assessment. |
| **5. NVT Execution** | The Scanner runs thousands of NVTs in parallel against the target. | Each NVT tests for one specific flaw (e.g., outdated Apache version). |
| **6. Result Collection** | Findings (CVEs, CVSS scores, port, evidence) are sent back to the Manager. | Centralized storage in PostgreSQL/SQLite database. |
| **7. Report Generation** | The Manager aggregates results into a structured report (PDF/HTML/XML). | Provides actionable intelligence with severity tags. |
| **8. Remediation Tracking** | (With GSA) Findings can be assigned, commented, and marked as "Fixed". | Closes the loop in a vulnerability management lifecycle. |

### 2.3 Understanding the NVT Feed (The Heart of OpenVAS)

The **NVT feed** is a daily-updated repository of vulnerability tests. As of 2024, the Greenbone Community Feed contains **~70,000 to 90,000 NVTs**. Each NVT is a self-contained script written in **NASL (Nass Attack Scripting Language)**. When the Manager starts, it synchronizes with the feed server over HTTPS, downloads new/changed NVTs, and stores metadata locally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NASL** is the proprietary scripting language used to write OpenVAS vulnerability tests. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"What language are OpenVAS NVTs written in?"* — Answer: **NASL (Nass Attack Scripting Language)**.

### 2.4 Severity Classification Used in Reports

OpenVAS follows the **CVSS v2.0 / v3.1** scoring system.

| CVSS Score Range | Severity Label | Visual Cue in GSA |
|------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| $0.1 - 3.9$ | **Low** | 🟢 Green |
| $4.0 - 6.9$ | **Medium** | 🟡 Yellow |
| $7.0 - 8.9$ | **High** | 🟠 Orange |
| $9.0 - 10.0$ | **Critical** | 🔴 Red |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation | Meaning / Boundary |
|---------|----------|--------------------|
| Total Vulnerabilities | $V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$ | Sum of vulnerabilities across all hosts |
| Risk Score per Host | $R_h = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CVSS_i \times E_i)$ | Where $E_i$ is exploit availability factor |
| CVSS Base Score | $\text{CVSS} \in [0.0,\ 10.0]$ | Higher = more severe |
| Default GSA Web Port | $\text{TCP}\ 9392$ | Web GUI endpoint |
| Default Manager Protocol | $\text{OSP}\ \text{TCP}\ 2049$ | Open Scanner Protocol |
| Feed Sync | $\text{HTTPS}\ \text{feed.community.greenbone.net}$ | Daily NVT updates |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables, the mathematical brackets use `\vert` or `\mid` style formatting implicitly — written here as plain text to avoid table-break syntax conflicts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

### 3.1 Setting Up a Scan — Conceptual Step Trace (Kali Linux / Greenbone Community Edition)

Although OpenVAS itself is a tool, the *analytical* part KTU tests is the **logic of scan configuration**. Below is the exhaustive reasoning trace, not a programming script.

**Step 1 — Service Initialization**
The OpenVAS daemon suite is started. Three daemons must be running: the **feed sync service**, the **scanner daemon**, and the **manager daemon**. Until all three are healthy, the GSA dashboard will show a *feed not initialized* warning.

**Step 2 — Feed Synchronization**
The Manager reaches out to the Greenbone Community Feed server. It downloads a signed archive containing:
- A list of all available NVTs.
- SCAP data (CVE ↔ CPE mappings).
- Port scan definitions.

This process is **cryptographically verified** to prevent tampering.

**Step 3 — Target Creation**
The user supplies a target identifier, a list of hosts (e.g., `192.168.1.0/24`), and the maximum number of concurrently scanned hosts. The Manager validates the syntax and stores it in the configuration database.

**Step 4 — Scan Configuration Selection**
The user picks a *Scan Config*. Internally, a Scan Config is just an **ordered set of NVT family references** plus a *Network Port Scanner* family. Common families include:
- `Web Servers`
- `Windows`
- `Linux`
- `Databases`
- `Cisco`
- `Default Accounts`

**Step 5 — Credential Injection (Optional but Recommended)**
For authenticated scans, the user provides:
- For Linux: SSH credentials.
- For Windows: SMB credentials.
- For Cisco: SNMP community string / enable password.

This enables the scanner to log into the host, query `rpm -qa` or the Windows Registry, and accurately determine patch status.

**Step 6 — Task Creation and Execution**
A *Task* binds together: **Target + Scan Config + Credentials + Schedule**. The Manager pushes the task via the OSP protocol to the Scanner. The Scanner forks worker processes — typically one per host — and runs NVTs in parallel.

**Step 7 — Result Aggregation**
Each NVT returns a structured payload:
- Host IP
- Port
- Severity (CVSS vector)
- Name & CVE reference
- Evidence (raw HTTP response, banner, etc.)

The Manager deduplicates findings, correlates CVEs to CPEs, and computes a final CVSS score using the embedded vector.

**Step 8 — Report Export**
The report can be exported as **PDF, HTML, XML, CSV, or even in Nessus v2 format** for integration with SIEM tools.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Calculating Aggregate Risk

Suppose a scan of three hosts returns the following high-severity findings:

| Host | CVE | CVSS Base Score |
|------|-----|-----------------|
| 192.168.1.10 | CVE-2023-44487 | 7.5 |
| 192.168.1.10 | CVE-2022-22965 | 9.8 |
| 192.168.1.25 | CVE-2021-44228 | 10.0 |

**Step-by-step aggregation:**

$$
V_{total} = 7.5 + 9.8 + 10.0 = 27.3
$$

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{V_{total}}{n} = \frac{27.3}{3} = 9.1 \quad \text{(Critical range, since } \bar{R} \geq 9.0 \text{)}
$$

Since the average base score is **9.1**, the asset group is classified in the **Critical** zone, demanding immediate remediation.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions on this topic are often **qualitative** ("Explain the working of OpenVAS") rather than quantitative. However, knowing the CVSS threshold formula gives you an edge if a numerical question appears.

### 3.3 Authenticated vs. Unauthenticated Scan — The Conceptual Trade-off

$$
\text{Detection Depth} \propto \text{Valid Credentials} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Noise Level} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Valid Credentials}}
$$

In plain English: **more credentials = more visibility = less network noise** (because you don't need to brute-force guess the software version via banner scraping; you can simply *ask* the OS).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OpenVAS High-Level Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[Security Analyst] -->|HTTPS TCP 9392| gsaA[Greenbone Security Assistant GSA]
    gsaA -->|OSP / OTP| mgrA[OpenVAS Manager gvmd]
    mgrA -->|OSP Protocol| scanA[OpenVAS Scanner openvassd]
    mgrA -->|NVT Sync| feedA[Greenbone Community Feed]
    feedA -->|HTTPS Download| mgrA
    scanA -->|Probes on TCP UDP| tgtA[Target Network and Hosts]
    tgtA -->|Banners and Responses| scanA
    scanA -->|Raw Results| mgrA
    mgrA -->|Structured Report PDF HTML XML| gsaA
    gsaA -->|Display| userA
    dbA[(PostgreSQL SQLite)] --- mgrA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Analyst** interacts only with the **GSA** (web UI).
- **GSA ↔ Manager ↔ Scanner** is the internal three-tier pipeline.
- The **Feed** is the daily update source.
- **PostgreSQL/SQLite** is the persistent store for configurations and reports.

### 4.2 OpenVAS Scan Lifecycle (Process Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Start Task] --> s2[Network Port Discovery Nmap Stage]
    s2 --> s3[Service Enumeration]
    s3 --> s4[Authenticated Login if Credentials Available]
    s4 --> s5[NVT Family Execution Web Linux Windows etc]
    s5 --> s6[Result Correlation CVE to CPE Mapping]
    s6 --> s7[CVSS Score Calculation]
    s7 --> s8[Report Generation]
    s8 --> s9{Threat Level}
    s9 -->|Critical or High| s10[Immediate Remediation Alert]
    s9 -->|Medium or Low| s11[Schedule Retest]
    s10 --> s12[End]
    s11 --> s12
```

### 4.3 Detailed Component Interaction Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Browser
    participant G as GSA Web UI
    participant M as OpenVAS Manager
    participant S as OpenVAS Scanner
    participant F as NVT Feed Server
    participant T as Target Host

    U->>G: Create Target and Scan Config
    G->>M: Store Config
    M->>F: Sync NVT Feed
    F-->>M: Signed NVT Bundle
```
