# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Datapipe. List any two legitimate uses.**

**Model Answer:**
Datapipe is a TCP tunneling utility that establishes a bidirectional byte stream between a local port and a remote port via an intermediate relay host, transparently forwarding data without inspecting the payload. It operates at the transport layer and is protocol-agnostic.

**Two legitimate uses:**
1. **Secure remote administration** — tunneling SSH/RDP across restricted networks for authorized IT staff.
2. **Application-layer traffic relaying** — forwarding legacy protocols across modern firewalls for business continuity.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is Datapipe a significant concern for Data Loss Prevention (DLP) systems?**

**Model Answer:**
Datapipe concerns DLP systems because it **bypasses content inspection** by encapsulating sensitive data inside encrypted or camouflage-protocol streams (e.g., HTTPS on port 443). Since DLP tools rely on **deep packet inspection (DPI)** of payload content, an encrypted Datapipe tunnel renders payload-based rules ineffective. Only **flow-based and behavioral analytics** (e.g., traffic-volume anomalies, destination-reputation checks) can flag such channels.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Datapipe channel. Label the local endpoint, relay, remote endpoint, and the direction of data flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the effective throughput $R_{eff}$ of a Datapipe channel given a link rate of 50 Mbps, MTU 1500 B, IP header 20 B, TCP header 20 B, encapsulation overhead 40 B, RTT 80 ms, and packet loss 0.5%. Comment on the bottleneck. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture Explanation:**

A Datapipe channel is a **three-tier topology** consisting of:

1. **Local Endpoint** — The internal client connects to a local listening socket bound on port $L_{port}$ (e.g., 127.0.0.1:9000).
2. **Relay Host (Pivot)** — A reachable intermediary (often a compromised host) that accepts the outbound tunnel and forwards bytes to the remote target.
3. **Remote Endpoint** — The ultimate destination service listening on $R_{port}$ (e.g., attacker-controlled server on 443).

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Local Client] --> P2[Datapipe Listener L_port]
    P2 --> P3[Relay Pivot Host]
    P3 --> P4[Remote Server R_port]
```

**Data flow:** Client → Local Listener → (encrypted tunnel) → Relay → Remote Service. The reverse path carries responses. The relay is essential when the local network has **egress filtering** that blocks direct outbound connections.

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Block diagram — **2 marks**
- Explanation of three tiers — **3 marks**
- Direction of data flow & role of relay — **2 marks**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $R_{link} = 50$ Mbps, $M = 1500$ B, $H_{ip} = 20$ B, $H_{tcp} = 20$ B, $H_{encap} = 40$ B, $RTT = 80$ ms, $P_{loss} = 0.005$.

**Step 1 — Maximum payload:**

$$L_{max} = 1500 - 20 - 20 - 40 = 1420 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Throughput efficiency:**

$$E_{thrpt} = \frac{1420}{1420 + 80} = \frac{1420}{1500} \approx 0.9467$$

**Step 3 — Effective throughput:**

$$R_{eff} = 50 \times 10^6 \times 0.9467 \times (1 - 0.005) = 50 \times 10^6 \times 0.9467 \times 0.995$$

$$R_{eff} \approx 47.10 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4 — BDP calculation (to identify the bottleneck):**

$$BDP = \frac{1420 \times 8}{0.080} = \frac{11360}{0.080} = 142{,}000 \text{ bps} = 0.142 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Comment on the bottleneck:**
The BDP of **0.142 Mbps** is far below the link capacity of 50 Mbps. This indicates that the **RTT is the bottleneck** — specifically, the channel is *latency-bound*, not bandwidth-bound. Increasing link capacity would not help; only **reducing RTT** (e.g., by deploying a geographically closer relay) or **enabling parallel TCP streams** would increase throughput.

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct $L_{max}$ calculation — **1.5 marks**
- $E_{thrpt}$ computation — **1.5 marks**
- $R_{eff}$ final result — **2 marks**
- BDP & bottleneck comment — **2 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **legitimate tunneling** and **malicious data exfiltration** using Datapipe. Discuss the role of **egress filtering** in mitigating Datapipe-based threats. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SOC team observes a baseline outbound traffic of $\mu = 1500$ packets/sec with $\sigma = 120$ packets/sec. Using the **3-sigma rule**, compute the detection threshold. If an attacker limits exfiltration to **$\mu + 1.5\sigma$**, will the alarm trigger? Justify with calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

| Aspect | Legitimate Tunneling | Malicious Exfiltration |
|---|---|---|
| **Authorization** | Explicitly approved by org | Unauthorized, covert |
| **Direction** | Often bidirectional interactive | Mostly unidirectional bulk upload |
| **Payload** | Interactive commands, session data | Compressed archives, credentials, PII |
| **Destination** | Known corporate IP / VPN gateway | Unknown / attacker-controlled IP |
| **Volume profile** | Bursty, low-volume | Sustained, high-volume (or low-and-slow) |
| **Time pattern** | Business hours | Off-hours, weekends |

**Role of Egress Filtering:**
Egress filtering is the practice of restricting *outbound* traffic to an **allow-list** of approved destinations and protocols. Against Datapipe:
- **Destination-based rules** block connections to unknown external IPs.
- **Port-based rules** force all outbound to use port 443, but Datapipe can *piggyback* on 443 — defeating naive port rules.
- **Protocol validation** (e.g., TLS fingerprinting via JA3/JA3S) can distinguish real HTTPS from Datapipe-on-443.

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Tabular differentiation — **3 marks**
- Egress filtering explanation — **2 marks**
- Limitations (Datapipe camouflage) — **2 marks**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $\mu = 1500$ pps, $\sigma = 120$ pps, $k = 3$.

**Step 1 — Compute detection threshold (additive):**

$$C_{detect} = 3 \times 120 = 360 \text{ pps}$$

$$\text{Alert Threshold} = 1500 + 360 = 1860 \text{ pps}$$

**Step 2 — Compute attacker's low-and-slow rate:**

$$\text{Attack Rate} = \mu + 1.5\sigma = 1500 + 1.5 \times 120 = 1500 + 180 = 1680 \text{ pps}$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$1680 \text{ pps} < 1860 \text{ pps} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Alarm does NOT trigger.}$$

**Justification:**
The attacker is operating at **1.5σ above the mean**, which lies *within* the 3σ confidence band. The 3-sigma rule covers **99.7%** of normal variation, so any rate below 3σ is statistically considered "normal." A sophisticated attacker using *low-and-slow* Datapipe exfiltration can remain stealthy. Defenders must therefore employ **multi-sigma analysis, seasonality-aware baselining, or machine-learning anomaly detection** to catch such sub-threshold attacks.

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct threshold calculation — **2 marks**
- Attack rate calculation — **2 marks**
- Comparison & justification — **3 marks**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** — Always convert ms → seconds in RTT, bytes → bits in bandwidth formulas. KTU examiners *deduct strictly* for unit mismatches.
> 2. **Confusing MTU with MSS** — MTU is the *link-layer* limit (1500 B); MSS is MTU minus IP+TCP headers. For Datapipe overhead, use MTU as the starting point.
> 3. **Skipping the "Comment" sub-part** — In derivation questions, the final numerical answer without the qualitative bottleneck analysis fetches only 60% marks.
> 4. **Wrong direction of data flow in diagrams** — Always show *both* uplink and downlink paths. One-way diagrams lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Conflating Datapipe with VPN** — Datapipe is *application-layer* tunneling without built-in encryption; VPN is *network-layer* with mandatory encryption. Examiners test this distinction.
> 6. **Not stating the sigma rule's coverage** — When answering threshold questions, explicitly mention that 3σ covers **99.7%** of data (per the 68-95-99.7 rule).

---

## 5.4 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Datapipe**

- ✅ **Datapipe** = bidirectional TCP byte-stream tunnel via a relay host.
- ✅ **Three-tier architecture:** Local → Relay → Remote.
- ✅ **Protocol-agnostic** — forwards raw bytes regardless of content.
- ✅ **Dual-use tool:** legitimate tunneling vs. malicious exfiltration / C2.
- ✅ **Camouflage ports:** 443 (HTTPS), 53 (DNS), 80 (HTTP).
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $L_{max} = M - H_{ip} - H_{tcp} - H_{encap}$
  - $E_{thrpt} = \dfrac{D_{useful}}{D_{useful} + O_{total}}$
  - $R_{eff} = R_{link} \times E_{thrpt} \times (1 - P_{loss})$
  - $BDP = \dfrac{P \times 8}{RTT}$
  - $C_{detect} = k \cdot \sigma_{baseline}$
- ✅ **Bottleneck** in Datapipe is usually **latency (RTT)**, not bandwidth.
- ✅ **DLP evasion:** encryption + camouflage defeats content inspection.
- ✅ **Defense layers:** egress filtering, JA3 TLS fingerprinting, flow analytics, UEBA, ZTNA.
- ✅ **Sigma rule coverage:** 1σ = 68%, 2σ = 95%, **3σ = 99.7%**.
- ✅ **Distinguish from VPN** — Datapipe is app-layer; VPN is network-layer with built-in crypto.
- ✅ **Recognize the "low-and-slow" exfiltration pattern** — sub-3σ traffic is stealthy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FPipe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FPipe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **FPipe (Foundstone Pipe)** is a **TCP/IP port forwarder / redirector utility** developed by **Foundstone Inc.** (later acquired by McAfee) that binds to a specified source port on a local host and transparently relays any incoming TCP/IP traffic to a remote destination host on a user-defined destination port. It operates at the **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** of the OSI model and is primarily used for **firewall traversal, traffic redirection, and penetration testing** scenarios.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, FPipe is classified as a **Network Traffic Manipulation Tool** — a class of offensive security utilities that allow an ethical hacker / penetration tester to redirect arbitrary TCP connections through a chosen local socket, making the traffic appear to originate from a permitted port or IP address. It requires **administrative (root / SYSTEM) privileges** on Windows because it creates **raw sockets** to control packet-level source addressing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Post Office Mail Redirection Service":**
> Imagine you sent a letter to your friend at their *old* address (Destination Port `21`), but your friend has relocated. The post office has a **redirection rule**: any mail arriving at the **old address (Source Port `80`)** is automatically forwarded to the **new address (Remote Host `192.168.1.50` on Port `3389`)**. The sender never knows the mail was redirected — they only see it going to the **old address** that they were authorized to write to.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Old address** = the FPipe *listening port* on the local host
> - **Post office** = the **FPipe process** running on the local machine
> - **New address** = the *real destination host:port* (e.g., a service behind a firewall)
> - **Sender's perception** = the firewall sees only allowed traffic to the *old address*

This is precisely how FPipe defeats port-based ACLs (Access Control Lists): the firewall sees traffic flowing only to **legitimate, permitted ports** (HTTP/80, HTTPS/443, DNS/53), but FPipe silently tunnels it to restricted services like RDP/3389, Telnet/23, or SMB/445.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classifications

The following standard metrics and protocol numbers are relevant for FPipe operations:

- **Default Transport Protocol:** **TCP** (Transmission Control Protocol) — FPipe is TCP-only and does **not** natively support UDP forwarding.
- **Privileged Port Range:** **0 – 1023** (requires root/SYSTEM to bind)
- **Ephemeral Port Range (Windows default):** **49152 – 65535**
- **Maximum Concurrent Connections (`-c` flag):** **1 – 1000** (default = 32)
- **TCP Header Default Size:** **20 bytes** (minimum, no options)
- **OSI Layer of Operation:** **Layer 4 — Transport Layer**
- **Socket Type Used:** **SOCK_STREAM** (stream-oriented, connection-oriented)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 PEEOT741 syllabus, FPipe is discussed under **Module 4 — Network Penetration Testing Tools**, specifically within the unit on *Traffic Redirection and Tunneling Utilities*. Students are expected to understand its **command-line flags, operational flow, and use-case in firewall evasion**, but **not** to perform hands-on exploitation on live systems without authorization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

FPipe operates through a clearly defined **client–proxy–server** three-tier architecture. The logical flow is:

1. **Initialization Phase**
   - The attacker / tester launches the FPipe executable on a **pivot host** (often a compromised machine inside the network, or a test machine).
   - FPipe parses its command-line arguments and validates parameter combinations.
   - It requests **raw socket privileges** from the OS — on Windows, this requires the process to run as **NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM** or as an Administrator.

2. **Listener Binding Phase**
   - FPipe creates a **TCP listening socket** bound to a specified **local IP address** (`-i` flag) and **source port** (`-s` flag).
   - This listener becomes the "front door" — any client traffic directed to this `IP:Port` will be accepted.
   - Example: `fpipe -l 53 -s 53 -d 10.10.10.5 -r 3389` makes FPipe listen on local port `53` (DNS — usually permitted outbound by firewalls).

3. **Inbound Connection Acceptance**
   - When an external client connects to the FPipe listener, FPipe accepts the connection on behalf of the destination.
   - The client believes it is talking **directly** to the destination service.

4. **Outbound Connection Establishment**
   - FPipe then opens a **separate outgoing TCP connection** from the local host to the **real destination** (`-d` flag for destination host, `-r` flag for destination port).
   - Critically, the **source port** of this *outgoing* connection is set to the same value as the listening port (`-s` value). This is the **core firewall-evasion trick** — the firewall sees outbound traffic originating from a "trusted" source port.
   - This source-port binding is implemented via the `setsockopt()` call with `SO_SNDBUF` and raw socket construction using `SOCK_RAW` or via Winsock extensions.

5. **Bidirectional Relay (Piping)**
   - FPipe now acts as a **transparent bidirectional proxy**:
     - Bytes received from the **client** → forwarded to the **server** (destination).
     - Bytes received from the **server** → forwarded to the **client**.
   - This byte-stream piping continues until either side closes the connection.

6. **Connection Lifecycle Completion**
   - When the client or server closes the socket, FPipe tears down both legs of the pipe.
   - If the `-p` (persistent) flag is set, the listener remains active and accepts a new connection.
   - If `-c N` is set, only **N concurrent** connections are allowed.

### 2.2 KTU Reference Sheet — Command-Line Parameters

The following table lists every major FPipe parameter in a KTU-friendly format. **No vertical pipes `|` are used inside table cells** to preserve markdown rendering integrity.

| Parameter | Full Form | Function | Example Value | Typical Use Case |
|-----------|-----------|----------|---------------|------------------|
| `-h` | Help | Display usage syntax | — | Reference / exam recall |
| `-?` | Help (alt) | Alternate help invocation | — | Same as `-h` |
| `-s` | Source Port | Local port to **bind the listener** AND spoof as outbound source port | `53`, `80`, `443` | Firewall evasion |
| `-d` | Destination Host | IP or hostname of the **real target** | `10.10.10.5` | Internal RDP/SMB server |
| `-r` | Destination Port | Port on the real target to forward traffic to | `3389`, `445`, `23` | Service behind firewall |
| `-i` | Bind IP | Specific local IP for **outgoing** connections | `192.168.1.100` | Multi-homed hosts |
| `-c` | Max Connections | Maximum concurrent client connections | `10`, `100` | Brute-force prevention |
| `-v` | Verbose | Prints detailed connection logs to stdout | — | Debugging / CTFs |
| `-p` | Persistent | Listener stays alive after each connection | — | Long-running relays |
| `-l` | Username:Password | Optional authentication tuple for `-p` mode | `admin:P@ss123` | Restricted pivots |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Cybersecurity

| Domain | Utility of FPipe |
|--------|------------------|
| **Penetration Testing** | Tunnels traffic through permitted ports to bypass egress filtering |
| **Red Teaming** | Establishes covert command channels mimicking DNS or HTTP traffic |
| **Network Forensics Education** | Demonstrates how source-port spoofing defeats stateless ACLs |
| **CTF Competitions** | Used in network pivoting challenges to reach hidden services |
| **Incident Response (defensive)** | Analysts study FPipe logs to detect redirection attempts |
| **Legacy App Support** | Redirects old protocol ports to new services without reconfiguring clients |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Firewall Evasion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Most corporate firewalls enforce **stateful egress filtering** with rules like:
> *"Allow outbound TCP only if source port is 80, 443, or 53"*.
>
> FPipe violates the **"source port = destination port" assumption** that weak ACLs rely on. By forcing the outbound connection's source port to match a permitted value (e.g., `53`), FPipe makes the malicious tunnel **look identical** to legitimate DNS traffic to a packet-filtering firewall. This is why modern **Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFWs)** and **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** engines inspect payload content — not just port numbers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Operation Trace

### 3.1 Canonical KTU Exam Scenario

> **Scenario (from KTU question bank pattern):**
> *"A penetration tester has compromised a host inside the corporate DMZ. The internal RDP server (10.10.10.50:3389) is blocked by the perimeter firewall, but outbound DNS traffic (port 53) is permitted. Demonstrate how FPipe can be used to reach the internal RDP server from outside."*

**Given Parameters:**
- Local pivot IP: `192.168.1.20`
- Internal RDP server: `10.10.10.50:3389`
- Permitted outbound port: `53` (DNS)
- Tester's external machine: `203.0.113.45`

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution Trace

**Step 1 — Reconnaissance**
The tester confirms via `nmap` that `10.10.10.50:3389` is reachable from the pivot host `192.168.1.20` but blocked from external IP `203.0.113.45`.

**Step 2 — Launch FPipe on the Pivot**

$$ \text{fpipe} -l \ 53 \ -s \ 53 \ -i \ 192.168.1.20 \ -d \ 10.10.10.50 \ -r \ 3389 \ -v $$

Parameter interpretation:

| Token | Meaning in this scenario |
|-------|--------------------------|
| `-l 53` | Listen on local port `53` for incoming clients |
| `-s 53` | Use `53` as the **spoofed source port** for outbound connections |
| `-i 192.168.1.20` | Bind outgoing connections to the DMZ pivot's IP |
| `-d 10.10.10.50` | Real destination = internal RDP server |
| `-r 3389` | Real destination port = RDP |
| `-v` | Verbose logging for the exam write-up |

**Step 3 — Client Connection**

From external machine `203.0.113.45`, the tester runs:

$$ \text{mstsc} \ /v: 192.168.1.20:53 $$

or equivalently:

$$ \text{rdesktop} \ 192.168.1.20:53 $$

The RDP client opens TCP connection to `192.168.1.20:53`. The **perimeter firewall sees only an outgoing connection to port 53** — a permitted protocol — and allows it.

**Step 4 — Internal Pipe Establishment**

FPipe on `192.168.1.20` accepts the connection on port `53` and immediately opens an **outbound** connection to `10.10.10.50:3389` **with source port `53`**:

$$ \text{Outbound:}\ 192.168.1.20:\textbf{53} \longrightarrow 10.10.10.50:3389 $$

The **internal firewall** (if it trusts source-port `53`) permits this. Note that 53 is also a TCP port, not just UDP — TCP/53 is used for **zone transfers** and is often allowed.

**Step 5 — Bidirectional Relay**

The tester types credentials into the RDP window. Bytes flow:

$$ \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{TCP}} \text{FPipe:53} \xrightarrow{\text{TCP src=53}} \text{RDP:3389} $$

$$ \text{RDP:3389} \xrightarrow{\text{TCP}} \text{FPipe:53} \xrightarrow{\text{TCP}} \text{Client} $$

The desktop session appears on `203.0.113.45`, **even though direct RDP was blocked**.

**Step 6 — Quantitative Connection Analysis (for marks)**

If 5 concurrent RDP users connect, the connection state table on the pivot is:

| Connection # | Client IP | Client Port | FPipe Local Port | Dest IP | Dest Port | Spoofed Src Port |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|------------------|---------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | 203.0.113.45 | 49152 | 53 | 10.10.10.50 | 3389 | 53 |
| 2 | 203.0.113.46 | 49153 | 53 | 10.10.10.50 | 3389 | 53 |
| 3 | 198.51.100.7 | 51200 | 53 | 10.10.10.50 | 3389 | 53 |
| 4 | 198.51.100.8 | 51201 | 53 | 10.10.10.50 | 3389 | 53 |
| 5 | 203.0.113.99 | 60000 | 53 | 10.10.10.50 | 3389 | 53 |

**Step 7 — Cleanup**

After testing, the tester kills the FPipe process:

$$ \text{taskkill} \ /F \ /IM \ \text{fpipe.exe} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Analytical Insight for the Exam:**
> The success of this attack relies on **three simultaneous conditions**:
>
> 1. **Stateful ACL trusts source port 53** — weak firewall policy.
> 2. **FPipe runs with admin/SYSTEM privileges** — required for raw socket source-port binding.
> 3. **No DPI inspects payload content** — payload is encrypted RDP, which DPI may not flag as malicious.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Feature | FPipe | netcat (nc) | SSH Local Port Forward | socat |
|---------|-------|-------------|------------------------|-------|
| Source-port spoofing | **Yes (built-in)** | Manual via `-p` | No | Yes (raw) |
| Cross-platform | Windows-focused | Unix + Windows | Unix/Linux | Unix + Windows |
| Persistence (`-p`) | **Yes** | Limited | Yes (always) | Yes |
| Built-in auth (`-l`) | **Yes** | No | Yes (SSH keys) | Limited |
| UDP support | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| KTU exam relevance | **High** | High | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FPipe Operational Architecture — Block-Level Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete FPipe redirection architecture, including attacker, pivot, firewall decision points, and the internal target.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["ATTACKER 203.0.113.45"] -->|TCP RDP request to 53| FW1["PERIMETER FIREWALL ACL Allows src-port 53"]
    FW1 -->|Permitted| P1["PIVOT HOST 192.168.1.20 FPipe LISTENER on port 53"]
    P1 -->|Accept client leg| P2["FPipe PROCESS Bidirectional Pipe"]
    P2 -->|Outbound TCP src-port 53| FW2["INTERNAL FIREWALL Trusts port 53"]
    FW2 -->|Permitted| T1["RDP SERVER 10.10.10.50:3389"]
    T1 -->|Desktop stream| FW2
    FW2 -->|Return traffic| P2
    P2 -->|Return stream| FW1
    FW1 -->|Return| A
```

### 4.2 FPipe Internal Logic — Two-Leg Socket Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["CLIENT SOCKET c addr A port X"] -->|leg 1 inbound| L1["FPipe LISTEN SOCK bind local port 53"]
    L1 -->|accept| R1["RELAY BUFFER in-memory queue"]
    R1 -->|leg 2 outbound setsockopt src=53| S2["DEST SOCKET connect host 10.10.10.50 port 3389"]
    S2 -->|bidirectional pipe| T2["TARGET SERVICE RDP"]
    R1 -.->|loop while connected| R1
    R1 -->|teardown on FIN| END["CLOSE both sockets release FD"]
```

### 4.3 KTU-Ready Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXTERNAL["EXTERNAL NETWORK"]
        AT["ATTACKER CLIENT"]
    end
    subgraph PERIM["PERIMETER"]
        FW["FIREWALL ACL Trusts dst-port and src-port 53"]
    end
    subgraph DMZ["DMZ PIVOT HOST"]
        FP["FPIPE PROCESS Source port 53 Destination 10.10.10.50:3389"]
    end
    subgraph INTERNAL["INTERNAL NETWORK"]
        RDP["RDP SERVER 10.10.10.50:3389"]
    end
    AT -->|dst-port 53| FW
    FW -->|ALLOW| FP
    FP -->|dst-port 3389 src-port 53| RDP
    RDP -->|RDP stream| FP
    FP -->|RDP stream| FW
    FW -->|stream| AT
```

### 4.4 Decision Logic for FPipe Parameter Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"What do you want to bypass"} -->|Egress firewall port restriction| P1["Pick permitted port as -s value"]
    Q1 -->|Ingress restriction| P2["Pick allowed inbound port as -l value"]
    Q1 -->|IP-based ACL| P3["Use -i to bind a trusted local IP"]
    Q1 -->|Rate limit| P4["Set -c to limit concurrent sessions"]
    P1 --> R["FPipe cmd: fpipe -l PORT -s PORT -d HOST -r PORT"]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    P4 --> R
    R --> Z["Test with client mstsc or rdesktop"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for Examiners:** All arrows in the Mermaid diagrams above represent **TCP/IP packet flow**, not data ownership. Solid arrows indicate the **forward direction** of the relayed stream; dashed arrows indicate **internal FPipe buffering**. These diagrams are block-level abstractions and intentionally omit per-packet TCP handshake details (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK) for readability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEOT741]`

**Q: Define FPipe. List any four command-line parameters of FPipe with their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **FPipe** is a TCP/IP port forwarder / redirector tool developed by **Foundstone** that creates a transparent TCP pipe between a local listening port and a remote destination host:port. It is used to bypass firewall ACLs by spoofing the source port of outbound connections.

**Four key parameters:**

| # | Parameter | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | `-s <port>` | Specifies the source port for outgoing connections |
| 2 | `-d <host>` | Sets the destination host IP/hostname |
| 3 | `-r <port>` | Sets the destination port to forward to |
| 4 | `-c <num>` | Limits maximum concurrent client connections |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for parameters — examiner pattern)*

---

#### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEOT741]`

**Q: Explain how FPipe defeats port-based firewall ACLs. Mention the OSI layer it operates at.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

FPipe defeats port-based ACLs by **forcing the source port of its outbound TCP connection to match a permitted value** (typically `53`, `80`, or `443`). A weak firewall sees only a connection from an allowed source port and allows it through, unaware that the actual destination is a restricted service such as RDP (3389) or SMB (445).

FPipe operates at the **OSI Transport Layer (Layer 4)** because it manipulates **TCP socket attributes** (source port) via `setsockopt()` and relays **byte streams** between two TCP endpoints.

*(1 mark for mechanism + 1 mark for source-port spoofing + 1 mark for OSI layer)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PEEOT741]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and operation of FPipe. Discuss its use in firewall evasion with a real-world scenario. (14 marks)**

**Model Solution Structure:**

**(a) FPipe Architecture — 7 marks**

1. **Three-tier model** — Attacker, Pivot (FPipe host), Internal Target.
2. **FPipe process internally** — contains a listener socket, a relay buffer, and an outbound connector.
3. **Socket-level mechanism** — FPipe uses `socket()`, `bind()`, `listen()`, `accept()` for the listener; `connect()` with custom `sockaddr_in` for outbound.
4. **Source-port binding** — achieved via raw sockets or Windows-specific socket options.
5. **Bidirectional byte relay** — using `select()` or `poll()` on both file descriptors.
6. **Connection lifecycle** — accept → connect → relay → teardown on FIN.
7. **Persistence flag** — `-p` keeps listener alive across sessions.

*Suggested diagram: (Refer to Section 4.2 block diagram above.)*

**(b) Firewall Evasion Scenario — 7 marks**

1. **Setup:** Internal RDP server `10.10.10.50:3389` blocked by perimeter firewall; outbound port `53` permitted.
2. **Launch:** `fpipe -l 53 -s 53 -i 192.168.1.20 -d 10.10.10.50 -r 3389 -v` on pivot.
3. **Connect:** External attacker runs `mstsc /v:192.168.1.20:53`.
4. **Firewalls see:** Allowed traffic on permitted ports only — no anomaly flagged.
5. **Result:** Full RDP session established; attacker gains graphical internal access.
6. **Detection countermeasure:** DPI, application-layer proxies, source-port-independent rules.
7. **Ethical note:** Authorized penetration testing only; unauthorized use violates IT Act 2000 (India) / similar statutes.

---

#### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEEOT741]`

**Q: Compare FPipe with SSH local port forwarding and netcat for traffic redirection. List the advantages and limitations of FPipe in modern networks. (14 marks)**

**Model Solution Structure:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis — 7 marks**

| Criterion | FPipe | SSH Local Forward (`ssh -L`) | netcat (`nc`) |
|-----------|-------|------------------------------|---------------|
| Source-port spoofing | Native | Not supported | Manual via `-p` |
| Encryption | None (plain TCP relay) | End-to-end SSH encryption | None |
| Authentication | Optional `-l` | SSH key / password | None |
| Platform | Windows-primary | Unix/Linux-primary | Both |
| Persistence | `-p` flag | Built-in (daemon mode) | Loop in shell script |
| Ease of use | High (single .exe) | Medium | Low (manual piping) |

**(b) Advantages & Limitations of FPipe — 7 marks**

**Advantages:**
1. Lightweight single executable — no installation required.
2. Native source-port binding simplifies firewall evasion.
3. Built-in verbose mode aids debugging.
4. Optional persistent listener with authentication.
5. Effective CTF / lab tool for teaching traffic redirection.

**Limitations:**
1. **Windows-only** in original distribution; no native Linux support.
2. **No encryption** — payload is relayed in plaintext, vulnerable to sniffing.
3. **TCP-only** — cannot forward UDP traffic.
4. **Requires admin/SYSTEM privileges** for raw sockets.
5. **Defeated by modern NGFW and DPI** that inspect payload content.
6. **Detectable** in modern EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) solutions.
7. **Ethical/legal risk** — misuse leads to criminal prosecution.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks on FPipe Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the admin privilege requirement** — FPipe **cannot** set source port without raw socket access. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this is omitted.
> 2. **Confusing `-l` and `-s`** — `-l` is the **listen port**, `-s` is the **spoofed source port**. They are often the same value but conceptually distinct. Examiners check this distinction.
> 3. **Writing netcat/SSH commands instead of FPipe parameters** — The question is specifically about **FPipe**, not generic port forwarding. Marks lost if wrong tool is discussed.
> 4. **Omitting the OSI layer** — Always state **Layer 4 (Transport)** explicitly.
> 5. **Missing the bidirectional nature** — FPipe is **not** unidirectional; it relays bytes **both ways**. Examiners test this in 2-mark sub-questions.
> 6. **No diagram in long-answer** — KTU 2024 scheme awards **2–3 marks** for "neat diagram". A block diagram of client–FPipe–server is mandatory.
> 7. **Ignoring ethical/legal context** — A sentence on *"authorized testing only / IT Act 2000 / Section 66"* earns 1 bonus mark from most evaluators.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FPipe (Module 4, PEEOT741)**
>
> - ✅ FPipe = **Foundstone Pipe**, a **TCP port forwarder / redirector**.
> - ✅ Developed by **Foundstone** (now McAfee); primarily a **Windows** tool.
> - ✅ Operates at **OSI Layer 4 (Transport)** using **TCP SOCK_STREAM** sockets.
> - ✅ Core trick: **Source-port spoofing** to bypass egress ACLs.
> - ✅ Requires **administrator / SYSTEM privileges** for raw socket access.
> - ✅ **TCP-only** — does **not** support UDP.
> - ✅ No payload encryption — traffic is relayed in **plaintext**.
> - ✅ Key flags: `-s` (spoofed source port), `-d` (dest host), `-r` (dest port), `-l` (listen port), `-i` (bind IP), `-c` (max conns), `-v` (verbose), `-p` (persistent).
> - ✅ Modern defenses against FPipe: **DPI, NGFW, application-layer proxies, EDR**.
> - ✅ Always state the **three architectural tiers**: Client → FPipe Pivot → Internal Target.
> - ✅ Always include **ethical/legal disclaimer** in long answers.
> - ✅ Diagrams earn **2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions — never skip them.
> - ✅ FPipe ≠ netcat ≠ SSH forwarding — distinguish clearly in comparison questions.
> - ✅ Default `-c` value is **32**; range is **1–1000**.
> - ✅ Privileged port range `0–1023` requires root; choose `53/80/443` for realism.
> - ✅ Connection lifecycle: `bind → listen → accept → connect (spoofed src) → relay → teardown`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WinRelay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** WinRelay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 WinRelay: NTLM Authentication Relay in Windows Networks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **WinRelay** is an open-source offensive security tool designed to perform **NTLM (NT LAN Manager) authentication relay attacks** against Windows-based network services. It acts as a transparent **Man-In-The-Middle (MITM)** proxy that intercepts legitimate NTLM authentication attempts from a victim client and relays them to a target server, thereby impersonating the victim and gaining unauthorized access to network resources without ever knowing or cracking the victim's password.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a fancy dress party wearing a convincing uniform of a bank employee. A genuine bank employee (Victim) walks up to a security guard (Server) and says, *"Here is my ID badge to enter the vault."* Before the guard can verify the badge, you (WinRelay) intercept the badge, run over to the vault door yourself, and show the same badge to the guard. The guard checks the badge, finds it authentic (because it is!), and opens the vault for you. 

The key insight is: **the badge was never copied or forged** — it was simply *relayed in real-time* from a legitimate user to a target service.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction — Relay vs. Brute Force**
> - **Brute Force / Pass-the-Hash (PtH)**: Requires the attacker to *possess* the password hash locally.
> - **Relay (WinRelay)**: The attacker only *sees the authentication handshake fly by* and forwards it. No password or hash is ever stored or cracked.

### 📋 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **NTLM** | A Microsoft authentication protocol using a challenge-response mechanism based on hashes. |
| **Relay** | Forwarding an authentication exchange from one party to another without modification. |
| **MITM** | Man-In-The-Middle — the attacker sits between the victim and the server. |
| **SMB** | Server Message Block — a Windows file/printer sharing protocol (common relay target). |
| **LDAP** | Lightweight Directory Access Protocol — used to query Active Directory (high-value target). |
| **Impersonation** | Acting as another user using their relayed credentials. |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024)**
> Tools like WinRelay are studied under *"Authentication Attacks"* in the Cyber Security curriculum to teach students how **protocol-level weaknesses** in NTLM are exploited. This knowledge directly informs defensive strategies such as enforcing **SMB/LDAP signing**, **EPA (Extended Protection for Authentication)**, and migrating to **Kerberos**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2. Operational Mechanism — How WinRelay Works

The NTLM authentication flow consists of **three sequential messages** (often called the *NTLM handshake*). WinRelay exploits the fact that NTLM has **no server-side binding to the original connection**.

### 🔄 The NTLM Three-Way Handshake (Pre-Relay)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. NEGOTIATE:} \quad & \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{NTLM Negotiate Message} \\
\text{2. CHALLENGE:} \quad & \text{Server} \rightarrow \text{Client} : \text{8-byte Server Challenge } (C_s) \\
\text{3. AUTHENTICATE:} \quad & \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{NTLM Response} = \text{Hash}(\text{Password}, C_s)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 🎯 The WinRelay Attack Flow (Step-by-Step)

1. **🎯 Target Selection**: The attacker runs WinRelay, specifying a target service (e.g., `LDAP://10.0.0.5`) and a **coercion method** to force a victim machine to authenticate to the attacker's IP.
2. **📡 Coercion / Lure**: The attacker tricks the victim's machine (e.g., using `PetitPotam`, `PrinterBug`, or `DFSCoerce`) into initiating an SMB/LDAP/HTTP connection back to the attacker's host. This forces the victim to send an NTLM `NEGOTIATE` message to the attacker.
3. **🔀 Challenge Capture**: WinRelay receives the `NEGOTIATE` and immediately opens a new authentication session to the *real target server*. It forwards the `NEGOTIATE` to the target.
4. **💥 Challenge Injection**: The target server replies with its 8-byte `C_s`. WinRelay **relays** this challenge back to the original victim client.
5. **🔑 Response Capture**: The victim computes the NTLM response using the challenge it *believes came from the attacker* (it actually came from the real target) and sends it back. WinRelay captures this response.
6. **🏆 Authentication Success**: WinRelay forwards the victim's `AUTHENTICATE` message to the real target server. The target verifies it — it's a valid response — and **establishes a session as the victim**.
7. **👤 Impersonation**: The attacker now has an authenticated session to the target as the victim (often a high-privilege machine account or service account), enabling actions like **LAPS password retrieval**, **Kerberos delegation abuse**, or **credential dumping**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Attack Succeeds**
> NTLMv1 and NTLMv2 (without **EPA** and without **server-side channel binding**) do not cryptographically tie the authentication to the original TCP/TLS session. This is a fundamental protocol design flaw from 1993 that WinRelay weaponizes.

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | NTLMv2 Response | $\text{NTLMv2} = \text{HMAC\text{-}MD5}(\text{NT\text{-}Hash}, \text{ServerChallenge} \Vert \text{ClientChallenge})$ | The cryptographic response that is relayed |
| 2 | MITM Prerequisite | $T_{relay} < T_{timeout}$ | Relay must complete faster than session timeout |
| 3 | Coercion Vector | $\text{Victim} \xrightarrow{\text{forced auth}} \text{Attacker}$ | Required to *initiate* the attack |
| 4 | Signing Bypass | $\text{If } \text{RequireSign} = \text{False} \rightarrow \text{Vulnerable}$ | Target must NOT enforce SMB/LDAP signing |
| 5 | Channel Binding (EPA) | $\text{Bind} = \text{Hash}(\text{Channel}, \text{Auth})$ | If present, relay is **broken** |
| 6 | Privilege Escalation | $\text{Compromise}_{\text{Machine}} \Rightarrow \text{AD Domain Compromise}$ | Machine accounts can have DCSync rights |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipeline Character Ban in Tables**: Note the use of `\Vert` instead of `\|` in the formulas above. This is mandatory to prevent the markdown table parser from breaking. (This warning is meta-pedagogical — exam answers in handwriting have no such issue!)

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Red Teams / Penetration Testers**: Use WinRelay in authorized engagements to demonstrate the real business risk of legacy NTLM.
- **Blue Teams / Defenders**: Study the attack to design **detection signatures** (e.g., abnormal NTLM authentication from non-domain sources, Event ID 4624 with Logon Type 3 followed by immediate privileged operations).
- **System Architects**: Justify the cost-benefit of migrating an organization from NTLM to **Kerberos with AES-256** and enforcing **SMB/LDAP channel binding (LDAP Channel Binding & Signing)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation & Attack Success Matrix

## 3. Mathematical Analysis of the Relay Attack

### 3.1 NTLM Authentication — The Cryptographic Chain

Let us derive why the relay is mathematically valid. The NTLMv2 protocol defines the response computation as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{NT-Hash} = \text{MD4}(\text{UTF-16LE(Password)}) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{ResponseKey} = \text{HMAC\text{-}MD5}(\text{NT-Hash}, \text{Username} \Vert \text{Domain}) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{NTLMv2\_Response} = \text{HMAC\text{-}MD5}(\text{ResponseKey}, \text{ServerChallenge} \Vert \text{ClientChallenge} \Vert \text{Timestamp} \Vert \text{TargetInfo})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic (textual explanation of each step):**

- **Step 1** computes the user's password hash. The user already possesses this; the attacker does not.
- **Step 2** derives a session key by mixing the hash with identity information (Username, Domain).
- **Step 3** produces the actual response sent over the wire. The **ServerChallenge** ($C_s$) is supplied by the server, the **ClientChallenge** by the victim, and the timestamp prevents replay.

### 3.2 Why the Relay is Valid

The target server performs the same verification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Server verifies:} \quad & \text{HMAC\text{-}MD5}(\text{Stored\_ResponseKey}, C_s \Vert C_c \Vert T \Vert \text{TargetInfo}) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Received\_Response}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Since the attacker forwarded the *exact* $C_s$ from the real server to the victim, and the victim computed the response using that identical $C_s$, the server's recomputation will match byte-for-byte. The signature is **mathematically genuine** — the server cannot distinguish relay from direct authentication without additional binding mechanisms.

### 3.3 Channel Binding Equation (The Defense)

To prevent relay, **EPA (Extended Protection for Authentication)** adds a *channel binding token (CBT)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CBT} = \text{Channel\_Binding\_Token} = \text{Hash}(\text{TLS\_Channel\_ID} \Vert \text{Service\_Principal\_Name}) \\
\text{Final\_Token} = \text{Bind}(\text{NTLM\_Response}, \text{CBT})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The CBT is computed from the *specific TLS channel* between client and server. Since WinRelay breaks the TLS tunnel and creates a *new* TLS session to the target, the CBT of the attacker-to-target channel will **not** match the CBT of the original victim-to-attacker channel. Verification fails → relay blocked. ✅

---

## 4. WinRelay Attack Success Probability Matrix

| Condition | Required State | If Absent |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Coercion vector available (PetitPotam, etc.) | `True` | Attack cannot initiate |
| Target does **not** enforce SMB/LDAP signing | `True` | Relay is rejected at handshake |
| Network reachability to target (no L3/L7 firewall) | `True` | Authentication cannot be relayed |
| Victim has admin/elevated session token | `True` | Limited post-exploitation |
| No **EPA / Channel Binding** on target service | `True` | Authentication terminated |

**Composite Success Score:**

$$
P_{success} = \prod_{i=1}^{5} C_i \quad \text{where } C_i \in \{0, 1\}
$$

If any $C_i = 0$, the attack fails entirely. This is why hardening **even one** of these conditions is sufficient to break the relay chain.

---

## 5. Worked Example — Conceptual Walkthrough

**Scenario:** An attacker (10.0.0.99) wants to compromise a Domain Controller (10.0.0.5) by relaying NTLM from a workstation (10.0.0.50).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Attacker runs:} \quad & \texttt{WinRelay.exe -t ldap://10.0.0.5 -c \textasciitilde PetitPotam} \\
\text{Workstation connects to:} \quad & \texttt{10.0.0.99 (SMB)} \rightarrow \text{Sends NTLM NEGOTIATE} \\
\text{Attacker relays NEGOTIATE to:} \quad & \texttt{10.0.0.5 (LDAP)} \\
\text{DC replies with Challenge } C_s = \text{0x4A2F...} \quad & \\
\text{Attacker sends } C_s \text{ back to Workstation} \quad & \\
\text{Workstation computes:} \quad & \text{NTLMv2\_Response} = \text{HMAC\text{-}MD5}(\text{Key}, C_s \Vert \text{stuff}) \\
\text{Attacker forwards Response to DC} \quad & \\
\text{DC validates:} \quad & \text{Match} \rightarrow \text{Session established as MACHINE\\$} \\
\text{Attacker:} \quad & \text{Reads LAPS password, performs DCSync}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The above shows a *relay* — the workstation is not aware its credentials are being abused, and the DC believes the authentication originated from a legitimate, expected source. The entire attack takes approximately **2–5 seconds** end-to-end.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Architecture & Attack Flow Diagrams

## 4. Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VictimWS[Victim Workstation 10.0.0.50] -->|Step 1 Forced SMB auth| WinRelay[WinRelay Tool 10.0.0.99]
    WinRelay -->|Step 2 Forward NTLM NEGOTIATE| TargetSrv[Domain Controller LDAP 10.0.0.5]
    TargetSrv -->|Step 3 Server Challenge| WinRelay
    WinRelay -->|Step 4 Relay Challenge to victim| VictimWS
    VictimWS -->|Step 5 NTLM Response| WinRelay
    WinRelay -->|Step 6 Forward Response| TargetSrv
    TargetSrv -->|Step 7 Session Established| WinRelay
    WinRelay -->|Step 8 Impersonate and Exfiltrate| Attacker[Attacker Console]
    Attacker -->|Step 9 DCSync or LAPS dump| TargetSrv
```

## 5. NTLM Relay Decision Tree (Defender View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming NTLM Authentication] --> B{Source IP trusted?}
    B -- No --> Z1[Block immediately]
    B -- Yes --> C{Channel Binding Token present?}
    C -- No --> D{SMB or LDAP Signing enforced?}
    C -- Yes --> Y1[Validate CBT matches session]
    D -- No --> E[RELAY POSSIBLE - Alert]
    D -- Yes --> F{EPA enforced on application?}
    F -- No --> G[RELAY POSSIBLE - Alert]
    F -- Yes --> H[Authentication Accepted - Legitimate]
    Y1 --> H
    E --> Z2[Quarantine source]
    G --> Z2
```

## 6. Coercion → Relay → Privilege Escalation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Coerce[Coercion Trigger PetitPotam PrinterBug DFSCoerce] --> Auth[Forced NTLM Auth]
    Auth --> Relay[WinRelay MITM]
    Relay --> Session[Authenticated Session as Victim]
    Session --> Exploit1[LAPS Password Read]
    Session --> Exploit2[Kerberos RBCD Abuse]
    Session --> Exploit3[DCSync Privilege]
    Exploit1 --> DomainAdmin[Full Domain Compromise]
    Exploit2 --> DomainAdmin
    Exploit3 --> DomainAdmin
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Exams**
> When asked to *"Draw the NTLM relay attack flow"* in a 14-mark question, students should label: **(1)** the three NTLM handshake messages, **(2)** the attacker as a transparent relay, **(3)** the resulting impersonation outcome. The diagrams above are exam-ready templates — redraw them neatly with clear arrows and component labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 7. KTU Model Question Paper — Module 4 (Aligned to 2024 Scheme)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> When answering NTLM relay questions, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Confusing *Pass-the-Hash* (requires hash possession) with *Relay* (no hash needed) — examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 2. Forgetting to mention **Channel Binding / EPA** as the primary defense.
> 3. Describing WinRelay as a "hacking software" without explaining the **protocol-level flaw** it exploits. Always tie the tool to the underlying *vulnerability* (lack of server-side channel binding in NTLM).
> 4. Skipping the **coercion step** in flow diagrams — examiners want to see the *trigger* mechanism.

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*What is an NTLM relay attack? How does it differ from a Pass-the-Hash attack?* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
An **NTLM relay attack** is a Man-In-The-Middle technique where an attacker intercepts a legitimate NTLM authentication exchange between a client and server, then forwards (relays) the credentials to a *different* server in real-time, impersonating the victim without ever knowing the password. Tools like **WinRelay** automate this.

The key **difference** from **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** is:
- **PtH**: The attacker must *possess* the NTLM hash of the victim (typically extracted from memory using Mimikatz). The hash is then injected into a new authentication session.
- **Relay**: The attacker *only observes* a live authentication handshake and forwards it — no hash is ever stored, extracted, or cracked. The original victim is active during the attack.

*(Expected answer length: 4–5 lines. Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for relay explanation, 1 mark for PtH contrast.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*List any three Microsoft recommendations to mitigate NTLM relay attacks.* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Enforce SMB and LDAP Signing**: Require all servers and clients to cryptographically sign authentication exchanges, preventing tampering and relay.
2. **Enable Extended Protection for Authentication (EPA)**: Implements *channel binding tokens (CBT)* that tie the NTLM session to the original TLS channel, breaking relay attempts.
3. **Disable NTLMv1 entirely** and prefer **NTLMv2** (or migrate to **Kerberos** with AES-256 encryption, which is immune to relay by design).
4. *(Bonus 4th point)* Implement **Network Access Control (NAC)** and segment networks to limit NTLM exposure.
5. *(Bonus 5th point)* Disable unnecessary NTLM-using services such as SMBv1.

*(1 mark each for any three correctly stated mitigations.)*

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: *Answer one full question from the module (14 marks), with internal choice between two questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *WinRelay in depth*

**(a) Explain the operational mechanism of the WinRelay tool in performing an NTLM relay attack. Include a labelled diagram of the three-message NTLM handshake and how the attacker sits between victim and target.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** WinRelay is an offensive security tool that exploits the lack of channel binding in the NTLM authentication protocol to perform Man-In-The-Middle relay attacks against Windows services such as SMB, LDAP, and HTTP.

**The NTLM Three-Message Handshake (2 marks):**

- **NEGOTIATE**: Client announces supported authentication features to the server.
- **CHALLENGE**: Server replies with an 8-byte random challenge ($C_s$).
- **AUTHENTICATE**: Client returns a cryptographic response computed as $\text{NTLMv2\_Response} = \text{HMAC\text{-}MD5}(\text{Key}, C_s \Vert C_c \Vert T \Vert \text{TargetInfo})$.

**Attack Flow (3 marks):**

1. **Coercion**: WinRelay uses auxiliary methods (PetitPotam, PrinterBug) to force the victim to initiate an SMB connection to the attacker's machine.
2. **MITM Position**: WinRelay intercepts the NTLM `NEGOTIATE` from the victim and opens a parallel authentication session to the chosen target.
3. **Challenge Relay**: The target's 8-byte challenge is forwarded to the victim.
4. **Response Capture & Replay**: The victim's NTLM response is captured and forwarded to the target.
5. **Session Establishment**: The target server authenticates the attacker as the victim.

**Diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw the NTLM three-message handshake with WinRelay positioned between the workstation and target server, showing message forwarding with dotted arrows.)*

---

**(b) Discuss the cryptographic reason why the NTLM relay attack is successful, and explain how Extended Protection for Authentication (EPA) defeats it. Mention the role of SMB/LDAP signing.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Why the Attack Succeeds Cryptographically (3 marks):**

The NTLM response is computed as:

$$
\text{Response} = \text{HMAC\text{-}MD5}(\text{ResponseKey}, \text{ServerChallenge} \Vert \text{ClientChallenge} \Vert \text{Timestamp} \Vert \text{TargetInfo})
$$

The server performs the **same** computation and compares it. Since WinRelay relays the *exact* server challenge byte-for-byte, the recomputation by the server matches the response — the server has no cryptographic way to know that the authentication was initiated from a *different* machine than the one presenting the response. There is **no cryptographic binding between the NTLM exchange and the underlying transport session (TCP/TLS)**.

**How EPA Defeats the Attack (2 marks):**

EPA introduces a **Channel Binding Token (CBT)**:

$$
\text{CBT} = \text{Hash}(\text{TLS Channel ID} \Vert \text{Service Principal Name})
$$

This token is generated from the *specific TLS channel* used in the original connection. Since WinRelay terminates one TLS session and opens a new one to the target, the CBTs **do not match**, and the server rejects the authentication. The relay is broken.

**Role of SMB/LDAP Signing (2 marks):**

Signing appends a **digital signature** to each SMB/LDAP packet using a session key derived from the NTLM exchange. Because the attacker does not possess the user's NT-Hash, they cannot generate valid signatures, and the target rejects relayed traffic. Enforcing signing at the Group Policy level (`Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications`) is one of the most effective hardening controls.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice (Defensive perspective)*

**(a) Describe the role of coercion techniques like PetitPotam in enabling NTLM relay attacks. List three such coercion methods and explain their working principle.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Coercion (2 marks):** NTLM is a *client-initiated* protocol. For a relay attack to work, the victim must be forced to authenticate *to the attacker first*. Coercion methods exploit legitimate Windows protocols that automatically authenticate remote users, providing the attacker with an NTLM `NEGOTIATE` message.

**Three Coercion Methods (5 marks, ~1.5 each):**

1. **PetitPotam (MS-EFSRPC)**: Exploits the Encrypting File System Remote Protocol. The attacker sends an `EfsRpcOpenFileRaw` request to the victim's `lsass`, which triggers SMB authentication *back to the attacker's machine*. Works without any user credentials.

2. **PrinterBug (MS-RPRN)**: Abuses the Print Spooler service. The attacker calls `RpcRemoteFindFirstPrinterChangeNotification` on a victim's print spooler, forcing it to authenticate to an arbitrary UNC path (the attacker's listener).

3. **DFSCoerce (MS-DFSNM)**: Leverages the Distributed File System Namespace Management protocol. A `NetrDfsRemoveStdRoot` or similar request causes the victim to authenticate to the attacker's host.

**Conclusion (any minor point):** All three are unauthenticated and work even against low-privilege users, making them potent initial-access vectors for relay chains.

---

**(b) Design a comprehensive defense-in-depth strategy for a Windows enterprise to mitigate NTLM relay attacks. Justify each control.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Layer | Control | Justification |
|-------|---------|---------------|
| **1. Protocol** | Disable NTLMv1; enforce NTLMv2 with EPA | Removes legacy weak variants; CBT prevents relay. |
| **2. Network Service** | Enforce **SMB Signing** & **LDAP Channel Binding & Signing** via GPO | Cryptographically rejects tampered/relayed packets. |
| **3. Authentication** | Migrate services to **Kerberos with AES-256** | Kerberos uses ticket-based mutual auth immune to NTLM relay. |
| **4. Patch Management** | Apply Microsoft patches for PetitPotam (CVE-2021-36942), PrintNightmare (CVE-2021-34527) | Removes the coercion vectors entirely. |
| **5. Hardening** | Disable Print Spooler and EFS RPC on servers not needing them | Reduces attack surface. |
| **6. Detection** | Monitor Event ID 4624 (Type 3) followed by Event 4662 (object access) by *non-admins* | Detects post-exploitation behavior of relayed sessions. |
| **7. Network** | Implement L7 firewall blocking outbound SMB from user subnets | Prevents coercion callbacks from succeeding. |

*(Award 1 mark per layer × 6 = 6 marks + 1 mark for synthesis/justification.)*

---

## 8. 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔁 **WinRelay** performs NTLM *relay* (no hash possession) — **NOT** Pass-the-Hash.
- 🧩 Attack requires **three ingredients**: *Coercion vector* + *MITM position* + *Unsigned/unprotected target service*.
- 🔐 NTLM has **no transport-level binding** — the root cryptographic flaw.
- 🛡️ **EPA (Channel Binding Tokens)** is the cryptographic fix.
- 📜 **SMB/LDAP Signing** enforced via GPO is the most practical enterprise defense.
- 🚫 **Migrate to Kerberos** wherever possible — Kerberos is not vulnerable to this class of attack.
- 🪤 Common coercion vectors to memorize: **PetitPotam, PrinterBug, DFSCoerce**.
- 📊 Composite success formula: $P_{success} = \prod C_i$ — break *any one* condition to defeat the attack.
- 🧠 For KTU exams, always **link the tool to the protocol flaw** — examiners reward conceptual depth.
- 🛑 Remember the difference: **NTLMv1 = vulnerable**, **NTLMv2 + EPA = mitigated**, **Kerberos = immune**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip**
> If a question asks *"How does WinRelay work?"*, always structure your answer as: **(1)** Protocol flaw in NTLM → **(2)** Coercion trigger → **(3)** MITM relay of three messages → **(4)** Session impersonation → **(5)** Mitigations. This five-part structure is the board examiner's expected answer template and scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Reconnaissance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT741 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Reconnaissance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Reconnaissance** is the *systematic, methodical, and covert process* of gathering maximum intelligence about a target computer network — its topology, active hosts, open ports, running services, operating systems, and potential vulnerabilities — prior to launching a cyber attack or conducting a defensive security audit.

According to the **MITRE ATT&CK Framework** (TA0043 – Reconnaissance), reconnaissance is the very first tactical phase of any cyber operation. It involves adversaries actively or passively collecting details that can be used to plan and execute subsequent operations. The objective is **Information Superiority** — achieving a complete map of the target's digital footprint before any exploitation attempt.

### 1.1.1 Sub-Categorization of Reconnaissance

| Type | Description | Detection Risk |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| **Passive Reconnaissance** | Gathering information without directly interacting with the target system. Uses third-party sources (WHOIS, DNS, Shodan, social media). | **Very Low / None** |
| **Active Reconnaissance** | Directly probing the target system using packets, pings, port scans, and queries. | **Medium to High** |

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy 🏠

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Burglar and the Neighborhood**

Imagine a **master burglar** planning to break into a house. He doesn't just kick the door down immediately.

*   **Passive Phase:** He sits across the street in a car, drinking coffee. He observes the family's routine, notes the delivery times, checks the building layout from public blueprints, and reads local newspaper announcements to find out when the family is on vacation. **He never touches the house.**
*   **Active Phase:** He walks up to the gate, rings the doorbell, jiggles the doorknob to see if it's locked, checks which windows are open, and pokes the fence to see if the dog barks. **He has now interacted with the property, and there is a higher risk of being caught on a security camera.**

**Network Reconnaissance is the digital equivalent.** The "house" is the target network, the "blueprints" are DNS records and WHOIS data, the "windows and doors" are open ports, and the "security camera" is the Intrusion Detection System (IDS).

---

## 1.3 Why Network Reconnaissance is the **Most Critical Phase**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Examiners frequently frame questions around the *Kill Chain* and the *attacker's mindset*. Reconnaissance is the foundation of the **Cyber Kill Chain** (Lockheed Martin Model). Without successful reconnaissance, the probability of a successful breach drops drastically.

The famous military principle applies directly: *"Amateurs talk strategy; professionals talk logistics and intelligence."*

**Key Metrics Used in Reconnaissance:**
*   **Footprint Size:** The total digital surface area exposed.
*   **Scan Time (T):** The duration of the probing operation, governed by the equation **T = (H × P) / R**, where H = Hosts, P = Ports, R = Rate (packets/second).
*   **Stealth Coefficient (S):** Inversely proportional to the packet rate and source visibility.

---

## 1.4 The Seven Pillars of Network Reconnaissance

1.  **Footprinting** — The outer perimeter mapping.
2.  **Scanning** — Probing for live assets.
3.  **Enumeration** — Extracting valid user accounts and shares.
4.  **Vulnerability Assessment** — Matching known CVEs.
5.  **OSINT (Open Source Intelligence)** — Leveraging public datasets.
6.  **Social Engineering Recon** — Mapping human assets.
7.  **War-driving / War-flying** — Physical-layer wireless mapping.

---

## 1.5 Quick Summary for First-Reading

> ✅ **In Simple Terms:** Network Reconnaissance is the *recon* (short for reconnaissance) phase where an attacker (or a defender) learns everything possible about a network. The more successful the recon, the easier the later phases of the attack become. It is the **"Know Your Enemy"** step of cybersecurity, borrowed directly from Sun Tzu's *The Art of War*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Reconnaissance Operational Pipeline

Network reconnaissance is not a single action; it is a **multi-stage pipeline**. Each stage produces intelligence that feeds the next. Below is the formal operational breakdown mapped to the **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)**.

### Stage 1: Footprinting (Outer Ring)

This is the broadest collection phase. The attacker maps the *external attack surface*.

*   **Domain Information:** WHOIS lookup, registrar details, name servers.
*   **IP Range Discovery:** Determining the assigned IP block (CIDR) of the organization via ARIN/RIPE/APNIC.
*   **DNS Intelligence (DNSINT):** Zone transfers (AXFR), brute-forcing subdomains, reverse DNS lookups.
*   **Email Harvesting:** TheHarvester, Hunter.io, LinkedIn scraping.
*   **Shodan / Censys Queries:** Searching for exposed IoT devices, webcams, and SCADA systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** In real-world penetration tests, ~70% of useful initial information is obtained from passive sources. A skilled attacker may spend *weeks* in this stage without ever sending a single packet to the target.

### Stage 2: Scanning (Mid Ring)

Once the external surface is known, the attacker transitions to active probing.

*   **Network Scanning:** Identifying live hosts using ICMP Echo, ARP sweeps, and TCP SYN/ACK probes.
*   **Port Scanning:** Determining which TCP/UDP ports are open, closed, or filtered.
*   **Service & Version Detection:** Banner grabbing to identify software versions (e.g., *Apache 2.4.49* — a vulnerable version).
*   **OS Fingerprinting:** Identifying the operating system via TCP stack behavior.

### Stage 3: Enumeration (Inner Ring)

This is the most intrusive stage. The attacker extracts specific details like user accounts, group memberships, SNMP strings, and SMB shares.

### Stage 4: Vulnerability Mapping

The collected data is cross-referenced with databases like **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** and **NVD (National Vulnerability Database)** to identify exploitable weaknesses.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations: Scanning Techniques

The **TCP Three-Way Handshake** is fundamental to understanding scanning:

$$ \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN}} \text{Server} $$
$$ \text{Client} \xleftarrow{\text{SYN-ACK}} \text{Server} $$
$$ \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK}} \text{Server} \implies \text{Connection Established} $$

Different scan types exploit deviations from this standard handshake.

### 2.2.1 The Six Primary Scan Types

| Scan Type | Packet Sent | Goal | Stealth Level |
|-----------|-------------|------|---------------|
| **SYN Scan (Half-Open)** | SYN | Detect open ports without completing handshake. | **Medium** |
| **Full Connect Scan** | SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK | Standard connection; logged heavily. | **Low** |
| **FIN Scan** | FIN | Closed ports reply with RST; open ports ignore. | **High** |
| **NULL Scan** | No flags set | Same logic as FIN; bypasses simple filters. | **High** |
| **Xmas Scan** | FIN + URG + PSH | "Lights up like a Christmas tree". | **High** |
| **ACK Scan** | ACK | Used to map firewall rulesets (filtered vs unfiltered). | **Medium** |

### 2.2.2 The Mathematics of Stealth Scanning

The probability of detection by an IDS can be modeled as:

$$ P_d = 1 - e^{-\lambda T} $$

Where:
*   $P_d$ = Probability of detection
*   $\lambda$ = Packet rate (packets per second)
*   $T$ = Time duration of the scan

> **Implication:** A lower packet rate $\lambda$ exponentially reduces the chance of triggering an IDS, which is why sophisticated attackers use *distributed scanning* (multiple source IPs) and *timing templates* (e.g., Nmap's `-T0` paranoid mode, which sends one packet every 5 minutes).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE** — This is a frequent Part A and Part B favorite.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Engineering Use |
|---------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Scan Time (T)** | $T = \dfrac{H \times P}{R}$ | Estimating audit duration in penetration tests. |
| **Detection Probability** | $P_d = 1 - e^{-\lambda T}$ | IDS/IPS threshold tuning. |
| **Stealth Score (S)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{\lambda \times \sigma}$ | Where $\sigma$ = source visibility (single IP vs botnet). |
| **Subnetting (Hosts)** | $H_{usable} = 2^n - 2$ | Network range mapping (n = host bits). |
| **CIDR Aggregation** | $\text{Network} = \text{IP}_{src} \text{ AND } \text{Mask}$ | Determining the target's address space. |
| **TCP Flags Field** | 9 bits (CWR, ECE, URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN) | Crafting custom scan packets. |
| **CVE Severity** | $\text{CVSS} = f(I, A, C) \in [0, 10]$ | Prioritizing remediation. |
| **OSINT Coverage** | $C_{osint} = \dfrac{\text{Found Assets}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | Measuring recon completeness. |

---

## 2.4 Countermeasures & Blue Team Perspective

Defending against reconnaissance requires a layered approach:

1.  **Network Segmentation:** Limits lateral visibility. A compromised DMZ host should not see internal HR systems.
2.  **Honeypots & Honeynets:** Decoy systems designed to detect and study attackers during recon.
3.  **Firewall ACLs (Access Control Lists):** Block unnecessary ICMP, restrict outbound DNS zone transfers.
4.  **Rate Limiting & IDS Tuning:** Drop SYN packets that exceed threshold $\lambda_{max}$.
5.  **WHOIS Privacy Protection:** Hides administrative contact details from public registries.
6.  **Deception Technology:** Tools like *Acalvio ShadowPlex* and *IllusionBLACK* create false scan responses.
7.  **Log Correlation (SIEM):** Aggregates logs from firewalls, IDS, and DNS to flag reconnaissance patterns (e.g., a single source IP hitting 1000 ports in 60 seconds).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Beyond Hacking:** Network reconnaissance is *not* only a malicious activity. Ethical applications include:
> *   **Penetration Testing:** Required by PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 audits.
> *   **Asset Inventory Management:** Enterprises use the same tools (Nmap, Nessus) to discover unauthorized shadow IT.
> *   **Incident Response:** First responders use recon techniques to map attacker infrastructure.
> *   **Insurance Underwriting:** Cyber-insurance firms perform recon on clients to assign risk scores.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Detailed Walkthroughs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every analytical step is fully expanded. No shortcuts.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Deriving the Reconnaissance Scan Time Equation

The standard equation used by auditors and ethical hackers to estimate the time required for a complete network scan is:

$$ T = \frac{H \times P}{R} $$

### Step 1: Define the Variables

Let us formally define each component:
*   $H$ = Number of active hosts in the target network.
*   $P$ = Number of ports to be scanned per host.
*   $R$ = Rate of probe transmission (packets per second).

### Step 2: Establish the Baseline Logic

A complete scan requires the auditor to send **at least one packet to every port of every host**. The total number of packets sent $N_p$ is therefore:

$$ N_p = H \times P $$

### Step 3: Introduce the Rate Variable

If packets are sent at a constant rate $R$ (packets/second), the total time $T$ in seconds is:

$$ T = \frac{\text{Total Packets}}{\text{Rate}} $$

### Step 4: Combine the Equations

Substituting $N_p$ from Step 2 into the equation from Step 3:

$$ T = \frac{H \times P}{R} $$

### Step 5: Apply the Time Unit Conversion

Since scan times are practically expressed in hours, we apply the conversion factor $3600$ seconds/hour:

$$ T_{hours} = \frac{H \times P}{R \times 3600} $$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An organization has **$H = 250$** live hosts. The auditor plans to scan the top **$P = 1000$** common ports. The scanning tool is configured to transmit at **$R = 100$** packets/second (Nmap's default `-T3` mode).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sec} &= \frac{H \times P}{R} \\
&= \frac{250 \times 1000}{100} \\
&= \frac{250000}{100} \\
&= 2500 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{hours} &= \frac{2500}{3600} \\
&\approx 0.694 \text{ hours} \\
&\approx 41.67 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A standard 250-host enterprise network can be fully port-scanned in approximately **42 minutes** using default settings. This is why attackers prefer *slow and distributed* scanning to avoid IDS detection.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Detection Probability Derivation

The probability that an IDS will detect a scan of duration $T$ at rate $\lambda$ is derived from the **Poisson Process**, which models random packet arrivals.

### Step 1: State the Poisson Assumption

Packets arrive at the IDS sensor according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$. The probability of *zero* packet arrivals in time $T$ is:

$$ P(0, T) = e^{-\lambda T} $$

### Step 2: Define the Detection Event

The IDS detects the scan if **at least one** packet is observed. The probability of detection is the complement of seeing zero packets:

$$ P_d = 1 - P(0, T) $$

### Step 3: Substitute the Poisson Formula

$$ P_d = 1 - e^{-\lambda T} $$

### Step 4: Analyze the Limiting Cases

*   **Case A — $\lambda T \to 0$** (very slow scan, short time): $P_d \to 0$. The IDS *cannot* detect the scan.
*   **Case B — $\lambda T \to \infty$** (very fast scan, long time): $P_d \to 1$. The IDS *will* detect the scan with certainty.

### Step 5: Practical Application

**Problem:** An attacker scans at $\lambda = 5$ packets/second for $T = 60$ seconds. What is the detection probability?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= 1 - e^{-\lambda T} \\
&= 1 - e^{-(5)(60)} \\
&= 1 - e^{-300}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $e^{-300} \approx 0$ (a vanishingly small number):

$$ P_d \approx 1.0 \quad (\text{or } 100\%) $$

**Conclusion:** Even a slow scan will be detected if it runs long enough. This is why skilled attackers limit scan duration and use **source IP rotation**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: CIDR Block Reconstruction from WHOIS

A WHOIS record returns an organization as **203.0.113.0 - 203.0.113.255**. The auditor must derive the CIDR.

### Step 1: Convert Bounds to Binary

Start IP:   `203.0.113.0`  = `11001011.00000000.01110001.00000000`
End IP:     `203.0.113.255` = `11001011.00000000.01110001.11111111`

### Step 2: Identify the Common Prefix

Both IPs share the first **24 bits**: `11001011.00000000.01110001`.

### Step 3: Apply the /24 Notation

$$ \text{CIDR} = 203.0.113.0/24 $$

### Step 4: Calculate Usable Hosts

Using the host formula $H_{usable} = 2^n - 2$ with $n = 32 - 24 = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{usable} &= 2^8 - 2 \\
&= 256 - 2 \\
&= 254 \text{ hosts}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The organization owns a /24 block supporting **254 usable host addresses**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reconnaissance Tool Comparison

| Tool | Type | Primary Function | OSI Layer | KTU Exam Frequency |
|------|------|------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Nmap** | Active | Host discovery, port scanning, OS fingerprinting. | L3–L4 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Wireshark** | Active/Passive | Deep packet capture and protocol analysis. | L2–L7 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Maltego** | Passive | OSINT relationship mapping (entities & links). | L7 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Shodan** | Passive | Search engine for internet-connected devices. | L7 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **theHarvester** | Passive | Email, subdomain, and name enumeration. | L7 | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Nessus** | Active | Comprehensive vulnerability scanner. | L4–L7 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Masscan** | Active | Massively fast port scanner (entire internet in minutes). | L3 | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Recon-ng** | Passive | Web reconnaissance framework (modular). | L7 | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Netcraft** | Passive | Web technology profiling. | L7 | ⭐⭐ |
| **Aircrack-ng** | Active | Wireless (802.11) reconnaissance and cracking. | L2 | ⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: A Complete Recon Attack Tree (Decision Logic)

**Step 1:** Determine the target domain.
**Step 2:** Perform WHOIS lookup.
  * If registrant is hidden → proceed with passive DNS only.
  * If registrant visible → harvest emails, names, phone numbers.
**Step 3:** Run DNS enumeration.
  * Attempt AXFR zone transfer.
  * If refused → brute-force subdomains using wordlists.
**Step 4:** Discover IP range.
  * Convert CIDR to live host list.
**Step 5:** Conduct host discovery.
  * ICMP sweep → identify responsive IPs.
  * ARP scan on local segments.
**Step 6:** Conduct port scanning.
  * Top 1000 ports first → escalate to full 65535 if open.
**Step 7:** Service & version detection.
  * Banner grab each open port.
**Step 8:** OS fingerprinting.
  * Match TCP/IP stack responses against the nmap-os-db.
**Step 9:** Vulnerability correlation.
  * Match identified software versions to CVE database.
**Step 10:** Prioritize targets.
  * Score: $Risk = (CVSS \times Exposure) / Mitigation$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords are used, and labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.1 The Cyber Reconnaissance Kill Chain Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START RECON PHASE] --> B[FIND DOMAIN AND IP BLOCK]
    B --> C[WHOIS LOOKUP]
    C --> D[DNS ENUMERATION AXFR AND BRUTE FORCE]
    D --> E[OSINT HARVEST SOCIAL MEDIA AND CODE REPOS]
    E --> F[HOST DISCOVERY ICMP AND ARP SWEEP]
    F --> G[PORT SCANNING TCP AND UDP]
    G --> H[SERVICE VERSION DETECTION BANNER GRAB]
    H --> I[OS FINGERPRINTING]
    I --> J[VULNERABILITY CORRELATION CVE MATCH]
    J --> K[PRIORITIZE TARGETS]
    K --> L[REPORT TO ATTACKER OR CLIENT]
    L --> M[END OF PHASE ONE]

    style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#51cf66,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Active vs Passive Reconnaissance

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PASSIVE_RECON
        P1[PUBLIC WHOIS] --> P2[DNS RECORDS]
        P2 --> P3[SHODAN DATABASE]
        P3 --> P4[OSINT SOCIAL MEDIA]
        P4 --> P5[GITHITU AND CODE LEAKS]
    end

    subgraph ACTIVE_RECON
        Q1[ICMP PING SWEEP] --> Q2[TCP SYN SCAN]
        Q2 --> Q3[BANNER GRABBING]
        Q3 --> Q4[OS FINGERPRINTING]
        Q4 --> Q5[VULN SCAN NESSUS]
    end

    P5 --> R[INTELLIGENCE FUSION LAYER]
    Q5 --> R
    R --> S[ATTACK PLAN GENERATION]

    style R fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 The TCP Three-Way Handshake (Foundation for Scan Logic)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant TargetHost
    participant Firewall

    Note over Attacker,Firewall: NORMAL CONNECTION ESTABLISHMENT
    Attacker->>TargetHost: SYN Packet Sent
    TargetHost-->>Attacker: SYN-ACK Response Port OPEN
    Attacker->>TargetHost: ACK Finalized

    Note over Attacker,Firewall: SYN SCAN HALF OPEN
    Attacker->>TargetHost: SYN Packet Sent
    TargetHost-->>Attacker: SYN-ACK Response
    Attacker->>TargetHost: RST Packet Sent No Full Connect
    Note right of Attacker: FAILS TO LOG AS FULL CONNECTION
```

---

## 4.4 Nmap Scan Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CHOOSE SCAN TYPE] --> B{NEED STEALTH?}
    B -- YES --> C{TARGET FIREWALL?}
    C -- YES --> D[FIN SCAN OR XMAS SCAN]
    C -- NO --> E[SYN SCAN HALF OPEN]
    B -- NO --> F{NEED FULL CONNECTION?}
    F -- YES --> G[TCP CONNECT SCAN]
    F -- NO --> H{NEED UDP?}
    H -- YES --> I[UDP SCAN SLOW]
    H -- NO --> J[DEFAULT NMAP SCAN]
    D --> K[RECEIVE RESPONSE]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[ANALYZE RESULTS]

```
